title: Influencing Endothelial Cells' Roles in Inflammation and Wound Healing Through Nucleic Acid Delivery
summary: Endothelial cells serve as critical regulators of both inflammation and wound healing by recruiting immune cells and producing proinflammatory cytokines that can modulate inflammatory responses. Endothelial cell proliferation and spreading are essential for vascular tissue formation and repair of damaged tissue, making these processes central to tissue regeneration. The study demonstrates that nucleic acid delivery to endothelial cells represents a promising therapeutic strategy to reduce unwanted inflammation and simultaneously promote tissue regeneration in inflammatory conditions. Polymeric delivery systems can be engineered to control the release and prolong the effects of therapeutic nucleic acids, enabling more precise modulation of endothelial cell function. These findings suggest that targeted nucleic acid delivery to endothelial cells offers a dual therapeutic approach applicable to inflammatory conditions like endometriosis, where reducing aberrant inflammation while promoting tissue repair is essential for clinical outcomes.

title: Unveiling Novel
summary: This article examines the inflammatory immune mechanisms underlying rheumatoid arthritis, a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by joint damage and synovitis driven by dysregulated cytokine production. The pathogenic cascade involves infiltrating CD4+ memory T cells and Th1 cells that create a pro-inflammatory environment by producing TNF-α, IL-1β, and IFN-γ, which directly cause cartilage damage through activation of fibroblast-like and macrophage-like synoviocytes. A critical mechanism involves peptidyl-arginine-deiminase (PAD2 and PAD4)-mediated citrullination of proteins, which generates anticitrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs) that form immune complexes triggering further macrophage activation and cytokine release. Multiple therapeutic strategies target these pathways, including conventional disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine), biologic agents targeting key cytokines (infliximab for TNF-α, anakinra for IL-1β, tocilizumab for IL-6), and B cell-depleting agents like rituximab. The novel therapeutic agent iguratimod demonstrates enhanced efficacy by specifically targeting PAD2 and PAD4 to suppress hypercitrullination and subsequent autoimmune activation. Protective mechanisms involving Th2 cell-derived IL-4 and its inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines suggest that balancing Th1/Th2 responses represents an important therapeutic consideration in RA management.

title: Dysregulated Inflammatory Response to Urogynecologic Meshes in Women with Diabetes and Its Implications
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Dysregulated Inflammatory Response to Urogynecologic Meshes in Women with Diabetes

Diabetes significantly impairs the inflammatory response to urogynecologic mesh implants, substantially increasing mesh complication risk through dysregulated immune signaling. In diabetic patients, mesh implantation triggers an aberrant inflammatory profile characterized by upregulation of the chemokines Ccl13 and Ccl24 (which recruit immune cells to inflamed tissue) coupled paradoxically with downregulation of the anti-inflammatory cytokine Il1a, creating a dysregulated immunological environment. This dysregulation involves altered macrophage and dendritic cell function, particularly impaired apoptotic cell clearance and sustained inflammasome activity, alongside increased expression of M2 macrophage markers (Mrc1, Mertk) and pro-phagocytic signals (CALR, AXL) that correlate with blood glucose levels. The epigenetic regulator Setdb2 strongly associates with the proinflammatory response in diabetic patients by correlating with Ccl13 and M2 macrophage markers, while the transcription factor Fosl1 links Il1a expression to dendritic cell markers in both groups. Suboptimal glycemic control following mesh implantation contributes to this immune dysregulation and impaired host immunity against infection, highlighting the critical need for optimized blood glucose management in diabetic women undergoing mesh-augmented surgical repair.

title: Nanoparticle-Neutrophils Interactions for Autoimmune Regulation
summary:
# Summary

Neutrophils function as critical mediators in both innate and adaptive immune responses, serving as first responders that migrate to tissues and modulate inflammation through multiple mechanisms including degranulation, neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation, and production of cytokines, chemokines, reactive oxygen species, and proteolytic enzymes. In autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, and systemic lupus erythematosus, dysregulated neutrophil activation and NET formation contribute to pathogenesis through tissue-damaging inflammatory cascades and autoantibody generation. Current therapeutic strategies to control neutrophil-driven autoimmunity include glucocorticoids, JAK inhibitors like tofacitinib, BTK inhibitors, and agents targeting neutrophil recruitment and elastase activity. Nanoparticles, particularly lipid-based systems, present a promising drug delivery platform for autoimmune regulation, though certain formulations with ionizable cationic lipids paradoxically trigger neutrophil activation and pro-inflammatory mediator release that can be mitigated by co-administration of immunosuppressants. Emerging therapeutic approaches leverage nanoparticle interactions with neutrophils to deliver immunomodulatory cargo, including liposomes containing autoantigens, which have demonstrated efficacy in halting autoimmunity in preclinical models of multiple sclerosis, type 1 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

title: Adoptive Transfer of Immunomodulatory Macrophages Reduces the Pro-Inflammatory Microenvironment and Increases Bone Formation on Titanium Implants
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that macrophage polarization state critically determines bone formation and inflammation around titanium implants. Rough-hydrophilic titanium surfaces promote M2 anti-inflammatory macrophage polarization, which increases mesenchymal stem cell recruitment and bone-to-implant contact, whereas hydrophobic surfaces favor pro-inflammatory M1 polarization. Adoptive transfer of M2 macrophages into mice reduced the pro-inflammatory microenvironment and significantly enhanced bone formation, while M1 macrophage transfer exacerbated inflammation and decreased bone-implant integration. M1 macrophages produce pro-inflammatory mediators including nitric oxide, reactive oxygen species, and cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-12, TNF-α), whereas M2 macrophages secrete anti-inflammatory molecules such as IL-10 and TGF-β that suppress M1-derived inflammatory signals. These findings suggest that immunomodulatory approaches targeting macrophage polarization toward an M2 phenotype could substantially improve titanium implant osseointegration by suppressing chronic inflammatory responses.

title: Trained immunity in hematopoietic stem cell-derived macrophages is defined by a distinct metabolic and epigenetic state
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that long-term hematopoietic stem cells from autoimmune mice serve as a persistent reservoir for trained immunity, a state of enhanced innate immune responses characterized by increased glycolytic metabolism and elevated production of inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. Using a pristane-induced systemic lupus erythematosus model, the authors demonstrate that bone marrow-derived macrophages from diseased mice exhibit hallmark trained immunity features, including enhanced bacterial killing capacity and increased glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation, both of which are mechanistically required for these enhanced immune functions as evidenced by inhibitor studies with 2-deoxyglucose and rapamycin. Notably, macrophages derived from hematopoietic stem cells of autoimmune mice maintain their enhanced bactericidal and pro-inflammatory properties even after transplantation, suggesting stable epigenetic reprogramming; however, in these trained macrophages the metabolic requirements become decoupled from functional outcomes, as they retain enhanced cytokine production without upregulating glycolysis or oxidative phosphorylation in response to immune challenges. These findings establish that autoimmune inflammation can durably reprogram hematopoietic stem cells to generate myeloid cells with altered metabolic and epigenetic states that perpetuate pathogenic immune responses.

title: IL-33/ST2 axis in diverse diseases: regulatory mechanisms and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

The IL-33/ST2 signaling axis represents a critical immunological pathway with broad implications for inflammatory and fibrotic diseases. IL-33, produced by epithelial, endothelial cells, macrophages, mast cells, and fibroblasts, exists in two functionally distinct forms: full-length IL-33 acts as an intracellular gene regulator in the nucleus, while mature IL-33 (generated by proteolytic cleavage) is approximately ten times more potent and mediates extracellular signaling through ST2 receptor binding. Upon tissue damage or necrosis, mature IL-33 is released and activates diverse immune responses by engaging ST2 receptors on innate immune cells (mast cells, ILC2, macrophages, eosinophils, basophils, NK cells) and adaptive immune cells (CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, regulatory T cells), thereby promoting both Th2-mediated type 2 immunity and type 1 immune responses. The pathway demonstrates complex regulatory mechanisms including post-translational modifications (SUMOylation, acetylation, ubiquitination) that fine-tune IL-33 activity and immune cell proliferation. IL-33/ST2 signaling contributes to pathological processes across multiple disease contexts including fibrosis, autoimmune diseases, central nervous system disorders, and cancer, where it exhibits paradoxical roles by simultaneously promoting tumor development while inhibiting invasion and metastasis, highlighting its therapeutic potential as a target for disease management.

title: Single-cell sequencing of peripheral blood mononuclear cells reveals immune landscape of monkeypox patients with HIV
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell sequencing study reveals distinct immune dysregulation in HIV-positive patients with monkeypox co-infection compared to HIV-negative MPXV patients. HIV-induced CD4+ T cell depletion and immunosuppression during acute MPXV infection resulted in elevated neutrophils and monocytes that mediate intense cell-cell interactions, while CD8+ T cells showed reduced expression of critical effector molecules including interferon-gamma, granzyme B, TNF, and interleukin-2, indicating functional exhaustion. B cell populations exhibited impaired B cell receptor signaling due to absent CD79B expression and repressed MAPK and WNT pathways during acute infection, though plasma cells were paradoxically elevated in the acute stage. HIV co-infection prolonged hospitalization to 18 days (versus 13 days in HIV-negative patients) and significantly increased susceptibility to secondary bacterial infections including gonococcal and purulent infections. Recovery phase in HIV+ patients was characterized by delayed restoration of CD8+ T cells and natural killer cells, while oxidative phosphorylation pathway activation in both B and T cells appeared to suppress protective immune effector functions during acute infection.

title: The Role of mtDNA Mutations in Atherosclerosis: The Influence of Mitochondrial Dysfunction on Macrophage Polarization
summary: Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) mutations contribute to atherosclerosis progression by triggering mitochondrial dysfunction and promoting pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization. mtDNA mutations cause increased reactive oxygen species production and activate pattern recognition receptors like Toll-like receptors and NLRP3 inflammasomes, which drive systemic inflammation. These mutations shift macrophage metabolism toward glycolysis and M1 pro-inflammatory polarization, while impairing the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype that depends on oxidative phosphorylation and mitochondrial fusion. Specific mtDNA mutations (m.4435A>G, m.15927G>A, m.3256C>T) are associated with arterial hypertension, coronary artery disease, and increased atherosclerosis risk. Mitochondrial dynamics proteins including Drp1 and Mfn2 regulate macrophage polarization through control of mitochondrial fission and fusion, with Drp1 promoting M1 polarization and inflammation while Mfn2 activates HIF1α-dependent glycolysis. Therapeutic approaches targeting mitochondrial function and macrophage polarization, including Drp1 inhibition and manipulation of mitochondrial dynamics, represent potential strategies to reduce atherosclerotic inflammation and plaque vulnerability.

title: Skin Telocyte Secretome as Conditioned Medium Prevents Profibrotic Differentiation of Skin Fibroblasts into Myofibroblasts
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that telocyte-derived conditioned medium (TC-CM) exerts potent anti-fibrotic effects on skin fibroblasts by antagonizing transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGFβ1)-induced myofibroblast differentiation. Telocytes, CD34+ stromal cells localized throughout skin, normally maintain tissue homeostasis through their interconnected networks; disruption of these networks correlates with fibrotic skin diseases. TC-CM, which contains extracellular vesicles and paracrine factors, effectively prevented TGFβ1-induced fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition by suppressing the expression of pro-fibrotic genes (FAP, ACTA2, COL1A1, COL1A2, FN1, CTGF) and proteins (α-smooth muscle actin, N-cadherin, fibronectin-EDA) while reducing fibroblast proliferation, migration, and contractility. Mechanistically, TC-CM inhibited TGFβ1-mediated ERK1/2 signaling pathway activation, a key upstream regulator of myofibroblast differentiation. These findings establish telocytes as important regulators of skin fibrosis prevention and suggest their secreted factors as potential therapeutic agents for fibrotic skin diseases, a mechanism potentially relevant to fibrotic complications in other organs including the endometrium.

title: The physiological role of macrophages in reproductive organs
summary: Macrophages are essential immune cells distributed throughout the reproductive tract including the ovaries, uterus, and fallopian tubes, where they regulate critical physiological processes through distinct functional phenotypes. In the ovaries, macrophages coordinate follicular development, ovulation, and corpus luteum maintenance partly through M1-mediated inflammatory and angiogenic pathways and macrophage-derived growth factors such as hepatocyte growth factor and epidermal growth factor. Within the uterus, macrophages facilitate endometrial remodeling, tissue repair, implantation, and decidualization while maintaining immune tolerance, with experimental macrophage depletion demonstrating functional necessity as it impairs folliculogenesis and ovulation. The M1/M2 macrophage balance is critical for reproductive health, as M1 macrophages activated by lipopolysaccharides and interferon-gamma promote inflammation and pathogen elimination, while M2 macrophages activated by IL-4, IL-13, and IL-10 suppress inflammation and promote tissue regeneration through distinct subtypes (M2a, M2b, M2c, M2d). Dysregulation of the M1/M2 balance impairs reproductive outcomes, including implantation failure, while excessive M1 macrophage activation causes chronic inflammation and tissue damage. These findings establish macrophages as central orchestrators of reproductive physiology whose functional balance is essential for normal fertility and pregnancy establishment.

title: Immune Evasion in Ovarian Cancer: Implications for Immunotherapy and Emerging Treatments
summary:
# Summary

This review examines immune evasion mechanisms in ovarian cancer, particularly high-grade serous ovarian cancer, which is linked to endometriosis and BRCA1/2 mutations. Ovarian tumors evade anti-tumor immunity through multiple pathways: regulatory T cells and regulatory B cells suppress CD8+ T cell responses, PD-L1/L2 checkpoint ligands inhibit T cell activation, and signaling cascades involving TGF-β, JAK-STAT, and NF-κB promote immune evasion. However, the article identifies several promising therapeutic targets, including CD20+ tumor-infiltrating B cells and plasma cells that associate with improved survival through tertiary lymphoid structures, IgA-mediated immune suppression involving dendritic cells and macrophages, and NK cell-based approaches enhanced by IL-15 signaling or iron-chelation therapy with deferiprone. Emerging interventions include XBP1 inhibition to restore dendritic cell function, GPAA1 inhibition to promote macrophage phagocytosis, and RXR signaling modulation to enhance peritoneal macrophage accumulation, collectively offering multifaceted strategies to overcome immune evasion and improve immunotherapy efficacy.

title: Perturbations of the endometrial immune microenvironment in endometriosis and adenomyosis: their impact on reproduction and pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are gynecological conditions affecting 5-10% of reproductive-aged women that significantly impair fertility through alterations in the endometrial immune microenvironment. Both conditions are associated with infertility, with endometriosis increasing female infertility risk twofold and adenomyosis affecting approximately 24.4% of infertile women over 40 years. Endometriosis impairs ovarian function, decreases oocyte retrieval, reduces fertilization and implantation rates, and increases pregnancy complications including placenta previa, while adenomyosis is linked to elevated miscarriage, preterm birth, preeclampsia, and postpartum hemorrhage rates. The pathophysiology centers on dysregulation of endometrial immune cells—particularly macrophages, natural killer cells, and T cells—which normally support menstrual cycling, embryo implantation, and pregnancy maintenance through coordinated inflammatory responses. In both conditions, alterations in immune cell populations and function contribute to defective endometrial receptivity, impaired angiogenesis remodeling, reduced maternal-fetal immune tolerance, and poor placentation. Specifically, endometriosis is characterized by increased macrophage density with a reduced M2 subpopulation and elevated M1 macrophages that promote pro-inflammatory responses, whereas healthy endometrium requires a reduction in M1 macrophages during the secretory phase and M2 macrophage-mediated tissue repair and angiogenesis to support pregnancy.

title: The significant role of IL-15, IL-22, IL-37, and caspase 9 in polycystic ovary syndrome: A case-control study in a sample of Iraqi women
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study of Iraqi women demonstrates that polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by a distinct inflammatory cytokine imbalance involving elevated IL-15 and IL-22 paired with significantly reduced IL-37 levels. IL-37, constitutively expressed in immune cells including monocytes, dendritic cells, and B cells, normally maintains immune homeostasis and suppresses inflammatory responses, but its deficiency in PCOS patients may contribute to the chronic low-grade inflammation characteristic of the condition. The pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-15 and IL-22 are secreted by macrophages and T-lymphocytes, and their elevation correlates with inflammatory markers and ovarian dysfunction, while IL-15 inversely associates with oocyte maturity in follicular fluid. These cytokine alterations are consistent across multiple age groups and body composition categories in PCOS patients, with IL-37 levels also negatively correlating with free androgen index and body mass index. The findings suggest that IL-22 and IL-37 hold promise as diagnostic biomarkers for PCOS, and that the inflammatory milieu—potentially amenable to intervention through exercise—contributes significantly to the pathophysiology of ovarian dysfunction in this condition.

title: Genetically predicted circulating immune cells and cytokines reveal the causal role of immune factors on female infertility: a two-sample mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study reveals that specific circulating immune cells and cytokines have causal roles in distinct forms of female infertility. Naive CD8+ T cells, the chemokine MIP1B, and interleukin-17 (IL-17) causally increase general infertility risk, while MIP1B paradoxically reduces ovarian infertility risk, suggesting subtype-specific protective mechanisms possibly through endometrial receptivity. Monocyte surface markers CD80 and CD14−CD16+ cells increase tubal infertility risk, whereas dendritic cell activation markers (CD86+ myeloid dendritic cells and HLA-DR+ NK cells) associate with uterine factor infertility. Multiple inflammatory cytokines—MIP1B, IL-17, and IL-18—causally contribute to cervical and vaginal infertility, though IL-18 demonstrates protective effects in other specified infertility categories. The study implicates immune-mediated tissue damage and dysregulation of maternal-embryonic immune tolerance, including elevated M1 macrophages and natural killer cells in endometriosis, as underlying mechanisms linking systemic immune dysfunction to reproductive failure across multiple reproductive tract compartments.

title: Inflammation in cancer: therapeutic opportunities from new insights
summary:
# Summary

Chronic inflammation is a major driver of cancer development, with an estimated 15–20% of tumor-related deaths linked to inflammatory processes. Pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6, interleukin-1β, and tumor necrosis factor-α promote tumor development through activation of transcription factors NF-κB and STAT3, which enhance cancer cell survival, proliferation, and the expression of carcinogenic genes. Anti-inflammatory drugs, particularly aspirin and other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, demonstrate significant anti-tumor effects across multiple cancer types including colorectal, gastric, endometrial, and breast cancers. The autophagic pathway plays a crucial regulatory role in suppressing chronic inflammation; defects in canonical autophagy or mitophagy lead to uncontrolled innate immune activation and increased production of inflammatory mediators. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting inflammasome activation, metabolic reprogramming of immune cells, and selective autophagy pathways offer promising strategies to reduce pro-tumorigenic inflammation and restore anti-tumor immunity within the tumor microenvironment.

title: Differential Expression of ARG1 and MRC2 in Retinal Müller Glial Cells During Autoimmune Uveitis
summary: Retinal Müller glial cells undergo significant molecular reprogramming during autoimmune uveitis, a sight-threatening inflammatory disease driven by autoreactive CD4+ T cells. Proteomic analysis of retinal tissue from horses with equine recurrent uveitis (a spontaneous model of the human disease) identified 310 proteins with differential abundance compared to healthy controls, with 211 proteins elevated in the uveitic state. In inflamed Müller glial cells, arginase 1 (ARG1) and major histocompatibility complex class II molecules were substantially enriched, whereas healthy cells showed elevated mannose receptor C-type 2 (MRC2) and thrombospondin 1 (THBS1). These expression patterns suggest distinct functional roles: the ARG1 and MHC class II upregulation in uveitic cells reflects their transition to immunomodulatory antigen-presenting cells that secrete interferon-gamma and other pro-inflammatory mediators, while the MRC2-THBS1 axis in healthy cells may support tissue homeostasis and immune tolerance. This differential proteome profile indicates that ARG1 and MRC2 serve as molecular markers distinguishing physiological from neuroinflammatory states in Müller glial cells, offering potential therapeutic targets for modulating immune responses in autoimmune uveitis.

title: Exploring Biomarkers for Malaria: Advances in Early Detection and Asymptomatic Diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews biomarkers for malaria detection, focusing on Plasmodium parasites transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes that cause the tropical disease in humans. The parasites employ multiple invasion mechanisms involving proteins such as GAMA, PfRH5, MSP-1, and EBA-175 to infect red blood cells, where they undergo asexual replication and evade immune detection through antigenic variation via PfEMP1, RIFIN, STEVOR, and SURFIN. Key diagnostic biomarkers include HRP2 (produced by P. falciparum and detectable in serum, plasma, cerebrospinal fluid, and urine), pLDH (correlated with parasitemia levels), hemozoin (a parasite digestion byproduct), and aldolase and glutamate dehydrogenase (involved in parasite metabolism). The infection triggers excessive inflammatory responses through TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 production by immune cells, causing schizont ruptures that stimulate monocytes and macrophages, resulting in clinical symptoms including fever, headaches, muscle aches, and vomiting. Diagnostic approaches include rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) detecting malaria antigens, PCR for Plasmodium DNA/RNA, and microscopy for parasitized red blood cells, though pfHRP2 gene deletion presents a challenge to HRP2-based detection methods.

title: Unveiling the Mechanisms of Pain in Endometriosis: Comprehensive Analysis of Inflammatory Sensitization and Therapeutic Potential
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic pain through a complex interplay of inflammatory, endocrine, and immune mechanisms. Multiple immune cell types, including M1 macrophages, neutrophils, and mast cells, drive pain development by secreting pro-inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, IL-1β) and activating NF-κB signaling pathways that promote neuroinflammation and nociceptive sensitization. Estrogen exacerbates this inflammatory milieu by activating GPR30 on mast cells, promoting chemokine and growth factor secretion, and synergizing with TNF-α to amplify IL-8 expression, while dysbiosis of the gut microbiota perpetuates inflammation through altered β-glucuronidase secretion and lipopolysaccharide-TLR4 signaling. Progesterone provides a counterbalance by inhibiting inflammatory responses and NF-κB signaling, making estrogen-progesterone imbalance critical to endometriosis-related pain pathogenesis. These mechanistic insights highlight multiple therapeutic targets spanning immune modulation, estrogen regulation, and microbiota restoration for managing endometriosis-associated chronic pain.

title: Genetic Variants of Interleukin-8 and Interleukin-16 and Their Association with Cervical Cancer Risk
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates genetic variants in interleukin-8 (IL-8) and interleukin-16 (IL-16) as risk factors for cervical cancer development in the context of human papillomavirus (HPV) infection. While HPV is the necessary causative agent, with high-risk types like HPV16 carrying a markedly increased cancer progression risk, the inflammatory chemokines IL-8 and IL-16 play critical supporting roles in cervical carcinogenesis by promoting angiogenesis, cell proliferation, migration, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and resistance to apoptosis. Specific genetic polymorphisms showed population-dependent associations with cervical cancer risk: the IL-8 rs4073 T allele correlated with elevated IL-8 plasma levels and increased cancer susceptibility, with TT homozygotes showing heightened lymph node metastasis risk, while IL-16 variants including rs4072111 and rs4778889 demonstrated significant associations in premenopausal and postmenopausal women respectively. Notably, several IL-16 variants (particularly rs11556218 G allele and rs4778889 C allele) were previously linked to endometriosis, suggesting a potential inflammatory pathway overlap between endometriosis and endometriosis-related malignancies. The findings underscore how genetic variation in inflammatory mediators modulates HPV-driven cervical cancer risk through altered cytokine expression levels.

title: The relationship between expression level and gene polymorphism of inflammatory factors and sepsis risk
summary:
# Summary

Bacterial or microbial infection activates immune cells including monocytes/macrophages, dendritic cells, and neutrophils to produce elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines—IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, and IFN-γ—which are significantly higher in sepsis patients compared to healthy controls. These pro-inflammatory mediators trigger systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) and can cause excessive organ and tissue damage, while anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-10 attempt to counter this response by inhibiting TNF-α, IFN-γ, and IL-6 production. Specific gene polymorphisms in inflammatory cytokine loci—including IL-1B -511 C/T, IL-6 -174 G/C, IL-10 -1082 G/A, TNF-α -308 G/A, and IFN-γ +874 A/T—are independently associated with increased sepsis risk, with certain genotypes (such as IL-1B TT and IFN-γ TT) conferring substantially elevated odds ratios. An imbalance between pro-inflammatory (SIRS) and anti-inflammatory (CARS) responses leads to multiple organ dysfunction syndrome and eventual multi-organ failure, with IL-6 levels serving as a biomarker for sepsis severity and disease progression.

title: Development of a novel postoperative adhesion induction model in cynomolgus monkeys with high reliability and reproducibility
summary: This study established a novel cynomolgus monkey model of postoperative adhesions (PA) that closely mimics human wound healing pathology. Surgical trauma initiates a well-characterized inflammatory cascade: trauma induces proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, CXCL1, CCL2) and the human-specific chemokine IL-8, which recruit neutrophils and activate myeloid leukocytes through TNF-α, IL-6, and IFN-γ signaling pathways. Concurrently, local hemorrhage triggers the coagulation cascade, leading to fibrin deposition that serves as a scaffold for fibroblast proliferation, extracellular matrix production, angiogenesis, and vascular ingrowth. By day 7, the injured tissue becomes populated with α-SMA-positive fibroblasts, CD31-positive endothelial cells, and CD68-positive macrophages, accompanied by epithelial-mesenchymal transition and collagen deposition. The authors demonstrate that IL-8 is a critical mediator in this process, as anti-IL-8 antibody treatment previously reduced both adhesion formation and endometriosis-related pathology in monkeys, suggesting IL-8 is a promising therapeutic target for preventing postoperative adhesions and their associated morbidities including bowel obstruction, chronic pain, and infertility.

title: Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Reveals Peripheral Immune Cell Senescence and Inflammatory Phenotypes in Patients with Premature Ovarian Failure
summary:
# Summary

Single-cell RNA sequencing reveals that premature ovarian failure (POF) is characterized by a dysregulated peripheral immune landscape driven by enhanced adaptive immunity and inflammatory monocyte activation. POF patients exhibit elevated proportions of T cells, NK cells, and B cells with increased expression of cytotoxic mediators including granzyme B (GZMB), granulysin (GNLY), and the chemokine CCL5, which collectively promote CD8+ T cell infiltration into ovarian tissue and contribute to autoimmune oophoritis. Non-classical monocytes emerge as key inflammatory effectors in POF, producing elevated CCL5 levels and expressing inflammatory markers (FCGR3A, CD16, TNF) that facilitate both lymphocyte activation and T cell recruitment. Mechanistically, reduced expression of transcriptional regulators TCF7 and LEF1 in T cells suggests a senescent phenotype, while downregulation of FOXP1 may accelerate ovarian aging through increased CDKN1A expression. These findings implicate inflammatory aging and aberrant immune cell senescence as central pathogenic mechanisms in POF, with mesenchymal stem cell-based restoration of ovarian function potentially operating through rebalancing of Th1/Th2 immunity and natural killer cell modulation.

title: Role of innate immune cells in multiple sclerosis
summary:
# Summary

Multiple sclerosis is characterized by a complex interplay of neurodegenerative and inflammatory processes involving both innate and adaptive immune responses. Autoreactive T cells and B cells trigger an autoimmune response against myelin-producing oligodendrocytes, resulting in demyelinated areas that disrupt electrical impulse conduction in the central nervous system. Multiple innate immune cells—including monocytes, lymphocytes, microglia, astrocytes, and macrophages—infiltrate white matter lesions during acute inflammation, while dendritic cells serve as critical regulators of T cell activation through both pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory pathways depending on their maturation state. Conventional dendritic cells, particularly the cDC2 subset expressing CD103, accumulate in perivascular regions and brain parenchyma during disease and are necessary for CNS T cell activation and disease pathogenesis, with chemokines such as monocyte chemotactic proteins and RANTES facilitating immune cell infiltration. Environmental and genetic risk factors including HLA-DR15 polymorphisms, Epstein-Barr virus infection, vitamin D deficiency, and childhood obesity contribute to MS susceptibility. Therapeutic strategies targeting dendritic cell maturation and promoting regulatory T cell expansion represent promising approaches to shift the immune response from pro-inflammatory toward anti-inflammatory states.

title: Macrophages: a double-edged sword in female reproduction and disorders
summary: Macrophages serve as critical modulators of reproductive tissue homeostasis through their involvement in tissue remodeling, decidualization, and innate immunity, while their polarization state determines whether they promote healing or pathology. These cells originate from multiple sources including the yolk sac, hematopoietic stem cells, and bone marrow, and can be activated into distinct phenotypes: M1 macrophages produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, IL-23) via TLR4 signaling in response to LPS and other stimuli, whereas M2 macrophages express anti-inflammatory markers and produce immunoregulatory factors including IL-10, TGF-β, and IGF-1 that suppress inflammation and promote tissue remodeling. The biased polarization toward M1 or M2 phenotypes contributes significantly to disease pathogenesis, as M2 macrophages drive angiogenesis and fibrosis while M1 macrophages exhibit enhanced glycolytic metabolism that generates ROS and NADPH for phagocytic capacity. Metabolically, M1 macrophages accumulate citrate and succinate through altered enzyme regulation, with succinate stabilizing HIF1α to sustain pro-inflammatory activity, whereas arginase activity in M2 macrophages depletes l-arginine and promotes polyamine and collagen production. This macrophage duality—protective during normal reproduction but pathogenic when polarization becomes imbalanced—underscores their "double-edged sword" role in female reproductive disorders including endometriosis.

title: The association between immune cells and acute kidney injury: insights from Mendelian randomization
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies immune dysregulation as central to acute kidney injury (AKI) pathogenesis, with multiple immune cell phenotypes serving as potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets. Activated B cell subsets expressing CD25 and CD38 show strong associations with acute renal failure, with hyperactivation driven by IL-2 signaling and CD38-mediated pathways exacerbating kidney injury through pro-inflammatory mechanisms. Conversely, myeloid dendritic cells and CD39+ regulatory T cells demonstrate protective effects by promoting anti-inflammatory responses and adenosine-mediated immune suppression, while plasmacytoid dendritic cells contribute to kidney dysfunction through type I interferon production. Monocyte activation markers, particularly CD64 on CD14- CD16- monocytes, correlate with kidney damage progression, and specific immune cell phenotypes predict rapid kidney function decline. The findings support the hypothesis that immunotherapy-associated AKI and broader renal injury mechanisms involve dysbalanced immune responses between pro-inflammatory effector cells and protective regulatory populations, suggesting that immunomodulatory interventions targeting B cell activation and dendritic cell function could represent novel therapeutic strategies for AKI prevention and treatment.

title: Rac1 overexpression promotes Treg-derived cytokines to mediate choroidal neovascularization in wet age-related macular degeneration
journal: Brazilian Journal of Medical and Biological Research
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Rac1 overexpression in regulatory T cells (Tregs) drives choroidal neovascularization in wet age-related macular degeneration (AMD) through enhanced production of immunosuppressive cytokines. Elevated Rac1 levels in wet AMD patient Tregs promote the expression of FoxP3 and increase secretion of IL-10 and TGF-β1, which are required for Treg stability and differentiation. Co-culture experiments revealed that wet AMD-derived Tregs strongly promote angiogenic responses in choroidal endothelial cells (HCECs) by inducing VEGFA and angiopoietin-2 expression. Therapeutic inhibition of Rac1 or neutralization of IL-10 and TGF-β1 suppressed both choroidal neovascularization and Treg populations in AMD models, while reducing pro-angiogenic factor levels. These findings identify Rac1-dependent Treg-derived IL-10 and TGF-β1 as key drivers of pathological angiogenesis in wet AMD and suggest that targeting this axis represents a viable therapeutic strategy for this blinding condition.

title: Study on HIF-PHI combined with iron supplement in treatment of renal anemia in rats
summary: This study demonstrates that roxadustat, a hypoxia-inducible factor–prolyl hydroxylase inhibitor (HIF-PHI), effectively treats renal anemia in chronic kidney disease rats through dual mechanisms: stabilizing HIF to promote erythropoietin production and iron absorption, while simultaneously suppressing the inflammatory microenvironment. Roxadustat inhibits hepcidin expression by reducing inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) and the damage-associated molecule HMGB1, thereby improving iron homeostasis and metabolism. The combination therapy of roxadustat with polysaccharide-iron complex (PIC) was superior to either agent alone, significantly improving hemoglobin levels, reducing kidney function markers (blood urea nitrogen and creatinine), and attenuating renal fibrosis in adenine-induced CKD rats. These findings establish that HIF-PHI treatment addresses both the erythropoietic deficit and the chronic inflammatory state underlying renal anemia, offering a comprehensive therapeutic approach superior to iron supplementation or erythropoietin-stimulating agent monotherapy.

title: Raman spectroscopy and bioinformatics-based identification of key genes and pathways capable of distinguishing between diffuse large B cell lymphoma and chronic lymphocytic leukemia
summary:
# Summary

This study employed Raman spectroscopy and bioinformatics to identify molecular distinctions between diffuse large B cell lymphoma (DLBCL) and chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), two clinically and prognostically distinct B cell malignancies. DLBCL is characterized by aggressive lymph node enlargement and organ invasion, with the ABC subtype exhibiting particularly poor outcomes through elevated BCR and NF-κB signaling, while CLL predominantly affects elderly patients with slower progression and features aberrant B cells expressing CD19, CD20, and regulatory markers (CD5, CD24, CD27) that promote immunosuppression via IL-10 production and recruitment of suppressor cells. Differentially expressed genes demonstrated that upregulated transcripts in DLBCL involve inflammatory response pathways, protein phosphorylation, and innate immunity, whereas downregulated genes in DLBCL (IL15, VCAM1, CCL4, CCL5) implicate enhanced lymphocyte recruitment and chemotaxis in CLL. Serological biomarkers further distinguished the diseases, with CLL patients showing elevated albumin and white blood cells while DLBCL patients exhibited increased liver enzymes and lipid profiles, enabling development of targeted therapeutic strategies including BCR inhibitors like ibrutinib.

title: Genetic polymorphisms of regulatory T cell‐related genes modulate systemic inflammation induced by viral hepatitis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: Regulatory T cells (Tregs) play a critical role in controlling inflammation during viral hepatitis, with their function tightly regulated by interleukin-2 and transforming growth factor-β, which promote the differentiation of naive CD4+ T cells into Tregs through upregulation of the transcription factor FOXP3. FOXP3 orchestrates Treg-mediated immune suppression by suppressing miR-142-3p, which normally inhibits adenylyl cyclase 9 (ADCY9); ADCY9 then generates cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP), a critical second messenger that enables Tregs to suppress effector T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Genetic polymorphisms in TGF-β1 and FOXP3 significantly modulate inflammatory markers in viral hepatitis patients: the rs1800469 A allele in TGF-β1 increases plasma TGF-β1 concentration, leading to elevated FOXP3 levels and reduced C-reactive protein, whereas the rs3761547 C allele in FOXP3 reduces FOXP3 expression, impairing Treg suppression and resulting in higher inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and Fibrosis-4 index). In contrast, IL-2 polymorphisms showed no significant association with systemic inflammatory markers in this patient cohort. These findings highlight how naturally occurring genetic variations in Treg-related genes modulate systemic inflammation and fibrotic outcomes in viral hepatitis through the IL-2/TGF-β/FOXP3/cAMP signaling axis.

title: The effect of chronic inflammation on female fertility
summary:
# Summary

Chronic inflammation significantly impairs female fertility through multiple pathways that reduce oocyte quality, suppress folliculogenesis, and diminish hormone production. Endometriosis exemplifies this pathology, causing ovarian masses, adhesions, germinal epithelium damage, and reduced oocyte numbers. The inflammatory cascade involves immune cells secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IFN-γ, IL-1β) that activate endothelial cells to promote leukocyte recruitment and tissue infiltration; neutrophils further amplify inflammation by releasing matrix metalloproteinases and reactive oxygen species, while macrophages differentiate into pro-inflammatory M1 or anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes that regulate folliculogenesis, ovulation, and tissue repair. The follicle microenvironment is particularly sensitive to cytokine imbalances, as follicle fluid cytokines and immune cells directly influence oocyte development and ovarian reserve, with pro-inflammatory mediators triggering granulosa cell apoptosis and follicle atresia. Environmental pollutants such as air particulates, phthalates, and airborne metals further exacerbate ovarian dysfunction by inducing inflammatory responses and epigenetic alterations that compromise follicular growth and ovarian reserve.

title: Advanced Therapeutic Approaches for Metastatic Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the complex signaling pathways driving metastatic ovarian cancer, with particular emphasis on the tumor microenvironment's inflammatory and immune-suppressive mechanisms. The TGF-β pathway emerges as a central regulator, promoting epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition through both canonical SMAD and non-SMAD pathways (PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MAPK/ERK), thereby facilitating peritoneal dissemination and metastasis while simultaneously impairing NK cells, cytotoxic T lymphocytes, and CD8+ T cell responses. Tumor-associated macrophages and cancer-associated fibroblasts amplify this protumoral environment by secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-10) and chemokines (CXCL1, CXCL2, CXCL10, CXCL12) that activate NF-κB signaling, which in turn promotes immune evasion, EMT, and angiogenesis through VEGF secretion. The hyperactivation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis confers chemoresistance, while TGF-β-induced matrix metalloproteinase secretion enables extracellular matrix degradation and peritoneal invasion. Collectively, these interconnected pathways create a permissive microenvironment that supports tumor progression, metastatic spread, and therapeutic resistance in ovarian cancer.

title: Pro-Fibrotic Macrophage Subtypes: SPP1+ Macrophages as a Key Player and Therapeutic Target in Cardiac Fibrosis?
summary: SPP1+ macrophages represent a pro-fibrotic macrophage subtype that drives fibrosis progression through multiple mechanisms. SPP1 (osteopontin), a matricellular protein, orchestrates this process by promoting monocyte-macrophage recruitment, regulating cytokine production, and activating inflammatory cells while enhancing their survival, adhesion, and migration. SPP1+ macrophages interact with activated fibroblasts and myofibroblasts to drive extracellular matrix remodeling, with their differentiation regulated by signals including CXCL4, GM-CSF, and IL-17A. These macrophages express multiple pro-fibrotic genes such as LGALS3, CCL2, PDGFB, and VEGFA, enabling them to promote hepatic stellate cell proliferation and mesenchymal cell activation. Importantly, SPP1 silencing using antibody-siRNA conjugates reduces fibrosis and improves cardiac function, positioning SPP1+ macrophages as a key therapeutic target. This mechanism parallels broader fibrotic processes where dysregulated extracellular matrix production—normally essential for tissue repair—becomes pathological, driving organ fibrosis and functional impairment.

title: Harnessing Macrophages in Cancer Therapy: from Immune Modulators to Therapeutic Targets
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) represent a critical immunosuppressive component in the tumor microenvironment, promoting angiogenesis, cancer cell proliferation, metastasis, and tissue remodeling through secretion of growth factors, proteases, and cytokines while simultaneously inhibiting CD8+ T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. TAMs originate from bone marrow-derived monocytes recruited via cytokines including CSF1, GM-CSF, IL-1β, and CCL2, with their differentiation and phenotype determined by local metabolites and cytokine signaling. The polarization of TAMs toward an immunosuppressive M2-like phenotype, characterized by elevated ARG1, VEGF, CD206, and CD204 expression with reduced MHC-II expression, is driven by factors such as tumor-derived lactic acid through HIF-1α-mediated mechanisms and GPCR-dependent ICER signaling. Notably, SPP1-expressing and MMP12-expressing TAM subsets are associated with poor clinical outcomes, while emerging evidence identifies C1Q+ TAMs and FOLR2+ mammary resident macrophages as potential anti-tumor populations that correlate with improved patient survival. Therapeutic strategies targeting TAM recruitment, differentiation, or polarization—including ferroptosis induction through APOC1 inhibition—represent promising approaches to reprogram the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment and enhance anti-tumor immunity.

title: Th17 Response in Uveitis: A Double-Edged Sword in Ocular Inflammation and Immune Regulation
summary:
# Summary

Th17 cells emerge as critical mediators of ocular inflammation in uveitis, orchestrating a complex inflammatory cascade through production of multiple cytokines including IL-17, IL-21, IL-22, and GM-CSF that recruit and activate neutrophils and macrophages while disrupting the blood-retinal barrier and causing retinal tissue damage. The differentiation of pathogenic Th17 cells depends on IL-23, IL-6, and IL-1β, with activation driven by macrophages and dendritic cells that produce high levels of IL-6 and IL-23, processes regulated by transcription factors like RORγt and STAT3. Despite their pro-inflammatory role, Th17 cells paradoxically exhibit immunoregulatory capacity by producing IL-10, IL-24, and TGF-β, and evidence demonstrates that elevated IL-17 levels correlate with active uveitis severity. Importantly, therapeutic targeting of IL-17 and attenuation of IL-21 effectively reduce ocular inflammation, while regulatory cytokines including IFN-γ, IL-27, and IL-10 ameliorate disease by limiting Th17 differentiation and effector functions. The gut microbiome plays a foundational role in Th17 cell induction, identifying potential upstream targets for intervention. These findings underscore the dual role of Th17 responses as both drivers of pathogenic inflammation and sources of immunoregulatory signals, suggesting that modulating rather than completely eliminating Th17 function may represent an optimal therapeutic strategy.

title: Molecular mechanism of tumor-infiltrating immune cells regulating endometrial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial carcinoma progression is regulated by a complex interplay of tumor-infiltrating immune cells with opposing roles in anti-tumor and pro-tumor immunity. CD8+ T cells and NK cells provide direct cytotoxic surveillance through perforin and granzyme release, while CD4+ T cells and dendritic cells coordinate adaptive immune responses via antigen presentation and co-stimulatory molecules (CD80/CD86). However, cancer cells evade immunity through multiple mechanisms: upregulating PD-L1 to exhaust T cells, expressing IDO to convert tryptophan into immunosuppressive kynurenine that inhibits CD8+ T cells and NK cells, and secreting TGF-β and IL-10 that suppress immune activation. Additionally, myeloid-derived suppressor cells and M2 macrophages promote tumor progression by producing immunosuppressive factors, while M1 macrophages support anti-tumor immunity through pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Immune checkpoint inhibitors like dostarlimab and pembrolizumab restore T cell function by blocking PD-1/PD-L1 signaling, while tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as lenvatinib target RET, VEGFR, and FGFR expressed by endometrial cancer cells. These therapeutic approaches show particular promise in high mutational burden subtypes including POLE-mutant and microsatellite instability-hypermutated cancers.

title: Acute Pancreatitis
summary:
# Summary: Inflammatory Immune Response in Acute Pancreatitis

Acute pancreatitis involves a complex inflammatory cascade initiated by pancreatic acinar cell injury and orchestrated by multiple proinflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-18, which trigger systemic acute phase responses and produce circulating biomarkers such as C-reactive protein and procalcitonin that correlate with disease severity. The IL-6/STAT3 signaling axis is particularly critical, promoting neutrophil recruitment through CXCL1 production and driving progressive inflammation; complement components C3 and C5 further amplify this response by regulating neutrophil recruitment and pancreatic stellate cell activation. Danger-associated molecular patterns including high mobility group box 1 and ATP released from dysfunctional acinar cells activate innate immunity, while the damage-associated pattern IL-33 released during necrosis stimulates neutrophil and macrophage infiltration through NF-κB pathway activation. Activin A emerges as a key amplifier of the inflammatory response, induced by lipopolysaccharide signaling and TNF-α production, and directly stimulates secretion of IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8. Regulatory mechanisms including IL-10 and lactate provide anti-inflammatory counterbalance, and a five-cytokine panel (angiopoietin-2, hepatocyte growth factor, IL-8, resistin, and TNF receptor) effectively predicts persistent organ failure in severe cases.

title: Induction of immune tolerance and altered cytokine expression in skin transplantation recipients
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

Preoperative transfusion of apoptotic lymphocytes, generated through 60Co gamma irradiation, induces immune tolerance and improves skin allograft survival by shifting the immune response from a pro-inflammatory Th1 phenotype to a regulatory Th2 phenotype. Apoptotic lymphocytes, when phagocytized by antigen-presenting cells, fail to provide necessary co-stimulatory signals required for T cell activation, thereby suppressing the cellular immune response responsible for acute graft rejection. In recipients receiving apoptotic lymphocytes, IL-4 and IL-10 mRNA expression (Th2-type cytokines) significantly increased in grafted skin while IFN-γ mRNA expression (Th1-type cytokine) substantially decreased, indicating a shift toward immune tolerance. Additionally, lymphocytes and monocytes appeared later in the dermis of preconditioned recipients, further supporting delayed graft rejection. These findings demonstrate that modulating Th1/Th2 cytokine balance through apoptotic cell therapy represents a viable strategy for enhancing transplant acceptance and understanding immune tolerance mechanisms relevant to inflammatory diseases.

title: The m6A reader HNRNPC is a key regulator in DSS-induced colitis by modulating macrophage phenotype
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies HNRNPC, an m6A-binding RNA-binding protein, as a critical regulator of macrophage-mediated inflammation in dextran sodium sulfate (DSS)-induced colitis. HNRNPC is significantly elevated in inflammatory macrophages during colitis and controls macrophage phenotype and function by regulating alternative mRNA splicing, particularly affecting stability of transcripts like Itgb7, which impacts cell chemotaxis, migration, and acute inflammatory responses. Knockdown of HNRNPC reduces macrophage activation in vitro and adoptive transfer of HNRNPC-deficient macrophages substantially alleviates colitis symptoms in vivo, demonstrating the functional importance of this protein. HNRNPC modulates interferon-alpha secretion and inflammatory cytokine production through its effects on mRNA processing, thereby controlling the balance between pro-inflammatory M1 macrophage differentiation and resolution of inflammation. These findings establish HNRNPC as a potential therapeutic target for inflammatory bowel disease and suggest a broader role for m6A-regulated RNA binding proteins in controlling macrophage-driven inflammation relevant to immune-mediated pathologies.

title: Update on the pathogenesis of endometriosis-related infertility based on contemporary evidence
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is the most prevalent cause of infertility, operating through multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms centered on inflammatory and hormonal dysregulation. The condition involves alterations in the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axes, leading to abnormal gonadotropin secretion, elevated cortisol levels, and impaired oocyte quality through increased oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. Endometriosis is fundamentally estrogen-dependent, with increased aromatase activity and elevated estrogen (E2) production driving epithelial-mesenchymal transition, inflammatory cytokine upregulation (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), and immune evasion through ERβ signaling. The gut microbiota, particularly Blautia and Dorea species, regulates estrogen metabolism and communicates with the hypothalamus via neuroactive metabolites to modulate gonadotropin-releasing hormone signaling. Additionally, progesterone resistance in endometriotic tissue—characterized by altered progesterone receptor ratios and reduced expression—prevents normal decidualization and further impairs fertility. These mechanistic insights highlight that endometriosis-related infertility results from a complex interplay of mechanical obstruction, hormonal imbalance, immune dysregulation, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition.

title: Placental mesenchymal stem cell–derived exosomes treat endometrial injury in a rat model of intrauterine adhesions
summary:
# Summary

Placental mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes (PMSC Exos) treat intrauterine adhesions (IUA) by delivering the microRNA miR-143, which suppresses MyD88 and downregulates the pro-inflammatory NF-κB signaling pathway elevated in IUA tissue. In a rat IUA model, PMSC Exos significantly improved endometrial thickness, increased glandular number, reduced fibrosis and fluid accumulation, and alleviated edema and congestion. The therapeutic mechanism involves miR-143 binding to the 3'-UTR of MyD88 to inhibit its expression, thereby reducing both inflammatory and fibrotic processes in damaged endometrium. These findings demonstrate that exosome-based therapy targeting the miR-143/MyD88/NF-κB axis effectively restores endometrial function and represents a promising regenerative approach for IUA, a condition that causes menstrual abnormalities, infertility, and recurrent miscarriages.

title: Targeting Atherosclerosis via NEDD4L Signaling—A Review of the Current Literature
summary: NEDD4L, an E3 ubiquitin ligase, plays a central role in atherosclerosis development through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving lipid metabolism, vascular inflammation, and immune cell polarization. NEDD4L promotes atherosclerosis progression by increasing oxidized LDL (oxLDL) uptake and foam cell formation, while simultaneously driving M1 macrophage polarization, which secretes pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. Additionally, NEDD4L enhances angiogenesis, vascular smooth muscle cell accumulation, and endothelial cell migration through Akt/eNOS signaling pathways, contributing to plaque instability and disease severity. Therapeutic approaches targeting NEDD4L, such as miR-30a-5p-mediated inhibition or exosomal NEDD4L knockdown, effectively reduce atherosclerosis by suppressing M1 polarization, limiting foam cell formation, and restoring endothelial function. These findings highlight NEDD4L as a critical therapeutic target for preventing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, which accounts for approximately 50% of deaths in Westernized countries and is closely associated with obesity and diabetes.

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cell Secretome: Potential Applications in Human Infertility Caused by Hormonal Imbalance, External Damage, or Immune Factors
summary:
# Summary

Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) represent a promising therapeutic approach for infertility by secreting bioactive factors that modulate immune responses and promote tissue repair. MSCs, obtainable from bone marrow, adipose tissue, and umbilical cord, secrete a complex array of proteins including growth factors (bFGF, VEGF, PDGF), anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, TGFβ), and immunomodulatory molecules (PGE2, IDO, PD-L1/L2) that collectively support angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and immunomodulation. When activated by inflammatory signals (TNFα, IL-1β, IFNγ), MSCs adopt an immunosuppressive phenotype that modulates multiple immune cell populations including T cells, B cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells. MSC-derived exosomes, which retain similar functionality while exhibiting low immunogenicity, effectively promote endometrial repair through mechanisms including anti-apoptotic signaling, fibrosis reduction via microRNAs (miR-340, miR-145-5p), and enhanced epithelial proliferation. These findings support MSC secretome-based therapies as viable treatments for infertility caused by endometriosis, uterine damage, and immune-mediated dysfunction.

title: Cross-Talk Between Cancer and Its Cellular Environment—A Role in Cancer Progression
summary:
# Summary

The tumor microenvironment (TME) functions as a critical regulator of cancer progression by orchestrating complex interactions between cancer cells, fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and adipocytes. Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) represent a major stromal component that secrete extracellular matrix proteins, matrix metalloproteinases, and soluble factors like transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) and MMP3, which collectively promote tumor cell invasion, migration, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Tumor-associated endothelial cells suppress anti-tumor immunity by blocking effector T lymphocyte infiltration while facilitating regulatory T cell activation through ICOSL signaling, and simultaneously upregulate FasL to directly kill CD8+ effector T cells. Metabolic competition between cancer cells and the TME, driven by hypoxia and reactive oxygen species, creates a toxic microenvironment that favors immune evasion and supports cancer cell survival through nutrient acquisition and angiogenesis. Cancer-associated adipocytes further promote tumor progression by secreting free fatty acids and adipokines that facilitate cancer cell invasion, while obesity-associated insulin resistance enhances susceptibility to multiple cancer types including endometrial cancer. These multifaceted stromal-tumor interactions establish an immunosuppressive and pro-proliferative niche essential for cancer advancement and therapeutic resistance.

title: Potential Benefits of Adding Alendronate, Celecoxib, Itraconazole, Ramelteon, and Simvastatin to Endometrial Cancer Treatment: The EC5 Regimen
summary:
# Summary

The EC5 regimen proposes a multi-targeted approach to endometrial cancer treatment by combining alendronate, celecoxib, itraconazole, ramelteon, and simvastatin to address multiple pathogenic pathways. Alendronate inhibits multiple matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, -2, -3, -8, -9, -12, -13, -20) that drive myometrial invasion, while also suppressing the Rho signaling pathway to limit tumor angiogenesis and activating gamma-delta T cells for direct malignant cell destruction. Celecoxib targets COX-2 and carbonic anhydrase isoforms elevated in endometrial cancer tissue, simultaneously reducing IL-6, a key driver of cancer invasion and an amplifying partner with aromatase in tumor progression. Combined inhibition of the mevalonate pathway by alendronate and simvastatin enhances antigen-specific anti-tumor immunity by promoting Th1 and cytolytic T cell responses. This therapeutic strategy addresses multiple interconnected mechanisms including inflammatory signaling, matrix degradation, tumor angiogenesis, and immune activation, offering potential to overcome the complex pathophysiology underlying endometrial cancer development and progression.

title: Nanotechnology-Based Therapies for Preventing Post-Surgical Adhesions
summary:
# Summary

Post-surgical adhesions, a frequent complication of abdominal surgery affecting up to 90% of open surgical cases, form through a complex interplay of inflammation, coagulation, and fibrin deposition in the peritoneum and abdomen. The pathogenic cascade is initiated when surgery triggers platelet activation and neutrophil/monocyte infiltration, leading activated platelets to release cytokines, chemokines, fibrinogen, and fibrin that promote excessive fibrin accumulation outpacing fibrinolysis. This process is further amplified by dysregulated inflammation, which downregulates the protein C pathway that normally inhibits fibrin formation, while angiogenesis and pericyte recruitment strengthen developing adhesions. Current and emerging therapeutic strategies target multiple points in this pathway: conventional barrier products like Seprafilm® and Interceed® physically prevent adhesion formation, while newer approaches including tissue plasminogen activator gene delivery and poly(dopamine) keratinocyte growth factor nanoparticles reduce fibrin deposition and promote mesothelial repair by suppressing collagen deposition and inflammatory responses. Notably, nanotechnology-based therapies represent a promising frontier, with nanoparticle formulations demonstrating superior efficacy in simultaneously reducing fibrosis while restoring peritoneal integrity, offering potential advantages over conventional mechanical and pharmacological interventions.

title: Herbal Immunostimulants and Their Phytochemicals: Exploring Morinda citrifolia, Echinacea purpurea, and Phyllanthus niruri
summary:
# Summary

Three herbal immunostimulants—Morinda citrifolia, Echinacea purpurea, and Phyllanthus niruri—enhance both innate and adaptive immune responses through complementary mechanisms relevant to inflammatory conditions like endometriosis. These plants activate macrophages and dendritic cells while regulating cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and stimulating T and B lymphocyte responses, with Morinda citrifolia primarily strengthening innate immunity and Echinacea purpurea enhancing adaptive immunity through T-cell activation and interferon-gamma production. Their immunomodulatory effects operate through key signaling pathways including NF-κB, MAPK, and JAK/STAT, with Phyllanthus niruri notably inhibiting NF-κB to reduce inflammation. The bioactive compounds responsible—flavonoids, saponins, triterpenoids, anthraquinones, and alkaloids—work synergistically to enhance macrophage activity and cytokine modulation. These findings position herbal-based immunostimulants as promising alternatives to conventional immunosuppressive drugs like corticosteroids, which often increase infection risk, offering potential therapeutic benefit for immune-dysregulated inflammatory disorders.

title: Exosomes derived from a mesenchymal-like endometrial regenerative cells ameliorate renal ischemia reperfusion injury through delivery of CD73
summary: This study demonstrates that exosomes derived from endometrial regenerative cells (ERC-Exo) ameliorate renal ischemia-reperfusion injury through the delivery and enzymatic activity of CD73, a key ectonucleotidase. CD73 catalyzes the hydrolysis of pro-inflammatory ATP to immunosuppressive adenosine, thereby activating purinergic signaling pathways that are dysregulated in renal injury. The therapeutic mechanism involves CD73-mediated macrophage polarization from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes through MAPK pathway regulation, coupled with reduced production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and increased anti-inflammatory IL-10. CD73-positive exosomes significantly improved renal function markers including serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen while reducing tubular epithelial cell necrosis and tissue damage. These findings highlight CD73 as a critical mediator within ERC-Exo's immunomodulatory potential, suggesting exosome-based cell-free therapy as a promising approach for treating acute kidney injury and preventing graft dysfunction following renal transplantation.

title: The TRPA1 cation channel is upregulated by cigarette smoke in mouse and human macrophages modulating lung inflammation
summary:
# Summary

Cigarette smoke upregulates the TRPA1 cation channel in both mouse and human alveolar macrophages, where it functions as a key modulator of inflammatory responses. TRPA1 activation by cigarette smoke components such as acrolein triggers calcium influx in macrophages and regulates the production of both pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-23) and anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10, TGF-β), with TRPA1 expression correlating with TGF-β increases during chronic smoke exposure. Selective TRPA1 antagonists (AP18, HC-030031) successfully inhibit smoke-induced macrophage activation and airway inflammation, while TRPA1-deficient mice develop emphysema more slowly and show altered cytokine profiles compared to wildtype animals exposed to cigarette smoke extract. These findings establish TRPA1 as a critical regulator of macrophage-mediated lung inflammation in response to cigarette smoke, positioning the channel as a potential therapeutic target for smoking-related lung diseases including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

title: Thymic stromal lymphopoietin contributes to endometriotic lesion proliferation and disease-associated inflammation
journal: The Journal of Immunology Author Choice
summary: Thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP) is an alarmin that drives endometriosis progression through promotion of cell proliferation and type 2 helper T cell (Th2) inflammation. TSLP is elevated in endometriotic lesions, peritoneal fluid, and serum of patients with endometriosis, where it is secreted by stromal and epithelial cells in response to IL-1β, IL-4, IL-13, and 17β-estradiol. Upon binding to its receptor complex, TSLP activates JAK-STAT signaling pathways that promote stromal cell proliferation and inhibit apoptosis while simultaneously inducing Th2 immune responses that produce IL-4, IL-5, IL-9, and IL-13, which form a positive feedback loop by further stimulating TSLP secretion. These Th2 cytokines also induce release of chemotactic and proangiogenic factors like eotaxin from endometriotic stromal cells, thereby contributing to lesion growth and chronic inflammation. The altered Th2-dominant immune response mediated by TSLP impairs the body's ability to clear ectopic endometrial tissue and promotes lesion establishment and progression. This mechanistic understanding identifies TSLP as a key therapeutic target for modulating the pathologic immune response in endometriosis.

title: Effect of novel IL-8 gene mutation on its protein structure and stability among ovarian cancer patients in Saudi Arabia
summary: Interleukin-8 (IL-8), a pro-inflammatory chemokine with angiogenic properties, plays a central role in ovarian cancer development and progression by promoting tumor growth, invasion, metastasis, and angiogenesis through interactions with CXCR1 and CXCR2 receptors. Elevated IL-8 levels in serum, ascitic fluid, and tumor tissues are strongly associated with advanced disease stages and poor clinical outcomes, while IL-8 also inhibits apoptosis and contributes to chemotherapy resistance. This study identified a novel non-synonymous single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP), IL-8 c.193G>A (Glu65Leu) in exon 2, present in 25% of Saudi Arabian ovarian cancer patients, which computational analyses predict causes deleterious effects on protein structure and stability. The Glu65Leu substitution disrupts hydrogen bonds and buried interactions critical for protein folding, potentially compromising IL-8's capacity to bind its receptors and activate downstream signaling. Previous studies have also linked other IL-8 polymorphisms (−251 A/T and +781 C/T) to ovarian cancer susceptibility through altered IL-8 expression and pro-inflammatory responses. These findings suggest that IL-8 genetic variants may represent important risk factors for ovarian cancer development, particularly in specific populations.

title: Targeting macrophage polarization by inhibiting Pim2 alleviates inflammatory arthritis via metabolic reprogramming
summary: This study identifies Pim2 kinase as a critical regulator of inflammatory arthritis through its control of macrophage metabolic reprogramming and polarization. Pim2 directly phosphorylates three key glycolytic enzymes—PGK1, PDHA1, and PFKFB2—to promote glycolysis and drive macrophages toward the proinflammatory M1 phenotype, which produces elevated levels of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. Inhibition of Pim2 using the small molecule inhibitor HJ-PI01 or the repurposed drug bexarotene suppresses glycolysis and shifts macrophage polarization toward the immunosuppressive M2 phenotype, which expresses anti-inflammatory mediators including IL-10 and TGF-β. Notably, Pim2 expression is significantly elevated in synovial tissue and CD68+ macrophages from rheumatoid arthritis patients and correlates with disease activity scores, establishing clinical relevance. A nanoparticle-based delivery system incorporating bexarotene demonstrates therapeutic efficacy in animal models, suggesting that targeting the Pim2-glycolysis axis represents a promising metabolic immunotherapy for inflammatory arthritis.

title: Inflammation-triggered Gli1+ stem cells engage with extracellular vesicles to prime aberrant neutrophils to exacerbate periodontal immunopathology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that inflammation-activated Gli1+ stem cells drive periodontal immunopathology primarily through extracellular vesicle (EV)-mediated neutrophil recruitment and aberrant activation. Upon inflammatory challenge, Gli1+ cells release EVs carrying surface-bound CXCL1, which engages CXCR2 on neutrophils to promote their migration, degranulation, autophagy, and neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation while simultaneously inhibiting apoptosis—processes that sustain pathological inflammation and alveolar bone destruction. The EVs stimulate neutrophil activation through reactive oxygen species generation and NF-κB signaling, leading to elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6). Genetic ablation of Gli1+ cells or pharmacological inhibition of EV release via Rab27a deletion significantly reduces neutrophil infiltration, NET formation, and alveolar bone loss, demonstrating that this Gli1+ cell-EV-neutrophil axis is critical for disease pathogenesis. These findings identify extracellular vesicles and the CXCL1-CXCR2 interaction as potential therapeutic targets, with GANT61 showing promise in ameliorating periodontal inflammation and bone loss.

title: Tissue adhesion after surgical interventions (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Postoperative tissue adhesions represent a significant clinical complication that can develop following surgical trauma, particularly due to peritoneal damage occurring in 70-100% of patients, and are associated with endometriosis, peritonitis, and inflammatory conditions. These adhesions cause substantial morbidity, manifesting as severe pain, nausea, reproductive dysfunction, chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and intestinal obstruction through disrupted organ mobility. The pathophysiology involves a coordinated inflammatory response where immune infiltration releases cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, TGF-β) and angiogenic factors (VEGF) that activate mesothelial cells, promote mesothelial-to-mesenchymal transition via TGFβ1, and drive excessive extracellular matrix deposition of collagen, fibronectin, and hyaluronic acid. Critical to adhesion resolution is the balance between fibrinolysis (mediated by tPA and uPA-driven plasmin activation) and fibrosis prevention; severe adhesions are characterized by elevated plasminogen activator inhibitor and reduced tissue plasminogen activator, tipping this balance toward pathological fibrosis. Therapeutic strategies target multiple pathways: VEGF inhibitors reduce angiogenic tissue proliferation, neurokinin receptor-1 antagonism increases matrix metalloproteinase activity to promote extracellular matrix degradation, and surgical techniques minimizing CO₂ insufflation pressure reduce peritoneal ischemia and trauma during laparoscopy.

title: Beyond cancer: The potential application of CD47-based therapy in non-cancer diseases
summary:
# Summary

CD47 is a "don't eat me" signal that interacts with integrin and signal regulatory protein alpha (SIRPα) to suppress macrophage phagocytosis by recruiting phosphatases SHP1 and SHP2, thereby inhibiting immune clearance in atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction, and pulmonary hypertension. CD47 expression is elevated in inflammatory conditions including atherosclerotic plaques and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, with upregulation driven by TNF-α through the NF-κB1 pathway and stabilization via the lncRNA MIAT-miR-149-5p axis. Anti-CD47 antibodies, SHP1 inhibitors, and engineered macrophages enhance efferocytosis and phagocytosis while reducing inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IFN-γ) and tissue damage, with combination therapies using atorvastatin showing improved outcomes in atherosclerotic plaques and myocardial infarction models. These findings support CD47-targeted immunotherapy as a potential treatment strategy for non-cancer inflammatory diseases, though clinical development remains limited by hematotoxicity concerns.

title: A Noninvasive Menstrual Blood-Based Diagnostic Platform for Endometriosis Using Digital Droplet Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay and Single-Cell RNA Sequencing
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth that causes recurrent bleeding and pain, with significant impacts on reproductive health. This study developed a novel noninvasive diagnostic platform using digital droplet enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ddELISA) enhanced with SiO2 nanoparticles to detect endometriosis-associated inflammatory markers—including IL-6, IL-10, and osteopontin (OPN)—in menstrual blood samples. Single-cell RNA sequencing analysis revealed distinct immune cell dysregulation in ectopic endometrium, including reduced B and NK cells but elevated macrophages, T cells, and mast cells compared to normal tissue. The study demonstrated that endometriosis ectopic and menstrual blood endometrial stromal cells promote M2 macrophage polarization and upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, IL-6, OPN), with OPN implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis through the RhoA/ROS signaling pathway. Menstrual blood emerged as a reliable biofluid source that replicates key inflammatory properties of endometriosis tissue, offering potential for accessible, rapid, and accurate noninvasive diagnosis. The ddELISA platform with enhanced sensitivity represents a promising diagnostic tool for endometriosis detection and could facilitate earlier identification of this debilitating condition.

title: Interleukin‐8/Matrix Metalloproteinase‐9 Axis Impairs Wound Healing in Type 2 Diabetes through Neutrophil Extracellular Traps‐Fibroblast Crosstalk
summary:
# Summary

In type 2 diabetes, neutrophils release elevated levels of neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) containing high concentrations of interleukin-8 (IL-8) and matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9), which activate fibroblasts and establish a pathological autocrine/paracrine feedback loop. Diabetic NETs induce fibroblasts to adopt a profibrotic phenotype characterized by increased collagen, α-smooth muscle actin, and CCN2/CTGF production, yet paradoxically these fibroblasts exhibit impaired migration and wound healing capacity due to NET-mediated effects. IL-8 acts as a central amplifier in this cascade, inducing MMP-9 production in neutrophils while simultaneously promoting its own expression, leading to excessive collagen degradation via MMP-9-mediated collagenase activity. These findings are corroborated in diabetic foot ulcers, where elevated NET-associated MMP-9 and IL-8 colocalize with neutrophil markers and correlate with poor glycemic control. Blocking IL-8 or MMP-9 with specific inhibitors, or dismantling the NET scaffold with DNase I, effectively restores fibroblast wound healing capacity, suggesting these represent viable therapeutic targets for improving diabetic wound repair.

title: Comparing surface immune markers in successful and non-viable ART pregnancies on the day of hCG measurement: a prospective pilot study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective pilot study examined surface immune markers in peripheral blood on the day of hCG measurement to identify immunological differences between successful and non-viable assisted reproductive technology (ART) pregnancies.
