The research revealed that miscarriage cases were characterized by reduced peripheral NK (pNK) cell CD69+ activation (1.6% versus 5.4%, P=0.02) and elevated CD57+ NK cells (45.4% versus 38.9%, P=0.025), with these markers identified as potential predictors of pregnancy loss risk. The study demonstrated that successful pregnancy maintenance involves a coordinated immune response including elevated B lymphocytes, Th2-skewed cytokine production (IL-4, IL-10, IL-13), and regulatory T cell (Treg) expansion, while pregnancy failure is associated with immune dysregulation characterized by insufficient Treg numbers, reduced NK cell activation, and elevated hyperpotent cytotoxic NK cells. Notably, three peripheral immune markers—Th1 percentage, NKT cells, and total CD57+—showed strong correlation with endometrial immune expression, suggesting that peripheral blood immunophenotyping may serve as an accessible biomarker for identifying patients at risk of adverse reproductive outcomes. These findings support immune dysregulation, particularly involving NK cell dysfunction and inadequate Treg-mediated maternal tolerance to fetal alloantigens, as a significant causative mechanism of pregnancy failure in ART cycles.

title: Association Between Circulating Cytokines and Endometriosis: A Mendelian Randomization Study
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies TRAIL (TNF-related apoptosis-inducing ligand) as a protective factor against endometriosis, with elevated TRAIL levels significantly reducing disease risk. Patients with endometriosis exhibit suppressed TRAIL in plasma and peritoneal fluid, which normally triggers apoptosis through death receptors DR4 and DR5, but endometriotic lesions show reduced expression of these pro-apoptotic receptors alongside elevated decoy receptors (DcR1/DcR2) that competitively inhibit apoptosis. The study reveals that pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and G-CSF downregulate TRAIL while upregulating matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP9) in neutrophils, and that interferons IFN-γ and IFN-α/β normally upregulate TRAIL expression. Additionally, DSG2 (desmoglein-2), a TRAIL-responsive adhesion molecule reduced in endometriotic tissue, participates in cGMP-PKG signaling and epithelial integrity; TRAIL-induced cleavage of DSG2 sensitizes cells to apoptosis by downregulating anti-apoptotic proteins Bcl-XL and Mcl-1. Endometriotic lesions further evade immune surveillance by releasing exosomes expressing immune suppressors (MICA/B, ULBP1-3, FasL) that inhibit NK cell cytotoxicity, while maintaining elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, IL-1β, TNF-α) that promote ectopic cell survival and establish a pathological inflammatory microenvironment.

title: Dual-targeted microbubbles for atherosclerosis therapy: Inducing M1 macrophage apoptosis by inhibiting telomerase activity
summary: This study presents a dual-targeted microbubble therapy (Ab-MMB1532) designed to treat atherosclerosis by selectively eliminating pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages through telomerase inhibition. The therapeutic agent combines a telomerase inhibitor (BIBR1532) with antibody-conjugated microbubbles containing magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, enabling CD86-targeted accumulation within atherosclerotic plaques. Ab-MMB1532 inhibits telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT) expression and subsequently induces caspase-3-mediated apoptosis in M1 macrophages while simultaneously suppressing the NF-κB signaling pathway, which normally amplifies pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6. Treatment with BIBR1532 downregulates key inflammatory genes (Tnf, Mmp-9) and anti-apoptotic genes (Clu, Bcl2a1d) while altering cellular metabolism through reduced nucleotide precursors, promoting both apoptosis and cellular senescence. In vivo, Ab-MMB1532 achieved a clinically significant 25.4% reduction in plaque burden compared to controls, establishing this approach as a promising anti-atherosclerotic strategy that specifically targets the pathogenic M1 macrophage population driving disease progression.

title: Impact of a Heterozygous C1RR301P/WT Mutation on Collagen Metabolism and Inflammatory Response in Human Gingival Fibroblasts
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that a heterozygous C1RR301P mutation in the C1R gene, which encodes a component of the classical complement pathway, significantly alters collagen metabolism and inflammatory responses in human gingival fibroblasts. Mutant fibroblasts (mhGFBs) exhibited reduced C1r protein levels alongside paradoxically elevated mRNA expression of procollagen α1(V), MMP-1, and IL-6, ultimately resulting in decreased collagen deposition and accumulation of unfolded collagens. The mutation renders fibroblasts unresponsive to inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-1β—which normally stimulate C1R/C1S and collagen gene expression in wild-type cells—suggesting dysregulated transcriptional regulation in the mutant context. These findings reveal that the C1RR301P mutation disrupts both the complement cascade and collagen homeostasis, providing mechanistic insights into how periodontal Ehlers-Danlos syndrome pathophysiology involves impaired matrix protein degradation and aberrant immune responses that compromise connective tissue integrity.

title: The Role of Killer Ig-like Receptors in Diseases from A to Z
summary:
# Summary

Killer Ig-like receptors (KIRs) are transmembrane glycoproteins expressed on natural killer cells and CD8+ T cells that regulate immune homeostasis through interactions with HLA class I molecules. The balance between inhibitory KIRs—which prevent NK cell attack on healthy cells expressing normal HLA—and activating KIRs that stimulate cytotoxic responses is critical for maintaining immune tolerance while enabling responses against infected or neoplastic cells. Specific combinations of KIR alleles and their corresponding HLA ligands influence disease susceptibility, progression, and outcomes across multiple pathologies including autoimmune disorders, cancer, and infections. An imbalance favoring inhibitory KIR-HLA interactions can dampen protective NK cell responses and promote autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis, whereas loss of HLA class I expression on tumor cells triggers NK cell activation. NK cells also produce inflammatory cytokines including interferon-γ and tumor necrosis factor-α when activated, contributing to both protective and pathogenic immune responses. These findings underscore the central role of KIR-HLA interactions in shaping individual immune competence and disease susceptibility across diverse conditions.

title: Mechanisms underlying the promotion of papillary thyroid carcinoma occurrence and progression by Hashimoto’s thyroiditis
summary:
# Summary

Hashimoto's thyroiditis (HT), an autoimmune condition driven by T-cell infiltration, chemokines, and cytokines, is significantly associated with papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) development, with epidemiological studies reporting comorbidity rates between 5-85%. The pathogenic mechanism involves dysregulation of regulatory T cells (Tregs), which are reduced in HT patients, allowing autoreactive CD4+ T cells and Th1/Th17 cells to promote a pro-inflammatory thyroid microenvironment through IFN-γ-mediated CXCR3A-CXCL10 signaling and secretion of TNF-α and IL-17. Chronic inflammation activates multiple oncogenic pathways including NF-κB signaling while cytokines, chemokines, and growth factors promote cell proliferation, inhibit apoptosis, and foster angiogenesis, creating conditions favorable for PTC initiation and progression. Paradoxically, HT in BRAF wild-type PTC acts as an independent protective factor, reducing recurrence risk and associated with lower rates of lymph node metastases and extrathyroidal extension, though patients with concurrent HT and PTC show increased multifocality. Key molecular drivers of PTC development include TSH stimulation, RET/PTC gene rearrangements, and dysregulated STAT6 and DMBT1 expression, while reduced BACH2 function and upregulated PD-1/PD-L1 expression further compromise immune tolerance.

title: The Mechanism of Xuanyu Tongjing Decoction Regulating NOD/NFκB Pathway to Inhibit Ectopic Tissue Inflammation to Reduce Ovarian Damage in Rats with Ovarian Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Xuanyu Tongjing Decoction (XYTJD), a traditional Chinese medicine, effectively treats ovarian endometriosis by suppressing the NOD/NFκB inflammatory pathway in ectopic tissues. The decoction downregulates expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-1β, which are elevated in endometriotic lesions and drive inflammation-related ovarian damage including dysmenorrhea, pelvic adhesions, and impaired follicle formation. XYTJD simultaneously upregulates the cytoprotective cAMP/PI3K/AKT pathway while reducing expression of apoptotic factors, thereby protecting oocyte formation and restoring ovarian function. Three key bioactive compounds—β-Hederin, Proanthocyanidin A2, and Cimiside E—were identified as the primary constituents responsible for pathway modulation. The mechanism operates by interrupting the pathogenic cycle wherein endometriotic lesions produce TNF-α, which activates NFκB signaling and inhibits telomerase activity, ultimately compromising follicle development and ovarian reserve.

title: The Role of IL-6 in the Diagnosis of Neonatal Sepsis and Its Influence on Maternal Mental Health
summary: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) emerges as a sensitive biomarker for diagnosing neonatal sepsis, demonstrating heightened levels in both early-onset and late-onset infections, particularly in culture-positive cases where it achieves 90% sensitivity. IL-6 functions as a central mediator of the inflammatory immune response by activating acute-phase reactants and promoting fever, working synergistically with C-reactive protein and procalcitonin to improve diagnostic accuracy across different infection types, with notably elevated levels in candida infections. Beyond its role in neonatal infection, elevated IL-6 associates with maternal peripartum depression and anxiety, suggesting a mechanistic link between maternal inflammatory activation and psychological complications during the perinatal period. Psychosocial stress during pregnancy activates macrophages through sympathetic signaling, leading to pro-inflammatory cytokine release that subsequently triggers neuroinflammation via microglia and astrocyte activation, establishing a pathway connecting maternal mental health to immune dysregulation. Additional maternal risk factors including infections, preterm birth, preeclampsia, and vitamin D deficiency elevate neonatal sepsis risk, while pregnancy complications drive arterial stiffness through inflammatory mechanisms. These findings underscore the interconnected nature of maternal inflammation, immune function, and psychological health in determining both neonatal outcomes and maternal peripartum mental disorders.

title: Regulatory B-Cells Are Associated Negatively With Regulatory T-Cells and Positively With Cytokines in Peripheral Blood of Pregnant Women
journal: American journal of reproductive immunology (New York, N.Y. : 1989)
summary:
# Summary

Regulatory B-cells (Bregs) play a complex and counterintuitive role in pregnancy immunotolerance, exhibiting a negative association with regulatory T-cells (Tregs) while simultaneously promoting anti-inflammatory responses. PD-L1+ Bregs, which express the immune checkpoint molecule PD-L1, are inversely correlated with all major Treg subsets (FoxP3+, Helios+, CTLA-4+, PD-1+, TIGIT+, and TIM-3+ Tregs), yet maintain immune suppression through production of IL-10, IL-35, and TGF-β. Bregs are elevated in early pregnancy decidual tissue and peripheral blood, where they positively correlate with IL-6 and IL-10 levels, and they suppress inflammation by impairing antigen-presenting cells and preventing dendritic cell maturation. Reduced Breg levels and decreased IL-10 and PD-L1 expression are significantly associated with poor pregnancy outcomes, including recurrent pregnancy loss and preterm delivery. These findings suggest that despite their inverse relationship with Tregs, Bregs represent a distinct immunoregulatory population essential for establishing and maintaining fetal-maternal tolerance, with dysfunction in this population contributing to immune-mediated pregnancy complications.

title: Immune microenvironment and molecular mechanisms in endometrial cancer: implications for resistance and innovative treatments
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the immune microenvironment and molecular mechanisms driving endometrial cancer progression and therapeutic resistance. The PI3K/Akt/mTOR and MAPK/ERK signaling pathways emerge as central drivers of tumor cell proliferation and drug resistance, with mutations in PIK3CA, PTEN, AKT1, and KRAS aberrantly activating these pathways and conferring resistance to standard inhibitors like everolimus and MEK inhibitors. The inflammatory microenvironment plays a critical role, with TNF-α and IL-6 activating NF-κB signaling to promote anti-apoptotic programs, while tumor-secreted TGF-β and CXCL10 foster an immunosuppressive milieu dominated by regulatory T cells and M2-type macrophages that support tumor growth and angiogenesis. Novel therapeutic strategies targeting these mechanisms—including selective AKT inhibitors (ARQ 092, ARQ 751), combination approaches with SERDs, MEK inhibitors, and multi-pathway suppressors like Ovatodiolide—show promise in overcoming resistance. Immunotherapeutic approaches, including dendritic cell vaccines and checkpoint blockade, are being explored, though resistance mechanisms such as PTEN deficiency limiting anti-PD-1 efficacy must be addressed through rational combination strategies that simultaneously target oncogenic signaling and immunosuppressive elements of the tumor microenvironment.

title: Modified Xi-Jiao-Di-Huang Decoction Alleviates Sepsis via Regulating Macrophage Polarization by Inhibiting the PIM2/NF-κB Pathway
summary: Modified Xi-Jiao-Di-Huang Decoction (MXJDH), a traditional Chinese medicine formulation, alleviates sepsis by inhibiting the PIM2/NF-κB signaling pathway and promoting a shift in macrophage polarization from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes. By suppressing PIM2 expression, MXJDH blocks NF-κB nuclear translocation, thereby reducing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and TNF-α while increasing anti-inflammatory IL-10 levels. This immunomodulatory mechanism effectively reduces systemic inflammation, decreases neutrophil infiltration, and restores organ function across multiple tissues including heart, lung, liver, kidney, and intestine in lipopolysaccharide-induced sepsis models. MXJDH treatment improved body weight recovery and prevented organ dysfunction without significantly reducing mortality, demonstrating its potential as a therapeutic intervention for sepsis-related inflammatory complications. These findings establish NF-κB signaling as a key regulator of macrophage polarization and identify the PIM2/NF-κB axis as a viable therapeutic target for managing sepsis-induced multi-organ dysfunction.

title: Peritoneal resident macrophages constitute an immunosuppressive environment in peritoneal metastasized colorectal cancer
summary: Peritoneal resident macrophages (PRMs) establish a potent immunosuppressive microenvironment that promotes peritoneal metastasized colorectal cancer (PM-CRC) by reducing antigen-presenting molecule expression while elevating immunosuppressive cytokines IL10 and VEGF. Two functionally distinct PRM subsets—C1QA+VSIG4+ and SPP1+VSIG4+ macrophages—define this "peritoneal immunosuppressive site" and express a characteristic anti-inflammatory signature including FOLR2, MRC1, TREM2, and MERTK, while downregulating pro-inflammatory genes. This immunosuppressive niche is particularly pronounced in the CMS4 molecular subtype of colorectal cancer and correlates with tumor burden as measured by the Peritoneal Carcinomatosis Index score. Critically, PM-CRC exhibits resistance to immune checkpoint blockade despite MSS status, a resistance driven by these tumor cell-extrinsic immunosuppressive mechanisms. Combined targeting of PRMs using anti-CSF1R and anti-PD1 antibodies significantly reduces tumor burden in vivo, suggesting that macrophage depletion combined with checkpoint blockade represents a promising therapeutic strategy to overcome the immunosuppressive barrier in peritoneal metastases.

title: Global research trends and focus on immunotherapy for endometrial cancer: a comprehensive bibliometric insight and visualization analysis (2012-2024)
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis reveals that the tumor microenvironment plays a critical role in endometrial cancer progression and immunotherapy response, with multiple immune suppressive mechanisms limiting anti-tumor immunity. Natural killer cells and dendritic cells normally combat tumors through cytokine secretion and antigen presentation, while macrophages, regulatory T cells, and myeloid-derived suppressor cells actively suppress anti-tumor immunity via immunosuppressive cytokines like TGF-β and IL-10. Tumor cells exploit the hypoxic microenvironment through lactate accumulation and PD-L1 expression to evade immune detection, creating barriers to effective immunotherapy. Immune checkpoint inhibitors, particularly PD-1/PD-L1 blockers, restore T cell function and show promise, especially in microsatellite-instable tumors with high neoantigen loads, while combination strategies with anti-VEGF agents are emerging as research hotspots. China leads in publication output for endometrial cancer immunotherapy research, though United States publications demonstrate higher citation impact, indicating global research intensity on this critical therapeutic area.

title: Role of belimumab in recurrent spontaneous abortions amongst patients with lymphocyte dysfunction: a retrospective case-control study
summary:
# Summary

Belimumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting B lymphocyte stimulator (BlyS), effectively treats recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA) in patients with B lymphocyte dysfunction by inhibiting B cell activation and reducing peripheral B cell populations. Elevated B cells in RSA patients promote autoantibody production and secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-4, IL-6, IL-17, TNF-α) that inhibit trophoblast cell proliferation and impair embryo implantation. Treatment with belimumab reduces B cell counts and percentages, enhances maternal-fetal immune tolerance, and decreases maternal immune rejection of the embryo, resulting in significantly higher live birth rates (88.23% in belimumab group versus 70.59% in controls) and reduced miscarriage rates. This study demonstrates that B lymphocyte dysfunction contributes to approximately half of RSA cases through autoimmune mechanisms, and belimumab's ability to trigger autoreactive B cell apoptosis and restore immune homeostasis offers a targeted therapeutic approach previously approved for systemic lupus erythematosus and antiphospholipid syndrome. The findings suggest belimumab is both safe and effective for RSA management associated with lymphocyte dysfunction.

title: Effects of cold environment exposure on female reproductive health and its regulatory mechanisms
summary: Cold environment exposure impairs female reproductive health through a multi-level inflammatory cascade involving both systemic and local immune activation. The study demonstrates that cold exposure elevates key reproductive hormones including AMH, FSH, LH, and estradiol while simultaneously disrupting normal estrous cycles and reducing ovarian follicle numbers through activation of inflammatory signaling pathways including cAMP, PI3K-Akt, and cytokine-cytokine receptor interactions. Cold exposure triggers marked increases in pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, and TNF-α) in ovarian and uterine tissues and peritoneal fluid, with the PERK/NRF2 pathway driving progesterone elevation and subsequent hormonal dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. The resulting inflammatory state impairs endometrial receptivity and follicular development while promoting structural damage to reproductive tissues, reduced fertility, and metabolic dysfunction including obesity-associated chronic low-grade inflammation. These mechanisms connecting cold stress to inflammatory-driven reproductive dysfunction suggest that environmental temperature regulation and management of inflammatory responses may be important considerations in addressing cold-related reproductive pathology, particularly relevant to endometriosis where inflammatory dysregulation is a central pathological feature.

title: A signature based on efferocytosis-related genes for the evaluation of prognosis and the tumour microenvironment in gastric cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study develops an efferocytosis-related gene (ERG) signature to predict prognosis and characterize the tumor microenvironment in gastric cancer. Efferocytosis—the process by which macrophages clear apoptotic cells—is closely associated with tumor progression and microenvironment remodeling. Apoptotic cells release "find-me" signals like CX3CL1 and expose phosphatidylserine "eat-me" signals that recruit and activate macrophages, while also producing immunosuppressive cytokines including sphingosine-1-phosphate and TGF-β that promote M2 macrophage polarization. The derived signature based on 133 significantly changed ERGs (SCERDEGs) identifies high-risk patients characterized by elevated M2 macrophages, suppressed immune activation, and reduced responsiveness to both immunotherapy and chemotherapy. Key prognostic genes including CXCL3, VCAN, and ZFP36 regulate macrophage-mediated immune suppression and tumor-promoting angiogenesis. This ERG-based signature effectively stratifies gastric cancer patients and reveals how tumors exploit efferocytosis to create an immunosuppressive microenvironment that limits therapeutic efficacy.

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cells in Veterinary Medicine—Still Untapped Potential
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) represent a versatile therapeutic tool in veterinary medicine, with sources including bone marrow, adipose tissue, umbilical cord, and dental pulp. These cells possess potent immunomodulatory and regenerative properties, functioning through multiple mechanisms including secretion of growth factors such as vascular endothelial growth factor and transforming growth factor-beta, promotion of angiogenesis and tissue repair, inhibition of fibrosis, and modulation of T cell responses and immune mediators. MSC-based therapies demonstrate therapeutic efficacy across diverse veterinary applications, including musculoskeletal disorders (osteoarthritis, tendonitis, ligament injuries), inflammatory diseases (inflammatory bowel disease, mastitis), endometrial pathology (endometriosis and endometrial fibrosis), and ocular diseases (corneal ulcers, keratitis). Notably, intrauterine MSC therapy has successfully treated endometrial fibrosis in mares while showing promise for mitigating endometriosis-related damage through anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic mechanisms. In dogs and horses, MSC treatments have produced documented improvements in joint function, pain reduction, and tissue healing, with enhanced cartilage regeneration achieved through MSC differentiation into chondrocytes. Despite these compelling preclinical and clinical findings, MSC-based therapeutics remain substantially underutilized in veterinary practice, representing significant untapped potential for improving animal health outcomes.

title: Substrate Stiffness Modulates TGF-β1-Induced Lineage Specification in Multipotent Vascular Stem Cells
summary:
# Summary

Multipotent vascular stem cells (MVSCs) display substrate stiffness-dependent lineage commitment that is further modulated by TGF-β1 signaling. On stiff substrates, MVSCs undergo enhanced focal adhesion assembly and cytoskeletal reorganization, leading to increased expression of smooth muscle cell (SMC) markers including myosin heavy chain, calponin 1, and smooth muscle α-actin, while soft substrates promote chondrogenic differentiation and upregulation of proinflammatory cytokines. TGF-β1 amplifies these stiffness-dependent effects, substantially increasing SMC marker expression on stiff substrates while simultaneously enhancing chondrogenic marker expression on soft substrates. These findings establish a mechanotransduction mechanism whereby extracellular matrix stiffness regulates MVSC lineage specification through substrate-dependent modulation of TGF-β1 signaling, with implications for understanding vascular remodeling in pathological conditions such as atherosclerosis and the inflammatory microenvironment in endometriosis.

title: N-acetylcysteine stimulates organelle malfunction in endometriotic cells via IFN-gamma signaling
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by chronic inflammatory dysregulation driven by innate immune cells that produce pro-inflammatory cytokines including interferon-gamma (IFN-γ), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), which promote endometriotic cell proliferation and adhesion while inhibiting apoptosis. N-acetylcysteine (NAC), an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, reduces endometriotic cyst volume and symptoms by inhibiting cell migration and proliferation through IFN-γ-dependent signaling mechanisms. NAC induces organelle malfunction specifically via endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress and IRE1 phosphorylation, an effect potentiated when combined with IFN-γ, and this IFN-γ signaling is required for NAC's antiproliferative efficacy as demonstrated by antagonist studies. Additionally, NAC and IFN-γ jointly alter multiple metabolic pathways in endometriotic cells including the Warburg effect, glutamate metabolism, and the citric acid cycle, while reducing glutathione metabolism. The differential modulation of cytokine-induced apoptosis by NAC—protective against IL-6 and TNF-α alone, but synergistically pro-apoptotic when combined with IFN-γ—reveals a context-dependent therapeutic mechanism that exploits organelle stress as a means to selectively eliminate endometriotic cells.

title: The role of macrophage polarization in ovarian cancer: from molecular mechanism to therapeutic potentials
summary: Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) predominantly adopt an immunosuppressive M2-like phenotype that critically promotes ovarian cancer progression through facilitation of tumor growth, angiogenesis, and metastasis. The M1/M2 macrophage ratio emerges as a key prognostic indicator, with elevated M1 macrophages associated with improved survival and enhanced chemotherapy sensitivity, while M2-polarized TAMs confer therapy resistance. Multiple signaling pathways regulate macrophage polarization: M1 polarization is driven by interferon-gamma, TNF-alpha, and toll-like receptor signaling through JAK/STAT1 and NF-κB pathways, whereas M2 polarization is activated by IL-4, IL-10, and TGF-beta through JAK/STAT6 and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathways, with metabolic factors like lactate accumulation additionally promoting immunosuppressive TAM phenotypes. Biomarkers including CD163, LILRB1, and TREM2 identify immunosuppressive macrophage populations that attenuate T-cell immunity, while chemokines and tumor-derived extracellular vesicles carrying microRNAs such as miR-200b modulate TAM phenotypes in the tumor microenvironment. Therapeutic interventions targeting macrophage recruitment and polarization, including CCL2/CCR2 and CSF1R inhibition, represent promising strategies to shift the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment toward anti-tumoral immunity and overcome chemotherapy resistance.

title: The immune-modulatory dynamics of exosomes in preeclampsia
summary: Exosomes are small extracellular vesicles that mediate critical immune communication between mother and fetus during pregnancy, functioning through transport of proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, and regulatory microRNAs. In preeclampsia, dysregulation of exosomal immune-modulatory functions contributes to pathogenesis, as the condition involves abnormal trophoblast invasion, defective spiral artery remodeling, and excessive systemic inflammation leading to hypertension and maternal organ dysfunction. Normally, exosomes promote maternal-fetal immune tolerance and regulate inflammatory responses through multiple mechanisms, including delivery of miR-146a and miR-21 that suppress toll-like receptor signaling and promote anti-inflammatory macrophage phenotypes, respectively. However, in preeclampsia, exosomes can paradoxically activate the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway, leading to caspase-1-mediated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18. The review highlights that exosomal surface molecules like phosphatidylserine and biomarkers including CD9, CD63, and CD81 facilitate immune regulation through antigen presentation and dendritic cell maturation, suggesting that understanding exosomal dysfunction in preeclampsia may reveal new therapeutic targets for this serious pregnancy complication.

title: AXL promotes inflammatory breast cancer progression by regulating immunosuppressive macrophage polarization
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the receptor tyrosine kinase AXL drives inflammatory breast cancer progression by promoting immunosuppressive M2 macrophage polarization within the tumor microenvironment. AXL signaling activates the STAT6 transcription pathway in tumor-associated macrophages, enhancing secretion of immunosuppressive chemokines including CCL20, CCL26, and epiregulin, which collectively establish an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. High AXL expression correlates with increased populations of M2 macrophages and decreased anti-tumor immune responses, ultimately promoting tumor growth, angiogenesis, and metastasis while enabling epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and therapy resistance. The selective AXL inhibitor TP-0903 effectively reduces IBC tumor growth and decreases CD206+ macrophage populations, demonstrating that AXL pathway inhibition represents a promising therapeutic strategy to overcome the immunosuppressive nature of inflammatory breast cancer.

title: Astrocytic FABP7 Alleviates Depression‐Like Behaviors of Chronic Unpredictable Mild Stress Mice by Regulating Neuroinflammation and Hippocampal Spinogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that astrocytic FABP7 alleviates depression-like behaviors induced by chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) through multiple interconnected mechanisms. CUMS reduces hippocampal FABP7 expression and its downstream target caveolin-1, simultaneously downregulating astrocytic markers and blood-brain barrier proteins while upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-6. FABP7 overexpression reverses these pathological changes by restoring caveolin-1 expression, enhancing astrocytic function via increased GFAP expression, and reinforcing blood-brain barrier integrity through upregulation of tight junction and aquaporin proteins. Additionally, FABP7 overexpression promotes synaptic plasticity by increasing postsynaptic proteins (PSD95, GluA1) and BDNF while suppressing neuroinflammation, thereby increasing dendritic spine density in hippocampal subregions. These findings establish FABP7 as a critical regulator linking astrocytic lipid metabolism, neuroinflammatory control, blood-brain barrier function, and hippocampal spinogenesis in depression pathophysiology.

title: Strain-dependent effects of Toxoplasma gondii on ovarian health and inflammation in a rat model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that different Toxoplasma gondii strains exert distinct and opposing effects on ovarian health and reproductive function through strain-specific inflammatory mechanisms. The highly virulent RH strain triggers a strong pro-inflammatory response by downregulating the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 while upregulating IFN-γ, leading to elevated inflammatory markers (CRP) and oxidative stress that result in impaired folliculogenesis and altered hormone levels (increased FSH, decreased LH and estradiol). In contrast, the less virulent VEG strain induces milder immune responses without significantly suppressing IL-10, and produces opposite hormonal effects with decreased FSH and increased LH and estradiol levels, alongside larger developing follicles. Chronic T. gondii infection broadly disrupts the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis through cytokine-mediated suppression of GnRH secretion and causes endometriosis, uterine atrophy, and reduced fertility in infected female rats. The strain-dependent differences in cytokine production, oxidative stress, and Treg cell apoptosis underscore how parasite virulence directly determines the severity of reproductive pathology, with potential relevance to understanding infectious contributors to endometriosis and reproductive dysfunction.

title: Hallmarks of Cancer Cachexia: Sexual Dimorphism in Related Pathways
summary:
# Summary

Cancer-associated cachexia involves a complex inflammatory cascade characterized by systemic inflammation, progressive weight loss, adipose tissue depletion, and skeletal muscle impairment driven by tumor-derived and immune cell-derived molecules. Pro-inflammatory cytokines TNFα, IL-1, and IL-6 orchestrate muscle breakdown through multiple mechanisms: TNFα and IL-1 activate NF-κB signaling, while TNFα and IFNγ directly downregulate myosin heavy chain expression; simultaneously, these cytokines suppress testosterone secretion and activate hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis signaling, which promotes muscle proteolysis and serotonin-mediated appetite suppression. Sex hormones exhibit protective effects through distinct anti-inflammatory mechanisms: testosterone stimulates IL-10 production and enhances lipolysis with preferential lean tissue preservation, while estrogen and progesterone suppress pro-inflammatory pathways including IL-6 transcription, TNFα signaling, and NF-κB activation. Males demonstrate greater cachexia severity through higher circulating IL-6 levels, increased monocyte-derived cytokine production, and heightened susceptibility to muscle wasting, whereas females benefit from estrogen-mediated immune modulation and reduced responsiveness to IL-6-induced weight loss. These findings reveal profound sexual dimorphism in cachexia pathogenesis with implications for sex-specific therapeutic interventions targeting inflammatory cytokine cascades and neuroendocrine dysregulation.

title: B Cell Lineage in the Human Endometrium: Physiological and Pathological Implications
summary:
# Summary

B cells represent a critical component of adaptive immunity in the endometrium, functioning through differentiation into antibody-producing plasmacytes and memory B cells that generate immunoglobulins involved in humoral immune responses and complement system activation. The endometrium maintains a specialized immunological environment through resident immunocompetent cells, including distinct B cell subsets (B-1, B-2, follicular, and marginal zone cells), which provide both innate and adaptive defenses against pathogens while supporting pregnancy establishment. B cell development is tightly regulated by transcription factors including PAX5, E2A, Ikaros, and BACH2, which orchestrate lineage commitment, V(D)J rearrangement, and differentiation through multiple signaling pathways including Fms-like tyrosine kinase 3, pre-BCR signaling, and Wnt signaling. The transition to plasmacyte differentiation involves coordinated upregulation of IRF-4 and suppression of PAX5, with critical roles for mTORC1 and endoplasmic reticulum stress pathways in controlling antibody secretion. Notably, dysregulation of B cell lineage cells is associated with pathological conditions including chronic endometritis and endometriosis, suggesting that aberrant B cell-mediated immune responses contribute to these reproductive tract disorders. Understanding B cell biology in the endometrium provides insights into both physiological immune homeostasis and mechanisms underlying inflammatory endometrial pathology.

title: Local and Systemic Endothelial Damage in Patients with CEAP C2 Chronic Venous Insufficiency: Role of Mesoglycan
summary:
# Summary

Chronic venous insufficiency involves a cascade of endothelial damage initiated by venous hypertension, which activates endothelial cells to produce inflammatory mediators and express adhesion molecules that recruit inflammatory cells. This inflammatory microenvironment triggers elevated levels of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2 and MMP-9), adhesion molecules (VCAM-1), inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and IL-8), and syndecan shedding products (SDC-1 and SDC-4), ultimately degrading the endothelial glycocalyx and causing valvular dysfunction and venous ulceration. Treatment with mesoglycan effectively reduced chronic venous disease symptoms and inflammatory markers by restoring endothelial glycocalyx integrity, decreasing MMP activity, reducing circulating levels of VCAM-1, IL-6, IL-8, and syndecan fragments, and increasing TIMP-2 levels to restore the protease-antiprotease balance. These findings demonstrate that targeting glycocalyx restoration represents a novel therapeutic strategy for managing the inflammatory pathology underlying chronic venous insufficiency.

title: Ganoderma lucidum spore powder alleviates rheumatoid arthritis-associated pain hypersensitivity through inhibiting accumulation, N1 polarization, and ROS production of neutrophils in mice
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Ganoderma lucidum spore powder (GLSP) alleviates rheumatoid arthritis-associated pain hypersensitivity by suppressing key neutrophil-driven inflammatory mechanisms in collagen-induced arthritis mice. GLSP treatment significantly reduced neutrophil accumulation, N1 pro-inflammatory polarization, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in affected joints, thereby diminishing mechanical allodynia and thermal hyperalgesia while also reducing paw swelling and synovial pathology. These anti-inflammatory effects occur through inhibition of the TNF-α signaling pathway, which normally drives both N1 polarization and ROS production in neutrophils. Additionally, GLSP downregulated neuropeptide expression (TRPV1 and CGRP) in dorsal root ganglion neurons, suggesting a dual mechanism that addresses both peripheral immune dysregulation and neuronal hyperexcitability underlying pain hypersensitivity. These findings reveal that GLSP's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties provide therapeutic potential for RA-associated pain, potentially offering an advantage over conventional immunosuppressants that offer limited analgesia.

title: Construction and Identification of Inflammation-Related TF–mRNA–miRNA Coexpression Network and Immune Infiltration in Parkinson's Disease
summary:
# Summary

This study constructed a transcription factor–mRNA–microRNA coexpression network to identify inflammation-related genes and immune alterations in Parkinson's disease. Four key inflammation-related differentially expressed genes (CXCR4, LEP, SLC18A2, and TAC1) were identified and validated as having high diagnostic value for PD. The research revealed that microglial and astroglial activation drives dopaminergic neuron death through α-synuclein-mediated pathways, while elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10, TGF-β) characterize PD pathology. Immune infiltration analysis demonstrated increased CD4 T-cells, B-cells, monocytes, and neutrophils in PD patients compared to healthy controls. Specific regulatory axes including GATA1-SLC18A2-miR-3671 and REST-TAC1-miR-330-3P were identified as critical nodes in the inflammatory network, with SLC18A2 and TAC1 expression positively correlating with immune cell function, suggesting their role as potential therapeutic targets in PD-associated neuroinflammation.

title: Serum interferon-gamma-induced protein 10 levels can help predict sarcopenia development in patients with primary hepatocellular carcinoma: A retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that serum IP-10 (interferon-gamma-induced protein 10) levels serve as a predictive biomarker for sarcopenia development in hepatocellular carcinoma patients. IP-10, a chemokine that binds CXCR3 receptors, exhibits a dual role in muscle metabolism: it promotes satellite cell proliferation and myogenic differentiation while simultaneously contributing to muscle destruction and atrophy. The study found that patients with high baseline IP-10 levels and elevated IP-10 ratios at one year were significantly more likely to develop sarcopenia, whereas low baseline IP-10 correlated with pre-existing muscle loss. IP-10 levels also associate with broader HCC pathology, including advanced cancer stage, reduced overall survival, and enhanced tumor progression through modulation of the tumor microenvironment. The inflammatory cytokine landscape in sarcopenia, involving dysregulated IFN-γ signaling and impaired T-cell responses, further connects IP-10 to muscle degeneration. These findings suggest that monitoring serum IP-10 could improve clinical stratification and prognostication of sarcopenia risk in HCC patients.

title: Effects of warm silver needle acupuncture combined with myofascial trigger point intervention on peripheral neutrophil autophagy and alleviation of chronic pelvic pain in women
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pelvic pain in women is characterized by elevated neutrophil autophagy and increased levels of multiple inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, and monocyte chemotactic protein-1 compared to healthy controls. Both warm silver needle acupuncture and myofascial trigger point intervention effectively alleviate chronic pelvic pain through a shared mechanism of suppressing neutrophil autophagy and reducing circulating inflammatory factors. The combined application of both interventions demonstrated particularly superior efficacy, producing more substantial reductions in inflammatory markers and greater improvements in pain relief and overall physical and mental health outcomes. These findings suggest that targeting neutrophil autophagy represents a viable immunological pathway for managing endometriosis-related chronic pelvic pain, with integrative treatment approaches offering enhanced clinical benefit.

title: Tumor-associated macrophages remodel the suppressive tumor immune microenvironment and targeted therapy for immunotherapy
journal: Journal of Experimental & Clinical Cancer Research : CR
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) play a pivotal role in promoting tumor progression and immune evasion by orchestrating the suppressive tumor microenvironment through multiple signaling pathways. TAMs are recruited to tumors via chemokines (CCL2, CCL5, CXCL12) and cytokines (CSF-1, IL-6, TGF-β, VEGF) secreted by cancer cells, tumor-associated neutrophils, and cancer-associated fibroblasts. Once recruited, TAMs polarize toward an immunosuppressive M2 phenotype through IL-4, IL-13, IL-6, IL-10, and other interleukins, which involves activation of PI3K/Akt/mTOR and TGF-β/Smad signaling pathways, while M1 polarization is promoted by IL-2, IL-12, and TLR4/NF-κB signaling. M2 macrophages suppress anti-tumor immunity by inducing CD8+ T cell dysfunction, recruiting regulatory T cells, and producing anti-inflammatory factors, while M1 macrophages activate T cell-mediated immune responses. Immune checkpoint inhibitors targeting the PD-1/PD-L1 pathway represent a key therapeutic strategy to overcome TAM-mediated immune suppression and reactivate CD8+ T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity.

title: Leukemia cells remodel bone marrow stromal cells to generate a protumoral microenvironment via the S100A8-NOX2-ROS signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that leukemia cells remodel bone marrow stromal cells (BMSCs) into a protumoral microenvironment through the S100A8-NOX2-ROS signaling pathway. S100A8, aberrantly expressed in acute myeloid leukemia (AML) cells, activates NOX2 via direct interaction with its regulatory subunit NCF2, triggering excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) production that suppresses BMSC proliferation and induces senescence. Senescent BMSCs undergo senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP), secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, CXCL5, and MIP-1β that further promote AML progression and reduce chemotherapy sensitivity. NOX2-generated ROS also facilitates mitochondrial transfer from BMSCs to AML cells and stimulates AML glycolysis through PFKFB3 activation. Proteomic analysis confirmed S100A8 and NOX2 upregulation in co-cultured AML cells, and S100A8 knockdown attenuated NOX2 activity, extracellular ROS levels, and cytokine secretion while restoring stromal cell viability. These findings establish S100A8 as a critical mediator of AML-BMSC cross-talk and identify the S100A8-NOX2-ROS pathway as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Neutrophil extracellular traps in diseases of the female reproductive organs
summary:
# Summary

Neutrophils play a central role in innate immune responses by producing key pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8) and forming neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs), web-like structures composed of DNA fibers, histones, and antimicrobial proteins including myeloperoxidase, neutrophil elastase, and matrix metalloproteinase-9. NET formation involves chromatin decondensation triggered by reactive oxygen species and serves important antimicrobial functions, though excessive NET production is implicated in pathological conditions affecting female reproductive organs, particularly endometriosis and cancers. In endometriosis, elevated neutrophil numbers in circulation and peritoneal fluid promote endometrial cell proliferation and are associated with chronic pelvic pain, while the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) correlates with disease severity markers including follicle-stimulating hormone and anti-Müllerian hormone levels. Within tumor microenvironments, polarized tumor-associated neutrophils can adopt either pro-tumor phenotypes that promote angiogenesis and metastasis through MMP-9 and arginase release, or anti-tumor phenotypes that generate reactive oxygen species and recruit pro-inflammatory macrophages, with high TAN density and elevated NLR predicting poor prognosis in breast, ovarian, and cervical cancers. Individual NET components possess specialized functions ranging from antimicrobial activities (calprotectin, lactoferrin, pentraxin 3) to tissue remodeling (MMP-9), highlighting the multifaceted contribution of neutrophil-derived factors to both protective immunity and disease pathogenesis.

title: Proteolytic degradation of Beta-Ig H3 (βigH3/TGFBI) can be quantified non-invasively in serum and predicts prognosis in patients with advanced pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
summary: In pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, the extracellular matrix protein βigH3 plays a critical dual role in tumor fibrosis and progression. TGF-β induces βigH3 expression in fibroblasts, macrophages, and cancer cells, where it binds collagens and activates intracellular signaling pathways (AKT, ERK, FAK, Paxilin) that promote cell migration and metastatic potential. However, proteolytic cleavage of βigH3 by collagenases releases a specific N-terminal fragment (cβigH3) into circulation that paradoxically associates with improved prognosis and longer overall survival independent of age, disease stage, and established markers like CA19-9. This counterintuitive finding suggests that active extracellular matrix degradation and βigH3 turnover reflect a less aggressive fibrotic phenotype compared to stable βigH3-enriched matrices, which correlate with poor treatment response. The authors developed a serum ELISA assay measuring cβigH3 as a non-invasive biomarker that distinguishes patient outcomes better than type III collagen formation markers (PRO-C3), offering potential for improved prognostic stratification in advanced pancreatic cancer.

title: Decoding multiple myeloma: single-cell insights into tumor heterogeneity, immune dynamics, and disease progression
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell study reveals that multiple myeloma exhibits substantial cellular heterogeneity, with four distinct myeloma subpopulations (C0 IGLC3+, C1 IGHA1+, C2 IGHG1+, and C3 IGHG4+) differing in immunoglobulin expression and metabolic signatures, with the C0 IGLC3+ subpopulation particularly engaged in ATP synthesis and oxidative phosphorylation. The neuroimmune axis significantly influences myeloma progression and therapeutic resistance through multiple mechanisms: the nervous system regulates immune responses, stress-induced neuroinflammation alters immune function, and neurotransmitters and neuropeptides directly impact immune cell activity and tumor growth. Myeloma cells evade immunity primarily through IL-6-mediated immunosuppression, PD-L1 checkpoint inhibition of T cells, and accumulation of immunosuppressive myeloid-derived suppressor cells alongside T cell exhaustion. While emerging immunotherapies including CAR-T cell therapy targeting BCMA and immune checkpoint inhibitors show promise, understanding this complex interplay between cellular diversity, immune evasion mechanisms, and neuroimmune regulation is essential for overcoming therapeutic resistance and improving patient outcomes.

title: Macrophage polarization in hepatocellular carcinoma: a lncRNA-centric perspective on tumor progression and metastasis
summary:
# Summary

Long non-coding RNAs, particularly HULC and MALAT1, orchestrate hepatocellular carcinoma progression by regulating macrophage polarization toward the pro-tumoral M2 phenotype in the tumor microenvironment. These lncRNAs modulate critical cytokines including IL-10 and TGF-β while interacting with epigenetic modifiers like EZH2, and they simultaneously activate oncogenic pathways including Wnt/β-catenin, PI3K/Akt, and NF-κB signaling that promote cellular proliferation, invasion, angiogenesis, and metastasis. M2 macrophage polarization suppresses anti-tumor immune responses and directly enhances tumor growth, whereas M1 macrophages conversely inhibit tumorigenesis through pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion. The lncRNA regulatory network includes tissue-specific modulators such as MM2P and GAS5, which respectively promote or inhibit M2 polarization via the IL-4/STAT6 and IRF4 pathways, and competing endogenous RNAs like LINC00662 that sequester miRNAs to activate Wnt/β-catenin signaling both autocrinally in tumor cells and paracrinally in macrophages. These multilayered lncRNA-mediated mechanisms represent potential therapeutic targets for disrupting the pro-tumoral immune landscape in hepatocellular carcinoma.

title: The Mechanisms and therapeutic effects of granulocyte colony-stimulating factor in reproduction
summary:
# Summary

Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF), primarily secreted by monocytes and macrophages, functions as a pleiotropic cytokine with critical roles in reproductive biology. Beyond its classical function of stimulating neutrophil proliferation, G-CSF is produced by granulosa cells during the late follicular phase and found in non-hematopoietic tissues including placental and trophoblast cells, where it activates multiple signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt, Erk1/2, Jak/STAT, and NF-κB. The cytokine enhances endometrial receptivity through diverse mechanisms: increasing endometrial pinopodes, inhibiting apoptosis, mobilizing bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells to damaged endometrial tissue, and upregulating reproductive markers including VEGF, LIF, integrins, and cytokeratins. Additionally, G-CSF modulates immune responses by increasing regulatory T cells and shifting the Th1/Th2 balance toward Th2 responses through increased IL-4, IL-5, and IL-10 secretion, thereby promoting an immune-tolerant environment favorable for pregnancy. These multifaceted effects position G-CSF as a potential therapeutic agent for improving implantation outcomes and addressing reproductive pathologies involving impaired endometrial function and immune dysregulation.

title: Over-activation and dysfunction of platelet-NK cell aggregates in HIV-infected individuals
summary:
# Summary

In HIV-infected individuals, platelet-NK cell aggregates accumulate and exhibit severe functional impairment that correlates with disease progression. These aggregates display paradoxical characteristics including both hyperactivation (elevated CD69) and exhaustion phenotypes (elevated CD39, LAG-3, and PD-1), coupled with increased apoptosis and lipid accumulation. The NK cells within these aggregates show dramatically reduced cytokine secretion (IFN-γ) and cytotoxic function (Granzyme B and Perforin), while forming these complexes inversely correlates with CD4 count and CD4/CD8 ratio. The mechanism involves dysregulated SLAM family signaling, where HIV upregulates SLAMF5 and SLAMF7 expression, leading to aberrant activation of downstream phosphorylated signaling molecules (p-Fyn, p-PLC-γ2, p-SHP-1, p-SHP-2) through platelet-NK cell interactions via SLAMF5. Notably, antiretroviral therapy fails to restore normal levels of these aggregates or platelet activation, suggesting that platelet-NK cell dysfunction represents a persistent immunopathological feature that contributes independently to HIV-associated inflammation and disease progression beyond viral replication control.

title: Interleukin-6 Is a Crucial Factor in Shaping the Inflammatory Tumor Microenvironment in Ovarian Cancer and Determining Its Hot or Cold Nature with Diagnostic and Prognostic Utilities
summary:
# Summary

Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a critical pro-inflammatory cytokine that shapes the ovarian cancer tumor microenvironment and drives aggressive disease by activating JAK/STAT3 and PI3K/AKT survival pathways, promoting cancer cell proliferation, migration, metastasis, and ascites formation while being associated with treatment resistance, recurrence, and poor survival outcomes. IL-6 exerts paradoxical effects on immune surveillance, initially promoting T cell infiltration and activation to generate "hot" immunogenic tumors, but subsequently driving chronic inflammation and expanding immunosuppressive cells including regulatory T cells, thereby shifting the microenvironment to a "cold" phenotype that facilitates immune evasion through upregulation of checkpoint molecules PD-1 and CTLA-4. IL-6 expression is stimulated by toll-like receptor ligands, TNF, and IL-1, while its blockade with agents such as tocilizumab can convert cold tumors to hot tumors by reducing inflammatory signaling, lowering Th17 and regulatory T cell populations, and disrupting pro-survival pathways, potentially sensitizing tumors to chemotherapy and immunotherapy. The dual role of IL-6 in mediating both pro-inflammatory immune activation and immunosuppression provides diagnostic and prognostic utility for stratifying ovarian cancer patients and represents a promising therapeutic target for overcoming treatment resistance.

title: Emerging Roles of C-C Motif Ligand 11 (CCL11) in Cancers and Liver Diseases: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Implications
summary:
# Summary

C-C motif ligand 11 (CCL11), also known as eotaxin-1, is a multifunctional chemokine that regulates immunity, angiogenesis, and tissue remodeling across diverse pathological conditions. CCL11 expression is tightly controlled by Th2-associated cytokines (IL-4, IL-10, IL-13) that activate STAT6 and NF-κB signaling pathways, while Th1 cytokines like IFN-γ suppress its production. In allergic and inflammatory diseases, CCL11 primarily drives eosinophil recruitment through CCR3 receptor signaling and establishes autocrine loops that perpetuate inflammation. In liver diseases, hepatic stellate cells and sinusoidal endothelial cells produce CCL11 in response to profibrotic mediators (TGF-β, PDGF), driving fibrogenesis and tissue injury through fibroblast activation. Additionally, multiple tumor cell types including ovarian, hepatocellular, and colorectal cancers produce CCL11, suggesting context-dependent roles in cancer progression that merit further therapeutic investigation.

title: Angiogenesis in rheumatoid Arthritis: Pathological characterization, pathogenic mechanisms, and nano-targeted therapeutic strategies
summary:
# Summary

Pathological angiogenesis is a critical driver of rheumatoid arthritis pathogenesis, promoting chronic synovitis and enabling immune cell infiltration into synovial tissue. Stromal cells release pro-angiogenic factors including VEGF, FGF, HGF, and angiopoietin that activate endothelial cells, while the DLL4/Notch-1 pathway regulates endothelial cell fate by upregulating VEGFR1 and downregulating VEGFR2. Inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 further drive neovascularization, supporting pannus formation and activation of osteoclasts, which leads to cartilage and bone destruction manifested as joint tenderness, stiffness, and swelling. Elevated markers of angiogenesis and microvascular density in RA synovium correlate strongly with disease severity. Nanomedicines represent an emerging therapeutic strategy to target these pathological angiogenic mechanisms in RA. Although the article focuses on rheumatoid arthritis, the angiogenic dysregulation mechanisms described are also implicated in endometriosis and other chronic inflammatory diseases.

title: Myeloid‐Derived Suppressor Cells in Cancer: Mechanistic Insights and Targeted Therapeutic Innovations
summary: Myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) are heterogeneous immune populations that potently suppress antitumor immunity through both immunological and non-immunological mechanisms, contributing to tumor progression and therapeutic resistance. Immunologically, MDSCs inhibit effector T cells, NK cells, and dendritic cells via secretion of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, with distinct MDSC subsets employing specialized mechanisms—polymorphonuclear MDSCs rely primarily on ROS/RNS production, monocytic MDSCs produce nitric oxide and arginase-1, and fibroblastic MDSCs secrete indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase to suppress T cell proliferation and expand regulatory T cells. Non-immunologically, MDSCs promote tumor progression by facilitating epithelial-mesenchymal transition, angiogenesis, and premetastatic niche formation. MDSC recruitment and differentiation are governed by cytokine-driven signaling pathways, particularly GM-CSF and G-CSF activation of JAK/STAT1/3 cascades, while the CD300ld molecule promotes STAT3 phosphorylation and upregulates S100A8/A9 to enhance PMN-MDSC recruitment. The immunosuppressive cytokines IL-10 and TGF-β, produced by MDSCs, further polarize the tumor microenvironment toward immune tolerance by promoting regulatory T cell differentiation and inhibiting cytotoxic lymphocyte activity. MDSCs serve as prognostic biomarkers for cancer progression and therapeutic response, positioning MDSC-targeting strategies as promising approaches to enhance immunotherapy efficacy.

title: Sympathetic hyperinnervation drives abdominal aortic aneurysm development by promoting vascular smooth muscle cell phenotypic switching
summary:
# Summary

Sympathetic hyperinnervation drives abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) development through osteoclast-like cell-derived semaphorin 4D (Sema4D), which promotes sympathetic nerve growth and subsequently induces vascular smooth muscle cell (VSMC) phenotypic switching via the eATP/P2rx4/p38 signaling pathway. This pathological remodeling triggers VSMC dedifferentiation, inflammatory cell infiltration, and reactive oxygen species generation, collectively compromising vascular integrity and promoting elastic fiber degradation and collagen deposition. Pharmacological or surgical inhibition of sympathetic innervation through coeliac ganglionectomy (CGX) or 6-hydroxydopamine treatment significantly reduced AAA incidence, aortic rupture mortality, and macrophage infiltration while decreasing inflammatory mediators including IL-6, MCP1, and CD68. Notably, Sema4D-deficient mice showed substantially ameliorated AAA progression, establishing this molecule as a critical upstream regulator. These findings identify sympathetic hyperinnervation as a novel therapeutic target, with antagonizing sympathetic signaling emerging as a potential preventive and therapeutic strategy for AAA disease.

title: CX3CR1+ Monocytes/Macrophages Promote Regional Immune Injury in Mesangial Proliferative Glomerulonephritis through Crosstalk with Activated Mesangial Cells
summary:
# Summary

In mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis, CX3CR1-expressing monocytes and macrophages interact with activated mesangial cells through the CX3CL1–CX3CR1 axis to drive regional immune injury in the glomerulus. Upon activation by CX3CL1, infiltrating monocytes/macrophages upregulate macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF), which then binds to CD74 receptors on mesangial cells, thereby activating the PI3K–AKT signaling pathway and promoting mesangial cell proliferation and inflammatory cytokine production (TNFα, IL-6, IL-1β). This aberrant crosstalk creates a pathogenic feed-forward loop wherein the MIF–CD74 interaction further enhances CX3CL1 expression on mesangial cells, amplifying monocyte/macrophage recruitment and glomerular damage. Pharmacological inhibition of CX3CR1 using AZD8797 or neutralization of CX3CL1 using quetmolimab effectively disrupts this axis, reducing proteinuria, glomerular cell proliferation, inflammatory cytokine expression, and immune infiltration in vivo. These findings identify the CX3CL1–CX3CR1–MIF–CD74–PI3K/AKT cascade as a critical therapeutic target in IgA nephropathy and related glomerular diseases.

title: Identification of potential biomarkers associated with immune cell infiltration patterns in Kawasaki disease via bioinformatics
summary: This bioinformatics study identified key immune infiltration patterns and biomarkers associated with Kawasaki disease, revealing a distinctive shift from adaptive to innate immune dominance. Kawasaki disease is characterized by elevated monocytes, neutrophils, and macrophages alongside reduced CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and B cells, a pattern that contributes to pathogenic vascular inflammation. The chemokine CCL2 plays a central dual role by recruiting inflammatory cells via CCR2 signaling while simultaneously promoting cardioprotective cardiomyocyte proliferation through JNK/STAT3 pathways, suggesting a complex balance between tissue damage and repair mechanisms. Cell cycle regulators AURKB, BUB1, and TOP2A are associated with reduced Th1 cell populations, linking proliferative dysfunction to immune dysregulation in disease pathogenesis. Elevated inflammatory cytokines including IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-18 drive vasculitis partly through CCL2-mediated activation of the Dectin-2/NLRP3 inflammasome and IL-1β production, while neutrophil infiltration directly damages endothelial cells. These findings establish multiple interconnected biomarkers that could facilitate early Kawasaki disease diagnosis and identify therapeutic targets within the inflammatory cascade.

title: Decreased CCL5 expression in endometrial stromal cells induces deficient CCR5
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by a dysregulated immune microenvironment involving impaired CD4+ T cell function and altered chemokine signaling. Ectopic endometrial stromal cells normally produce CCL5, which recruits the specialized CCR5+CD4+ T cell subset—cells that display a highly activated, cytotoxic phenotype and robustly produce interferon-gamma. However, in endometriosis patients, ectopic endometrial stromal cells show decreased CCL5 production, resulting in diminished recruitment of CCR5+CD4+ T cells to ectopic lesions and reduced interferon-gamma secretion. Mouse studies with CCL5-deficient endometrium demonstrated that this deficit directly promotes ectopic lesion growth and progression, indicating that the impaired CCL5-CCR5 axis represents a critical mechanism by which local immune tolerance permits ectopic endometrial implant survival. These findings establish that decreased CCL5 expression and the resulting deficient CCR5+CD4+ T cell infiltration constitute key contributors to endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Natural Killer Cell Immune Checkpoints and Their Therapeutic Targeting in Cancer Treatment
summary: Natural killer cells serve as critical mediators of antitumor immunity through their cytotoxic and cytokine-secreting functions, a process tightly regulated by immune checkpoint molecules expressed on their surface and intracellularly. Multiple checkpoint receptors including NKG2A, KIRs, and TIGIT, along with intracellular regulators such as BIM, Cbl-b, and EZH2, regulate NK cell functional states and their capacity for cytotoxicity and cytokine production. Tumor cells evade NK cell-mediated immunity by expressing inhibitory ligands that engage these checkpoint molecules, analogous to PD-L1–PD-1 interactions on T cells, leading to NK cell exhaustion. NK cell development and function are orchestrated by transcription factors T-BET and EOMES, as well as multiple cytokines including IL-2, IL-7, IL-12, IL-15, and IL-18 that work at various developmental stages. Notably, elevated KIR expression on peripheral NK cells has been identified in endometriosis patients, suggesting checkpoint molecule dysregulation may contribute to the inflammatory immune dysfunction characteristic of this condition. Therapeutic strategies targeting these immune checkpoints, including CAR-NK cell therapies and checkpoint blockade approaches, represent emerging avenues for enhancing NK cell-mediated antitumor immunity.

title: Effect of Herbal Bioactive Compounds on the Angiogenic Factors and Modulation of Inflammatory Mediators in the Patients with the Deep Second-Degree Burn: A Single-Blinded, Randomized Clinical Trial
journal: Medical Journal of the Islamic Republic of Iran
summary:
# Summary

This randomized clinical trial demonstrates that Swalin herbal ointment, composed primarily of hemp oil, sesame oil, and bioactive compounds, significantly accelerates wound healing in deep second-degree burn patients through dual mechanisms of enhanced angiogenesis and reduced inflammation. The ointment increases tissue levels of key angiogenic factors—VEGF, FGF, and PDGF—which stimulate endothelial cell proliferation, migration, and differentiation to promote new blood vessel formation essential for tissue repair. Simultaneously, Swalin reduces systemic inflammatory mediators IL-6 and TNF-α by day 14, suppressing the excessive immune response that impairs healing and can trigger cellular apoptosis. The bioactive constituents linoleic acid, quercetin, and kaempferol from hemp and sesame oils mediate these effects by modulating inflammatory cytokine production and enhancing wound closure rates. In contrast, silver sulfadiazine, the conventional burn treatment, inhibits keratinocyte regeneration and proved inferior to the herbal formulation. This herbal approach represents an effective alternative therapy that balances the pro-angiogenic signals necessary for tissue regeneration with anti-inflammatory control to optimize the wound-healing environment.

title: Monocytes and cervical ripening: a narrative review of prolonged labor pathophysiology
summary:
# Summary

Monocytes play a central role in cervical ripening during labor through a tightly regulated inflammatory cascade that becomes dysregulated in prolonged labor. Upon recruitment to the cervix via pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines like CCL2, monocytes differentiate into macrophages and release key inflammatory mediators including interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha, which activate matrix metalloproteinases to degrade collagen and extracellular matrix components essential for cervical softening, effacement, and dilation. These pro-inflammatory cytokines simultaneously promote uterine smooth muscle contractions, coordinating cervical remodeling with labor progression. However, dysregulation of monocyte recruitment and activation—manifesting as either excessive prolonged inflammation or inadequate inflammatory response—impairs cervical ripening and leads to cervical dystocia, contributing to prolonged labor and increased cesarean section requirements. Therapeutic strategies targeting monocyte function modulation or inflammatory regulation through anti-inflammatory drugs and cytokine inhibitors offer potential approaches to normalize cervical ripening and labor progression when dysregulation occurs.

title: Leukocyte dynamics in female reproductive health: roles and mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Leukocytes, particularly neutrophils and macrophages, play central roles in female reproductive health by mediating menstruation, ovulation, endometrial repair, and pregnancy establishment through coordinated tissue remodeling and immune regulation. Neutrophils facilitate menstrual shedding and endometrial degradation via matrix metalloproteinase release, while macrophages support tissue clearance, angiogenesis, and endometrial repair across the menstrual cycle. Chemokines CCL2 and CXCL12 recruit leukocytes to reproductive tissues, with estrogen and progesterone regulating this recruitment and modulating immune cell activity—progesterone specifically promoting an anti-inflammatory environment through regulatory T cell expansion and immunosuppressive cytokine production (IL-10, TGF-β). During pregnancy, uterine natural killer cells and decidual macrophages establish immune tolerance by remodeling spiral arteries and maintaining immune homeostasis, while regulatory T cells prevent maternal immune rejection of fetal antigens. Dysregulation of these leukocyte populations contributes to reproductive pathologies including endometriosis and recurrent pregnancy loss through chronic inflammation and impaired immune tolerance, and leukocyte-mediated low-grade inflammation is associated with polycystic ovary syndrome.

title: PRMT5 inhibition reduces hyperinflammation in a murine model of secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis∗†
summary:
# Summary

Protein arginine methyltransferase 5 (PRMT5) is significantly elevated in T cells, NK cells, monocytes, and dendritic cells during secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH), a life-threatening hyperinflammatory condition. PRMT5 catalyzes symmetric dimethylation of arginine residues and regulates critical inflammatory pathways including NF-κB-driven transcription, alternative splicing, and Th17 CD4 T-cell differentiation, thereby promoting inflammatory cytokine production such as interferon-γ and IL-6. Treatment with PRT382, a selective PRMT5 inhibitor, substantially ameliorated secondary HLH in mice by reducing hyperinflammation, organ enlargement, anemia, and myeloid cell expansion while restoring lymphocyte populations and reducing intracellular interferon-γ in both T and NK cells. Notably, PRT382 proved more effective than ruxolitinib, a JAK inhibitor, at suppressing cytokine production in immune cells, suggesting PRMT5 inhibition targets a distinct pathogenic mechanism in HLH-driven inflammation. These findings establish PRMT5 as a key regulator of immune cell activation in secondary HLH and identify PRMT5 inhibition as a promising therapeutic strategy for treating this severe inflammatory disorder.

title: Causal association between blood and urine biomarkers, immune cells, and bladder cancer: A Mendelian randomization and mediation analysis
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization and mediation analysis identified multiple causal pathways linking blood biomarkers, immune cells, and bladder cancer risk. Elevated serum calcium significantly increased bladder cancer risk through dysregulation of calcium signaling and calcium channel expression (particularly CaV1.3), while sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) provided protective effects by regulating steroid hormone action and inhibiting estradiol-mediated cell growth pathways. Specific immune cell populations mediated these associations: naive CD4+ T cells and HLA-DR+ monocytes promoted bladder cancer risk, whereas CD20+ B cells and CD19+ transitional B cells were protective, with CD20+ B cells notably attenuating calcium-mediated carcinogenic effects. The study also revealed that multiple biomarkers including inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein), micronutrient imbalances (high calcium, low magnesium), and trace metals (copper, zinc) contributed to bladder cancer progression through distinct mechanisms including altered glycosylation patterns and oxidative stress in the tumor microenvironment. Diagnostic biomarkers such as urine DNA methylation analysis and the Bladder EpiCheck assay emerged as valuable tools for cancer detection and recurrence prediction.

title: Effects of Nonablative Er‐YAG Laser on Human Endometrial Stromal Cells (hESCs): A Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that nonablative Er:YAG laser treatment of human endometrial stromal cells induces therapeutic effects relevant to endometriosis and implantation failure through multiple mechanisms. The laser upregulates matrix metalloproteinase-2 (MMP-2) and insulin-like growth factor binding protein-1 (IGFBP-1) while simultaneously suppressing the pro-inflammatory cytokine tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), thereby shifting the immune response from Th1 toward Th2 cell activity. Heat generated by the laser activates heat shock proteins (HSP70), which orchestrate tissue remodeling, neocollagenesis, and collagen fibril shrinkage through transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β) signaling. Notably, the Er:YAG laser downregulates vascular endothelial growth factor-A (VEGF-A) while promoting decidualization, mimicking progesterone's immunomodulatory and reproductive effects without causing the tissue damage associated with endometrial scratching. These findings suggest that nonablative Er:YAG laser therapy could benefit patients with luteal phase deficiency and recurrent implantation failure by enhancing endometrial receptivity and reducing the inflammatory milieu characteristic of endometriosis.

title: Origin of Hypofunctional CD103+ NK Cells in Cirrhosis‐Associated Ascites
summary:
# Summary

In cirrhosis-associated ascites, a distinctive population of CD103+ NK cells emerges with a hypofunctional, tissue-resident phenotype, distinct from circulating and liver NK cells. This CD103+ subset is induced by the synergistic action of IL-15, IL-21, and TGFβ present in ascitic fluid, alongside elevated levels of other proinflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-12, and IFNγ. These CD103+ NK cells display a less differentiated phenotype characterized by reduced expression of cytotoxic effector molecules (perforin, granzyme B, granulysin) and differentiation markers (T-bet, NKG2C, CD57), while expressing elevated tissue-residency markers (CXCR6, CCR5, CD49a) comparable to intraepithelial NK cells found in the duodenum. Despite their tissue-resident characteristics, these ascitic CD103+ NK cells demonstrate significantly impaired functionality, including reduced degranulation capacity and hyporesponsiveness to IL-12 + IL-18 stimulation, while simultaneously suppressing autologous CD4+ T cell proliferation. The emergence of this hypofunctional NK cell subset in the immunologically dysregulated environment of cirrhotic ascites likely represents a maladaptive immune response that contributes to the altered immune homeostasis characteristic of decompensated liver cirrhosis.

title: Evaluation of Proinflammatory Cytokines Concentrations in Plasma, Peritoneal, and Endometrioma Fluids in Women Operated on for Ovarian Endometriosis—A Pilot Study
summary: This pilot study demonstrates that proinflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8 play central roles in ovarian endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms. The researchers found significantly elevated levels of IL-6 and TNF-α in endometrioma fluid compared to peritoneal and plasma fluids, with these three cytokines showing strong positive correlations in endometriotic compartments, suggesting coordinated inflammatory activation. Beyond their elevated concentrations, these cytokines promote endometriosis progression through distinct pathways: TNF-α induces cyclooxygenase-2 overexpression and matrix metalloproteinase-9 deregulation while stimulating M2 macrophage polarization, IL-6 inhibits apoptosis of ectopic implants and promotes their migration, and IL-8 directly stimulates endometrial cell proliferation in a dose-dependent manner. Notably, IL-8 showed the strongest biomarker potential, with plasma IL-8 levels correlating with local peritoneal and endometrioma fluid levels regardless of disease stage, positioning it as a sensitive marker for endometriotic inflammation severity. Anti-cytokine therapeutic approaches, including anti-IL-6 receptor antibodies and anti-IL-8 antibodies, have demonstrated efficacy in experimental models by reducing lesion volume and local inflammation. These findings highlight the interconnected inflammatory network in endometriosis and identify specific cytokines as potential therapeutic targets.

title: iNOS Mediates High-Fat Diet-Associated Aggravation of Complete Freund’s Adjuvant-Induced Inflammatory Pain
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that short-term high-fat diet (HFD) significantly aggravates Complete Freund's Adjuvant (CFA)-induced inflammatory pain in mice through inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS)-mediated mechanisms. HFD feeding elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF at inflamed tissues and enhanced M1-like macrophage polarization, resulting in heightened mechanical and thermal hyperalgesia compared to normal diet controls. The pathogenic pathway involves iNOS upregulation leading to increased nitric oxide production, which activates the NO/cGMP signaling pathway and enhances cyclooxygenase activity to promote pro-inflammatory prostaglandin E2 biosynthesis. Pharmacological inhibition of iNOS using 1400W effectively reversed CFA-induced pain in HFD-fed mice, significantly reducing both mechanical and thermal hyperalgesia despite maintaining elevated inflammatory marker expression. These findings suggest that iNOS represents a critical therapeutic target for managing inflammatory pain conditions, including endometriosis-associated pain, particularly in the context of dietary risk factors that promote metabolic dysfunction and immune dysregulation.

title: Blockade of IL-1 family cytokines in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines IL-1 family cytokines as central mediators of rheumatoid arthritis pathogenesis and therapeutic targets. IL-1β and IL-18 drive synovial inflammation and bone erosion through activation of NLRP3 inflammasomes and downstream signaling cascades including NF-κB and MAPK pathways, while insufficient anti-inflammatory antagonists such as IL-1Ra and IL-37 permit disease progression. Multiple genetic variants in IL-1 genes and related regulatory molecules (PTPN22, STAT4, TNFAIP3, PADI4) combine with environmental factors including smoking, occupational exposures, and dietary sodium to amplify inflammatory responses and protein citrullination.
