IL-1 cytokines perpetuate disease through multiple mechanisms: activating RANKL-mediated osteoclastogenesis and bone resorption, promoting Th17 and CD4+ T cell differentiation, and upregulating VEGF-mediated angiogenic cascades that facilitate synovial fibroblast proliferation. Clinical validation of IL-1 blockade through biologics such as anakinra and canakinumab demonstrates the therapeutic efficacy of targeting these pathways, establishing IL-1 family cytokines as pivotal nodes in rheumatoid arthritis immunopathology.

title: Platelets in cancer and immunotherapy: functional dynamics and therapeutic opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Platelets play a multifaceted and protumorigenic role in cancer progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Elevated platelet counts are associated with increased malignancy risk and poorer clinical outcomes, and platelets directly promote cancer cell proliferation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and therapy resistance by delivering growth factors such as TGF-β, VEGF, and PDGF while establishing immunosuppressive microenvironments. During metastasis, platelets coat circulating tumor cells via integrin αIIbβ3 and P-selectin interactions, providing both physical protection from natural killer cells and immune evasion through transfer of adhesion molecules and secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines including IL-10 and TGF-β. Platelets also support angiogenesis through VEGF sequestration and release of platelet-derived microparticles that induce endothelial transcription of pro-angiogenic factors, while simultaneously maintaining tumor vascular integrity and facilitating pre-metastatic niche formation through CXCL5 and CXCL7 secretion. Notably, platelet receptor disruption—targeting integrin αIIbβ3, P-selectin, or glycoprotein VI—along with anti-estrogen therapies that impede platelet-supported angiogenesis and NKG2A blockade to restore natural killer cell activity, represent emerging therapeutic strategies to reverse platelet-mediated protumorigenic effects.

title: Role of interleukin-16 in human diseases: a novel potential therapeutic target
summary: Interleukin-16 (IL-16) is a multifunctional cytokine that plays a central role in inflammatory immune responses through multiple mechanisms involving cell cycle regulation, chemoattraction, and proinflammatory signaling. Upon caspase-3-mediated cleavage, IL-16 generates functional fragments that regulate cell growth and act as potent chemoattractants for CD4+ and CD9-expressing cells, with elevated levels found at sites of inflammation, infection, and cancer. IL-16 is widely expressed by diverse immune cells including T cells, B cells, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and mast cells, where its secretion is regulated by MAP kinase and calcineurin-dependent pathways. The cytokine signals primarily through CD4 binding to activate downstream mediators including p56lck, protein kinase C, and STAT6, while co-ligation with CCR5 enhances signaling responses. Notably, in endometriosis, IL-16 is released via the gasdermin E-mediated pyroptotic pathway, positioning it as a key inflammatory mediator in this condition. These findings establish IL-16 as a potential therapeutic target for managing inflammatory diseases, particularly endometriosis, where disrupting IL-16 release or signaling could attenuate pathological inflammation.

title: Association between the Alpha-1-acid glycoprotein concentrations and depression in US adult women: a cross-sectional study from NHANES 2021–2023
summary: This cross-sectional study of US adult women from NHANES 2021–2023 demonstrates a strong positive association between elevated alpha-1-acid glycoprotein (AGP) concentrations and depression risk, with participants in the highest AGP quartile showing 72% greater odds of depression compared to the lowest quartile. AGP is an acute-phase inflammatory biomarker produced by hepatocytes, pneumocytes, and vascular endothelial cells that circulates in plasma and cerebrospinal fluid, where its synthesis is stimulated by pro-inflammatory mediators including prostaglandin E2 and cytokines. The pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1, IL-6, and TNF-α upregulate AGP expression and independently promote depression through multiple neural pathways: IL-1 downregulates neurotrophic factors BDNF and NGF, IL-6 promotes astrocyte apoptosis, and TNF-α enhances glutamate neurotoxicity by upregulating glutaminase and inhibiting glutamate transporter activity. These inflammatory mediators collectively disrupt neural circuitry signaling and contribute to the core depressive symptoms of depressed mood and anhedonia. The association between AGP and depression was consistent across younger adults and non-Hispanic White participants, and AGP demonstrated superior prognostic accuracy for depression compared to C-reactive protein, establishing systemic inflammation as a key mechanistic link to depression pathogenesis.

title: Tracking inflammation status for improving patient prognosis: A review of current methods, unmet clinical needs and opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Inflammatory responses are orchestrated by cytokines produced by white blood cells that regulate cellular proliferation and differentiation through coordinated signaling networks. Key pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, and IL-8 form positive feedback loops that amplify inflammation, while anti-inflammatory cytokines like IL-4 and IL-10 provide negative regulation. Elevated circulating cytokines activate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, triggering cortisol and ACTH secretion, which in turn suppress cytokine responses through negative feedback. Pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1, IL-6, and TNF-α stimulate hepatic production of acute phase proteins such as C-reactive protein and serum amyloid A, which serve as biomarkers for inflammation and are associated with adverse clinical outcomes in conditions ranging from acute coronary syndrome to post-surgical complications. Dysregulated cytokine responses, including cytokine storms, can lead to immunoparalysis and multiple organ failure, highlighting the critical importance of monitoring inflammation status for predicting patient prognosis and guiding therapeutic interventions such as IL-6 receptor antagonists.

title: Impact of a moderate-intensity aerobic exercise intervention on systemic and uterine natural killer cells in women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how moderate-intensity aerobic exercise affects natural killer cells in women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss, a condition associated with altered NK cell function and elevated inflammatory responses. The researchers found that a three-month exercise intervention induced phenotypic and functional changes in peripheral blood NK cells, specifically reducing expression of CD161, NKp30, and NKG2A receptors—markers associated with pro-inflammatory activity—while also significantly decreasing interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) production by these cells. These changes suggest that exercise promotes a shift toward a less inflammatory NK cell phenotype, potentially through mechanisms involving reduced activation signals that normally drive cytokine production. Importantly, exercise did not alter the overall frequency of NK cell subsets or other lymphocyte populations, indicating selective modulation of existing cells rather than redistribution. The findings suggest that aerobic exercise may help restore immune homeostasis in women with recurrent pregnancy loss by dampening the excessive NK cell cytotoxicity associated with this condition, though whether these phenotypic improvements translate to improved pregnancy outcomes requires further investigation.

title: Murine modeling of menstruation identifies immune correlates of protection during Chlamydia muridarum challenge
summary:
# Summary

This murine study demonstrates that the menstrual cycle regulates immune protection against Chlamydia muridarum infection through coordinated changes in natural killer (NK) cell localization and inflammatory signaling. Progesterone withdrawal during the late luteal phase triggers endometrial remodeling, which activates a pro-inflammatory immune response characterized by increased NK cell infiltration into the lower female reproductive tract and elevated interferon-gamma (IFNγ) production. Challenge with C. muridarum during the late luteal phase (days 7–9 of pseudopregnancy) resulted in significantly reduced bacterial replication, and this protection was entirely dependent on NK cells, as their depletion during this period eliminated the immune advantage and enabled productive infection. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that NK cells during progesterone withdrawal are specifically enriched for inflammatory and cytotoxic programs, positioning them as critical mediators of innate immune defense at the genital barrier. These findings establish that cyclical hormonal changes coordinate innate immune readiness at mucosal surfaces, with direct implications for understanding susceptibility windows to sexually transmitted infections like Chlamydia trachomatis and their sequelae including pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility.

title: Integrating multi-omics data to optimize immunotherapy in endometrial cancer: a comprehensive study
summary:
# Integrating Multi-Omics Data to Optimize Immunotherapy in Endometrial Cancer

This study employs multi-omics integration to identify biomarkers predicting immunotherapy response in endometrial cancer, comparing responders (R) and non-responders (NR) to immune checkpoint inhibitors like pembrolizumab and dostarlimab that target PD-1/PD-L1 pathways. Responders demonstrate elevated immune activation characterized by higher expression of chemokines, interferons, interleukins, and enrichment of M1-like tumor-associated macrophages that inhibit cancer cell proliferation, alongside increased infiltration of macrophages and neutrophils. Non-responders exhibit enriched angiogenesis, tumor proliferation, and cellular differentiation pathways driven by M2-like macrophage polarization, elevated WNT and MAPK signaling, and higher mutation frequencies in genes like CTNNB1 and RPRD1B with DNA mismatch repair defects. The study identifies that responders have higher immune scores, elevated checkpoint molecule expression, and better predictive TIDE and EaSIeR scores, while older patients (age ≥65 years) show improved immunotherapy response rates. These multi-omics findings establish a comprehensive framework for predicting immunotherapy efficacy and identifying mechanisms underlying immune resistance in endometrial cancer.

title: Integrating Genomics and Molecular Biology in Understanding Peritoneal Adhesion
summary:
# Summary

Peritoneal adhesion formation in endometriosis involves a complex interplay of inflammatory, angiogenic, and fibrotic molecular pathways. TGF-β signaling acts as a central orchestrator, promoting fibroblast chemotaxis and proliferation while upregulating its own expression in macrophages and fibroblasts; however, the specific isoform present critically determines outcomes, as TGF-β3 promotes healing with reduced fibrosis whereas TGF-β and TGF-β2 promote scarring. Inflammatory mediators including COX-2, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β, along with cell adhesion molecules (integrins, cadherins, selectins, and immunoglobulin superfamily members), drive adhesion formation and recruit immune cells through leukocyte-endothelial interactions. Dysregulated fibrinolysis represents a key mechanism whereby elevated PAI-1 suppresses fibrin degradation and promotes persistent fibrin scaffolds that serve as matrices for fibroblast migration, while impaired fibrinolytic balance (tPA/PAI-1) perpetuates adhesion development. Adipose tissue contributes to adhesion pathology through upregulation of TGF-β and VEGF, and hypoxic signaling via HIF-1α stimulates both angiogenesis and fibroblast activation in expanded adipose tissue. Therapeutically, hyaluronic acid-based agents like Seprafilm® effectively reduce adhesion incidence by inhibiting leukocyte and fibroblast adhesion, downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines, and modulating mesothelial-to-mesenchymal transition pathways.

title: Analysis of the Expression Patterns of Tumor Necrosis Factor Alpha Signaling Pathways and Regulatory MicroRNAs in Astrocytic Tumors
summary: High-grade astrocytic tumors (grades 3-4) are characterized by marked upregulation of TNF-α and IL-1β, pro-inflammatory cytokines that bind to surface receptors (TNFR1 and TNFR2) to activate multiple downstream signaling cascades including NF-κB, MAPK, and JNK pathways. This sustained inflammatory signaling promotes tumor progression through enhanced cell migration, angiogenesis, apoptosis resistance, and expansion of glioma stem-like cells, while also driving tumor infiltration into adjacent tissues through increased adhesion molecule expression. Elevated levels of TNF-α, IL-1β, and MAP3K8 are independently associated with poor overall survival, and their promoter regions show hypomethylation in high-grade tumors, indicating epigenetic activation of the inflammatory axis. Conversely, regulatory microRNAs that suppress this pathway—including miR-34a-3p and miR-30 family members targeting TNF-α and IL-1β respectively—are markedly downregulated in high-grade tumors, while opposing microRNAs targeting downstream kinases are upregulated, collectively creating a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that facilitates malignant progression.

title: NK cell dysfunction in antiphospholipid syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is characterized by antiphospholipid antibodies directed against phospholipids and β2-glycoprotein I that drive thrombotic events and pregnancy complications through disruption of natural killer (NK) cell-mediated immune regulation. NK cells normally play critical roles in endometrial tissue remodeling, vascular function, and placental formation, with uterine NK cells binding to HLA-E and HLA-G on trophoblasts to promote immune tolerance and regulate placental invasion. In APS, antiphospholipid antibodies disrupt this delicate balance, leading to NK cell dysfunction characterized by reduced circulating counts in thrombotic APS patients but elevated counts in obstetric APS patients, ultimately contributing to pre-eclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and recurrent pregnancy loss. NK cell activation occurs through antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity and cytokine stimulation (IL-2, IL-15, IL-12), resulting in release of cytotoxic mediators including granzymes, perforin, FasL, and TRAIL. Therapeutic strategies such as intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) ameliorate pregnancy outcomes by reducing NK cell numbers and cytotoxicity while inhibiting antiphospholipid antibodies, highlighting the central role of NK cell dysfunction in APS-related pregnancy pathology.

title: Advances in hydrogels for capturing and neutralizing inflammatory cytokines
summary:
# Summary

Inflammatory cytokines IL-1 and TNF-α drive pathological inflammation through interconnected signaling cascades that are central to endometriosis and other chronic inflammatory diseases. Both cytokines activate NF-κB and MAPK pathways, with IL-1 additionally promoting NLRP3 inflammasome assembly, leading to amplified production of secondary cytokines including IL-6 and IL-8. These cytokines collectively promote osteoclast differentiation and bone loss, stimulate degradative enzyme secretion from chondrocytes and fibroblasts, induce vascular adhesion molecule expression, and increase vascular permeability—collectively contributing to tissue destruction and disease progression. Therapeutic hydrogel platforms offer a promising approach to modulate these inflammatory cascades by directly capturing and neutralizing circulating cytokines through glycosaminoglycan-mediated electrostatic interactions and antibody-based specificity. These engineered hydrogels can interact with both cell membranes and nanoparticles to achieve broad-spectrum cytokine sequestration, potentially disrupting the inflammatory signaling networks that perpetuate endometriosis and related chronic inflammatory pathologies.

title: Fecal CD200 as a Measure of Immunosuppressive CD200L and Proinflammatory CD200S at the Feto‐Maternal Interface
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how intestinal and endometrial immune regulation affect recurrent miscarriage, particularly in diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D). The researchers found that epithelial cells express immunosuppressive CD200L, which normally inhibits the proinflammatory degranulation of stromal CD56+ NK cells that contain CD200S. In IBS-D patients, decreased epithelial CD200L expression is associated with increased degranulation of these NK cells, leading to enhanced release of proinflammatory mediators. Intestinal-derived lipopolysaccharide (LPS) activates macrophages to produce TNF-α and interferon-γ, which synergistically trigger blood coagulation and embryonic ischemia, while CD200L normally suppresses this pathogenic cascade. Since colonic epithelial cells express CD200L that can be detected in stool, and CD200S+ cells are present in the endometrium with increased activity during menstruation and the luteal phase, the authors propose a common mucosal immune system mechanism whereby colonic immune dysregulation mirrors endometrial pathology. This model suggests that restoring CD200L expression or modulating intestinal NK cell activation could provide therapeutic targets for IBS-D-associated recurrent pregnancy loss.

title: CGRP alleviates lipopolysaccharide-induced ARDS inflammation via the HIF-1α signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) alleviates lipopolysaccharide-induced acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) by inhibiting the HIF-1α signaling pathway. CGRP binds to its receptor RAMP1 and shifts macrophage polarization from pro-inflammatory M1 toward anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes, reducing pulmonary inflammation, oxidative stress (ROS, MDA, MPO), and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) in both in vitro and in vivo ARDS models. Elevated CGRP expression inversely correlates with ARDS severity and positively correlates with patient survival, suggesting a protective endogenous response. Mechanistically, CGRP suppresses HIF-1α expression, and pharmacological HIF-1α activation with DMOG reverses CGRP's therapeutic effects on macrophage polarization and lung injury, confirming that HIF-1α pathway inhibition is central to CGRP's protective mechanism. These findings identify CGRP as a potential therapeutic agent targeting macrophage-mediated inflammation in ARDS through HIF-1α modulation.

title: Age-related and postmenopausal breast cancer progression and treatment management: The significance of pro-inflammatory cytokines and CXC chemokines
summary:
# Summary

Aging and postmenopausal status significantly increase breast cancer risk and progression through multiple interconnected inflammatory mechanisms. Cellular senescence and age-related changes to the extracellular matrix promote cancer cell invasion, while menopause-induced estrogen depletion drives visceral fat accumulation and obesity-triggered inflammation characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8/CXCL8, TNF) and CXC chemokines (CXCL1, CXCL9, CXCL10, CXCL12). These inflammatory mediators, predominantly produced by Th1 cells, macrophages, and monocytes, form amplification loops that stimulate cancer cell development and invasion while simultaneously creating immunosuppressive microenvironments through IL-6/IL-8/IL-18 networks that reduce anti-tumor immunity and promote multidrug resistance. The HER2-IL-6-STAT3 signaling cascade drives breast cancer stem cell self-renewal and resistance to anti-HER2 therapy, while aging itself reduces tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and dampens checkpoint immunotherapy responses (anti-CTLA4 and anti-PD-L1). Targeting IL-6 signaling through receptor inhibitors shows promise in preventing bone metastases, highlighting the therapeutic potential of anti-inflammatory strategies in managing age-related and postmenopausal breast cancer progression.

title: 内异停方通过TREM1/TLR4/NF-κB信号通路抑制巨噬细胞M1极化预防子宫内膜异位症术后复发
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Neiyiting (NYT), a traditional Chinese herbal formula, prevents post-surgical recurrence of endometriosis by suppressing macrophage M1 polarization through the TREM1/TLR4/NF-κB signaling pathway. In a rat model of post-operative endometriosis recurrence, NYT treatment significantly reduced recurrent lesion volume and decreased M1 macrophage markers including CD86, iNOS, IL-1β, and TNF-α. Mechanistically, TREM1 expressed on myeloid cells acts as a key trigger, activating TLR4 and downstream NF-κB phosphorylation to drive the pro-inflammatory polarization of macrophages and excessive secretion of inflammatory cytokines that promote endometrial cell adhesion and angiogenesis. NYT suppresses this cascade by downregulating TREM1, TLR4, and NF-κB expression and phosphorylation, thereby reducing M1 macrophage differentiation and inflammatory mediator production. The therapeutic effects were confirmed in in vitro co-culture experiments where TREM1 overexpression promoted M1 polarization and endometrial cell proliferation, effects that were reversed by NYT treatment or TREM1 knockdown. These findings establish TREM1/TLR4/NF-κB signaling as a critical inflammatory pathway in post-surgical endometriosis recurrence and validate NYT as a potential preventive therapeutic targeting macrophage-driven inflammation.

title: Research trends and hotspots of recurrent spontaneous abortion with immune dysfunction: A bibliometric analysis from 2004 to 2024
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric analysis reveals that recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA) is fundamentally driven by immune dysfunction at the maternal-fetal interface, with multiple immune cell populations playing critical roles. The core pathogenic mechanism involves overactivation of natural killer (NK) cells and reduced numbers of regulatory T cells (Tregs), both of which disrupt the delicate immune tolerance required for successful pregnancy. Early recurrent abortion is characterized by excessive Th1/Th17 pro-inflammatory responses with elevated interferon-gamma and tumor necrosis factor-alpha, causing embryonic damage, while late miscarriage is more closely associated with autoimmune conditions like antiphospholipid syndrome that promote placental thrombosis. Uterine NK cells, which constitute the majority of endometrial immune cells during early pregnancy, are essential for placental formation and immune regulation; aberrant uNK activity impairs placental development in patients with recurrent abortion. Emerging therapeutic approaches target these immunological abnormalities, including lymphocyte immunotherapy, cyclosporine A to promote immune tolerance, and TNF-α inhibitors to reduce inflammation, reflecting the shift toward personalized immunomodulatory interventions for improving pregnancy outcomes.

title: IL-24 producing regulatory T and B lymphocytes in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary: IL-24 Producing Regulatory T and B Lymphocytes in Endometriosis

This study identifies regulatory T cells (Tregs) and regulatory B cells (Bregs) as previously unreported sources of IL-24, a member of the IL-10 family cytokine. The researchers found that IL-24–producing Tregs and plasmablasts are significantly elevated in women with endometriosis, while IL-24–producing immature B cells are reduced, suggesting an altered regulatory immune landscape in this disease. Given that endometriosis is characterized by an imbalance in pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokine production, along with elevated levels of IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α, the elevated IL-24–producing regulatory cells may contribute to immune suppression against endometriotic lesions. IL-24 exhibits pleiotropic functions including inhibition of invasion, migration, angiogenesis, and induction of apoptosis—activities consistent with a tumor-suppressive phenotype—while also regulating immune responses through modulation of plasma cell differentiation and mitochondrial signaling. The concentration-dependent effects of IL-24, including promotion of IL-10 production at low doses and activation of apoptotic pathways at high doses, indicate a complex role in immune homeostasis that may be dysregulated in endometriosis.

title: Gut microbiota constituents may affect hypertrophic scarring risk through interaction with specific immune cells in a two-step, two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This two-sample Mendelian randomization study reveals that gut microbiota composition causally influences hypertrophic scarring risk through specific immune cell populations, establishing a mechanistic link between the microbiota-immune-scar axis. Certain bacterial genera—including Subdoligranulum, Adlercreutzia, and Coprococcus 1—exert bidirectional effects on scarring by modulating distinct B and T cell subsets: Subdoligranulum increases risk by activating BAFF-R on CD20− CD38− B cells, while Adlercreutzia increases risk via CD8+ T cell activation, and Coprococcus 1 decreases risk through suppression of CD24+ IgD+ CD38+ B cells. Conversely, protective bacterial taxa like Clostridiaceae family and elevated populations of CD8+ T cells and IgD− CD38− B cells reduce hypertrophic scar formation. The study demonstrates that dysbiosis—characterized by elevated Adlercreutzia, Fusicatenibacter, and Subdoligranulum alongside decreased Clostridiaceae—shifts immune responses toward a pro-fibrotic Th2 phenotype that secretes IL-4, IL-6, and IL-10, cytokines that directly induce skin tissue fibrosis and collagen deposition. These findings implicate the gut-skin-immune axis in wound healing pathology and suggest that microbiota-targeted interventions, including probiotics and selective bacterial enhancement, may represent novel preventive strategies for hypertrophic scar formation.

title: Global research trends in liver regeneration and immunomodulation: A perspective from bibliometric analysis
summary: Liver regeneration is a complex immunomodulatory process in which both innate and adaptive immunity coordinate hepatocyte proliferation and tissue repair. Innate immune cells including Kupffer cells and natural killer cells initiate regeneration by secreting pro-regenerative cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α), while macrophages contribute transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β). Regulatory T cells (Tregs) maintain immune homeostasis by secreting IL-10 and TGF-β to inhibit excessive immune responses and prevent hepatocyte damage, whereas CD8+ T cells produce interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) to further regulate the regenerative process. Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) enhance liver regeneration by upregulating autophagy proteins and reducing Kupffer cell infiltration, providing an alternative therapeutic approach. Current research hotspots include hepatocyte growth factor-c-Met signaling, extracellular matrix remodeling, and angiogenesis, with emerging applications in treating hepatocellular carcinoma through immunomodulatory therapies including immune checkpoint inhibitors and cancer vaccines.

title: Redefinition of Synovial Fibroblasts in Rheumatoid Arthritis
summary:
# Summary

This study redefines the role of synovial fibroblasts (SFs) in rheumatoid arthritis from passive bystanders to active drivers of autoimmune pathology. Loss of immune tolerance and heterogeneous reprogramming of SF subsets trigger the transformation of healthy synovium into an inflamed, pathological state characterized by ectopic germinal center formation and excessive production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. Distinct SF subsets—particularly the FAPα+THY1+ and CD34-THY1+ populations—exhibit elevated expression of IL-6, IL-33, IL-34, RANKL, and matrix metalloproteinases, and orchestrate immune cell infiltration while suppressing apoptosis of inflammatory cells like macrophages and lymphocytes. IL-1β and TGF-β1 from synovial T cells synergistically reprogram the proinflammatory FAPα+THY1+ subset, promoting metabolic adaptations and upregulation of adhesion molecules that enhance immune cell recruitment and bone erosion. Therapeutic targeting of SF phenotypic reprogramming and its upstream molecular drivers represents a promising strategy to restore immune balance and halt RA progression.

title: Anti-cancer effects of genistein supplementation and moderate-intensity exercise in high-fat diet-induced breast cancer via regulation of inflammation and adipose tissue metabolism in vivo and in vitro
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that genistein supplementation combined with moderate-intensity exercise provides synergistic anti-cancer effects in high-fat diet-induced breast cancer through multiple mechanistic pathways. Genistein exerts its anti-tumor activity by inhibiting estrogen receptors, suppressing breast cancer cell proliferation and invasion, inducing apoptosis via regulation of Bcl-2/Bax signaling, and decreasing angiogenic factors including matrix metalloproteinases and vascular endothelial growth factor. Moderate-intensity exercise prevents cancer development and progression by releasing myokines that regulate immune responses and promote anti-inflammatory macrophage polarization. The combination treatment significantly reduced tumor volume and incidence while augmenting pro-tumoral M1 macrophages and diminishing M2 macrophage populations through suppression of JAK1/STAT6 signaling, thereby shifting the tumor microenvironment toward an anti-cancer state. Additionally, the combined intervention prevented adipose tissue wasting and suppressed cancer-induced body weight loss by regulating adipogenesis, lipolysis, and systemic inflammation. These findings highlight genistein and exercise as effective non-pharmacological strategies for breast cancer prevention and treatment in the context of high-fat diet-associated metabolic dysfunction.

title: The Relationship between Proinflammatory Molecules and PD-L1 in Patients with Obesity Who Underwent Gastric Sleeve Surgery—A Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study examined obesity-associated inflammation and immune dysfunction in patients undergoing gastric sleeve surgery. Obesity was characterized by elevated proinflammatory molecules including IL-18, MCP-1, and C-reactive protein, alongside paradoxically reduced PD-L1 levels—a key immune checkpoint protein that normally inhibits T-cell activity. The proinflammatory terrain in obesity is driven by macrophage and immune cell infiltration into adipose tissue, where stressed adipocytes produce inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β that perpetuate chronic inflammation. Sleeve gastrectomy produced promising therapeutic effects, significantly reducing proinflammatory markers (IL-18 and MCP-1) while simultaneously restoring PD-L1 levels, indicating weight loss reverses both the inflammatory and immune dysregulation associated with obesity. The downregulation of PD-L1 in obesity appears mechanistically linked to TNF-α and IL-6-driven suppression, with restoration following surgical intervention suggesting that normalizing the proinflammatory terrain restores immune homeostasis. These findings support bariatric surgery as an effective intervention for simultaneously addressing obesity-related chronic inflammation and immune checkpoint dysfunction.

title: Risk of neurodevelopmental disorders in the offspring of mothers with a history of endometriosis in Taiwanese women
summary:
# Maternal Endometriosis and Neurodevelopmental Disorders in Offspring

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by excessive secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-α, primarily produced by endometriosis cells and peritoneal macrophages. These elevated cytokines drive maternal immune activation that can cross the placenta and disrupt fetal neurodevelopment, as these same cytokines are expressed in the developing nervous system and have been mechanistically linked to central nervous system injury and impaired neurodevelopment. A large Taiwanese cohort study found that offspring of mothers with endometriosis history demonstrated significantly elevated risks for developmental delay and cerebral palsy, with the highest risk for cerebral palsy occurring within the first 36 months postnatally. Beyond inflammatory mechanisms, maternal endometriosis was associated with multiple adverse perinatal outcomes including preterm birth, low birth weight, and lower Apgar scores, alongside elevated maternal comorbidities such as gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, autoimmune disorders, and anxiety that may further contribute to developmental vulnerability. The dysregulated signaling pathways in endometriosis, including JAK/STAT, PI3K/Akt, and Wnt/β-catenin, perpetuate the chronic inflammatory milieu that poses neurodevelopmental risk to offspring during critical fetal and early postnatal windows.

title: A Randomized Controlled Pilot Study Evaluating the Safety and Efficacy of Nifuroxazide in Patients with Ulcerative Colitis
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that nifuroxazide, when combined with mesalamine, effectively treats ulcerative colitis by suppressing key inflammatory pathways central to disease pathogenesis. The drug inhibits the JAK2/STAT3 signaling cascade and blocks IL-6/STAT3 axis activation, thereby reducing the production of proinflammatory cytokines that damage epithelial tight junctions and induce enterocyte apoptosis. Patients receiving nifuroxazide plus mesalamine achieved superior clinical outcomes compared to mesalamine alone, including higher response rates (76% versus 56%) and remission rates (56% versus 24%), alongside significant reductions in serum IL-6, NF-κB, STAT3, and C-reactive protein levels. The combination therapy also substantially improved quality of life measures and symptom severity scores. These findings underscore the therapeutic potential of dual inhibition targeting the IL-6/STAT3 inflammatory axis in UC, offering a promising strategy to reduce colorectal cancer risk associated with chronic inflammatory bowel disease.

title: Mechanistic Aspects of Inflammation and Oxidative Stress and Their Association With Thyroid Cancer Risk
summary:
# Summary: Inflammation, Oxidative Stress, and Thyroid Cancer Risk

Obesity promotes thyroid cancer development through interconnected inflammatory and metabolic pathways. Excessive fat accumulation increases pro-inflammatory factors and reactive oxygen species (ROS), which drive chronic systemic inflammation and oxidative DNA damage—particularly 8-OHdG formation in thyroid cancer patients. The adipokine leptin is elevated in thyroid cancer and activates pro-tumorigenic signaling (PI3K/AKT pathway, cell proliferation, reduced apoptosis), while adiponectin, which is reduced in cancer patients, exerts protective effects through AMPK activation and inhibition of tumor growth pathways including mTOR and PI3K/AKT. Obesity also increases estrogen levels through elevated aromatase expression in adipose tissue, promoting thyroid cancer cell proliferation via estrogen receptor signaling. Additionally, obesity-associated adipose tissue macrophages produce TNF-α and IL-6, which amplify inflammation, activate STAT3 signaling, and enhance tumor invasiveness and angiogenesis. Notably, elevated exhausted T cells in obese patients correlate with improved anti-PD1 immunotherapy responses, suggesting potential therapeutic implications in this population.

title: MiR-124 and MiR-155 as Therapeutic Targets in Microglia-Mediated Inflammation in Multiple Sclerosis
summary: Multiple sclerosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by microglial activation, demyelination, and axonal damage in the central nervous system. Microglia exist in two phenotypic states: pro-inflammatory M1 microglia produce damaging cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, and reactive oxygen species, while anti-inflammatory M2 microglia release neuroprotective factors such as IL-10, TGF-β, and neurotrophic elements like BDNF and IGF-1. MicroRNA dysregulation plays a critical role in MS pathogenesis, with miR-124 acting as an anti-inflammatory regulator that suppresses microglial activation and is significantly downregulated in MS, whereas miR-155 is elevated and promotes inflammatory processes. These microRNAs represent therapeutic targets because they directly control the switch between M1 and M2 microglial phenotypes and regulate the neuroinflammatory cascade. Restoring miR-124 expression or inhibiting miR-155 could potentially rebalance microglial function, reduce demyelination and axonal damage, and alleviate MS-associated cognitive dysfunction, pain, and fatigue.

title: Transitional Lesions, One More Step Towards Understanding the Pathogenesis of Adenomyosis
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by invagination of endometrial glands and stromal cells into the myometrium, causes abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and subfertility through mechanisms involving repeated mechanical or hormonal stress at the endometrial-myometrial interface. The tissue injury and repair (TIAR) hypothesis proposes that this cyclic stress triggers cellular and molecular responses leading to junctional zone remodeling and progressive adenomyosis development, with transitional lesions providing evidence for this progressive pathogenesis. Notably, both transitional and established adenomyotic lesions display significantly reduced populations of conventional immune cells including T cells, NK cells, B cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells compared to normal eutopic endometrium. In contrast, mast cells are markedly elevated in transitional lesions and play a central role in adenomyosis pathogenesis by secreting inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins and cytokines), vascular endothelial growth factor, and other factors that promote tissue invasion, angiogenesis, tissue remodeling, and neurogenic inflammation. Activated mast cells contribute specifically to adenomyosis-related dysmenorrhea and pain, suggesting that mast cell-directed therapies such as Mifepristone, which inhibits mast cell activation and degranulation, represent potential treatment strategies for adenomyosis.

title: An assembled molecular signaling map of interleukin-24: a resource to decipher its multifunctional immunoregulatory role in pathophysiological conditions
summary: Interleukin-24 (IL-24), also known as melanoma differentiation-associated gene-7 (MDA-7) and suppressor of tumorigenicity-16 (ST16), is a pleiotropic cytokine from the IL-10 family with dual pro- and anti-inflammatory properties that is produced primarily by activated monocytes and T helper 2 cells. IL-24 exerts its multifunctional effects through heterodimeric receptor complexes composed of IL-20R1/IL-20R2 or IL-22R1/IL-20R2, activating multiple downstream signaling pathways including JAK-STAT, PI3K-AKT, MAPK, and mTOR to regulate immune responses, tissue homeostasis, and host defense mechanisms. The cytokine demonstrates potent anti-tumor activity through multiple integrated mechanisms including the induction of apoptosis via endoplasmic reticulum and oxidative stress pathways, activation of autophagy through BECN1 and ATG5, cell cycle arrest in the G2-M phase, and suppression of angiogenesis by downregulating endothelial cell markers. Additionally, IL-24 inhibits cancer cell migration and invasion by downregulating matrix metalloproteinases and suppressing adhesion molecules, while simultaneously promoting CD4+ and CD8+ T cell activation and proliferation to enhance immune-mediated anti-tumor responses. Clinical translation of IL-24 as a therapeutic agent, exemplified by Phase I trials using adenoviral vectors (Ad-IL-24), has demonstrated the ability to induce T cell activation and systemic cytokine release, supporting its potential application in cancer immunotherapy and inflammatory disease management.

title: Innate Lymphoid Cells in Reproductive Health and Disease
summary:
# Summary

Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) play central roles in both reproductive health and endometriosis pathogenesis. In healthy reproductive cycles, uterine NK cells (uNK cells) expand through the progesterone-induced IL-15 pathway and support critical pregnancy functions including spiral artery remodeling, placental formation, and fetal growth through production of growth factors and immunoregulatory cytokines. Endometriosis is characterized by dysregulated ILC populations: reduced numbers of protective ILC3 cells that normally support endometrial integrity, elevated ILC2 cells driven by the alarmin IL-33, and altered uNK cell receptor expression with increased inhibitory receptors (KIR2DL1, KIR3DL1, NKG2A, PD-1) and decreased activating receptors (NKp46, NKp30, NKG2D). The IL-33/ILC2 axis promotes disease progression through epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis, and blocking IL-33 or its receptor ST2 represents a promising non-hormonal therapeutic approach. These findings establish ILC dysfunction as a key mechanism underlying endometriosis pathology and suggest multiple immunological targets for therapeutic intervention.

title: miRNA/mRNA analysis of increased TGF-β pathways drive epithelial-mesenchymal transition and regulatory T cell differentiation
summary:
# Summary

Chlamydia trachomatis infection drives endometriosis-associated pathology through dysregulation of microRNA expression, which triggers multiple pathogenic cascades. The dysregulated miRNAs suppress EMT-inhibiting and Treg-promoting miRNAs while enhancing Th17-promoting miRNAs, creating an immunological environment that simultaneously activates both pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive responses. Elevated TGF-β signaling through the TGFB2-TGFBR2-SMAD3-ZEB2 axis promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and Treg differentiation, with EMT facilitating tissue fibrosis and scarring of the oviducts. Endometriotic tissues exhibit upregulated innate and adaptive immune signaling, including enhanced expression of genes involved in antibody production, germinal center formation, and acute phase responses such as IL1B and PTGS2. These pathogenic changes result in classical reproductive complications including pelvic inflammatory disease, chronic pelvic pain, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and adverse pregnancy outcomes. The miRNA-mRNA regulatory network thus establishes a self-perpetuating cycle where microbial infection drives pathological remodeling while immune tolerance mechanisms simultaneously suppress protective inflammatory responses.

title: Toll-like receptor-mediated immune imbalance in asthma: controversies, breakthroughs, and future directions
summary:
# Summary

Toll-like receptors (TLRs) play a critical "double-edged sword" role in asthma pathogenesis by both promoting and inhibiting inflammatory responses. TLR activation stimulates airway epithelial cells to release pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-33, IL-25, and thymic stromal lymphopoietin, which drive Th2-skewed immune responses and airway hyperresponsiveness, while certain TLR signals conversely inhibit allergic responses through type I interferon induction and regulatory T cell promotion. The TLR2/MyD88 pathway offers protective effects by upregulating IL-10 and reducing lung eosinophilia, whereas TLR2 deficiency paradoxically enhances IgE production and exacerbates allergic responses. By contrast, TLR3/TRIF pathway activation exacerbates asthma by promoting airway inflammation, inflammatory cell infiltration, and myofibroblast differentiation, particularly during viral infections. These complex, often contradictory functions of different TLRs suggest that targeted modulation of specific TLR pathways, rather than global TLR suppression, may represent a promising therapeutic strategy for asthma management.

title: Single-cell technologies and spatial transcriptomics: decoding immune low - response states in endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer exhibits complex immune evasion mechanisms that challenge current immunotherapy approaches, with disease risk driven by hormonal factors including estrogen exposure, obesity, and metabolic conditions such as diabetes and polycystic ovary syndrome. Single-cell sequencing and spatial transcriptomics technologies reveal distinct immunological subtypes: p53-mutated endometrial cancers display elevated regulatory T cells, M2 macrophages, and PD-L1+ macrophages creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment associated with poorest survival, while NSMP subtypes demonstrate an immune-desert phenotype with minimal CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocyte infiltration. Cancer-associated fibroblasts, tumor-associated neutrophils, and mesenchymal stromal cells collectively suppress immune responses through secretion of TGF-β and IL-6, and the presence of exhausted CD8+PD-1+ T cells and elevated myeloid-derived suppressor cells further impair antitumor immunity in treatment-resistant disease. Checkpoint inhibitors effectively target microsatellite instability-high and mismatch repair-deficient tumors, but prove less effective in the majority of microsatellite stable cases, highlighting the need for improved biomarkers. Integration of single-cell and spatial transcriptomic data enables identification of discrete immune cell populations, characterization of tumor spatial architecture, and discovery of novel therapeutic targets, facilitating development of multi-omics biomarkers for precise risk stratification and treatment selection.

title: Deciphering the Dialogue between Brain Tumors, Neurons, and Astrocytes
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines the intricate crosstalk between glioblastoma cells, neurons, and astrocytes that collectively drives brain tumor progression and therapeutic resistance. Glioblastoma cells integrate into the brain parenchyma and alter brain functionality through multiple mechanisms, including the production of extracellular vesicles carrying oncogenic miRNAs and lncRNAs that reprogram neighboring astrocytes toward a pro-tumoral phenotype. Reactive astrocytes, initially tumor-suppressive, are converted into tumor-promoting cells that secrete pro-invasive chemokines (MCP-4, CXCL5, glial-derived neurotrophic factor) and growth factors (transforming growth factor-β, fibroblast growth factor, epidermal growth factor) while simultaneously creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment through anti-inflammatory cytokines and physical shielding via connexin-43-mediated gap junctions. Key signaling axes driving progression include the CHI3L1-IL-13Rα2 pathway promoting glioblastoma proliferation and the IL-6-MMP14 axis mediating tumor migration and invasion, with elevated expression of both factors strongly associated with reduced survival. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting ion channels and ion transporters show promise when combined with standard temozolomide treatment, offering potential avenues to disrupt the tumor-astrocyte dialogue and overcome treatment resistance.

title: Regulatory network analysis of Dclk1 gene expression reveals a tuft cell-ILC2 axis that inhibits pancreatic tumor progression
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a tuft cell-ILC2 axis that suppresses pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) progression by analyzing the regulatory network of the Dclk1 gene, which marks distinct pancreatic cell populations. Dclk1 expression identifies two functionally opposing cell populations: Dclk1+ acinar-to-ductal metaplasia (ADM)-like cells that promote tumor growth, and Dclk1+ tuft-like cells that restrain tumor progression through secretion of angiotensinogen. Generation of protective Dclk1+ tuft-like cells requires the transcription factor SPIB and is sustained by a paracrine loop where type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) and cancer-associated fibroblasts secrete IL-13 and IL-33, which expand tuft cell populations. ILC2s further inhibit PDAC progression by activating dendritic cells and CD8+ T cells, demonstrating an anti-tumorigenic immune response. These findings reveal that tuft cell-derived angiotensinogen and the supporting ILC2-CAF paracrine network represent a critical intrinsic brake on pancreatic tumor development, with potential therapeutic implications for enhancing this protective axis.

title: Tumor-associated mesenchymal stem/stromal cells in tumor microenvironment and carcinogenesis
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-associated mesenchymal stem/stromal cells (TA-MSCs) represent a distinct population of stromal cells that play a critical role in promoting tumor progression, metastasis, and immune evasion across multiple cancer types. TA-MSCs exhibit enhanced secretion of pro-inflammatory and pro-tumoral cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, TNFα, and SDF-1α, which facilitate macrophage M2 polarization, cancer cell epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and angiogenesis. Through multiple immunosuppressive mechanisms—including IDO-mediated T cell inhibition, PD-L1 expression, and adenosine-mediated immune suppression—TA-MSCs actively suppress anti-tumor immune responses and promote cancer stem cell proliferation and self-renewal. Additionally, TA-MSCs confer therapeutic resistance, notably mediating 5-fluorouracil resistance in gastric cancer through CTCF-PD-L1 signaling, while their selective migration to inflammatory sites and presence in metastatic lesions, ascites, and lymph nodes underscore their systemic involvement in cancer dissemination. These findings establish TA-MSCs as key orchestrators of the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment and potential therapeutic targets for enhancing cancer treatment efficacy.

title: Immunopathological insights into endometriosis: from research advances to future treatments
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by profound immunological dysregulation, functioning as a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease driven by imbalanced immune cell populations across both innate and adaptive immunity. Key immune abnormalities include accumulation of alternatively activated M2-like macrophages that secrete pro-angiogenic and pro-fibrotic factors (VEGF, NGF, TGF-β1), reduced natural killer cell cytotoxicity due to elevated inhibitory receptors and elevated immunosuppressive ligands (HLA-E, HLA-G), and impaired dendritic cell maturation with increased immature and tolerogenic populations. The inflammatory microenvironment—characterized by elevated chemokines like MCP-1 and cytokines including IL-10 and TGF-β—facilitates ectopic endometrial cell survival, angiogenesis, and lesion growth while simultaneously impairing ovarian function and female fertility. Current established treatments including NSAIDs and hormone therapies exert immunomodulatory effects, while emerging immune-based approaches such as immune cell therapies, cytokine-targeting strategies, and immune checkpoint inhibitors show promise for future therapeutic intervention in this complex immunological disease.

title: Neutrophil Extracellular Traps and Endometriosis: Insights from a Case-Control Study
summary:
# Summary

Neutrophil Extracellular Traps (NETs) are elevated in endometriosis patients and play a central role in disease pathogenesis by promoting peritoneal inflammation and tissue remodeling. This case-control study demonstrates that circulating markers of NET formation—including circulating free DNA, nucleosomes, and neutrophil elastase—are significantly higher in endometriosis patients and correlate positively with both infertility and pain severity as measured by visual analog scale scores. NETs exert their pathogenic effects through multiple mechanisms: they release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNFα and IL-1β) that enhance tumor cell adhesion, disrupt endothelial cell junctions to increase vascular permeability, and facilitate endometrial fibrosis and ectopic lesion development. The study reveals that sex hormones, particularly estrogen and progesterone, regulate the NETosis process (NET formation), suggesting a mechanistic link between endocrine dysregulation and neutrophil-mediated inflammation in endometriosis. These findings position NETs and their associated markers as potential biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis and severity assessment, and suggest that targeting NET formation or NET-derived inflammatory mediators may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for managing this chronic inflammatory condition.

title: CD317 stabilizes TNFR1 and confers the anti-inflammatory functions of MSCs via NF-κB/TSG6 pathway
summary:
# Summary

CD317 enhances the anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive functions of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) by stabilizing tumor necrosis factor receptor 1 (TNFR1) within lipid rafts, thereby promoting NF-κB pathway activation and upregulation of TSG6. The direct interaction between CD317 and TNFR1 facilitates formation of a functional TNFR1 signaling complex, which upon TNF-α stimulation leads to heightened NF-κB activity, including increased p65 nuclear translocation and elevated expression of the immunosuppressive molecule TSG6. CD317+ MSCs demonstrate enhanced capacity to suppress pro-inflammatory cytokine production (IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-1β) and inhibit lymphocyte proliferation compared to CD317-negative cells, with these effects being largely dependent on TSG6 expression as evidenced by knockdown and overexpression studies. The CD317/NF-κB/TSG6 axis represents a critical mechanism through which CD317+ MSCs exert their therapeutic potential in inflammatory diseases including acute lung injury and inflammatory bowel disease. This work establishes CD317 as a key regulator of MSC immune modulation and suggests that engineering MSCs to overexpress CD317 may enhance their therapeutic efficacy in treating endometriosis and other inflammatory conditions.

title: Growth hormone combined with estrogen improves intrauterine adhesion fibrosis by downregulating endometrial microbial citraconic acid to target β-catenin protein
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that combined growth hormone and estrogen therapy effectively treats intrauterine adhesions (IUA) by suppressing a microbiome-derived metabolite pathway. The treatment ameliorates endometrial damage and fibrosis while promoting endometrial regeneration through multiple mechanisms: reducing pro-fibrotic markers (collagen I, TGF-β1), decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6), and enhancing anti-inflammatory and growth-promoting factors (IL-4, IL-10, IGF-1, SDF-1, and TSP-1). Mechanistically, growth hormone activates the GH receptor-JAK-STAT pathway to stimulate cell proliferation and tissue growth, while the combination therapy downregulates endometrial citraconic acid—a microbial metabolite with high affinity for β-catenin—thereby inhibiting the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway implicated in fibrosis. Additionally, dysbiosis in IUA (characterized by reduced Lactobacillus and elevated Gardnerella and Prevotella) contributes to disease pathogenesis, suggesting that the therapeutic effects may involve restoration of beneficial endometrial microbiota. This integrated approach targeting both hormonal signaling and microbiota-metabolite pathways offers a novel strategy for IUA treatment.

title: A CCR5 antagonist enhances the radiosensitivity of hepatocarcinoma in a mouse model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that maraviroc, a CCR5 antagonist, substantially enhances the radiosensitivity of hepatocellular carcinoma by disrupting the CCL5-CCR5 axis that drives myeloid-derived suppressor cell (MDSC) accumulation in the tumor microenvironment. Conventional fractionated radiotherapy (CFRT) upregulates CCL5 secretion, which promotes polymorphonuclear MDSC (PMN-MDSC) differentiation, migration, and immunosuppressive activity against T cells; maraviroc combined with CFRT reverses this pathway by reducing plasma CCL5 levels, inhibiting PMN-MDSC migration and differentiation, and restoring T-cell proliferation. The combination therapy significantly suppressed HCC growth in mice, whereas neither maraviroc nor CFRT monotherapy alone demonstrated meaningful tumor control, indicating synergistic effects. Notably, maraviroc does not directly kill tumor cells but instead remodels the immunosuppressive microenvironment by blocking PMN-MDSC-mediated T-cell suppression, thereby restoring antitumor immunity. These findings establish the CCR5-CCL5 axis as a tractable therapeutic target for enhancing radiotherapy efficacy through immunomodulation rather than direct cytotoxicity.

title: The expression and clinical significance of IL-27 and Foxp3 in oral squamous cell carcinoma
summary: This study investigated the expression and clinical significance of IL-27 and its components (IL-27p28 and EBI3) alongside Foxp3 in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) tissues. IL-27p28, EBI3, and Foxp3 were all significantly elevated in OSCC tissues compared to normal controls, with expression levels of IL-27p28 and EBI3 strongly associated with lymph node metastasis and Foxp3 correlated with clinical stage. IL-27, a heterodimeric cytokine composed of IL-27p28 and EBI3 subunits produced primarily by monocytes and dendritic cells, functions as an immune checkpoint regulator that promotes immunosuppressive responses through multiple mechanisms including upregulation of PD-L1 and LAG-3 on T cells and induction of IL-10 release from regulatory T cells (Tregs). The study suggests that elevated IL-27 and Foxp3 expression in OSCC tissues drives tumor development and progression by activating JAK/STAT signaling pathways and enhancing Treg differentiation and function, thereby creating an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. These findings establish IL-27 and Foxp3 as potential prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets in OSCC, with implications for understanding immune evasion mechanisms in this malignancy.

title: Calycosin suppresses the activating effect of granulocyte-macrophage-colony-stimulating factor-producing T helper cells on macrophages in experimental atherosclerosis
summary: This study demonstrates that calycosin, a natural isoflavone compound, suppresses atherosclerosis progression by inhibiting GM-CSF-producing T helper (ThGM) cells and their pro-atherogenic effects on macrophages. ThGM cells, a specialized CD4+ T cell subset that produces high levels of GM-CSF, IL-2, TNF, and CCL20, directly activate macrophages to produce inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF) and enhance their uptake of oxidized LDL, promoting foam cell formation and plaque development. Calycosin reduces both the frequency and activity of aortic ThGM cells while suppressing their production of pathogenic cytokines, effects mediated through upregulation of the nuclear receptor NR4A3, which promotes T cell anergy and exhaustion. The compound simultaneously reduces Th1 cell frequency and macrophage activation, thereby diminishing atherosclerotic plaque formation in ApoE−/− mice fed a high-fat diet. These findings establish a novel mechanism by which calycosin targets the ThGM-macrophage axis as a therapeutic strategy for atherosclerosis, with potential applications for other inflammatory diseases characterized by aberrant T cell-mediated immune responses.

title: Stem cell treatments for female reproductive disorders: a comprehensive review
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines stem cell-based therapies for female reproductive disorders, particularly endometriosis-associated infertility and conditions like primary ovarian insufficiency and Asherman syndrome. Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) and pluripotent stem cells (ESCs/iPSCs) offer significant therapeutic potential through dual mechanisms: tissue repair via differentiation and immune modulation through paracrine secretion of growth factors (VEGF, GDF9, BMP15), cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), and immunomodulatory molecules (TSG-6). These secreted factors activate key signaling cascades—including Wnt/β-catenin, BMP/SMAD, and PI3K/AKT pathways—that promote tissue regeneration, angiogenesis, and suppress inflammatory responses critical in endometriosis pathology. Clinical evidence demonstrates that MSC-derived treatments can restore ovarian function, increase primordial follicles, and reduce fibrosis, while pluripotent stem cells enable regeneration of damaged reproductive tissues through controlled differentiation into specialized cell types. This review positions stem cell therapy as a promising alternative to conventional hormone replacement and ovulation induction therapies, offering functional restoration rather than symptomatic management for reproductive disorders.

title: Co-occurrence of endometriosis with psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis: Genetic insights (Review)
summary:
# Summary

This review establishes a bidirectional association between endometriosis and psoriasis/psoriatic arthritis, revealing shared immunological mechanisms underlying these inflammatory conditions. Endometriosis involves immune system dysregulation that drives chronic inflammation, angiogenesis, and aberrant T- and B-lymphocyte activation, with elevated autoantibodies and deficient regulatory T cell (Treg) function. Similarly, psoriasis is characterized by dysregulated Th1/Th17 responses, reduced Tregs producing suppressive cytokines (IL-10, IL-35, TGF-β), and enhanced keratinocyte proliferation driven by IFN-γ-producing Th1 cells. Critically, women with endometriosis show substantially increased prevalence of multiple autoimmune and inflammatory comorbidities, including rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, inflammatory bowel disease, and multiple sclerosis. Genetic analysis reveals shared susceptibility loci: TNF-α rs1800629, FOXP3 rs3761549, and HLA-DRB1 rs660895 single nucleotide polymorphisms are associated with all three conditions, suggesting common genetic predisposition to immune dysregulation. These findings support understanding endometriosis as an autoimmune-mediated disorder with shared inflammatory pathways and genetic architecture comparable to classic autoimmune diseases.

title: Immunological Factors in Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: Mechanisms, Controversies, and Emerging Therapies
summary:
# Summary

Recurrent pregnancy loss involves dysregulation of multiple immunological mechanisms essential for maternal-fetal tolerance. Regulatory T cells, which produce immunosuppressive cytokines including transforming growth factor-beta and interleukin-10, are significantly reduced in women with recurrent pregnancy loss and play a critical role in preventing fetal rejection and maintaining placental development through spiral artery remodeling. Conversely, elevated pro-inflammatory Th1 and Th17 cell responses, characterized by increased interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and interferon-gamma, disrupt the delicate Th1/Th2 balance, impair trophoblast invasion, and reduce endometrial receptivity. Emerging therapeutic approaches include regulatory T cell transfer and expansion via low-dose interleukin-2, cytokine-targeted inhibitors (anti-TNF-α, anti-IL-17, anti-IL-6R), and mesenchymal stromal cell therapy to restore immunological tolerance and enhance endometrial receptivity. Advanced immune profiling technologies combining high-dimensional flow cytometry, single-cell transcriptomics, and machine learning are identifying genetic polymorphisms in immune-regulatory genes and enabling personalized therapeutic strategies for immunologically mediated pregnancy loss.

title: The Role of Macrophage-Derived Netrin-1 in Inflammatory Diseases
summary:
# Summary

Netrin-1, a laminin-related molecule encoded by the NTN1 gene, plays a multifaceted role in inflammatory and tissue regeneration processes, with macrophage-derived netrin-1 emerging as a critical mediator. This protein is widely expressed across tissues including the brain, lungs, heart, liver, intestine, and kidney, and is produced by nerve cells, tumor cells, macrophages, and endothelial cells. Netrin-1 exerts dual immunomodulatory functions by both inducing and inhibiting leukocyte chemotaxis through different receptor pathways, while simultaneously promoting angiogenesis, cell survival, and neurovascular regeneration via binding to receptors including DCC, Neogenin, UNC5 family members, and CD146. In particular, microglial netrin-1 protects neurons during ischemic stroke by binding to UNC5a and inhibiting apoptosis, thereby enhancing survival outcomes. M2 macrophages, which are promoted through netrin-1 signaling, produce anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-10 and TGF-β and support tissue repair and wound healing, contrasting sharply with pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages that generate reactive oxygen species and pro-inflammatory mediators. These findings establish netrin-1 as a pivotal regulator bridging macrophage function with inflammatory resolution and tissue regeneration in multiple pathological contexts.

title: Serum Concentrations of Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor in Polish Patients with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Are Associated with Cardiovascular Risk and Autoantibody Profiles
summary: This study reveals a paradoxical relationship between vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) serum concentrations and cardiovascular risk in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) patients. High VEGF levels correlated strongly with anti-phospholipid antibodies (anti-prothrombin and anti-beta2 glycoprotein I) and significantly increased the risk of cardiac disorders (odds ratio = 8.0), while low VEGF concentrations were associated with reduced risks of atherosclerotic lesions, vasculitis, and certain autoantibody profiles including anti-double-stranded DNA and anti-endothelial cell antibodies. VEGF is produced by macrophages, fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and smooth muscle cells, and its production is stimulated by inflammation and IL-17, both of which drive atherosclerosis progression through the IL-17–IL-23 axis in SLE. The detrimental cardiovascular effects of elevated VEGF likely result from its promotion of neovascularization within atherosclerotic plaques, suppression of endothelial repair mechanisms, and activation of smooth muscle cells that initiate atherosclerotic processes. These findings suggest that VEGF concentrations serve as an important biomarker linking autoimmune dysregulation to accelerated atherosclerosis in SLE patients and may help stratify cardiovascular risk based on specific autoantibody profiles.

title: Porcine decellularized nerve matrix hydrogel attenuates neuroinflammation after peripheral nerve injury by inhibiting the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB axis
summary:
# Summary

Porcine decellularized nerve matrix (pDNM) hydrogel promotes functional recovery after peripheral nerve injury by suppressing neuroinflammation through inhibition of the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB signaling axis. The hydrogel reduces pro-inflammatory M1 macrophage polarization and the associated production of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, while simultaneously promoting M2 macrophage differentiation and anti-inflammatory cytokine secretion (TGF-β and IL-10). Treatment with pDNM-gel reduces key components of the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB pathway at both protein and gene expression levels, preventing macrophage phenotype transformation toward the pro-inflammatory state. Functionally, pDNM-gel treatment significantly improves locomotor recovery, nerve conduction velocity, and gastrocnemius muscle regeneration, with enhanced expression of axonal and myelin-related proteins including neurofilament-H, MBP, GAP43, and Pmp22. These findings demonstrate that pDNM-gel acts as a biomaterial scaffold that shifts the macrophage response from inflammatory to reparative, providing a novel therapeutic approach for peripheral nerve injury recovery.

title: Identifying fibroblast-derived sFRP2 as a therapeutic target and engineering siRNA therapy for uterine scarring
summary: Uterine scarring, a common consequence of surgical procedures including cesarean sections and dilatation and curettage, leads to serious reproductive complications such as infertility, pelvic pain, and adverse pregnancy outcomes. This study identifies secreted frizzled-related protein 2 (sFRP2), highly expressed in uterine fibroblasts from scarred tissue, as a critical mediator of scar formation through activation of non-canonical Wnt/calcium signaling pathways. sFRP2 overexpression induces myofibroblast differentiation and excessive extracellular matrix deposition—including collagen and fibrinogen—while promoting angiogenesis via VEGFA upregulation, all hallmarks of pathological fibrosis. Conversely, sFRP2 knockdown effectively inhibits these fibrotic processes in both human and mouse uterine fibroblasts. The authors demonstrate that lipid nanoparticle-delivered siRNA targeting sFRP2 successfully suppresses sFRP2 expression, reduces uterine scar formation, and improves pregnancy outcomes in vivo. These findings establish sFRP2 as a therapeutic target for treating uterine scarring and offer a promising RNA interference-based approach to restore uterine function and prevent pregnancy-related complications.

title: Microbiome Against the Background of the Complex Aetiology in Sarcoidosis—What Do We Already Know?
summary:
# Summary

Sarcoidosis is a complex inflammatory disorder characterized by non-necrosing granuloma formation, whose etiology involves the interplay of genetic susceptibility, environmental factors, and autoimmunity. Multiple HLA gene variants influence disease progression and presentation, with specific alleles conferring protection or predisposing to chronic or acute (Löfgren syndrome) manifestations, while non-HLA genes like ANXA11 and XAF1 further modulate granuloma durability. The respiratory tract microbiome plays a significant role in sarcoidosis development, with Cutibacterium acnes and other bacteria detected in the majority of active cases and granulomatous lesions, potentially through production of immunomodulatory metabolites like short-chain fatty acids. Environmental triggers including silica dust, smoke exposure, and elevated leptin in obese patients activate macrophages and promote an exaggerated Th1/Th17 response characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-2, TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-17), which drive and sustain granuloma formation through pattern recognition receptor signaling. Treatment approaches include corticosteroids and immunosuppressants such as methotrexate, azathioprine, and TNF-α inhibitors for patients with poor prognosis or granulomas in critical organs.

title: Celiac Disease and Skin Diseases: A Bidirectional Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

This bidirectional Mendelian randomization study establishes celiac disease (CD) as a causal risk factor for multiple skin conditions, particularly atopic dermatitis and cellulitis, through distinct immunological and nutritional mechanisms. CD triggers chronic intestinal inflammation via HLA-DQ presentation of gluten peptides to T cells, leading to intestinal mucosal damage and increased permeability that facilitates allergic reactions and skin disease development. Atopic dermatitis pathogenesis involves skin barrier dysfunction, aberrant immune responses, and microbial dysbiosis, with shared genetic factors including CTLA-4 polymorphisms and elevated FOXP3+ regulatory T cells in both conditions. In contrast, CD increases cellulitis susceptibility through nutrient malabsorption causing hypoproteinemia, edema, and splenic insufficiency that compromise immune defense against bacterial pathogens. The CD-to-skin disease causal relationships remained robust after adjusting for lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, and BMI, whereas reverse causality was not demonstrated. Notably, no causal relationship was found between CD and urticaria or pruritus, suggesting mechanistic specificity in CD-associated dermatological manifestations.

title: Topography-based implants for bone regeneration: Design, biological mechanism, and therapeutics
summary:
# Summary: Topography-Based Implants for Bone Regeneration

Implant surface topography critically regulates bone regeneration through coordinated control of osteogenic cell behavior and macrophage-mediated immune responses. Nano-structured features, particularly titanium nanotubes of 30-140 nm diameter and hierarchical microgroove patterns, promote osteoblast adhesion and differentiation while suppressing osteoclast activity, partly through activation of integrin-dependent signaling pathways including MAPK, PI3K/Akt, and Wnt/β-catenin cascades. These surface structures also skew macrophage polarization toward the M2 anti-inflammatory phenotype, which secretes pro-regenerative cytokines such as transforming growth factor-β and bone morphogenetic protein-2 that promote osteogenesis and angiogenesis. By contrast, nano- and micro-nano-structured surfaces reduce macrophage adhesion and suppress pro-inflammatory cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), thereby limiting the deleterious M1-mediated inflammation that impedes healing. The inflammatory phase is initiated by hematoma formation, which recruits neutrophils and macrophages that clear debris and establish the foundation for osteogenic cell recruitment, demonstrating that strategic manipulation of surface topography can harness innate immune responses to enhance bone regeneration therapeutically.

title: The immune microenvironment in endometrial carcinoma: mechanisms and therapeutic targeting
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial carcinoma exhibits a profoundly immunosuppressive microenvironment dominated by M2-polarized tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) that secrete IL-10, TGF-β, and PGE2 while producing pro-angiogenic VEGF and expressing the checkpoint ligand PD-L1, thereby directly inhibiting CD8+ T cell function. Myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) and cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) further suppress anti-tumor immunity through secretion of arginase, reactive oxygen species, and multiple growth factors (FGF, HGF, IGF), while extracellular vesicles transport oncogenic miRNAs, immunosuppressive ligands, and drug efflux pumps that collectively promote tumor progression and chemoresistance. Elevated lactic acid promotes M2 polarization, and multiple signaling pathways—including PI3K/Akt, JAK/STAT3, and NF-κB—are activated by these immune and stromal-derived factors to enhance tumor cell survival and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. High densities of M2 TAMs and MDSCs correlate with advanced disease stage, increased tumor grade, and reduced overall survival. PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors represent a promising therapeutic approach to restore T cell anti-tumor activity in this immunologically permissive microenvironment.

title: Exploring the Immune System's Role in Endometriosis: Insights Into Pathogenesis, Pain, and Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis pathogenesis involves dysregulation of multiple immune cell populations, including macrophages, natural killer cells, neutrophils, dendritic cells, T cells, and B cells, which collectively contribute to a chronic inflammatory environment that sustains ectopic lesion growth and perpetuates pain. A critical imbalance exists between pro-inflammatory T helper 17 cells (which are elevated) and regulatory T cells (which are diminished), with IL-17 signaling driving both inflammatory changes and pain mechanisms through sensitization of nerve endings and modulation of dendritic cells. Macrophages show impaired phagocytic function while paradoxically producing pro-inflammatory cytokines and angiogenic factors that enhance ectopic tissue vascularization; additionally, they interact with nerve endings to promote pain pathology. Natural killer cells exhibit diminished cytotoxicity due to upregulation of inhibitory receptors, allowing ectopic tissue to evade immune surveillance, while neutrophils and dendritic cells further promote angiogenesis. The dysregulated inflammatory state triggers peripheral and central nervous system sensitization, promoting nerve fiber growth that exacerbates chronic pelvic pain and can lead to pelvic adhesions and infertility. Emerging therapeutic approaches targeting the IL-17 pathway show promise for alleviating endometriosis-associated pain by modulating this central inflammatory cascade.

title: Activated eosinophil plays a role in promoting fibrosis in endometriotic lesion
summary:
# Summary

Eosinophils accumulate prominently in endometriotic lesions, particularly in ovarian endometriomas, where they promote pathological fibrosis through activation of stromal and epithelial cells. Hypoxia within endometriotic lesions stimulates stromal cells to produce granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), which activates eosinophils that then release major basic protein and other factors to activate fibroblasts and upregulate plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1) in endometrial cells. The eosinophil-PAI-1 axis drives collagen deposition and progressive fibrosis, which directly contributes to severe endometriosis symptoms including dyspareunia and infertility. Notably, standard hormonal therapies including progestin and GnRH analogs fail to reduce eosinophil accumulation, suggesting that eosinophil-targeting strategies such as anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibodies represent a novel therapeutic approach distinct from conventional endometriosis treatments. These findings identify eosinophil-mediated pathways as previously uncharacterized drivers of endometriotic fibrosis, offering potential new therapeutic targets for managing this chronic inflammatory disease.

title: Influence of Immune Cells and Circulating Inflammatory Cytokines on Pathological Scars: A Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identified multiple immune cell populations and inflammatory cytokines causally associated with pathological scar formation, including hypertrophic scars and keloids. Elevated levels of specific T cell subsets—particularly resting and activated CD4+ regulatory T cells, naive CD4+ T cells, and CD25-expressing T cells—increase hypertrophic scar risk, while CD8+ T cells exert protective effects. Monocyte and natural killer cell phenotypes show divergent associations, with certain HLA-DR+ monocyte populations reducing risk while CD16+ monocyte subsets and specific NK cell markers elevate it. Circulating cytokines also play critical roles, with IL-10 and TRAIL reducing scar formation through anti-inflammatory mechanisms and inhibition of fibroblast-to-myofibroblast differentiation, whereas PD-L1, stem cell factor, and GDNF promote pathological scarring. These findings reveal that pathological scars result from dysregulated immune cell composition and inflammatory signaling, suggesting potential therapeutic targets through immune modulation or cytokine-directed interventions.

title: Dynamic crosstalk between HSCs and liver microenvironment: multicellular interactions in the regulation of liver fibrosis
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Hepatic stellate cell (HSC) activation is a central driver of liver fibrosis, regulated through complex multicellular crosstalk involving hepatocytes, cholangiocytes, and immune cells.
