Damaged hepatocytes release multiple damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs)—including DNA, HMGB1, mitochondrial DNA, and extracellular vesicles containing miR-128-3p and miR-1297—that directly activate HSCs via pattern recognition receptors like TLR9 and P2Y14, or indirectly through ROS accumulation and iron overload. Hepatocyte-derived signals including TGF-β1, OPN, and NINJ2-regulated PDGF-BB promote HSC activation, while dying hepatocytes also recruit macrophages through ATP and CD47 signaling, which can be therapeutically targeted to reduce fibrosis. Cholangiocytes contribute to fibrosis through WNT pathway activation of HSCs and MCP-1-mediated fibroblast transformation, while bile acids activate HSCs via TGR5 and S1PR2 signaling and promote neutrophil accumulation through MAPK/Egr-1 pathways. This multifaceted crosstalk establishes HSC activation as the convergence point of hepatocyte injury, immune activation, and cholestatic signaling in liver fibrosis pathogenesis.

title: Increased CD163+ Macrophage Activation and High Expression of CD163 Promote Granulosa Cell Apoptosis in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elevated CD163+ macrophage activation drives granulosa cell apoptosis in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) through a pro-inflammatory pathway. In PCOS patients and DHEA-induced PCOS mouse models, both M1 and M2 macrophages are increased in ovarian and uterine tissues with concurrent upregulation of CD163 expression, and serum soluble CD163 (sCD163) levels are significantly elevated. M1-polarized macrophages promote granulosa cell apoptosis in vitro by secreting inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α, which cleaves membrane-bound CD163 into sCD163, a marker of macrophage activation. The inflammatory microenvironment created by CD163+ macrophage activation impairs granulosa cell function, disrupts normal ovarian niche physiology, and contributes to aberrant follicle development and anovulation characteristic of PCOS. These findings identify CD163+ macrophage-driven inflammation as a key mechanism linking immune dysregulation to ovarian dysfunction and infertility in PCOS.

title: Mouse Tumor Tissue‐Derived Extracellular Vesicles Induce Angiogenesis Through VEGF Production From Macrophages
summary: This study demonstrates that tumor-derived extracellular vesicles (tEVs) promote angiogenesis indirectly through macrophage recruitment and polarization rather than by directly stimulating endothelial cells. Tumor tEVs from multiple mouse cancer types (melanoma, bladder, and colorectal carcinoma) activate macrophages toward an M2-like phenotype characterized by elevated CD206 and arginase-1 expression, which then produce vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). The angiogenic pathway depends on MEK, MAPK, and NF-κB signaling in macrophages, while proteinase K treatment eliminates tEV activity, indicating protein-mediated effects. Notably, tEVs promote macrophage migration more effectively than VEGF alone, and macrophage depletion with clodronate liposomes blocks tEV-induced angiogenesis entirely, confirming that macrophage-derived VEGF, not direct endothelial stimulation, mediates the pro-angiogenic effects. The resulting blood vessels are functionally integrated into systemic circulation and exhibit increased vascular permeability. These findings establish tEVs as critical intercellular communication mediators in the tumor microenvironment that coordinate immune and vascular responses through macrophage-dependent mechanisms.

title: Molecular Mechanisms of Chronic Pain and Therapeutic Interventions
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pain encompasses three distinct mechanisms—nociceptive pain from tissue injury, neuropathic pain from nerve damage, and nociplastic pain from nervous system sensitization—each involving different pathological pathways. The article identifies immune-inflammatory mechanisms as central to chronic pain pathogenesis, with nociceptors functioning as immune sensors that express receptors for cytokines and chemokines while simultaneously producing inflammatory mediators. Chemokine signaling pathways, particularly CXCL1/CXCR2 and CCL2/CCR2, drive chronic inflammatory pain development through immune cell recruitment and neuronal sensitization. Complement cascade components (C3a and C5a) and macrophage-derived mediators including IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6, and prostaglandin E2 promote nociceptor hyperexcitability through peripheral sensitization. Nerve growth factor (NGF) emerges as a critical amplifier of pain signaling, upregulating pain-associated ion channels (TRPV1, ASIC) and activating intracellular kinase cascades that enhance neuronal excitability and mast cell degranulation. These molecular mechanisms establish inflammation and immune activation as therapeutic targets for managing chronic pain conditions associated with tissue pathology and neuroinflammation.

title: The silent protectors: how neutrophils influence female fertility during cancer treatment – a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Chemotherapy and radiotherapy trigger inflammatory responses that recruit neutrophils to ovarian tissue, where these immune cells play a dual and paradoxical role in female fertility outcomes. Neutrophils release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and reactive oxygen species that accelerate follicular atresia and disrupt follicular development and hormonal balance, contributing to chemotherapy- and radiation-induced premature ovarian failure. However, neutrophils also possess reparative capacity, releasing growth factors such as vascular endothelial growth factor and fibroblast growth factor that promote tissue healing and regeneration of damaged ovarian tissue following cancer treatment. Additionally, neutrophils release matrix metalloproteinases that degrade extracellular matrix components, further complicating the ovarian microenvironment. Understanding this complex inflammatory dynamic is essential for developing protective strategies to preserve female fertility during cancer treatment, as the balance between neutrophil-mediated tissue damage and tissue repair ultimately determines ovarian recovery outcomes.

title: Unveiling the Pathological Landscape of Intrauterine Adhesion: Mechanistic Insights and Exosome-Biomaterial Therapeutic Innovations
summary: Intrauterine adhesion (IUA) is a fibrotic disorder initiated by endometrial injury from procedures such as curettage or abortion, leading to infertility and recurrent pregnancy loss through abnormal endometrial fibrosis. Inflammation serves as the primary driver of pathology, activating multiple fibrotic signaling cascades including TGF-β/Smad and WNT/β-catenin pathways while simultaneously inducing epithelial-mesenchymal transition through pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, TGF-β) and ferroptosis-mediated tissue damage. Dysregulated cellular processes including impaired autophagy and excessive ferroptosis contribute to aberrant extracellular matrix accumulation and myofibroblast differentiation, with M2 macrophages secreting GAS6 to further activate stromal cell transdifferentiation via AXL/NF-κB signaling. Current surgical treatment (transcervical resection of adhesions) provides only short-term anatomical restoration, prompting investigation of exosome-based therapies that modulate fibrosis, inflammation, and EMT while exerting potent antifibrotic and tissue repair effects. Emerging therapeutic strategies employ exosome delivery systems enhanced by biomaterials such as hyaluronic acid hydrogels and decellularized extracellular matrix scaffolds to enable sustained and targeted therapeutic delivery, while mesenchymal stem cell-derived stanniocalcin-1 enhances macrophage phagocytic capacity to resolve inflammation.

title: A granulin-positive macrophage subtype in mycobacterial granulomas alleviates tissue damage by limiting excessive inflammation
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies granulin-positive (grna.2+) macrophages as a specialized immune subset that limits pathological inflammation during mycobacterial infection while maintaining effective host defense. Mycobacterial infection induces expansion of grna.2+ macrophages within granulomas, where they suppress excessive inflammatory responses by dampening pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNFα, IL-1β, and IL-6. Depletion of grna.2+ macrophages leads to early cytokine surge, increased macrophage death, impaired T cell infiltration, higher bacterial loads, and formation of necrotic granulomas, demonstrating their critical role in balancing immune control with tissue protection. The anti-inflammatory function of grna.2+ macrophages appears linked to TNFα-TNFSF1a and IL-1β-PYCARD signaling pathways, and similar GRN-positive macrophages were identified in tuberculosis patient samples, suggesting translational relevance. These findings establish grna.2+ macrophages as essential regulators that alleviate collateral tissue damage while preserving antimicrobial immunity, a mechanism with potential implications for understanding immunopathology in chronic infections and inflammatory diseases.

title: How lactate and lactylation shape the immunity system in atherosclerosis (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Atherosclerosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by lipid metabolism dysfunction and the accumulation of lactate within atherosclerotic plaques, where it functions as both a metabolic byproduct and a potent signaling molecule regulating immune responses. Lactate and its post-translational modification, lactylation, emerge as critical regulators of immune cell polarization and metabolism, with lactylation specifically modulating macrophage polarization, T-cell differentiation, and B-cell metabolic function. The acidic microenvironment created by lactic acid accumulation facilitates oxidized low-density lipoprotein binding to proteoglycans and stabilizes hypoxia-inducible factor-1α, promoting pro-angiogenic responses and plaque formation. Adaptive immunity plays a central role in atherosclerosis progression, with pro-inflammatory Th1 cells driving lesion development and plaque instability through foam cell formation and macrophage pro-inflammatory polarization, while regulatory T cells and anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-5 and IL-13 suppress disease progression. These findings demonstrate that lactate-mediated metabolic reprogramming and epigenetic regulation fundamentally influence immune cell function and atherosclerotic plaque stability, positioning lactate and lactylation as potential therapeutic targets in atherosclerotic disease management.

title: Mathematical modelling of macrophage and natural killer cell immune response during early stages of peritoneal endometriosis lesion onset
journal: Journal of the Royal Society Interface
summary:
# Mathematical Modelling of Early-Stage Peritoneal Endometriosis Immune Response

This study develops a mathematical model examining how macrophages and natural killer (NK) cells control endometrial cell clearance during early peritoneal endometriosis lesion formation. Retrograde menstruation deposits endometrial cells into the peritoneal fluid, where normally M1-type (pro-inflammatory) macrophages and cytotoxic NK cells clear these cells; however, ectopic endometrial cells exhibit reduced apoptotic marker expression and resistance to NK cell killing, limiting immune recognition. The model reveals that reduced immune clearance and detection capacity represents a critical driver of disease, with immune dysfunction creating bistable dynamics where even partial improvement cannot reverse established lesions due to hysteresis effects. In affected individuals, high retrograde influx and endometrial cell attachment promote sustained M1 activation and inflammation, yet paradoxically, late-stage disease associates with shift toward anti-inflammatory M2 macrophages that promote lesion growth through angiogenesis and proliferation. The findings predict that defective immune cytotoxicity combined with impaired detection of ectopic cells permits disease establishment and transition, suggesting that restoring innate immune surveillance could represent a therapeutic target for early endometriosis intervention.

title: Predictive Biomarkers for Immunotherapy in Endometrial Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial carcinoma exhibits distinct molecular subtypes with varying immunotherapy responsiveness determined by tumor mutational burden and immune checkpoint expression. POLE-mutated and mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) endometrial carcinomas achieve ultramutation rates through frameshift mutations, generating abundant neoantigens that enhance CD8+ T cell recognition and immune infiltration, making these tumors highly responsive to anti-PD-1 therapies like dostarlimab. In contrast, NSMP and copy-number high (CNH) carcinomas carry lower mutation burdens and reduced neoantigen production, resulting in poor immunotherapy candidates. The tumor immune microenvironment critically modulates therapeutic outcomes through multiple mechanisms: the PD-1/PD-L1 axis promotes T cell exhaustion, while immunosuppressive cells including regulatory T cells, M2 macrophages, and myeloid-derived suppressor cells inhibit anti-tumor immunity through TGF-β, IL-10, and IL-6 production. Tumor mutational burden emerges as a key predictive biomarker for immunotherapy response, with higher TMB correlating with increased tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and neoantigen load, thereby predicting favorable outcomes to checkpoint blockade.

title: Preventive role of CD163-positive macrophages in postoperative peritoneal adhesions
summary:
# Summary

Surgical trauma activates macrophages to release pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6, which regulate mesothelial cells and promote postoperative peritoneal adhesion formation through inflammatory activation, abnormal fibrinolysis, and aberrant extracellular matrix remodeling. CD163-positive macrophages accumulate in the serous and subserosa layers at sites of postoperative inflammation, where they interact with mesothelial cells to enhance the fibrinolytic system and reduce PAI-1 secretion, thereby preventing adhesion formation. Genetic studies demonstrate that CD163 deficiency markedly increases adhesion index in knockout mice, confirming the protective role of CD163-positive macrophages. Mechanistically, CD163-positive macrophages regulate adhesion molecules and suppress Serpine1 expression, which encodes PAI-1, while also secreting matrix metalloproteinases involved in tissue remodeling. These findings establish CD163-positive macrophages as key mediators that prevent postoperative adhesion formation by orchestrating a balance between inflammatory response and fibrinolysis at the wound healing site.

title: Prdm12 governs an epigenetic checkpoint linking neuroimmune cross-talk to CD8+ T cell exhaustion-suppressed antitumor immunity
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies Prdm12 as a critical epigenetic checkpoint that links neuroimmune signaling to CD8+ T cell exhaustion and impaired antitumor immunity. The nociceptor-derived neuropeptide CGRP activates the CGRP-RAMP1 axis, which upregulates Prdm12 expression and promotes T cell exhaustion through H3K9me3-mediated repression of effector genes including Rgs1, Trib1, Nr4a2, and Sgk1. Prdm12 ablation markedly enhances CD8+ T cell antitumor activity by augmenting effector transcriptional programs, increasing cytokine production (IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α), and promoting memory precursor differentiation while reducing exhaustion markers. In vivo, Prdm12-knockout CD8+ T cells substantially inhibit tumor growth and increase tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte frequency, while remaining resistant to CGRP-induced exhaustion. These findings reveal a neuroimmune-epigenetic regulatory axis controlling T cell fate and demonstrate that disrupting this pathway could enhance cancer immunotherapy by suppressing the CD8+ T cell exhaustion program imposed by neuronal signaling.

title: Effects of spinal versus general anesthesia on serum oxidative stress markers and cytokine release after abdominal hysterectomy: a non-randomized trial
summary: This study compared spinal and general anesthesia effects on oxidative stress and inflammatory markers in patients undergoing abdominal hysterectomy. General anesthesia significantly increased malondialdehyde (MDA), a marker of lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress, while reducing catalase (CAT) antioxidant enzyme activity postoperatively. In contrast, spinal anesthesia better preserved antioxidant defenses, maintaining higher superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity. General anesthesia also triggered a pronounced pro-inflammatory response, elevating the cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 postoperatively and increasing the pro-inflammatory to anti-inflammatory IL-6/IL-10 ratio, whereas spinal anesthesia produced minimal cytokine changes. These findings suggest that spinal anesthesia mitigates surgical stress-induced oxidative damage and excessive inflammatory responses compared to general anesthesia, potentially reducing complications and improving postoperative recovery in patients undergoing abdominal hysterectomy.

title: The effect of NF-kB and MAPK mediated Proinflammatory microenvironment on renal aging and amyloid deposition in elder rats
summary:
# Renal Aging, Inflammation, and Amyloid Deposition in Elder Rats

Aging triggers a low-grade proinflammatory state characterized by elevated TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1 in kidney tissue, which activates NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways to sustain chronic inflammation. This inflammatory microenvironment disrupts the renal barrier by downregulating occludin through TNF-α and IL-6, leading to structural kidney damage including glomerulosclerosis, tubular dilatation, epithelial desquamation, and increased apoptosis. The proinflammatory cytokines also induce endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress via GRP78 activation and trigger the acute phase response, elevating serum amyloid A (SAA) which is cleaved into AA amyloid deposits associated with systemic amyloidosis. Although amyloid accumulation was not detected in kidney tissue itself, the study demonstrates that aging-induced low-grade inflammation through NF-κB and MAPK pathways drives the pathogenic conditions supporting renal dysfunction and aging-related diseases. These findings establish chronic inflammation as a central mechanism linking aging to kidney degeneration and highlight the interconnected roles of TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1, ER stress, and inflammatory signaling in age-related pathology.

title: Single-cell analysis of follicular fluid reveals dysregulation of ovulatory immune function in PCOS patients undergoing ovarian stimulation
summary:
# Summary

Single-cell analysis of follicular fluid from PCOS patients undergoing ovarian stimulation reveals a fundamental dysregulation of the ovulatory immune response compared to controls. While normal ovulation involves controlled recruitment of pro-inflammatory cytokines and immune cells—including macrophages, Th22 cells, and tissue-resident dendritic cells—PCOS follicles display a chronic low-grade inflammatory state with paradoxically reduced local inflammatory cytokine production and a displacement of this response to the periphery. PCOS peripheral immune cells exhibit elevated pro-inflammatory transcriptional programming with heightened expression of TNF, IL-6, IL-18, and IL-1β, alongside skewed macrophage and T cell populations that maintain abnormal activation even after ovulation induction. Notably, control follicles uniquely activate canonical ovulatory signaling pathways (JAK-STAT, IL-17, NF-κB, and TNF), whereas PCOS follicles instead upregulate autoimmune disease pathways and genes associated with infectious and lipid dysregulation. These findings indicate that PCOS-associated infertility stems not merely from systemic inflammation but from a fundamental failure of local follicular immune cells to mount the appropriate acute inflammatory response required for successful ovulation.

title: Single-cell technology reveals the crosstalk between tumor cells and immune cells: driving immune signal transduction and inflammation-mediated cardiac dysfunction in the tumor microenvironment of colorectal cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study employs single-cell RNA sequencing to delineate the inflammatory landscape of colorectal cancer, revealing that a proliferative MKI67+ tumor cell subpopulation (MTRS) functions as a key driver of immune signal transduction and inflammation within the tumor microenvironment. The MKI67+ cells interact with tumor-associated macrophages through the MIF-(CD74+CD44) signaling axis, facilitating macrophage-mediated immunological signaling that promotes tumor proliferation while simultaneously triggering inflammatory responses that contribute to cardiac dysfunction. HMGA1, a critical transcription factor enriched in this proliferative subpopulation, regulates colorectal cancer cell proliferation, motility, and invasion; HMGA1 knockdown suppresses these aggressive phenotypes and represents a viable immunotherapy target. Tumor-associated macrophages polarize into M1 and M2 phenotypes and release cytokines, chemokines, and growth factors that broadly influence pro-inflammatory leukocytes, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts, while tumor cells evade immunity through PD-L1 expression and secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines like IL-10 and TGF-β. The study underscores chronic inflammation as a unifying pathophysiological mechanism linking colorectal cancer to cardiovascular dysfunction and identifies the tumor cell-macrophage interaction as a therapeutic vulnerability.

title: Neutrophils in cancer: At the crucial crossroads of anti‐tumor and pro‐tumor
summary:
# Summary

Neutrophils play a dual and complex role in cancer, simultaneously promoting and inhibiting tumor progression through their presence in the tumor microenvironment. Neutrophils are involved in multiple pro-tumor processes including cancer initiation, progression, proliferation, metastasis, and angiogenesis, while also serving as biomarkers for therapeutic response. Neutrophil recruitment and survival are tightly regulated by multiple pathways: G-CSF and related cytokines (GM-CSF, IL-6, IL-17, IL-23) control neutrophil generation and differentiation, while chemokine gradients involving CXCR1/CXCR2 and CXCL1/CXCL2 direct neutrophil trafficking to tumors. Neutrophil phenotype—specifically polarization toward pro-tumor N2 or anti-tumor N1 subsets—is determined by the tumor microenvironment through TGF-β signaling, hypoxia, and IL-6/STAT3/ERK pathways. Low-density neutrophils expressing LOX-1 acquire myeloid-derived suppressor cell (MDSC) characteristics and potently suppress anti-tumor T-cell responses, whereas high-density neutrophils maintain anti-tumor activity. Understanding these regulatory mechanisms is essential for therapeutic targeting of the neutrophil response in cancer.

title: Inflammatory cytokine interleukin-18 prevents murine miscarriage by inducing appropriate inflammation
summary: This study demonstrates that interleukin-18 (IL-18), produced primarily by myometrial smooth muscle cells, plays a critical protective role against pregnancy loss by orchestrating balanced immune responses at the feto-maternal interface. Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) challenge induces IL-18 production and triggers miscarriage in mice; however, neutralization of IL-18 significantly increases abortion rates even at subthreshold LPS doses, establishing IL-18 as essential for pregnancy maintenance. IL-18 activates both type 1 (interferon-γ) and type 2 (interleukin-4) immune pathways through NK cells, T cell subsets, and tissue-resident lymphocytes, which signal through STAT1 and STAT6 phosphorylation respectively in the decidua and myometrium. Combined supplementation with interferon-γ and interleukin-4 markedly improves pregnancy outcomes, whereas individual cytokine therapy provides only partial protection. These findings reveal that IL-18 prevents miscarriage not through suppressing inflammation but by promoting appropriate balanced inflammatory responses, challenging the conventional model that all pregnancy-related inflammation is detrimental and suggesting IL-18 could be therapeutic in infection-associated reproductive failure.

title: Reconstitution of T cell‐mediated immunity by umbilical cord‐derived mesenchymal stem cells in ulcerative colitis
summary: Umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells (UMSCs) effectively treat ulcerative colitis by reconstituting T cell-mediated immunity and suppressing multiple pro-inflammatory pathways. UMSC therapy modulates T lymphocyte function, inhibits interactions between T cells and B/myeloid cells, and suppresses DUOX2-mediated oxidative stress, while simultaneously reducing production of key inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, IL-12, and IL-17A. At the molecular level, UMSC therapy inhibits the JAK-STAT3 signaling pathway in Th17 cells, suppresses T follicular helper cell development and differentiation while restoring regulatory T cell function, and reduces CD8+ T cell exhaustion. Clinical data demonstrate that UMSC therapy achieves 80.8% clinical response and 46.2% remission rates at two months, with improvements in colonic mucosa, albumin levels, and hemoglobin, while reversing the pathological expansion of pro-inflammatory T cell subsets characteristic of inflamed ulcerative colitis mucosa. These findings suggest UMSC-based therapy represents a promising immunomodulatory approach for managing inflammatory bowel disease through comprehensive reconstruction of mucosal immune homeostasis.

title: Menstrual blood-derived mesenchymal stromal cell extracellular vesicles – a potential tool for tissue regeneration and disease detection
summary: Menstrual blood-derived mesenchymal stromal cells (MenSCs) and their extracellular vesicles (MenSC-EVs) represent a promising therapeutic platform with enhanced regenerative capabilities compared to other mesenchymal stem cell sources. MenSCs express characteristic pluripotency markers (Oct-4, SOX2, NANOG, SALL-4) and mesenchymal surface markers (CD44, CD73, CD90, CD105) while maintaining low immunogenicity, making them readily accessible for clinical applications without invasive procedures. MenSC-EVs mediate tissue regeneration and wound healing through secretion of abundant growth factors (VEGF, HGF, EGF, FGF, HIF-1α) and carry microRNAs including miR-21 and miR-4443 that regulate cellular survival and proliferation pathways. Notably, miR-4443 in MenSC-EVs suppresses ACSS2 to activate the PI3K/AKT pathway, promoting endometrial stem cell migration and proliferation with implications for endometriosis pathology. MenSC-EVs show particular promise for female reproductive tissue regeneration and endometriosis management, including assessment of endometriosis-related infertility, while also serving as disease biomarkers across multiple female reproductive disorders. The enhanced regenerative properties of MenSC-EVs combined with their immunomodulatory capacity position them as a valuable tool for both therapeutic intervention and diagnostic evaluation in reproductive endometriosis.

title: High-dimensional immune profiling of follicular fluid and systemic circulation reveals distinct immune signatures in women with polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by distinct inflammatory immune signatures in both systemic circulation and follicular fluid, driven by persistent low-grade inflammation that disrupts normal ovulatory processes. Women with PCOS display elevated systemic levels of multiple pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-4, IL-6, IL-9, IL-10, TNF-α, IL-18, and MCP-1, along with increased circulating leukocyte counts encompassing lymphocytes, neutrophils, and natural killer cells. Within ovarian follicles, PCOS patients show abnormal immune cell composition characterized by increased classical monocytes and Th17 cells but decreased CD4 T naïve cells, central memory cells, and activated regulatory T cells, alongside elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines and angiogenic factors (VEGF, EGF) that promote excessive ovarian blood flow. These altered immune populations and pro-inflammatory markers contribute to ovulatory dysfunction by disrupting the delicate immune regulation required for follicle development, oocyte maturation, and follicular rupture. The findings suggest that immune dysregulation represents a key pathogenic mechanism in PCOS, with potential therapeutic implications for targeting the inflammatory milieu underlying this reproductive disorder.

title: Postoperative Adhesions: Current Research on Mechanisms, Therapeutics and Preventative Measures
summary: Postoperative adhesions represent a significant clinical problem, causing chronic pain, small bowel obstruction, and infertility through complex inflammatory and coagulation mechanisms. The formation of adhesions initiates with fibrin deposition on injured tissue surfaces, which is regulated by a tightly balanced fibrinolytic system where tissue-plasminogen activator and urokinase plasminogen activator activate plasminogen to plasmin, which degrades fibrin, while plasminogen activator inhibitors suppress this process. Inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-12 promote both coagulation and adhesion formation by upregulating tissue factor expression, creating a bidirectional relationship where coagulation enhances inflammation and inflammation activates the coagulation cascade. Angiogenesis further drives adhesion development through VEGF and other factors that stimulate myofibroblast differentiation, excessive extracellular matrix deposition, and collagen accumulation over three distinct phases progressing from membranous adhesions to fibrotic, collagen-rich adhesions. The process is perpetuated by hypoxic environments that enhance VEGF production and reactive oxygen species that sustain chronic inflammation, while therapeutic strategies targeting the fibrinolytic system, inflammatory mediators, or angiogenic pathways offer potential preventative approaches to reduce postoperative adhesion formation.

title: METTL14-mediated m6A methylation promotes macrophage M2 polarization via YTHDF1-Socs1 axis to accelerate skin wound healing
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that METTL14-mediated m6A methylation promotes skin wound healing by enhancing Socs1 expression through the YTHDF1-Socs1 axis. METTL14 increases m6A methylation on Socs1 mRNA, enabling YTHDF1 recognition and translation of Socs1 protein, which in turn drives M2 macrophage polarization. Socs1 overexpression significantly accelerated mouse skin wound healing by enhancing granulation tissue formation, collagen deposition, and vascular formation, while shifting the macrophage population from pro-inflammatory M1 to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes. Socs1-overexpressed macrophages elevated production of wound-promoting cytokines (VEGF and TGF-β1) while suppressing pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-12 and TNF-α), thereby enhancing angiogenesis and fibroblast function. These findings establish an epitranscriptomic regulatory mechanism whereby METTL14-mediated m6A methylation controls Socs1 expression to orchestrate macrophage-mediated immune responses essential for efficient wound healing.

title: Narrative Review of Chronic Inflammation in Uterine Myoma: Lack of Specialized Pro-Resolving Lipid Mediators (SPMs) and Vitamin D as a Potential Reason for the Development of Uterine Fibroids
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids result from complex interactions between cytokines, steroid hormones, and growth factors, including local hypoxia, gonadal steroids, and vascular endothelial growth factors that drive cell proliferation and extracellular matrix deposition. A chronic inflammatory microenvironment characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1, TNF-α, and others) promotes fibroid growth, with these cytokines also increasing estrogen synthesis and TGF-β-mediated fibrotic changes through immune cells like monocytes and macrophages. Vitamin D deficiency significantly correlates with higher fibroid prevalence and severity, while sufficient vitamin D levels reduce myoma incidence by approximately 32% through multiple mechanisms including suppression of estrogen signaling, inhibition of fibrotic protein expression, and promotion of apoptosis. The active resolution of inflammation depends on specialized pro-resolving lipid mediators, which appear to be deficient in fibroid pathogenesis, suggesting that lack of these mediators combined with vitamin D insufficiency perpetuates chronic inflammation and fibroid development. Genetic variants in the vitamin D receptor (VDR TT genotype) and genes affecting vitamin D synthesis further influence individual susceptibility, offering potential therapeutic targets through vitamin D supplementation and strategies to promote inflammation resolution.

title: Precision Therapeutic and Preventive Molecular Strategies for Endometriosis-Associated Infertility
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent disorder that causes pelvic pain and infertility through a complex interplay of hormonal dysregulation, immune dysfunction, epigenetic alterations, and oxidative stress that collectively promote lesion persistence and disrupt critical reproductive functions. The disease involves aberrant estrogen signaling pathways, including upregulation of aromatase (CYP19A1) via PGE2-mediated SF-1 activation and alternative estrogen synthesis routes, coupled with progesterone resistance and altered estrogen receptor β signaling that promotes inflammation and inhibits apoptosis. Key inflammatory mediators including IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-17A drive chronic inflammation, angiogenesis, and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition while simultaneously impairing granulosa cell function and oocyte quality through cytokine-mediated downregulation of fertility-associated genes. Oxidative stress and epigenetic silencing of critical regulators further compromise both lesion clearance and reproductive competence, while dysregulated signaling through PI3K/AKT/mTOR and Wnt/β-catenin pathways enhance lesion survival and progression. Emerging non-hormonal therapeutic strategies including MAPK and PI3K/AKT inhibitors, alongside epigenetic modulators targeting aberrant methylation patterns, offer promising precision approaches to address the multifactorial molecular drivers of endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: Nanoparticles for Cancer Immunotherapy: Innovations and Challenges
summary:
# Summary

Nanoparticles represent a transformative platform for cancer immunotherapy by simultaneously enhancing drug delivery and modulating immune responses through mechanisms including the enhanced permeability and retention effect. The tumor microenvironment actively suppresses anti-tumor immunity through multiple pathways: hypoxia-inducible factor 1α promotes tumor cell growth, while M2 macrophages secrete immunosuppressive cytokines that inhibit the cytotoxic functions of M1 macrophages. Recent innovations overcome these barriers through engineered nanoparticles—such as siRNA-loaded particles that downregulate HIF-1α and CD73, MnO2 nanoparticles that reduce hypoxia, and IL-12-loaded polymers that reprogram M2 macrophages toward anti-tumor M1 phenotypes—resulting in significantly reduced tumor growth and enhanced antitumor immunity. Stimuli-responsive nanoparticle systems with pH- or redox-sensitive triggers enable targeted payload release in the acidic tumor microenvironment, while FDA-approved polymers like PLGA and PCL provide sustained drug release and improved cellular uptake. These multifunctional nanoparticle strategies address major challenges in cancer immunotherapy by overcoming chemotherapy resistance, enhancing immune cell activation, and reducing the protective effects of the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

title: Role of isoflavones in multiple sclerosis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the therapeutic potential of isoflavones, phytoestrogens primarily found in soy and legumes, for treating multiple sclerosis by targeting multiple pathological mechanisms. Isoflavones reduce oxidative stress and inflammation while enhancing myelin repair, thereby slowing MS progression. These bioactive compounds exert their effects through multiple pathways: they interact with estrogen receptors expressed on immunogenic cells (dendritic cells, B cells, T cells, macrophages, and natural killer cells), modulate steroid-metabolizing enzymes (aromatase and 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase), and engage glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid receptors. In MS pathology, chronic inflammation driven by autoreactive T cells and B cells, particularly Th17 cells producing pro-inflammatory cytokines, leads to demyelination and oligodendrocyte death, with oxidative stress from reactive oxygen species further perpetuating tissue damage through mitochondrial dysfunction and neuronal death. By suppressing these interconnected inflammatory and oxidative pathways, isoflavones offer a multi-targeted approach to mitigating the autoimmune neuroinflammation characteristic of MS.

title: Inflammatory, Functional, and Compositional Changes of the Uterine Immune Microenvironment in a Lymphangioleiomyomatosis Mouse Model
summary:
# Summary

TSC1/2 mutations drive uncontrolled proliferation through mTORC1 pathway activation, leading to the development of lymphangioleiomyomatosis-like disease in a mouse model where uterine LAMCore cells originate and metastasize to the lungs. The mutation triggers a profound inflammatory immune dysregulation characterized by marked reductions in uterine NK cells and their increased apoptosis, coupled with elevated systemic levels of multiple pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-12, TNFα, and IFNγ. Neutrophils and granulocytes become prominent inflammatory contributors, expressing high levels of IL-1a, IL-1b, and chemotactic ligands Cxcl2 and Cxcl3, while macrophages produce increased MCP-1/CCL-2. The loss of functional NK cell surveillance—evidenced by depleted uNK populations expressing reduced NKG2D activating receptor signaling—facilitates LAMCore cell immune evasion and metastasis, as demonstrated by increased pulmonary metastases following NK cell depletion. The uterine microenvironment exhibits enriched extracellular matrix organization pathways and altered chemokine/cytokine expression patterns, creating conditions reminiscent of endometriosis-associated immune dysfunction where uNK cell abnormalities contribute to pathology. These findings identify NK cell depletion and pro-inflammatory skewing as critical mechanisms enabling LAMCore cell metastatic progression, with potential therapeutic implications for mTORC1 inhibition.

title: Inhibition of VEGFR1 TK Signaling in Peritoneal Macrophages Suppresses Endometriosis Development
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that VEGFR1 tyrosine kinase signaling in peritoneal macrophages drives endometriosis development through promotion of neovascularization and lymphangiogenesis. Peritoneal macrophages, particularly those expressing VEGFR1, accumulate in endometriotic lesions where they secrete pro-angiogenic cytokines and contribute to lesion growth. Genetic deletion of VEGFR1 tyrosine kinase in mice (TK-/- mice) suppressed endometrial implant establishment and reduced both angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis compared to wild-type controls. This protective effect was mediated by a shift toward pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages and away from alternatively activated M2 macrophages, with TK-/- peritoneal macrophages exhibiting increased M1-related gene expression and reduced M2-phenotype markers. Transfer of TK-/- macrophages into wild-type mice replicated these beneficial effects, directly demonstrating that VEGFR1 inhibition in macrophages is sufficient to suppress endometriosis. These findings identify VEGFR1 tyrosine kinase signaling as a potential therapeutic target for endometriosis management.

title: Comprehensive review of macrophage models: primary cells and immortalized lines across species
summary:
# Summary

Macrophages are fundamental phagocytic cells that maintain tissue homeostasis by modulating immunological responses and secreting diverse cytokines, with their function critically dependent on polarization state and tissue origin. Tissue-resident macrophages originate from either embryonic precursors (such as microglia and Kupffer cells) or bone marrow-derived monocytes that differentiate from hematopoietic stem cells, enabling specialized functions in different anatomical compartments. Macrophage polarization toward pro-inflammatory M1 or anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes—induced by stimuli such as LPS/IFN-γ and IL-4/IL-13 respectively—controls the balance of immune and inflammatory responses, while dysregulation of this M1/M2 ratio is associated with pathological conditions including infections, atherosclerosis, obesity, asthma, and malignancies. Primary macrophage models such as bone marrow-derived macrophages (BMDMs), peritoneal macrophages, alveolar macrophages, and Kupffer cells express characteristic surface markers and demonstrate distinct metabolic and secretory profiles suited for different experimental approaches, though immortalized cell lines provide alternative tools for long-term studies. Macrophage dysfunction leads to serious developmental and tissue repair deficits, while specialized subsets including regulatory macrophages and tumor-associated macrophages modulate immune suppression and tumor microenvironment dynamics independent of classical M1/M2 classifications.

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cells: A Therapeutic Approach in Fertility Restoration in Premature Ovarian Insufficiency
summary: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) represent a promising therapeutic approach for treating premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), a condition causing infertility and multiple systemic complications including depression, anxiety, osteoporosis, and cardiovascular disease before age 40. MSCs improve ovarian function through multiple mechanisms including paracrine activity, immunomodulation, and tissue repair, whereby they home to damaged ovarian tissue, reduce local inflammation, promote follicle survival, and stimulate angiogenesis while protecting granulosa cells from apoptosis. These cells secrete bioactive molecules including growth factors (VEGF, HGF, IGF-1), cytokines (IL-6, IL-10), and extracellular vesicles that collectively reduce inflammation, inhibit apoptosis, and promote angiogenesis. MSC transplantation clinically improves ovarian reserve, enhances follicular development, increases estrogen and anti-Müllerian hormone production, and reduces FSH levels in preclinical POI models induced by chemotherapy, autoimmune mechanisms, or genetic factors. MSC-derived extracellular vesicles are emerging as cell-free alternatives with similar therapeutic efficacy, while newly identified very small embryonic-like stem cells in the ovaries may contribute to oocyte generation and demonstrate resistance to chemotherapy. These findings establish MSCs as a viable regenerative medicine strategy for restoring fertility and mitigating the broader clinical consequences of POI.

title: RUNX1 promotes denervation-induced muscle atrophy by activating the JUNB/NF-κB pathway and driving M1 macrophage polarization
summary: This study identifies RUNX1 as a critical transcription factor promoting denervation-induced muscle atrophy through a multi-level inflammatory pathway. RUNX1 is upregulated in denervated muscle, where it directly binds the JUNB promoter to activate JUNB transcription, which subsequently activates NF-κB signaling through p65 phosphorylation. Activation of this RUNX1-JUNB-NF-κB axis drives muscle wasting by inducing atrophy markers (MuRF1 and Atrogin-1) while suppressing anabolic pathways. Additionally, RUNX1 promotes M1 macrophage polarization, increasing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and TNF-α that further amplify muscle proteolysis through ubiquitin-proteasome systems. JUNB knockdown experiments confirmed its essential role, as suppressing JUNB reversed RUNX1-induced inflammatory cytokine release, NF-κB activation, and atrophy marker expression. These findings establish the RUNX1-JUNB-NF-κB axis as a central regulatory hub linking neuromuscular junction dysfunction and macrophage-mediated inflammation to skeletal muscle atrophy.

title: Dual Role of Natural Killer Cells in Early Pregnancy: Immunopathological Implications and Therapeutic Potential in Recurrent Spontaneous Abortion and Recurrent Implantation Failure
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the dual role of natural killer (NK) cells in early pregnancy, particularly decidual NK cells (dNK cells), which normally facilitate immune tolerance while supporting placental development through trophoblast invasion and spiral artery remodeling. Under physiological conditions, dNK cells maintain low cytotoxicity and instead secrete immunomodulatory cytokines—interferon-γ, vascular endothelial growth factor, and placental growth factor—that are essential for successful placental vascular remodeling and immune regulation via interactions with regulatory T cells. NK cell dysfunction manifests through two pathological mechanisms: overactivation leading to excessive cytotoxicity and trophoblast damage causes recurrent spontaneous abortion and implantation failure, while underactivation resulting in reduced cytokine secretion impairs vascular remodeling with similar clinical consequences. The authors detail how multiple regulatory pathways maintain dNK cell balance, including progesterone-induced apoptosis, glycodelin-mediated cytotoxicity suppression, inhibitory killer immunoglobulin-like receptors (KIRs) that suppress activation, and chemokines that control NK cell migration to decidual tissue. These findings suggest that restoring proper NK cell function through targeting specific signaling pathways or cytokine modulation represents a therapeutic opportunity for treating pregnancy complications associated with immune dysfunction.

title: Stem cell and CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing technology in Alzheimer’s disease therapy: from basic research to clinical innovation
summary: Alzheimer's disease is characterized by amyloid-beta (Aβ) plaque accumulation, tau protein tangles, and neuroinflammation driven by activated microglia and astrocytes releasing proinflammatory factors. Gene-edited induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and neural stem cells (NSCs) represent promising therapeutic approaches, reducing Aβ and tau accumulation while improving cognitive function through secretion of neurotrophic factors like BDNF and GDNF that promote neurogenesis and synaptic plasticity. CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing targeting pathogenic variants in APP, PSEN1, and PSEN2 genes offers disease-modifying potential, while mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) suppress neuroinflammation by inhibiting microglia overactivation via IDO and PGE2 pathways and promoting anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes. Complementary pharmacological strategies including secretase inhibitors, cholinesterase inhibitors, and anti-Aβ immunotherapies such as aducumab further support cognitive preservation. Human iPSC-derived models enable both disease mechanism elucidation and high-throughput drug discovery, establishing stem cell therapy combined with precision gene editing as a translational bridge from basic research to clinical innovation in Alzheimer's treatment.

title: The role of the microbiome in endometrial carcinoma: Pathogenesis, biomarkers, and therapeutic prospects
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how microbial dysbiosis contributes to endometrial carcinoma development through multiple interconnected pathways. Dysbiosis disrupts the microbiota's normal regulatory functions, modulating chronic inflammation, estrogen metabolism, and immune responses that collectively promote malignant transformation. Unique microbial signatures show promise as diagnostic biomarkers and targets for microbiome-directed interventions including probiotics and dietary modifications, which enhance treatment outcomes. The tumor-associated microbiome varies among endometrial cancer molecular subtypes, suggesting personalized therapeutic strategies may improve efficacy. Beyond microbiota, established risk factors including obesity, insulin resistance, and Lynch syndrome converge with dysbiosis to amplify endometrial cancer risk through dysregulation of key signaling pathways (PI3K/AKT, RTK/RAS/β-catenin) and epigenetic alterations in DNA methylation and histone modifications. These findings highlight the microbiome as a critical but modifiable factor in endometrial carcinogenesis, positioning microbiome-targeted therapies alongside conventional treatments as promising approaches to reduce disease incidence and improve patient outcomes.

title: Modulation of NK Cell Properties by ESKAPE Group Bacteria
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how ESKAPE group bacteria modulate natural killer (NK) cell function through bacterial metabolites and toxins, with implications for reproductive health and pregnancy complications. NK cells express toll-like receptors TLR2 and TLR5, which enable recognition of bacterial components and contribute to their cytotoxic functions in uterine tissue. Exposure to supernatants from various ESKAPE bacteria differentially affects NK cell properties: K. pneumoniae, Enterobacter species, and S. aureus suppress NK cell cytotoxicity and reduce IL-10 and NKG2A mRNA levels, while E. faecium and A. baumannii enhance cytotoxicity against placental cells. Dysbiotic colonization of reproductive tissues with multidrug-resistant ESKAPE bacteria, including E. faecalis and P. aeruginosa, is associated with early pregnancy loss and recurrent miscarriage, potentially through bacterial induction of NK cell apoptosis and immunosuppressive microenvironments. These findings suggest that pathogenic bacteria-mediated alterations in decidual NK cell function may represent a critical mechanism linking genital tract infections to adverse pregnancy outcomes in endometriosis and other reproductive disorders.

title: Effect of Estrogen on Sirt1 Signaling in Human Macrophages
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals sex-dependent effects of estrogen (E2) on macrophage inflammatory signaling through Sirt1 and related pathways. In female macrophages, E2 exerts anti-inflammatory effects by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α and IL-6 through NFκB inhibition, while simultaneously enhancing mitochondrial Sirt3 expression to alleviate the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype. Conversely, in male macrophages, E2 promotes pro-inflammatory activity by upregulating NFκB-p65 expression and increasing IL-1β production. The study demonstrates that E2 paradoxically reduces nuclear Sirt1 expression while increasing acetylation of Sirt1 target proteins (p53 and Ku70), suggesting complex post-translational mechanisms that differ between sexes and involve estrogen receptor α (ERα), ERβ, and GPER30. These sex-specific immunomodulatory effects of estrogen provide mechanistic insights into why premenopausal women exhibit lower serum TNF-α levels and higher antioxidant enzyme expression compared to men and postmenopausal women, with potential implications for understanding endometriosis-associated inflammatory pathways.

title: The Role of STAT-3 and IL-26 Signaling Pathways in Leiomyoma Pathophysiology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that STAT-3 and IL-26 signaling pathways are significantly elevated in uterine leiomyomas and play central roles in fibroid pathophysiology. STAT-3 is highly expressed in smooth muscle cells and fibroblasts within leiomyomas, where it promotes cell proliferation, suppresses apoptosis, drives extracellular matrix remodeling, and induces vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF)-mediated angiogenesis. IL-26, secreted by Th17 cells, binds to its cognate receptor complex and activates both STAT-1 and STAT-3 to amplify these pro-tumorigenic processes. The study identifies multiple upstream activators, including leptin and the proinflammatory cytokine IL-6, both of which phosphorylate and activate STAT-3 to increase fibroid cell proliferation and fibrotic responses. Notably, the microRNA miR-29b suppresses STAT-3 and downstream proliferation genes, suggesting a potential therapeutic target. Additionally, multiple cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8, IL-12p70, and IFN-γ show dysregulation in uterine fibroid patients, with reduced interferon-gamma in serum reflecting altered immune homeostasis. These findings suggest that pharmacological inhibition of STAT-3 and IL-26 signaling may offer a therapeutic strategy for treating uterine leiomyomas and their associated clinical symptoms including menstrual irregularities, pelvic pain, and infertility.

title: Probiotic and Vitamin D Ameliorate TNBS-Induced Colitis by Targeting Mucosal Barrier and Neutrophil Infiltration
summary: This study demonstrates that combined probiotic and vitamin D supplementation attenuates experimentally induced colitis by targeting two critical pathogenic mechanisms: mucosal barrier dysfunction and neutrophil-mediated inflammation. Both probiotics and vitamin D independently reduce colonic damage and suppress neutrophil recruitment and activation, with their therapeutic effects enhanced when administered together, associated with elevated serum levels of the active vitamin D metabolite 1,25(OH)₂D₃. Probiotics amplify vitamin D signaling by increasing vitamin D receptor expression and enhancing absorption, while vitamin D repolarizes pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages toward the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype to promote tissue repair. The mucosal barrier, comprising goblet cell-derived mucus and epithelial tight junctions, serves as a critical defense mechanism whose disruption perpetuates immune cell recruitment and inflammatory cascade activation in inflammatory bowel disease. These findings suggest that the synergistic interplay between probiotics and vitamin D—mediated through enhanced barrier integrity and reduced neutrophil-driven inflammation—offers a dual-targeting therapeutic approach relevant to understanding microbiota-immune interactions in gastrointestinal and systemic inflammatory conditions.

title: Transforming treatment paradigms: Focus on personalized medicine for high‐grade serous ovarian cancer
summary: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) represents a complex disease driven by multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on genomic instability and immune dysregulation. Nearly universal TP53 mutations in HGSOC impair the p53 tumor suppressor's ability to maintain genomic stability, leading to uncontrolled cellular proliferation and tumor growth, while BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations further elevate cancer risk by compromising homologous recombination repair pathways. The tumor microenvironment plays a critical role in HGSOC pathogenesis, with cancer-associated fibroblasts secreting pro-tumorigenic factors that promote angiogenesis, metastasis, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and immunosuppression through mechanisms involving regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, and tumor-associated macrophages that inhibit anti-tumor CD8-positive T cell responses. Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction contribute to tumorigenesis and platinum resistance, while specific mitochondrial DNA mutations like ND2 F40L activate anti-apoptotic signaling through nuclear factor-κB pathways. HGSOC likely originates from fallopian tube epithelium precursor lesions including secretory cell outgrowths and p53 signatures, with risk modulated by hormonal and reproductive factors such as parity, contraceptive use, and conditions like endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease.

title: Ghrelin’s potential as a therapeutic target for chronic inflammatory diseases: evidence from human endometrial stromal cells
summary: This study demonstrates that ghrelin, a 28-amino acid peptide primarily produced by the stomach and binding to the GHSR receptor widely expressed in reproductive tissues, exhibits potent anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties in human endometrial stromal cells relevant to endometriosis pathology. In cultured hEM15A endometrial cells, ghrelin treatment produced dose-dependent reductions in pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α (86% reduction) and IL-6 (64% reduction) at 1000 μM concentration, while simultaneously enhancing the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10. Beyond its well-established roles in appetite and energy regulation, ghrelin stimulated endometrial stromal cell proliferation and growth without compromising cell viability, suggesting protective effects against inflammatory damage. The anti-inflammatory mechanisms likely involve GHSR-mediated inhibition of critical inflammatory signaling pathways including NF-κB and MAPK, which regulate both innate and adaptive immune responses. These findings position ghrelin as a promising therapeutic target for endometriosis and other chronic inflammatory diseases, combining immune homeostasis restoration with tissue healing and regeneration potential.

title: Postoperative adhesion formation: the role of peritoneal macrophages and targeting therapy
summary: Peritoneal macrophages emerge as central orchestrators of postoperative adhesion formation through their multifaceted regulation of coagulation, inflammation, fibrosis, and fibrinolysis. Upon surgical injury, these macrophages recognize tissue damage and produce coagulation factors (FV, FVII, FX) that activate the coagulation cascade, while simultaneously secreting inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, IL-12) and chemokines (CCL5, CXCL9, CXCL10) that amplify the inflammatory response. Additionally, peritoneal macrophages secrete TGF-β and IL-10, which activate mesothelial cells and fibroblasts to promote tissue remodeling and fibrin deposition, while also producing TGF-β1 and VEGF-α that drive fibroblast proliferation and matrix deposition. The activated coagulation cascade produces thrombin, which cleaves fibrinogen to form fibrin clots, while the fibrinolytic system—activated by tissue plasminogen activator and urokinase—generates plasmin to degrade excess fibrin and extracellular matrix. Postoperative adhesions result from the dysregulation of these four interconnected processes and lead to serious complications including small bowel obstruction, chronic abdominal pain, and secondary female infertility. Targeting peritoneal macrophage function and the downstream inflammatory, coagulation, and fibrotic pathways represents a promising therapeutic strategy to prevent adhesion formation following abdominal or pelvic surgery.

title: GPR15 differentially regulates the effects of cigarette smoke exposure on Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis
summary: Cigarette smoke exposure has paradoxical and opposite effects on the two major inflammatory bowel diseases, with GPR15 serving as a critical molecular switch determining disease outcomes. Smoke upregulates GPR15 expression through epigenetic mechanisms, which then directs opposing T cell responses: in TNBS-induced colitis models, GPR15 promotes pathogenic Th17 cell differentiation and exacerbates Crohn's disease-like inflammation, while in DSS-induced colitis, GPR15 promotes protective regulatory T cell (Treg) homing and reduces ulcerative colitis-like disease severity. GPR15 deletion ameliorates smoke-induced Crohn's disease by suppressing Th17-mediated inflammatory pathways and reducing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), but conversely worsens DSS-induced colitis by impairing Treg cell function and eliminating the protective TGF-β signaling response. These findings reveal GPR15 as a mechanistic hub linking environmental smoke exposure to differential T cell trafficking and immune regulation, with critical implications for understanding why smoking presents opposing epidemiological associations with the two IBD subtypes and suggesting GPR15 as a potential therapeutic target for disease subtype-specific intervention.

title: Inflammatory mechanisms and therapeutic advances in chronic endometritis
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis is a persistent inflammatory disorder of the endometrium caused by microbial infections that activate toll-like receptor and NOD-like receptor signaling pathways, leading to dysregulated immune responses characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, T-helper 17 cells, and M1 macrophages. The NLRP3 inflammasome plays a central role in pathogenesis, with endoplasmic reticulum stress, oxidative stress, and damage-associated molecular patterns such as HMGB1 triggering NLRP3 activation and subsequent IL-1β production that impairs endometrial receptivity and function. This chronic inflammation is strongly associated with infertility, recurrent implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and various reproductive pathologies including endometriosis. Antibiotic therapy with agents such as doxycycline and metronidazole demonstrates efficacy in treating chronic endometritis and significantly improves clinical pregnancy and live birth rates. Emerging therapeutic approaches including probiotics to restore microbial balance and pharmacological modulators such as Epimedium glycosides that inhibit TLR4/NF-κB signaling while activating Nrf2 show promise in alleviating LPS-induced endometrial inflammation.

title: Dysfunction of natural killer cells promotes immune escape and disease progression in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Natural killer cell dysfunction plays a critical role in endometriosis by enabling immune escape and disease progression. In endometriosis patients, NK cells display impaired cytotoxic capacity due to aberrant receptor-ligand interactions and altered phenotypic distribution, with reduced NKG2D expression limiting recognition of ectopic endometrial lesions. Multiple immunosuppressive mechanisms converge to inhibit NK cell function, including elevated TGF-β and IL-6 that suppress cytotoxic granule expression, IL-10-mediated upregulation of inhibitory receptors (NKG2A and PD-1), and soluble ICAM-1 secretion by ectopic cells that blocks NK-cell synapse formation. Ectopic endometrial tissue upregulates HLA-G and the NKG2A ligand HLA-E, establishing inhibitory checkpoint pathways analogous to cancer immune evasion. Emerging therapeutic strategies to restore NK cell immunity include checkpoint blockade (anti-NKG2A, anti-PD-1), IL-2-mediated NK cell activation to generate potent lymphokine-activated killer cells, and adoptive NK cell transfer. These findings underscore NK cell exhaustion as a central driver of the persistent 50% infertility and 70% chronic pelvic pain burden in endometriosis patients.

title: Changes in the M1/M2 ratio due to andrographolide therapy for deep endometriosis: An experimental study using a BALB/C endometriosis model
summary:
# Summary

This experimental study investigated how andrographolide, an active compound from Andrographis paniculata, treats deep endometriosis by modulating macrophage polarization in a BALB/C mouse model. Endometriosis pathogenesis involves a dysregulated innate immune response where M2 macrophages promote disease progression, ectopic lesion development, and vascularization, while M1 macrophages suppress these pathological processes. Andrographolide functions as an anti-inflammatory and immunostimulatory agent that shifts the M1/M2 macrophage ratio toward the protective M1 phenotype, effectively reducing M2 CD163 macrophages in peritoneal fluid and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and VEGF. The compound achieves these effects through multiple mechanisms: enhancing phosphoinositide 3-kinase and endothelial nitric oxide synthase expression, inhibiting the Smad2/Smad3 pathway that normally drives M2 polarization, and leveraging its flavonoid content to block inflammatory processes. Notably, andrographolide treatment produced M1/M2 ratio shifts comparable to dienogest and visanne, the standard pharmacological therapies for endometriosis, suggesting it represents a promising phytotherapeutic alternative for managing this chronic inflammatory condition.

title: Bidirectional Interaction Between PGE2-Preconditioned Mesenchymal Stem Cells and Myofibroblasts Mediates Anti-Fibrotic Effects: A Proteomic Investigation into Equine Endometrial Fibrosis Reversal
summary: Endometrosis, a prevalent fibrotic disease in mares, causes pathological remodeling of endometrial structure and function through transdifferentiation of stromal cells into myofibroblasts, which express α-smooth muscle actin and accumulate excessive collagen types I and III. Inflammatory cytokines including TGF-β1, TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β drive myofibroblast differentiation and fibrotic progression. Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2)-preconditioned equine endometrial mesenchymal stem cells (ET-eMSCs) reverse this process by reducing myofibroblast expression of α-SMA, collagen genes (COL1A1, COL3A1), and matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9), thereby promoting extracellular matrix remodeling and tissue reorganization. The therapeutic effects are mediated through the ET-eMSC secretome, which contains growth factors, microRNAs, and extracellular vesicles that inhibit fibrosis and apoptosis while enhancing angiogenesis and immune modulation. This bidirectional interaction between PGE2-primed ET-eMSCs and myofibroblasts establishes mesenchymal stem cell-based interventions as a promising regenerative strategy for reversing endometrial fibrosis and restoring reproductive function.

title: Microencapsulated 3D culture of human umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells enhances their therapeutic effect on intrauterine adhesion
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that microencapsulated three-dimensional culture of human umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells (3D-hUCMSCs) significantly enhances their therapeutic efficacy for treating intrauterine adhesion (IUA), a fibrotic condition characterized by endometrial damage and inflammation. The 3D culture approach substantially improves cell proliferation, stemness gene expression (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4, c-MYC), and cell viability compared to conventional 2D culture. Mechanistically, 3D-hUCMSCs suppress the pro-fibrotic TGF-β1/Smad3 signaling pathway, which normally drives collagen and α-SMA deposition, thereby reducing fibrosis and endometrial inflammation. In a murine IUA model, 3D-hUCMSCs effectively restored endometrial thickness, increased gland numbers, reduced inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-1), and decreased fibrotic markers, ultimately improving fertility outcomes. These findings indicate that microencapsulation in sodium alginate and collagen provides an optimal microenvironment that preserves stem cell properties while amplifying the anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic therapeutic effects necessary for IUA treatment.

title: Tumor Immune Microenvironment and Checkpoint Inhibition in Clear Cell Ovarian Carcinoma: Bridging Tumor Biology and Clinical Application in Immunotherapy
summary:
# Summary

Clear cell ovarian carcinoma exhibits a paradoxical immune microenvironment characterized by elevated PD-L1 expression and immune cell infiltration despite aggressive disease and poor clinical outcomes. Oncogenic KRAS and MYC mutations drive immune evasion through coordinated upregulation of checkpoint ligands (PD-L1, CD47, CD276) and pro-tumoral cytokines (TGF-β, IL-6, IL-10), while simultaneously downregulating antigen presentation via MHC-I loss and interferon signaling. The concurrent activation of PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling further promotes immunosuppression by expanding myeloid-derived suppressor cells and regulatory T cells while impairing dendritic cell maturation. Notably, elevated CD8+ cytotoxic T cell infiltration, typically associated with favorable prognosis, paradoxically correlates with worse disease-free survival in this context, suggesting that checkpoint inhibitor-based immunotherapy may benefit patients with high PD-L1 and microsatellite instability. These findings establish the molecular basis for checkpoint inhibition as a targeted therapeutic strategy to overcome immune evasion in clear cell ovarian carcinoma.

title: The Interleukin-8-CXCR1/2 Axis as a Therapeutic Target in Peritoneal Carcinomatosis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the interleukin-8 (IL-8)–CXCR1/CXCR2 axis as a central driver of peritoneal carcinomatosis pathology. IL-8, produced by diverse cell types including immune cells, fibroblasts, and tumor cells, is elevated in the peritoneal microenvironment where it binds CXCR1 and CXCR2 receptors to promote tumor growth, angiogenesis, chronic inflammation, epithelial–mesenchymal transition, and immune evasion. IL-8 signaling recruits and activates neutrophils, which then release proangiogenic factors and contribute to a tumor-permissive microenvironment, while also activating fibroblasts and polarizing macrophages toward protumoral M2-like phenotypes. Blocking IL-8 or its receptors using monoclonal antibodies (BMS-986253) or small-molecule inhibitors (SX-682, AZD5069, navarixin) reduces tumor growth and enhances immune checkpoint inhibitor efficacy, making this axis a promising therapeutic target. The relevance of IL-8 extends to other inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, suggesting broader therapeutic applications beyond cancer.

title: Genetic Variants in Cytokines IL-10 and IL-17A Are Associated with the Risk and Severity of Sporadic Parkinson’s Disease in Mexican Patients
summary:
# Summary

This study identified genetic variants in the interleukin-10 (IL-10) and interleukin-17A (IL-17A) genes as significant risk factors for Parkinson's disease in Mexican patients. The IL-17A_rs8193036 variant substantially increased PD risk (OR = 1.91), while the IL-10_rs1800896 minor allele provided protective effects (OR = 0.28), suggesting that genetic differences in anti-inflammatory and pro-inflammatory cytokine production influence disease susceptibility. The findings support the inflammatory hypothesis of PD pathogenesis, showing that dysregulated immune responses characterized by elevated Th17 cells and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-6, IL-1β, IFN-γ, IL-17A) combined with reduced regulatory T cells and IL-10 production contribute to dopaminergic neurodegeneration. IL-17A drives this pathology through microglial activation and blood-brain barrier disruption, while IL-10 provides neuroprotection by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokines and suppressing IL-17A production. These findings establish cytokine genetic variants as potential biomarkers for PD risk stratification in this population.

title: Nerves, nodes, and neoplasia
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-draining lymph nodes represent critical hubs where anti-tumor immunity and immune evasion are balanced through complex neural regulation. Sensory and sympathetic nerve fibers extensively innervate lymph nodes, where they modulate immune responses through neurotransmitters and neuropeptides including norepinephrine, substance P, and calcitonin gene-related peptide. Sympathetic innervation suppresses anti-tumor immune responses via β-adrenergic signaling that induces lymphocyte retention and reduces inflammation, while sensory denervation paradoxically enhances tertiary lymphoid structure formation and reduces tumor growth. Tumors actively remodel their draining lymph nodes before metastasis by altering fibroblastic reticular cell function and lymph node vasculature to create immunosuppressive niches. Within these remodeled microenvironments, conventional dendritic cells, CD4+ helper T cells, and B cells support CD8+ T cell priming and memory formation, while multiple checkpoint molecules and reduced IL-7 production by fibroblastic reticular cells promote immune tolerance. These findings suggest that targeting neural regulation of lymph node immunity may enhance anti-tumor responses and overcome immune evasion mechanisms.

title: Vegetable intake is associated with lower psychological stress via increased anti-inflammatory responses in pregnant women with chronic diseases
summary:
# Summary

Pregnant women with chronic diseases experience elevated psychological stress and heightened inflammation characterized by increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-1β) produced by macrophages and mast cells. This study demonstrates that vegetable consumption mitigates stress levels specifically in women with chronic diseases by upregulating the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10, which suppresses pro-inflammatory responses and maintains immune balance by inhibiting Th1 cells, natural killer cells, and macrophages. Vegetables exert these protective effects through their bioactive compounds, particularly polyphenols and fiber, which reduce inflammation and modulate gut physiology. Notably, higher vegetable intake was significantly associated with elevated IL-10 levels and lower psychological stress only in pregnant women with chronic disease history, indicating a disease-specific protective interaction. Since psychological stress during pregnancy is linked to adverse outcomes including preeclampsia and low birth weight, the anti-inflammatory pathway activated by vegetable consumption represents a dietary intervention strategy for improving pregnancy outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: M1-like macrophages regulate T cell infiltration in colorectal cancer through P2X4 receptor
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates how the P2X4 receptor on tumor-associated macrophages drives M1-like polarization and enhances anti-tumor immunity in colorectal cancer. P2X4 receptor activation mediates calcium influx, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction and mitochondrial DNA release, which subsequently activates the cGAS-STING-IFNB1 pathway. This pathway activation promotes STAT1/STAT2 phosphorylation and drives robust expression of M1-associated inflammatory genes including CD80, CXCL10, TNF, and IL-6. P2X4-expressing tumor-associated macrophages enhance CD8+ T cell survival and cytotoxicity, and clinically, reduced P2X4 expression in colorectal cancer tissues correlates with poorer patient prognosis. Notably, P2X4 antagonists can suppress macrophage infiltration and inflammatory signaling in experimental disease models, suggesting therapeutic potential for modulating this purinergic signaling pathway to enhance anti-tumor immune responses.

title: Immune Cells Mediate the Causal Pathway Linking Multisite Chronic Pain to Asthma: A Mediation Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study reveals that multisite chronic pain causally increases asthma risk through immune-mediated mechanisms. The research identified robust genetic correlations between multisite chronic pain and asthma (correlation coefficient 0.442), with genetically determined chronic pain conferring over twofold increased asthma risk. Specific immune cell populations—including activated CD4 regulatory T cells, activated and secreting CD4 regulatory T cells, and CD39+ CD4+ T cells—partially mediate this causal relationship, alongside dysregulated Th2 and ILC2 cell activation and impaired regulatory T cell function. Pro-inflammatory cytokines interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha upregulate both conditions by sensitizing peripheral nociceptors and amplifying airway hyper-responsiveness. The association is specific to multisite pain locations (back, knee, neck/shoulder, and abdominal pain) but not generalized pain types, suggesting that systemic immune dysregulation and sustained inflammation link chronic pain pathophysiology to asthma susceptibility through shared inflammatory pathways.

title: Innate Lymphoid Cells in HIV pathogenesis and in the Human Female Genital Tract
journal: Current opinion in HIV and AIDS
summary:
# Summary

Innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) are tissue-resident immune cells that play a critical role in maintaining mucosal barrier integrity and providing antiviral protection in the female genital tract, a primary site of HIV acquisition. ILCs are classified into three major subsets—ILC1, ILC2, and ILC3—that produce distinct cytokine profiles (IFN-γ/TNF, IL-4/IL-5/IL-13, and IL-17/IL-22, respectively) and contribute to different aspects of immune defense, including direct antiviral responses and epithelial barrier reinforcement. The integrity of the genital mucosal barrier, maintained by ILCs, epithelial cells, mucus, and neutrophil-derived antimicrobial factors, is essential for preventing HIV infection, as epithelial barrier dysfunction, persistent inflammation, and dysbiosis significantly increase HIV susceptibility. However, chronic HIV infection alters ILC populations and function, and human ILCs differ substantially from mouse counterparts in surface markers and cytokine responsiveness, highlighting the importance of studying human-specific mechanisms in the context of HIV pathogenesis. Sex hormones further regulate immune cell distribution and function in the female genital tract, suggesting that hormonal and immunological factors together influence HIV acquisition risk through effects on barrier protection and innate immune capacity.

title: Steroid hormone regulation of immunometabolism and inflammation
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how steroid hormones regulate immune cell function through coordinated control of metabolic pathways and inflammatory responses. Steroid hormones—including estrogens, progestogens, androgens, and glucocorticoids—modulate immune activation by regulating intracellular metabolic reprogramming and gene transcription through both genomic and non-genomic signaling pathways. Different immune cell subsets employ distinct metabolic strategies: activated T cells and pro-inflammatory macrophages favor glycolysis and glutamine metabolism to support proliferation and cytokine production, while memory T cells and anti-inflammatory macrophages rely on fatty acid oxidation and oxidative phosphorylation for sustained function and tissue repair. Key transcriptional regulators like HIF-1α and mTOR drive metabolic switching in response to pattern recognition receptor and cytokine receptor signaling, with changes in substrate transporter expression (GLUT1, glutamine transporters, CPT1A) directly controlling energy substrate availability. Glucocorticoids suppress pro-inflammatory responses by promoting immune cell apoptosis and inhibiting production of pro-inflammatory mediators like IL-1β and IL-6, while anti-inflammatory signals via IL-10 enhance oxidative phosphorylation and suppress glycolysis. These findings reveal an intimate connection between steroid hormone signaling, metabolic reprogramming, and immune cell differentiation that has implications for understanding sex-based differences in inflammatory diseases like endometriosis.

title: Biomimetic optimization of silicone breast implant integration: insights into wound healing and the foreign body response
summary:
# Summary

Silicone breast implants trigger a foreign body response that shares mechanisms with normal wound healing but ultimately leads to unwanted complications including fibrous encapsulation and capsular contracture.
