The response begins when implant surface properties govern protein adsorption, which activates immune cells—particularly macrophages that polarize into pro-inflammatory M1 and pro-fibrotic M2 phenotypes, both contributing to excessive fibrosis. When macrophages fail to engulf the implant through frustrated phagocytosis, they form foreign body giant cells that perpetuate chronic inflammation and pathological remodeling. The response progresses through five phases: protein adsorption, acute inflammation, chronic inflammation, foreign body giant cell formation, and fibrous encapsulation. Biomimetic implant modifications, such as micro-texturing, can mitigate the foreign body response and reduce capsular contracture by altering surface properties that influence macrophage polarization and downstream fibrotic outcomes. Understanding these integrated wound healing and immune mechanisms provides insights for optimizing implant design to minimize complications.

title: GM-CSF+ Th: a central player in autoimmunity
summary: GM-CSF-producing T helper cells emerge as central orchestrators of autoimmune pathology across multiple diseases including multiple sclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, and type 1 diabetes. These cells act as critical amplifiers of inflammation by activating and recruiting myeloid cells, which mediate subsequent tissue destruction, while simultaneously establishing vicious feedback loops that enhance their own differentiation and promote other effector T cells. GM-CSF+ Th cells are induced through multiple signaling pathways including IL-12/STAT4, IL-23/RORγt, and IL-7/IL-2/STAT5 axes, with transcriptional regulators like BHLHE40, STAT3, STAT4, and STAT5 controlling their development and DNA methylation regulating GM-CSF promoter activity. Disease-specific phenotypic variations occur, with GM-CSF+ Th cells exhibiting Th1-like characteristics in rheumatoid arthritis, co-expressing both Th1 and Th17 markers in multiple sclerosis, and distinct profiles in inflammatory bowel disease. Negative regulators including TGF-β, IFN-γ, and IL-10 constrain GM-CSF production to prevent excessive inflammation, while genetic deletion of the CSF2 gene in CD4+ T cells renders mice resistant to experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis, highlighting the therapeutic potential of targeting this pathway.

title: Gaps in maternal-fetal interface rejection response: chronic histiocytic intervillositis
summary: Chronic Histiocytic Intervillositis (CHI) is a severe placental inflammatory condition characterized by breakdown of maternal-fetal immune tolerance, leading to significant adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage and fetal growth restriction. The pathology involves abnormal activation of multiple immune cell populations, including elevated CD68+ monocyte-macrophages, CD4+ and CD8+ T lymphocytes, alongside reduced regulatory T cells, combined with dysregulated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) and impaired anti-inflammatory responses (IL-10, TGF-β). CHI can be triggered by autoimmune diseases, viral infections including SARS-CoV-2 and cytomegalovirus, and breakdown of key tolerance mechanisms including the CD200/CD200R axis and CD39-mediated adenosine production that normally suppress maternal immune responses. The protective maternal-fetal interface mechanisms involve non-classical HLA molecules (HLA-G, HLA-C), tolerogenic immune cells producing immunosuppressive factors, and checkpoint pathways (PD-L1/CTLA-4), which become dysfunctional in CHI. In CHI placentas, reduced CD200R and CD39 expression, combined with elevated inflammatory adhesion molecules (ICAM-1) and toll-like receptors (TLR1), further perpetuate the pathogenic inflammatory state and compromise the syncytiotrophoblast's immunoregulatory capacity.

title: Decoding immune low-response states in ovarian cancer: insights from single-cell and spatial transcriptomics for precision immunotherapy
summary:
# Summary

This study decodes immune suppression mechanisms in ovarian cancer, particularly high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), using single-cell and spatial transcriptomics to identify barriers to immunotherapy. TP53 mutations, present in nearly all HGSOC cases, drive chromosomal instability and tumor growth by disrupting p53-mediated cell cycle arrest and apoptosis, while BRCA1/2 mutations causing homologous recombination repair defects increase neoantigenic load but paradoxically promote immune evasion through upregulation of the PD-1-PD-L1 checkpoint pathway and CD8+ T-cell exhaustion. The tumor microenvironment exhibits multiple suppressive mechanisms, including abundant FOXP3+ regulatory T cells and tissue-resident NK cells with elevated NKG2A that suppress CD8+ T-cell function, alongside enriched M2-type macrophages secreting immunosuppressive cytokines (TGF-β, IL-10, VEGF) that associate with advanced disease and poor prognosis. T cells in the tumor exhibit a depleted phenotype with persistent co-inhibitory receptor expression (PD-1, LAG-3, TIM-3) and reduced effector cytokines, driven by transcription factors TOX, NR4A, and IRF2, with additional suppression via follicular regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells that induce T-cell senescence. These findings highlight critical immune suppression layers that likely explain the variable response to checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy and suggest multiple therapeutic targets for enhancing antitumor immunity in ovarian cancer.

title: AMY109, an interleukin-8 antibody, as a potential target for endometriosis treatment: a commentary
summary: Interleukin-8 (IL-8) is a key inflammatory mediator in endometriosis, produced by multiple cell types including neutrophils, macrophages, lymphocytes, endothelial cells, and epithelial cells, with elevated levels found in peritoneal fluid and endometriotic lesions of affected women. IL-8 exerts pro-inflammatory and pro-angiogenic effects by binding to CXCR1 and CXCR2 receptors on immune and endothelial cells, promoting endothelial cell migration and survival while facilitating endometrial stromal cell adhesion to fibronectin, processes critical to lesion development. Sex steroids, particularly estradiol and progesterone, stimulate IL-8 production from endometrial endothelial cells, linking hormonal dysregulation to inflammatory pathways in endometriosis. AMY109, a long-acting anti-IL-8 monoclonal antibody, represents a novel nonhormonal therapeutic approach that neutralizes IL-8 signaling and has demonstrated efficacy in reducing both endometrial cell adhesion and pain symptoms in primate models of surgically induced endometriosis. This antibody-based strategy offers a convenient monthly injection regimen and addresses IL-8's established role in disease progression and symptom severity over the past three decades of research. The development of AMY109 highlights the therapeutic potential of targeting IL-8-mediated inflammatory and angiogenic processes as an alternative to conventional hormone-based treatments for endometriosis.

title: Fallopian tubes influences sperm selection and fertilization success
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that the fallopian tube creates a selective microenvironment for sperm through dynamic proteomic changes, with healthy sperm inducing beneficial protein expression and signaling pathways that support capacitation, hyperactivation, and fertilization success. Exposure to sperm triggered significant proteomic alterations, including upregulation of 90 proteins involved in inflammation, angiogenesis, coagulation, and metabolic processes, while fallopian tube epithelial cells actively promote sperm survival, motility, and selection through secreted factors. Critically, sperm quality determines the inflammatory response: healthy sperm reduce pro-inflammatory pathway activation, whereas sperm with high DNA fragmentation activate inflammatory pathways through toll-like receptors (TLR-1, TLR-2, TLR-6), potentially impairing fertilization. Key upregulated proteins including haptoglobin (HP), tissue plasminogen activator (TPA), and fibrinogen (FGB) modulate immune responses—notably, HP suppresses lymphocyte activity and regulates T helper cells—suggesting protective mechanisms against excessive inflammation. These findings indicate that sperm quality directly influences the inflammatory state of the fallopian tube, with balanced inflammatory responses essential for normal pregnancy outcomes, while dysregulated inflammation from poor-quality sperm may contribute to reduced fertilization success.

title: Inflammatory Cytokine Signatures Are Associated With Disease Burden and Comorbidity of Episodic Migraine and Endometriosis
summary: This study identifies a synergistic inflammatory signature linking episodic migraine and endometriosis through elevated proinflammatory cytokines. Women with episodic migraine alone exhibit increased IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α compared to healthy controls, with these cytokines directly correlating with headache frequency, migraine severity, and disability scores. Notably, patients with both conditions show even higher cytokine levels, demonstrating a synergistic amplification of systemic inflammation that is not observed in endometriosis alone. IL-6 emerges as particularly clinically relevant in comorbid disease, correlating with multiple markers of disease burden including dysmenorrhea severity and immune dysregulation. These cytokines drive pain through both peripheral and central sensitization mechanisms, with TNF-α additionally promoting endometrial lesion invasiveness while modulating nociceptive signaling. The findings suggest that shared inflammatory pathways, potentially modulated by estrogen fluctuations, underlie the epidemiologic comorbidity between these two chronic pain conditions.

title: Distinct Suppression of Prednisone on Endometrial Immune Cells in Women With Reproductive Failure
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that prednisone selectively suppresses specific endometrial immune populations implicated in reproductive failure. Prednisone significantly reduced CD3−CD56+CD16− regulatory natural killer cells (from 6.12% to 4.00%) and CD68+CD16− macrophages (from 0.62% to 0.27%), while leaving other immune cell populations including CD3+ T cells, NK-T cells, and cytotoxic NK cells unchanged. The corticosteroid also decreased the functional interaction and clustering between these regulatory NK cells and macrophages, which normally facilitate critical reproductive processes including angiogenesis, decidualization, and trophoblast invasion. However, the suppression of CD68+CD16− macrophages—which exhibit anti-inflammatory M2-like properties essential for endometrial preparation—may paradoxically impair implantation by disrupting the immunotolerant microenvironment. These findings suggest that prednisone's therapeutic benefit in reproductive failure may be limited to women with specifically elevated regulatory NK cell populations, rather than offering universal benefit to all patients.

title: The role of extracellular vesicles in the communication between endometrial cancer cells and tumour-associated macrophages: a review
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles, particularly exosomes, serve as critical mediators of communication between endometrial cancer cells and the tumor microenvironment, driving cancer progression through multiple mechanisms. Cancer cell-derived extracellular vesicles promote tumor growth and metastasis by facilitating proliferation, migration, and invasion while simultaneously polarizing tumor-associated macrophages toward an immunosuppressive M2-like phenotype through exosomal miRNA-21, which suppresses anti-tumor immunity. Specific exosomal cargo including LGALS3BP, NEAT1, and miR-27a-5p enhance endometrial cancer cell proliferation and invasion through pathways involving integrin signaling, STAT3 activation, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Conversely, certain exosomal microRNAs such as miR-148b, miR-320a, and miR-503-3p exhibit tumor-suppressive functions by inhibiting proliferation and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Circulating exosomal biomarkers including LGALS3BP, SERPINA5, and small heat shock protein are elevated in endometrial cancer patients and correlate with increased recurrence risk and reduced survival, positioning exosomes as valuable diagnostic and prognostic indicators for disease management.

title: Mesenchymal stem cells therapy for immune thrombocytopenia in pregnancy: a case report
summary:
# Summary

Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) in pregnancy presents a clinical challenge due to immune-mediated platelet destruction and the risk of maternal hemorrhage and fetal thrombocytopenia. This case report demonstrates that umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells (UC-MSCs) effectively treat ITP by restoring platelet counts through multiple immunomodulatory mechanisms. UC-MSCs promote megakaryocyte maturation and thrombopoietin production while suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-6) and inhibiting antiplatelet antibody secretion and T helper cell proliferation. The therapeutic benefit is further enhanced during pregnancy through HLA-G expression, which suppresses natural killer and T lymphocyte activation, and by synergy with progesterone's immunosuppressive effects. Unlike conventional treatments such as corticosteroids—which carry risks of maternal hyperglycemia, hypertension, and fetal complications—MSC therapy offers a safer, immunomodulatory alternative for managing ITP in pregnant patients.

title: The immunological paradox of tubal ectopic pregnancy: unraveling immune tolerance amidst inflammation via single-cell sequencing
summary:
# Summary

Using single-cell sequencing, this study reveals that tubal ectopic pregnancy involves a distinct immunological paradox characterized by simultaneous pro-inflammatory and tolerogenic immune responses at implantation sites. CD4+ memory T cells are enriched at ectopic implantation sites where they exhibit dual pro-inflammatory and immune-tolerant functions, while CD8+ effector and memory T cells are reduced and functionally impaired, contributing to defective immune surveillance. The macrophage landscape shifts dramatically toward an M2-dominant phenotype with increased monocytes, which collectively promote chronic inflammation, angiogenesis, and tissue remodeling—creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment that facilitates abnormal embryo implantation in the fallopian tube. Gene expression analysis confirms that differentially expressed genes in CD4+ and CD8+ T cell populations are associated with altered immune regulation, abnormal hormone signaling, and impaired antigen presentation. Clinical markers including reduced neutrophil-to-lymphocyte and systemic immune-inflammation indices correlate with treatment success, suggesting that immunological imbalance is central to ectopic pregnancy pathogenesis. These findings establish immune cell composition and function as critical determinants of tubal ectopic pregnancy and identify potential therapeutic targets for intervention.

title: Th17 cell pathogenicity in autoimmune disease
summary:
# Summary: Th17 Cell Pathogenicity in Autoimmune Disease

T helper 17 (Th17) cells are CD4+ T cells defined by expression of the transcription factor RORγt that play dual roles in immunity: while they provide critical protection against bacterial and fungal infections at mucosal barriers through production of IL-17A, IL-17F, and IL-22, dysregulation of these cells drives pathogenic autoimmunity in multiple sclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and psoriasis. The pathogenic functions of Th17 cells are mediated through IL-17A and GM-CSF, which stimulate stromal cells and macrophages to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF), breach the blood-brain barrier through reactive oxygen species and matrix metalloproteinase production, and promote demyelination by inhibiting oligodendrocyte maturation. Notably, Th17 cells display remarkable plasticity, converting into ex-Th17 cells that produce even higher levels of IFNγ and GM-CSF, substantially increasing inflammatory potential. Therapeutic targeting of IL-17A and IL-23 has demonstrated clinical benefits in autoimmune diseases; however, IL-17A blockade paradoxically worsens inflammatory bowel disease, highlighting the context-dependent nature of Th17 cell function and the importance of understanding tissue-specific immunity when developing targeted therapeutics.

title: Decidualised endometrial stromal cell‐derived extracellular vesicles induce bystander decidualisation and cAMP‐mediated attenuation of natural killer cell cytotoxicity
summary:
# Summary

Decidualisation—the transformation of endometrial stromal cells into secretory decidual cells—is essential for blastocyst implantation and immune tolerance at the maternal-fetal interface, yet defective decidualisation contributes to implantation failure and miscarriage. This study reveals that decidualised stromal cells undergo significant metabolic reprogramming, including reduced glucose consumption and increased methionine production, while simultaneously triggering an immunomodulatory shift that enables regulation of uterine natural killer (NK) cells. Critically, extracellular vesicles (EVs) derived from decidualised endometrial stromal cells propagate decidualisation in bystander cells and attenuate NK cell cytotoxicity through cAMP-mediated mechanisms, including upregulation of the immunosuppressive molecule HLA-G and promotion of the CD56bright NK cell subset. These findings identify decidualised stromal cell-derived EVs as key mediators of endometrial immune tolerance and suggest that analysing endometrial EVs could serve as a non-invasive biomarker to assess decidualisation capacity and diagnose endometrial dysfunction in conditions like endometriosis and recurrent implantation failure.

title: Ovulation releases G-CSF to induce peritoneal neutrophil influx and netosis, facilitating peritoneal seeding of high-grade serous carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

Physiological ovulation triggers a pro-metastatic inflammatory cascade in the peritoneal cavity by inducing neutrophil influx and neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation, a process dependent on G-CSF derived from follicular fluid. G-CSF stimulates neutrophil recruitment and activates the PAD4 enzyme, which citrullinates histones to drive NETosis through a reactive oxygen species-dependent mechanism. The extracellular DNA and soluble factors released by NETs promote clustering, anchorage-independent growth, and enhanced invasion of cancer cells, facilitating peritoneal seeding and progression of serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas (STICs) toward high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. Inhibiting PAD4 with GSK484 significantly reduces both NET formation and tumor cell seeding in vivo, identifying PAD4 as a therapeutic target to disrupt the ovulation-driven carcinogenesis pathway. These findings reveal an unexpected mechanistic link between a normal physiological process and cancer progression, with implications for understanding both endometriosis-associated malignancy and ovarian cancer development.

title: Novel circulating microRNA signature for early detection and prognostication of checkpoint inhibitor-related pneumonitis
summary:
# Summary

Immune checkpoint inhibitors enhance antitumor T-cell activity but can cause immune-related adverse events, including checkpoint inhibitor-related pneumonitis (CIP), a potentially severe pulmonary complication. This study identified a circulating microRNA signature comprising miR-193a-5p and miR-378a-3p found in both extracellular vesicles and serum that effectively distinguishes CIP from non-CIP patients and from infectious pneumonia, providing a non-invasive diagnostic tool. The three-microRNA panel, when combined with lymphocyte levels and inflammatory markers (IL-6, IL-10, IL-17A, IL-35), significantly enhanced diagnostic accuracy and was independently associated with overall survival in CIP patients. Extracellular vesicle-derived miRNAs and their circulating counterparts represent promising biomarkers for early detection and prognostication of checkpoint inhibitor-related pneumonitis, addressing a critical clinical need in immunotherapy monitoring.

title: Macrophages—Target and Tool in Tumor Treatment: Insights from Ovarian Cancer
summary: Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) represent critical drivers of ovarian cancer progression and therapeutic resistance, with their pro-tumor functions primarily mediated through M2-like polarization. M2 macrophages promote tumor growth through multiple mechanisms, including secretion of angiogenic factors (VEGF, PGF), matrix-degrading enzymes (MMPs) that facilitate invasion and metastasis, and immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β) that inhibit anti-tumor immunity. M2 polarization is regulated by STAT-3 and STAT-6 signaling downstream of IL-4 and IL-13, while M1 polarization—associated with anti-cancer inflammatory effects—is mediated by STAT-1/NF-κB signaling. CCL2 secretion by M2-like TAMs recruits additional monocytes, amplifying the immunosuppressive microenvironment and correlating with poor prognosis, while immune checkpoint molecules like PD-L1 and CD47–SIRPα signaling further suppress anti-tumor immunity. Understanding macrophage plasticity and the mechanisms driving M2 polarization provides therapeutic targets for converting the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment toward anti-cancer immunity in ovarian cancer.

title: Macrophages in Glioblastoma and How Non-Coding RNAs Impact Their Differentiation
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how non-coding RNAs regulate macrophage differentiation and function within the glioblastoma tumor microenvironment, with implications for understanding immune suppression in cancer. Tumor-associated macrophages, which constitute 20-40% of the glioblastoma microenvironment, are predominantly polarized toward an M2-like immunosuppressive phenotype through recruitment by tumor-derived cytokines including VEGF and CSF-1, and subsequently suppress anti-tumor T cell responses through IL-10 and TGF-β secretion. Multiple non-coding RNAs—including miRNAs (miR-21, miR-25, miR-125b, miR-1246), long non-coding RNAs (HOTAIR, H19, NEAT1), and other regulatory RNAs—are packaged into exosomes and extracellular vesicles by glioblastoma cells, where they modulate macrophage polarization toward the M2 phenotype and activate pro-tumor signaling pathways such as STAT3 and Akt. These findings suggest that targeting non-coding RNA-mediated macrophage polarization represents a potential therapeutic strategy to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment and improve efficacy of checkpoint inhibitor therapies like anti-PD-1 drugs in glioblastoma treatment.

title: Tear Fluid Biomarkers in Diabetic Ocular Surface Disease: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review identifies tear fluid biomarkers that characterize diabetic ocular surface disease through multiple inflammatory and biochemical pathways. Interleukin-6 emerges as a central mediator, elevated in diabetic patients and particularly in those with dry eye syndrome, where it activates matrix metalloproteinase-9, stimulates Th17 differentiation, and promotes interleukin-1β secretion leading to lacrimal gland apoptosis. Tumor necrosis factor-α is similarly elevated in diabetes and associated with retinal cell death and blood-retinal barrier disruption, while vascular endothelial growth factor drives pathological angiogenesis and microvascular dysfunction through NET formation, which can be inhibited therapeutically with DNase I. Dysregulated microRNAs including reduced miR-23a and elevated miR-9-5p, miR-143-3p, and miR-218-5p serve as biomarkers for diabetic retinopathy severity, and elevated structural proteins (lipocalin-1, S100A8/A9, IGFBP-3) alongside altered trace elements and advanced glycation end products collectively reflect systemic metabolic disruption. These tear biomarkers, particularly VEGF combined with lipocalin-1, show promise for non-invasive diabetic retinopathy screening and early detection of ocular complications.

title: Systemic Inflammation at the Crossroad of Major Depressive Disorder and Comorbidities: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Inflammation emerges as the primary biological mechanism underlying major depressive disorder (MDD), characterized by low-grade systemic inflammation with dysregulated immune activation. MDD patients exhibit elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and CCL molecules, while simultaneously showing reduced expression of anti-inflammatory mediators such as IL-4 and cytokine signaling suppressors (SOCS1-3), with inflammatory markers directly correlating with depressive symptom severity. The persistent low-grade inflammation in MDD explains its frequent bidirectional co-occurrence with cardiometabolic disorders, autoimmune conditions, and chronic pain disorders, while also contributing to reduced antidepressant treatment response. Central to MDD pathology is neuroinflammation involving microglial activation and NLRP3 inflammasome assembly, which intersects with the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis through pro-inflammatory cytokine-triggered glucocorticoid dysregulation and subsequent loss of negative feedback control. Additionally, dysregulation of the kynurenine pathway by elevated pro-inflammatory molecules generates neurotoxic quinolinic acid, further perpetuating neuroinflammatory responses. Females demonstrate a twofold higher MDD risk and elevated inflammatory levels compared to males, highlighting sex-based biological differences in inflammation-driven depression pathogenesis.

title: Serum VEGF Potential as a Biomarker for Diagnosis of Female Unexplained Infertility: Does Atopy Matter?
summary:
# Summary

Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) emerges as a potential serum biomarker for unexplained female infertility, with serum concentrations significantly elevated in infertile women compared to fertile controls. The study reveals that allergic sensitization, particularly to pet allergens such as cat allergen Fel d 1, correlates with higher VEGF levels, and women with both unexplained infertility and allergic sensitization show the highest VEGF concentrations. VEGF's role in infertility appears multifaceted: it mediates angiogenesis and vascular development critical for embryo implantation, while simultaneously regulating immune tolerance through recruitment of regulatory T cells and modulation of Th1/Th2 balance. Chronic low-grade inflammation driven by allergens may aberrantly elevate VEGF production by mast cells and keratinocytes, disrupting both endometrial receptivity and vascular homeostasis necessary for successful pregnancy. These findings suggest that atopy-associated immune dysregulation contributes to unexplained infertility through VEGF dysregulation, indicating that serum VEGF combined with allergen sensitization assessment may improve diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in reproductive medicine.

title: Distinct roles of HMOX1 on tumor epithelium and macrophage for regulation of immune microenvironment in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical immunoregulatory role for heme oxygenase-1 (HMOX1) in ovarian cancer through opposing functions in tumor epithelium and macrophages. HMOX1 expression is reduced in ovarian cancer epithelial cells but elevated in tumor-associated macrophages; low epithelial HMOX1 drives TGF-β1 secretion that activates three immunosuppressive macrophage subtypes (SPP1+, FOLR2+, and C1QC+) through PI3K/AKT/NF-κB signaling, while elevated macrophage HMOX1 further activates these same populations via NF-κB, collectively suppressing cytotoxic T lymphocyte activity and promoting PD-1 inhibitor resistance. Low HMOX1 expression is associated with poor progression-free survival, reduced CD8+ T cell infiltration, and correlates with advanced disease and elevated tumor markers. Notably, HMOX1 activation by the natural compound carnosol synergizes with anti-PD-1 antibodies to overcome immunosuppression, and the HMOX1/TGF-β1/PI3K/AKT/NF-κB/SPP1/FOLR2/C1QC axis components may serve as biomarkers for predicting immunotherapy response. These findings identify ferroptosis regulation as a key mechanism controlling ovarian cancer immune microenvironment and suggest HMOX1 activation as a therapeutic strategy to enhance checkpoint inhibitor efficacy.

title: The dual nature of immunotherapy in female reproductive disorders: immune homeostasis and clinical challenges
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the critical role of immune homeostasis in female reproductive health and the paradoxical challenges posed by immunotherapy in these conditions. Decidual natural killer (dNK) cells and regulatory T (Treg) cells, operating through immune checkpoint molecules, maintain maternal-fetal immune tolerance and fetal-maternal exemption through coordinated suppression of cytotoxic pathways and upregulation of adhesion proteins. Disruption of this delicate immune balance directly contributes to reproductive pathologies including endometriosis and recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA), wherein dysregulation of the PD-1/PD-L1 axis, reduced Treg frequencies, and pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization compromise tolerance. However, immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapies, while therapeutically beneficial for cancer, paradoxically trigger reproductive toxicity through inflammatory cytokine storms, Treg cell depletion, and dysregulation of the estrogen-immune axis, leading to menstrual disturbances, premature ovarian insufficiency, and diminished ovarian reserve in up to 12% of treated premenopausal women. Vaginal microbiota composition and estrogen-mediated T cell reprogramming further modulate this immune landscape, highlighting the complex interplay between systemic immunotherapy and reproductive function that clinicians must carefully navigate.

title: Modulation of Mouse Dendritic Cells In Vitro by Lactobacillus gasseri Postbiotic Proteins
summary:
# Summary

Lactobacillus gasseri OLL2809 exerts immunomodulatory effects on dendritic cells through distinct mechanisms depending on whether whole bacteria or cell-free supernatant is used. While irradiated whole bacteria strongly induce pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-12 and IL-6) and anti-inflammatory IL-10, the postbiotic supernatant selectively suppresses IL-12 and IL-6 production while maintaining IL-10 induction, and markedly reduces dendritic cell co-stimulatory markers (CD40, CD80, CD86). Proteinase K sensitivity experiments reveal that the immunomodulatory metabolites in the supernatant possess a proteinaceous nature, with surface-layer proteins (SLPs) identified as key active components that dose-dependently induce all tested cytokines. In the context of endometriosis, L. gasseri OLL2809 demonstrated clinical efficacy by preventing endometrial tissue growth in murine models and improving quality of life in patient studies, suggesting that probiotic-derived postbiotics may modulate the aberrant inflammatory immune response characteristic of this disease. These findings indicate that lactobacillus postbiotics suppress pro-inflammatory cytokine production through protein-based metabolites while maintaining beneficial anti-inflammatory signaling, offering a novel immunotherapeutic approach for endometriosis management.

title: Oral Administration of Limosilactobacillus reuteri KBL346 Ameliorates Influenza Virus A/PR8 Infection in Mouse
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that oral administration of *Limosilactobacillus reuteri* KBL346, both in live and heat-inactivated forms, significantly ameliorates influenza A/PR8 infection in mice by modulating immune responses and gut microbiota composition. The probiotic treatment improved body weight loss, increased survival rates, and reduced viral titers in the lungs while decreasing plasma immunoglobulin G levels, indicating enhanced viral clearance. Mechanistically, *L. reuteri* KBL346 downregulated inflammatory gene expression including interferon-γ, toll-like receptor 2, and interleukin-10, while simultaneously upregulating protective genes such as *Adamts4*, which is expressed in damage-responsive fibroblasts and promotes post-infection tissue recovery. The probiotic also altered gut microbial community structure and composition, with changes in microbial diversity correlating with severity of body weight loss. These findings suggest that *L. reuteri* KBL346 operates through a dual mechanism of immune modulation and microbiome-mediated protection, offering potential therapeutic benefits for influenza infection through restoration of intestinal barrier integrity and balanced immune homeostasis.

title: Distinct inflammatory profiles across migraine states: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that distinct inflammatory cytokine profiles characterize different migraine states, with interleukin-6 (IL-6) emerging as a central biomarker elevated across episodic migraine, chronic migraine, and acute migraine attacks. Transforming growth factor-β and C-X-C motif ligand 8 are similarly elevated in episodic migraine, while IL-1β and IL-10 specifically increase during active migraine attacks, and adiponectin associates with migraine chronification. The underlying mechanism involves cytokine-mediated activation of the trigeminovascular system, where pro-inflammatory cytokines released during systemic inflammatory responses or triggered by migraine-specific stimuli (such as nitroglycerin or cortical spreading depression) drive neuroinflammatory cascades central to migraine pathophysiology. Notably, no significant cytokine differences were detected between migraine with and without aura, suggesting distinct inflammatory mechanisms across clinical phenotypes. These findings establish inflammation as a key pathophysiological component of migraine and suggest that targeting specific cytokine pathways may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for managing different migraine presentations.

title: Efficacy of glucocorticoids treatment in recurrent embryo implantation failure
summary:
# Summary

Recurrent embryo implantation failure (RIF) is a complex reproductive disorder with significant immune and inflammatory components, particularly involving dysregulation of uterine natural killer (uNK) cells and T cell responses. In RIF patients, elevated uNK cell populations and excessive Th1 cell-mediated immunity produce pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and IFN-γ that damage extravillous trophoblasts and suppress their growth, while cytotoxic uNK cells directly compromise trophoblast invasion through perforin and granzyme-mediated mechanisms. Glucocorticoids, particularly oral prednisone, exert therapeutic potential by suppressing these inflammatory and immune reactions, promoting trophoblast cell proliferation and invasion, normalizing intrauterine cytokine expression and NK cell activity, and enhancing human chorionic gonadotropin secretion. However, recent large multicenter randomized controlled trials have demonstrated that oral prednisone treatment did not significantly improve live birth rates in RIF patients despite these mechanistic benefits. Additional immune pathologies contributing to RIF include antiphospholipid antibodies, which induce a procoagulant state and impair trophoblast function through downregulation of critical adhesion molecules. These findings suggest that while glucocorticoids effectively target immune dysregulation in RIF, their clinical efficacy in improving pregnancy outcomes remains limited and warrants further investigation into optimized treatment strategies.

title: Th1 and Th2 cells in equine endometrosis and their interactions with endometrial fibroblasts
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the role of T helper (Th)1 and Th2 cell subsets in equine endometrosis, a chronic degenerative endometrial condition characterized by fibrosis and infertility. Both Th1 and Th2 cells are pivotal drivers of fibrosis through distinct mechanisms: Th1 secretome regulates metabolic, cell cycle, and extracellular matrix (ECM) pathways, while Th2 secretome promotes tissue remodeling and signaling pathways. When mare endometrial fibroblasts were treated with Th1-conditioned medium, fibroblast proliferation and viability increased despite selective downregulation of fibrotic markers (FN1, ACTA2, MMP2, TIMP2) and upregulation of immunomodulatory genes (TIMP1, COL6A5, CSF3R, ALOX15, NTRK1, PDCD1LG2). Similarly, Th2-conditioned medium enhanced fibroblast proliferation and viability while modulating gene expression, including upregulation of chemokines (CCL26, CXCL9) and downregulation of genes linked to inflammation and tissue remodeling (PTGER3, GPX3, ADAMTS8). These findings demonstrate that the balance between Th1 and Th2 cell-mediated immune responses critically influences endometrial fibroblast activity and ECM remodeling, providing mechanistic insights into how persistent immune dysregulation contributes to endometrosis pathogenesis.

title: Mesenchymal stem cell therapy in bovine reproduction: Mechanistic insights, clinical applications, and translational challenges
summary:
# Summary

Mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapy represents a promising alternative to conventional antibiotic and hormonal treatments for bovine reproductive disorders such as endometritis, cystic ovarian disease, and infertility, which collectively cause substantial economic losses in cattle production. MSCs exert their therapeutic effects through multiple interconnected mechanisms: they migrate to damaged tissues via stromal-derived factor-1α signaling, differentiate into specialized reproductive cell types including granulosa and ovarian epithelial cells, and secrete paracrine factors including cytokines (interleukin-10, transforming growth factor-β) and growth factors (vascular endothelial growth factor, hepatocyte growth factor, fibroblast growth factor) that promote angiogenesis, epithelial regeneration, and tissue repair. At the immunological level, MSCs suppress excessive inflammation by downregulating pro-inflammatory T lymphocytes and M1 macrophages while promoting polarization toward reparative M2 macrophages, thereby limiting chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Preclinical and animal studies demonstrate that MSC therapy improves pregnancy rates, restores ovarian activity and hormone levels (estrogen and anti-Müllerian hormone), and enhances oocyte quality and endometrial regeneration. Cell-free approaches using MSC-derived secretomes and exosomes show comparable regenerative potential, offering safer translational pathways for clinical application.

title: Resident memory macrophages and trained innate immunity at barrier tissues
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how tissue-resident macrophages develop trained innate immunity—a long-lasting enhanced or reduced immune responsiveness to subsequent pathogenic challenges. Epigenetic modifications and metabolic rewiring, particularly through fatty acid oxidation and glutaminolysis pathways, are key drivers of innate immune memory in alveolar macrophages. Local immunological exposure to microbial components like lipopolysaccharide and β-glucan, as well as respiratory pathogens including influenza A virus and SARS-CoV-2, induces memory macrophage phenotypes characterized by upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and enhanced efferocytosis markers such as MERTK. Trained alveolar macrophages generated through vaccines (BCG, adenovirus-vectored, and MV130), microbial exposure, or pathogen recovery provide enhanced protection against heterologous pathogens and improve outcomes in pulmonary infections and fibrosis. While trained innate immunity typically confers protective benefits through disease tolerance mechanisms, it can paradoxically contribute to maladaptive inflammation in certain contexts, particularly with allergen exposure. The development of memory macrophages involves both adaptive immune signals, including CD4+ and CD8+ T cell-derived interferon-gamma, and innate pathways such as mTOR signaling.

title: Genetic predisposition to systemic inflammatory proteins is causally associated with inflammatory bowel disease: Insights from multi-omics association study and single-cell RNA-sequencing analysis
summary: This multi-omics study reveals that genetic predisposition to specific systemic inflammatory proteins causally influences inflammatory bowel disease risk through dysregulated cytokine signaling. Genetically elevated IL-10 and IL-13 levels demonstrate suggestively positive associations with IBD overall and Crohn disease specifically, while CCL3 elevation similarly increases Crohn disease risk; conversely, higher CXCL10 levels associate with reduced IBD risk, suggesting protective mechanisms. Interleukins and chemokines operate as critical circulating regulators in intestinal inflammation, with dysregulation of their homeostasis driving recurrent gastrointestinal inflammation, though notably, causal relationships with ulcerative colitis were not supported. Single-cell transcriptomic analysis identified key receptor-ligand interactions including IL-10 receptor components (IL-10RA/RB), IL-13 receptors (IL-13RA1/RA2), and the CXCR3-CXCL10 axis, alongside enriched expression of IBD-associated genes in colon tissue cell populations. The Ascl2 transcription factor functions as a negative regulator of pathogenic Th17 differentiation by promoting IL-10 production, thereby alleviating intestinal inflammation. These findings establish a mechanistic framework linking genetic inflammatory protein variants to IBD pathogenesis and identify therapeutic targets within the cytokine-chemokine signaling network.

title: The Impact of Intrauterine Devices on the Risk of Ureaplasma Species Overgrowth: A Review of the Literature
summary:
# Summary

Ureaplasma species are commensal organisms of the urogenital tract that can cause significant pathology when they overgrow, including bacterial vaginosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, and adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preterm labor and premature rupture of membranes. Intrauterine device (IUD) use, particularly copper IUDs, is linked to increased risk of Ureaplasma overgrowth and urogenital infections through multiple mechanisms: IUDs promote biofilm formation, irregular bleeding elevates vaginal pH favoring anaerobic bacterial growth, and Ureaplasma's urease activity further alkalinizes the vaginal environment while reducing protective Lactobacillus abundance. IUD insertion triggers a heightened local inflammatory response characterized by elevated cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and IFN-γ), and these same inflammatory mediators are implicated in endometriosis development through promotion of endometrial tissue proliferation and fibrosis via Th2 and T regulatory cell pathways. Ureaplasma complications are strongly correlated with host-specific immune responses rather than bacterial load alone, suggesting that the inflammatory milieu created by IUDs may exacerbate both Ureaplasma-related pathology and endometriosis progression. First-line treatments include azithromycin, doxycycline, and erythromycin for resistant strains.

title: The Effect of Intrauterine Autologous Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells (PBMCs) Administration in Modulating the Immunologic Profile Aberrations in Repeated Implantation Failure (RIF) Women
summary: Intrauterine administration of autologous peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) represents a novel immunomodulatory approach for treating repeated implantation failure by rebalancing dysregulated immune responses. PBMC therapy significantly elevates pro-inflammatory Th17 cells and natural killer cells while simultaneously reducing regulatory T cells and their associated markers (FoxP3 and PTEN), thereby shifting the immune microenvironment toward a pro-implantation phenotype. This immunological remodeling is accompanied by increased serum levels of inflammatory cytokines IL-1β, IL-17, and TNF-α, while suppressing anti-inflammatory mediators IL-10 and TGF-β. The underlying mechanism involves downregulation of microRNAs (mir-10a and mir-20b) that normally constrain Treg cell differentiation, coupled with upregulation of matrix metalloproteinases MMP-2 and MMP-9 that facilitate trophoblast invasion and implantation. Clinical outcomes demonstrate that PBMC-treated women achieve significantly higher pregnancy and live birth rates compared to routine treatment, establishing immune system rebalancing as a viable therapeutic strategy for implantation failure associated with aberrant immunotolerance.

title: Macrophages in endometriosis: key roles and emerging therapeutic opportunities—a narrative review
summary: Macrophages emerge as central orchestrators of endometriosis pathophysiology, contributing to the disease's characteristic triad of pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility through dysregulated immune responses. In endometriosis lesions, macrophages adopt predominantly pro-inflammatory and alternatively activated phenotypes that secrete excessive amounts of inflammatory mediators including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-α, and monocyte chemoattractant protein-1, while simultaneously promoting angiogenesis, fibrosis, and extracellular matrix remodeling. Notably, macrophage polarization patterns vary with disease stage: early-stage endometriosis is characterized by M1-like pro-inflammatory macrophages, whereas advanced disease shifts toward an M2-like anti-inflammatory microenvironment that paradoxically supports lesion persistence through tissue remodeling and immunosuppression. The review identifies impaired macrophage cytotoxicity and phagocytic capacity as critical factors allowing ectopic endometrial implants to escape immune surveillance and establish chronically inflamed lesions. Emerging therapeutic strategies target macrophage recruitment and activation through mechanisms including inhibition of CD47–signal regulatory protein α interactions and modulation of macrophage polarization, offering promising avenues to restore normal immune homeostasis and alleviate endometriosis-associated symptoms.

title: High levels of a comprehensive set of matrix metalloproteinases in endometriotic lesions: validating the key role of cellular senescence in endometriosis pathogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cellular senescence plays a key pathogenic role in endometriosis by establishing elevated levels of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-2, and MMP-3) in endometriotic lesions compared to normal endometrium. The senescence-associated secretory phenotype in these lesions promotes the expression of MMPs through upregulation by inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, TNF-α, TGF-β, IL-2, and IL-27, which collectively drive chronic inflammation and extracellular matrix remodeling characteristic of the disease. Senescent fibroblasts accumulate in endometriotic tissue, as evidenced by elevated senescence markers (p16INK4a, reduced lamin B1, and increased senescence-associated β-galactosidase activity), and secrete pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-1, IL-6, IL-8) and MMPs that perpetuate tissue remodeling. The study identifies oxidative stress and MAPK pathway dysregulation as upstream triggers that activate the senescence cascade, with p16INK4a activation of retinoblastoma proteins sustaining cell cycle arrest. These findings establish MMPs as both biomarkers and therapeutic targets in endometriosis and suggest that senolytic agents targeting senescent cells may offer novel therapeutic strategies for this chronic inflammatory condition.

title: MON-153 Bacterial endotoxin lipopolysaccharide dependent TNFα-signaling dysregulates proinflammatory and anti-inflammatory miRNAs in endometrial stromal cell pathogenesis
summary: This study demonstrates that bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) drives pathogenic inflammation in endometrial stromal cells through TNFα-dependent signaling mechanisms. LPS exposure upregulates TNFα and its receptor TNFR1, along with multiple proinflammatory cytokines including IL1β, IL6, IL8, and the chemokine CCl5, while simultaneously reducing anti-inflammatory microRNAs in normal endometrial stromal cells to levels comparable to diseased endometrial stromal cells from endometriosis patients. The LPS-TNFα-signaling axis activates NF-κB phosphorylation in a dose- and time-dependent manner, which subsequently upregulates the same panel of inflammatory mediators. Ultimately, this LPS-dependent TNFα signaling pathway through NF-κB activation dysregulates the expression of proinflammatory, anti-inflammatory, and proangiogenic microRNAs, mechanisms that contribute to endometrial lesion proliferation. These findings suggest that bacterial endotoxin exposure may contribute to the estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory state characteristic of endometriosis through microRNA dysregulation. This work identifies TNFα signaling and NF-κB pathway activation as critical nodes in LPS-mediated endometrial pathogenesis.

title: Genistein enhances anti-PD-1 efficacy in melanoma by suppressing regulatory T cell differentiation and activity
summary:
# Summary

Genistein enhances anti-PD-1 immunotherapy efficacy in melanoma by suppressing regulatory T cell (Treg) differentiation and function through inhibition of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. Genistein treatment dose-dependently reduces expression of key Treg markers including Foxp3, CTLA-4, and LAG-3, while decreasing immunosuppressive cytokine production (IL-10) and attenuating Treg-mediated suppression of helper T cell proliferation and effector cytokine production. Mechanistically, genistein blocks phosphorylation of PI3K and AKT, which are critical regulators of Treg development and homeostasis, as demonstrated by rescue experiments using the AKT activator SC-79. Combined genistein and anti-PD-1 therapy synergistically inhibits tumor growth and enhances apoptotic cell death in melanoma models, accompanied by increased CD8+ T cell infiltration, elevated CD8+/Treg ratios, and amplified pro-inflammatory cytokine responses. These findings suggest that targeting Treg-mediated immunosuppression via PI3K/AKT inhibition represents a viable strategy to potentiate checkpoint inhibitor efficacy in cancer immunotherapy.

title: Embryo-maternal cross-talk: key players in successful implantation and live birth rates
summary:
# Summary

Successful embryo implantation and live birth depend on finely orchestrated embryo-maternal immune cross-talk regulated by sex hormones and trophoblast-derived factors. Progesterone drives decidualization and induces IL-15 production, which expands endometrial natural killer (NK) cells that secrete angiogenic factors (VEGF, ANGPT2, PGF) critical for spiral artery remodeling. Concurrently, progesterone and estrogen regulate chemokines (CX3CL1, CXCL10, CCL2) that recruit peripheral NK cells and monocytes to the decidua, where they differentiate into decidual macrophages and dendritic cells producing both pro-inflammatory and immunoregulatory cytokines. Trophoblast-expressed HLA-G activates KIR2DL4 receptors on decidual mast cells, triggering release of leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) and MMP9, which promote trophoblast migration and tube development. The endometrium simultaneously creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment through HLA-G, PAEP, and IDO production, while macrophage-derived cytokines (IL-1β, LIF) enhance fucosylated epithelial structures that increase embryo adhesion. Regulatory T cells expand during the window of implantation to reduce cytotoxic activity, establishing pregnancy tolerance essential for successful implantation and fetal development.

title: Immunotropic Effects of Steroid Hormone Medicines in Combination with Plasma-Treated Solution in Women of a Reproductive Age and Postmenopausal Women
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the immunomodulatory effects of steroid hormones and plasma-treated solutions (PTS) in reproductive and postmenopausal women. Steroid hormones—including progesterone, dexamethasone, medroxyprogesterone acetate, and gestabutanoil—suppress pro-inflammatory cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) through glucocorticoid receptor activation and promotion of regulatory T cell differentiation, while simultaneously enhancing anti-inflammatory responses. Plasma-treated solutions, which accumulate reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS), similarly reduce IL-6 and inflammatory markers while increasing the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10. When combined, PTS and steroid hormonal medicines produce synergistic suppression of cytokine production and RONS, with dexamethasone showing the most potent effects in postmenopausal women by maximally reducing TGF-β and stimulating IL-10. These findings suggest that combined hormone and plasma therapy may offer enhanced anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to conditions like endometriosis characterized by excessive inflammatory immune responses.

title: Macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) and the tumor ecosystem: a tale of inflammation, immune escape, and tumor growth
summary: Macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) is a pleiotropic cytokine produced by the anterior pituitary gland in response to corticotrophin-releasing hormone, as well as by immune cells including monocytes, macrophages, B cells, and T cells, that plays a central role in orchestrating both pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive responses. MIF engages multiple signaling pathways by binding to CD74, CXCR2, and CXCR4 receptors, thereby activating ERK, PI3K-Akt, NF-κB, and STAT3 signaling cascades that drive the production of inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) and suppress p53-dependent apoptosis in immune cells. In the tumor microenvironment, MIF promotes immunosuppression by polarizing macrophages toward an M2 phenotype that secretes IL-10, inhibiting dendritic cell maturation and IL-12 expression, stimulating regulatory T cell development through IL-2 modulation, and expanding myeloid-derived suppressor cells that produce arginase-1 and TGF-β. Additionally, MIF enhances angiogenesis through increased VEGF release and promotes neutrophil infiltration, while paradoxically activating natural killer cells through upregulation of NKG2D and DNAM-1. This cytokine's multifaceted role in immune escape and tumor promotion positions MIF as a critical mediator in cancer immunology and suggests therapeutic potential targeting this pathway in inflammatory and neoplastic diseases.

title: Hematological factors in women with Asherman syndrome and primary infertility: An overview
summary:
# Summary

Asherman syndrome, characterized by intrauterine adhesions following uterine trauma, represents a significant cause of primary infertility in women. The pathogenesis involves dysregulated hematological cascades, including abnormal coagulation and fibrinolysis, with elevated tissue factor and fibrinogen coupled with reduced antithrombin III and plasmin activity, leading to excessive fibrin deposition and adhesion formation. Chronic inflammation mediated by interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha perpetuates fibrotic changes and impairs epithelial cell proliferation, while dysregulated angiogenic signaling through VEGF and angiopoietin pathways compromises endometrial vascularization and tissue healing. The resulting ischemic injury creates a self-perpetuating cycle of inflammation and fibrosis that maintains the hostile uterine microenvironment. Therapeutic strategies including anticoagulant therapy, anti-inflammatory agents, and exogenous angiogenic factors represent potential interventions to restore normal hemostatic balance and promote endometrial regeneration.

title: The Role of Interleukin-8 (IL-8) in Treatment-Resistant Depression: A Review of Mechanisms, Biomarker Potential, and Therapeutic Implications
summary:
# Summary

This review examines interleukin-8 (IL-8) as a key inflammatory mediator in treatment-resistant depression, a condition increasingly recognized as involving neuroinflammatory processes alongside classical proinflammatory cytokines like IL-6 and TNF-α. IL-8 exerts diverse effects through binding to receptors CXCR1 and CXCR2 expressed on neurons, astrocytes, and glial cells, influencing microglial activation, synaptic plasticity, blood-brain barrier integrity, and neurovascular function. The cytokine is induced by TNF-α and IL-1β via transcriptional regulators including NF-κB, AP-1, and HIFs, and its expression is elevated by depression-associated factors such as stress, obesity, metabolic syndrome, and circadian disruption. Elevated serum IL-8 levels are found in treatment-resistant depression patients and may predict both treatment response and depressive symptom severity, while ketamine therapy notably reduces IL-8 within 24 hours, suggesting a mechanistic link between IL-8 reduction and therapeutic response. Emerging evidence indicates that interventions including exercise, mindfulness-based therapies, and anti-inflammatory agents can reduce IL-8 levels, pointing toward potential therapeutic strategies for inflammatory subtypes of depression.

title: Fusobacteriumnucleatum: Pathophysiological and Clinical Involvement in Inflammatory Bowel Diseases, Colorectal Cancer and Cardiovascular Diseases
summary: Fusobacterium nucleatum, a Gram-negative anaerobe commonly found in oral biofilms, has emerged as a significant pathogenic factor in inflammatory bowel diseases, colorectal cancer, and cardiovascular disorders through multiple molecular mechanisms. In inflammatory bowel disease, the bacterium adheres to intestinal epithelium via the FadA adhesin, which engages E-cadherin to activate β-catenin signaling, while simultaneously disrupting tight junctions and increasing mucosal permeability that drives Th17-dominated immune responses and chronic inflammation. In colorectal cancer, Fusobacterium nucleatum promotes tumor growth, metastasis, and chemoresistance through β-catenin activation, immune evasion via the Fap2-TIGIT interaction, and M2 polarization of tumor-associated macrophages, with enrichment correlating strongly to advanced tumor stage and poor prognosis, particularly in right-sided lesions. Beyond the gastrointestinal tract, systemic dissemination of the bacterium has been detected in atherosclerotic plaques and endocardial tissues, where it contributes to vascular inflammation, atherogenesis, endothelial dysfunction, foam cell formation, and plaque instability through TLR4/NF-κB signaling and oxidative stress. The bacterium's pathogenic capacity is amplified by biofilm formation and the coordinated action of virulence factors including RadD and FadA that promote epithelial invasion and barrier disruption. These findings position Fusobacterium nucleatum as a potential therapeutic target and prognostic biomarker across multiple inflammatory and neoplastic diseases.

title: Clustering of Uterine Natural Killer Cells Around Uterine Glands in Women with Recurrent Implantation Failure and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: An Immunohistochemical Study
summary:
# Summary

Uterine natural killer (uNK) cells exhibit critical immunomodulatory functions essential for successful pregnancy, including control of embryo implantation, trophoblast migration and invasion, and spiral artery remodeling. Dysregulation of uNK cell activity is implicated in recurrent implantation failure and recurrent pregnancy loss, though the study found that absolute uNK cell count alone does not significantly predict these outcomes, suggesting that uNK cell activation and clustering patterns are more clinically relevant than quantity. uNK cells form CD56+ clusters around endometrial glands in the stratum functionale, where they produce pro-angiogenic factors including vascular endothelial growth factor C and placental growth factor that drive vascular remodeling. Recruitment and clustering of uNK cells is orchestrated by chemotactic gradients involving stromal cell-derived factor 1 (SDF-1) and IL-6-mediated JAK/STAT signaling, with uNK cells expressing CXCR4 receptors that guide their migration and integrin and cadherin-mediated adhesion molecules that facilitate cell-cell interactions in the decidua. Once clustered, uNK cells secrete cytokines and growth factors that establish immune tolerance, promote tissue remodeling, and enable successful fetal-maternal interactions, indicating that understanding uNK cell function and spatial organization rather than mere cell counts is fundamental to comprehending implantation success and pregnancy complications.

title: A narrative review on the role of IL-1β, IL-8, and the endometrial matrix in heavy menstrual bleeding pathogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review explores the inflammatory pathogenesis of heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) through the coordinated actions of IL-1β and IL-8 in disrupting endometrial homeostasis. IL-1β, primarily secreted by activated macrophages, neutrophils, and endometrial stromal cells, drives HMB through multiple mechanisms: it upregulates matrix metalloproteinases (particularly MMP-2 and MMP-9), downregulates their natural inhibitors (TIMPs), and promotes extracellular matrix degradation that weakens structural integrity. Concurrently, IL-1β stimulates VEGF production and increases vascular permeability, fostering abnormal angiogenesis and fragile blood vessels. IL-8, produced by endometrial epithelial and stromal cells, acts as a potent neutrophil chemoattractant through CXCR1/CXCR2 binding, triggering neutrophil-derived MMP-9 and elastase release that further degrades collagen and laminin while disrupting endothelial junctions. Critically, IL-1β synergizes with TNF-α to amplify IL-8 upregulation, creating a self-perpetuating inflammatory cycle that overwhelms protective tissue regeneration mechanisms. These coordinated inflammatory mediators collectively explain elevated bleeding in HMB patients through combined mechanisms of altered vasculature, compromised ECM integrity, and excessive immune cell infiltration.

title: TET3-overexpressing macrophages are a unifying pathogenic feature with therapeutic potential in chronic inflammatory diseases
summary: This study identifies TET3-overexpressing macrophages, termed Toe-Macs, as a unifying pathogenic feature across multiple chronic inflammatory diseases including endometriosis, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis, and non-small cell lung cancer. Toe-Macs are activated by TGF-β1 and CCL2 derived from the disease microenvironment and produce elevated levels of proinflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6), chemokines, and immunosuppressive factors like PD-L1 through TET3-driven upregulation of NLRP3, IL1B, TGFB1, CCL2, and CD274. TET3 facilitates this pathogenic program by recruiting phosphorylated STAT3 to target gene promoters, creating an open chromatin state that sensitizes macrophages to NLRP3 inflammasome activation and cytokine production. Critically, selective elimination of Toe-Macs through myeloid-specific Tet3 knockout or the small-molecule degrader Bobcat339 effectively attenuates disease progression across all three diseases without discernible side effects, while increasing tumor-infiltrating CD8+ T cell responses. These findings establish TET3-expressing macrophages as a conserved therapeutic target across diverse chronic inflammatory conditions, with both genetic and pharmacological approaches showing translational potential.

title: Exploring the mechanisms of mutual influence between lactylation and macrophage polarization in the context of disease
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how lactylation—an epigenetic modification driven by lactate metabolism—reciprocally influences macrophage polarization in inflammatory contexts relevant to disease progression. Lactate, a glycolytic byproduct, is converted to lactyl-CoA, which serves as a substrate for histone lactylation at sites like H3K18, directly activating genes associated with the anti-inflammatory M2 macrophage phenotype. The balance between lactylation "writers" (P300/CBP, GCN5, HBO1) and "erasers" (HDACs, SIRTs) dynamically regulates this epigenetic switch, with elevated lactylation promoting M2 polarization and suppressing pro-inflammatory M1 responses. Lactate simultaneously functions as a signaling molecule through GPR81 receptor activation and supports M2 differentiation by facilitating oxidative phosphorylation metabolism, while M1 macrophages rely on glycolytic lactate production to sustain inflammatory cytokine secretion. This metabolic-epigenetic axis, where lactate links cellular metabolism to gene expression control via histone modification, represents a critical mechanism by which the tumor microenvironment and inflammatory tissues shift macrophage phenotypes toward immune tolerance and tissue remodeling, with implications for disease pathology including endometriosis.

title: M2 Macrophages are Major Mediators of Germline Risk of Endometriosis and Explain Pleiotropy With Comorbid Traits
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies M2 macrophages as major mediators of endometriosis genetic risk through their production of interleukin-1 (IL1A and IL1B), which act as central hubs in endometriosis susceptibility pathways. M2 macrophages express high levels of IL1A and IL1B, which bind to IL1 receptors on endometrial epithelial and stromal cells, disrupting epithelial organization and differentiation through feedback loops involving NF-κB signaling. The authors demonstrate that anakinra, an IL1 receptor antagonist, significantly reduces spontaneous and evoked pain, decreases tissue burden, and substantially reduces angiogenesis-promoting factors like VEGF in endometriosis lesions. Additionally, the study reveals pleiotropy between endometriosis and comorbid inflammatory conditions—M2 macrophage-mediated IL1 signaling is associated with arthritis, asthma, irritable bowel syndrome, and chronic pain conditions, while endometriosis itself is linked to fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, allergies, and rheumatoid arthritis. These findings establish IL1 signaling as a therapeutic target and provide mechanistic insight into why endometriosis patients often experience multiple comorbid inflammatory and pain disorders.

title: Aberrant Subsets of Regulatory T Cells and their Correlations with Serum IL-2 in Patients with Rheumatoid Arthritis
summary:
# Summary

Regulatory T cell (Treg) dysfunction is central to rheumatoid arthritis pathogenesis, characterized by a critical imbalance in Treg subsets. Patients with RA display significantly reduced naive Tregs and elevated memory Tregs compared to healthy controls, with memory Treg percentage correlating with disease activity severity. IL-2 positively correlates with naive Treg counts and plays a modulatory role in Treg homeostasis, suggesting its importance in maintaining immune tolerance. Concurrently, an elevated Th17/Treg ratio drives pathogenic inflammation through Th17-mediated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, osteoclastogenesis, and bone erosion. Tregs normally suppress inflammation by secreting IL-10 and TGF-β to inhibit T effector cells, yet this regulatory capacity is compromised in RA. These findings collectively indicate that aberrant Treg subset composition and reduced IL-2-mediated Treg maintenance contribute significantly to the autoimmune dysregulation and inflammatory pathology observed in rheumatoid arthritis.

title: Decoding immune-metabolic crosstalk in ARDS: a transcriptomic exploration of biomarkers, cellular dynamics, and therapeutic pathways
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic study identifies metabolic reprogramming and immune cell dysfunction as central mechanisms in acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), revealing that metabolic reconfiguration directly regulates immune cell subtypes while maintaining energy balance and suppressing excessive inflammation. Machine learning analysis identified three key biomarkers—SMARCD3, TCN1, and RPL14—all associated with chemokine signaling pathways and immune-metabolic dysregulation in ARDS. Functional studies demonstrated that SMARCD3 and TCN1 critically regulate mitochondrial function, oxidative stress, apoptosis, glucose metabolism, and inflammatory cytokine production, making them superior therapeutic targets compared to RPL14. The study maps distinct metabolic signatures across immune cells: M1 macrophages and Th1/Th17 cells employ glycolysis and glutaminolysis for pro-inflammatory responses, while M2 macrophages utilize oxidative phosphorylation and fatty acid oxidation for tissue repair. Selenium and Cyclosporine A were identified as compounds potentially targeting the candidate biomarkers, offering therapeutic avenues to modulate immune-metabolic crosstalk. These findings establish metabolic reprogramming as both a pathogenic driver and viable therapeutic target for ARDS through coordinated suppression of immune activation and restoration of metabolic homeostasis.

title: A Focus on Inflammatory and Bacterial Biomarkers in Secondary Peritonitis
summary:
# Summary

This article examines inflammatory and bacterial biomarkers in secondary peritonitis, identifying multiple indicators of disease severity and microbial involvement. Procalcitonin, interleukin-6, high mobility group box 1, C-reactive protein, and neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin are strongly associated with secondary peritonitis and correlate with organ failure outcomes. Mesothelial cells function as front-line sentinels that release pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, CXCL1, CCL2) when activated by bacterial lipopolysaccharides, amplifying local inflammation. The peritoneal cavity becomes colonized by diverse polymicrobial communities including gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter, Bacteroides fragilis) and gram-positive species, often forming biofilms that protect pathogens from immune clearance. Therapeutic interventions such as peritoneal lavage effectively reduce systemic inflammation by decreasing TNF-α and IL-6 while elevating the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10, while complementary approaches like catalpol inhibit NF-κB signaling to suppress pro-inflammatory responses. These findings emphasize the critical role of inflammatory biomarkers in predicting clinical outcomes and guide both diagnostic assessment and therapeutic strategies in peritonitis management.

title: Addressing Meta-Inflammation in the Comprehensive Management of Chronic Pain
summary: Meta-inflammation, a chronic low-grade inflammatory state driven by metabolic dysregulation, poor dietary habits, and gut microbiota alterations, represents a key mechanism underlying chronic pain conditions including endometriosis. Pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β perpetuate this inflammatory milieu, which triggers a cascade of neuroinflammatory events through persistent cytokine signaling that leads to central sensitization and heightened pain perception. At the molecular level, inflammatory mediators including prostaglandins and bradykinins activate peripheral nociceptors, initiating calcium influx and cellular depolarization that promotes release of additional pro-inflammatory cytokines and perpetuates chronic inflammatory states characterized by elevated NGF, PGE2, and chemokines like CCL2 and CXCL1. Therapeutic interventions targeting meta-inflammation include dietary modifications such as Mediterranean and DASH diets supplemented with omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, curcumin, and vitamin D, alongside pharmacological approaches including metformin, GLP-1 agonists, and SGLT-2 inhibitors that address underlying metabolic dysfunction. This comprehensive management approach recognizes that meta-inflammation shares common pathways across multiple chronic diseases including obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and endometriosis, suggesting that addressing the inflammatory substrate may provide broad therapeutic benefit for chronic pain management.

title: Proteome-wide Mendelian randomization identifies β-nerve growth factor as a target for endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies β-nerve growth factor (β-NGF) as a causal driver of endometriosis, with a 2.23-fold increased risk association. β-NGF promotes endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms: it stimulates aberrant neuronal outgrowth and hyperinnervation in ectopic lesions while driving pain signaling through sensitization of nociceptive neurons. NGF binding to its receptor TrkA activates pro-survival and proliferative pathways (PI3K/Akt and MAPK/ERK), promoting ectopic endometrial cell proliferation and inhibiting apoptosis, while also stimulating endothelial cell proliferation and angiogenesis to support lesion growth. Additionally, NGF activates immune cells including mast cells and macrophages, which release pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) that further promote endometrial cell adhesion and proliferation. Elevated NGF levels are consistently detected in peritoneal fluid, serum, and lesion tissue of endometriosis patients and correlate with chronic pelvic pain severity. The study identifies Fasinumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting NGF, as a promising therapeutic candidate, alongside four additional drug compounds, offering potential new treatment strategies for endometriosis-associated pain and disease progression.

title: Macrophages and neutrophils in ovarian cancer microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) and neutrophils (TANs) are critical regulators of ovarian cancer progression, with M2-like TAMs predominating in the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment where they promote chemoresistance, metastasis, angiogenesis, and extracellular matrix remodeling. M2 macrophages are activated by IL-4, IL-10, IL-13, and TGF-β, and utilize fatty acid oxidation to maintain an immunosuppressive state while expressing pro-tumoral factors including VEGF-A and CD163. TAM-tumor cell interactions upregulate PD-L1 and associated immunosuppressive mediators (IL-6, IL-10, VEGF), which promote carboplatin resistance that can be reversed by PD-L1 silencing. The recruitment of monocytes via the CCL2/CCR2 axis drives their differentiation into M2-like TAMs, while conventional immune checkpoint inhibitors show limited efficacy in ovarian cancer due to the immunologically "cold" microenvironment that suppresses effector T cell activation and infiltration. High-grade serous carcinoma, accounting for 80% of epithelial ovarian cancers, exhibits mutations in TP53, RB1, FOXM1, CCNE1, and NOTCH3, with incessant ovulation serving as the highest risk factor for tumorigenesis through reactive oxygen species-mediated epithelial cell DNA damage.

title: T cell-driven sustained inflammation and immune dysregulation mimicking immunosenescence for up to three years post-COVID-19
summary: This study demonstrates that persistent T cell-driven inflammation characterizes long COVID, with distinct Th17 subsets playing opposing roles in disease chronicity. LTB+ Th17 cells, which are enriched during acute infection and sustained by elevated S100A8 and IL-16, drive chronic inflammation and correlate strongly with long COVID symptoms including anxiety, chest pain, and sensory disturbances. In contrast, RORC+ Th17 cells exhibit anti-inflammatory properties and are negatively associated with symptom severity, particularly muscle weakness. Beyond three years post-infection, COVID-19 convalescents display signs of immunosenescence marked by reduced naive CD4+ and CD8+ T cells and MAIT cells, alongside persistent elevation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-17, IFN-γ, IL-1β, IL-4, IL-9). The sustained inflammation is driven primarily by Th17 cells rather than monocytes, with multiple regulatory factors including PTPRC, SIRPA, and S100A8 maintaining LTB+ Th17 cell persistence. These findings suggest that dysregulated Th17 differentiation and prolonged alarmin signaling establish a self-perpetuating cycle of immune dysfunction underlying long COVID pathogenesis.

title: Cellular Senescence in Endometrium: A Pivotal Regulator in Physiological Remodeling and Pathological Disorders
summary: Cellular senescence plays a dual role in endometrial physiology and pathology, functioning as both a physiological regulator of decidualization and embryo implantation and a driver of chronic inflammatory diseases including endometriosis. During normal reproduction, progesterone-induced decidualization triggers controlled acute senescence of endometrial stromal cells through the FOXO1-DIO2 axis, which promotes implantation-essential factors while senescent cells are efficiently cleared by decidual natural killer cells. However, pathological accumulation of senescent cells establishes a proinflammatory microenvironment through senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) components including IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8, which activate NF-κB and JAK/STAT signaling pathways and recruit macrophages and neutrophils that perpetuate chronic inflammation and fibrosis. In endometriosis, accumulated senescent cells sustain both chronic inflammation and angiogenesis through persistent SASP signaling, while progesterone withdrawal further exacerbates inflammation by relieving NF-κB pathway suppression. Therapeutic interventions including quercetin, metformin, and rapamycin target this senescence-inflammation axis through distinct mechanisms—eliminating senescent cells, suppressing NF-κB/SASP signaling via AMPK activation, and inhibiting mTOR respectively—offering promising strategies for treating endometriosis and other endometrial pathologies.

title: Tryptophan-kynurenine pathway: a possible new mechanism for the prevention and treatment of reproductive system-related diseases
summary:
# Summary

The tryptophan-kynurenine pathway represents a critical metabolic system controlling inflammatory and immune responses relevant to reproductive diseases, including endometriosis. This pathway, which accounts for 95% of tryptophan metabolism, is initiated by the rate-limiting enzymes indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO) and tryptophan 2,3-dioxygenase (TDO), which convert tryptophan to kynurenine, followed by sequential enzymatic steps producing metabolites such as kynurenic acid and 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid. IDO expression is upregulated by inflammatory cytokines including interferon-gamma (IFN-γ), TNF-α, and interleukin-6 (IL-6) through multiple signaling pathways, particularly STAT1 and STAT3, and drives immune tolerance through T cell differentiation to regulatory T cells while suppressing natural killer cell activity. In endometriosis specifically, elevated IDO expression in ectopic endometrial tissue induces IL-33 secretion, perpetuating the inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of the disease. These findings suggest that modulating tryptophan-kynurenine pathway metabolism and IDO activity may provide novel therapeutic targets for preventing and treating endometriosis and other reproductive system disorders.

title: Reciprocal activation between M1 macrophages and trophoblasts through CXCL9/STAT1/ZEB1/CCL2 axis promotes recurrent spontaneous abortion
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a pathogenic reciprocal activation axis between M1 macrophages and trophoblasts that promotes recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA).
