M1 macrophages produce elevated CXCL9 in RSA decidual tissues, which binds CXCR3 on trophoblasts to activate JAK1/2-STAT1 signaling, leading to STAT1-mediated induction of IRF1 and suppression of the EMT transcription factor ZEB1. This signaling cascade triggers a reverse epithelial-mesenchymal transition characterized by E-cadherin upregulation and N-cadherin/vimentin downregulation, thereby impairing trophoblast migration and invasion. The affected trophoblasts then upregulate CCL2 through ZEB1-IRF1 interaction, which recruits additional M1 macrophages and perpetuates the inflammatory cycle. Therapeutic blockade of CXCL9 or inhibition of CXCR3 and JAK/STAT1 signaling reversed these effects and reduced embryo resorption in vivo, offering potential therapeutic targets for RSA and related pregnancy complications like endometriosis-associated reproductive failure.

title: Vitamin D3 ameliorates inflammation and autonomic dysfunction in a rat model of reflux esophagitis via modulation of IL-6 and TNF-α
summary:
# Summary

Reflux esophagitis induces mucosal inflammation, elevated IL-6 and TNF-α expression, and autonomic dysfunction characterized by impaired heart rate variability, with these inflammatory cytokines directly impairing vagus nerve activity and reducing parasympathetic control. Vitamin D3 supplementation ameliorates reflux esophagitis through suppression of multiple inflammatory pathways, including downregulation of IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-8, while also inhibiting reactive oxygen species production and regulating TGF-β signaling involved in epithelial barrier breakdown. Prophylactic vitamin D3 administration demonstrated superior efficacy compared to therapeutic dosing in preserving esophageal mucosal integrity, restoring autonomic function parameters (SDNN, HF, LF, and LF/HF ratio), and preventing histological damage including basal cell hyperplasia and lymphocytic infiltration. Correlation analyses revealed significant negative associations between IL-6 levels and heart rate variability indices, establishing the mechanistic link between inflammatory cytokine suppression and autonomic restoration. These findings suggest vitamin D3's protective effects stem from comprehensive immune modulation and restoration of gut-brain signaling, making it a promising preventive therapeutic for reflux-associated diseases.

title: The Role of Inflammatory Factors in the Pathogenesis of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus and May Be Potential Biomarkers for Its Diagnosis and Prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies inflammatory cytokines as key pathogenic factors and potential biomarkers in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM). Significantly elevated expression of IL-1, IL-6, IL-10, and TNF-α was detected in GDM patients, and specific genetic polymorphisms in these cytokine genes (IL-1β −511 C/T, IL-6 −174 G/C, TNF-α −308 G/A, and IL-10 −1082 G/A) were associated with GDM risk, with certain genotypes producing higher cytokine levels. Among these inflammatory markers, IL-6 demonstrated the highest diagnostic accuracy for GDM detection (AUC = 0.849), while IL-1β and IL-10 showed moderate diagnostic value, suggesting their utility as non-invasive screening tools for GDM risk stratification. The underlying mechanism involves inflammation-induced insulin resistance, wherein TNF-α and related inflammatory factors impair insulin sensitivity through multiple signaling pathways. These findings underscore the critical role of the inflammatory immune response in GDM pathogenesis and support the clinical application of inflammatory cytokines as diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers for improved early detection and risk assessment.

title: Identification of CXCL8 as a potential gene associated with lymph node metastasis in papillary thyroid carcinoma through bioinformatics analysis
summary: This bioinformatics study identifies CXCL8 (interleukin-8) as a significantly elevated chemokine in papillary thyroid carcinoma that strongly correlates with lymph node metastasis and adverse clinicopathological features. CXCL8 promotes tumor progression through multiple mechanisms, including enhancing cell proliferation, migration, and invasion via activation of the PI3K-Akt, RAF/MAPK/ERK, and NF-κB signaling pathways following binding to CXCR1 and CXCR2 receptors. Pan-cancer analysis reveals CXCL8 upregulation across numerous malignancies including endometrioid cancer, establishing its broad oncogenic relevance. The immunological profile shows that elevated CXCL8 associates with infiltration of immunosuppressive cell populations (activated dendritic cells, neutrophils, M0 macrophages, and mast cells) while inversely correlating with anti-tumor immune cells (CD8+ T cells, M1 macrophages, and plasma cells), suggesting CXCL8-driven immune evasion. In endometriosis, CXCL8 secretion from endometrial stromal cells impairs natural killer cell cytotoxic activity, linking this inflammatory chemokine to disease pathogenesis. These findings position CXCL8 as a potential therapeutic target and prognostic biomarker, with implications for immunotherapy selection in CXCL8-low versus CXCL8-high tumors.

title: Emerging roles of natural killer cell ligands—HLA-E, HLA-F, HLA-G, MICA, and MICB—in in vitro fertilization outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how non-classical HLA molecules and their natural killer cell ligands regulate maternal-fetal tolerance and reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction. HLA-G, the primary focus, exerts immunomodulatory effects by binding inhibitory receptors (ILT2, ILT4, KIR2DL4) on immune cells and stimulating anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β) and angiogenic factors (VEGF), thereby promoting implantation and pregnancy success. Elevated soluble HLA-G levels correlate with successful implantation and ongoing pregnancies, while reduced HLA-G expression is associated with preeclampsia, recurrent implantation failure, and miscarriage; the HLA-G 14-bp insertion allele impairs mRNA stability and is linked to recurrent IVF failure, whereas the deletion allele enhances expression and improves outcomes. Maternal KIR genotype interactions with fetal HLA-C are critical—the KIR AA genotype with HLA-C2 fetuses suppresses angiogenesis and increases preeclampsia and implantation failure risk, while KIR B haplotypes with HLA-C2 promote higher pregnancy rates through enhanced NK cell cytokine secretion. Complementary immunological strategies, including corticosteroids, tacrolimus, and granulocyte-colony stimulating factor, show promise in improving IVF outcomes by enhancing endometrial receptivity and modulating immune tolerance, particularly in patients with immunological dysfunction.

title: The pivotal role of cytokines in liver disease pathogenesis and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

Cytokines serve as central mediators in liver disease pathogenesis, regulating inflammation, cell differentiation, proliferation, and apoptosis through multiple immune pathways. The inflammasome complex, activated by danger signals, promotes maturation and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-18, and IL-37 by activating caspase-1, ultimately triggering cell pyroptosis and contributing to aseptic inflammatory diseases and cancer development. Dendritic cells, lymphocytes, and macrophages collectively drive systemic inflammatory responses, with macrophages generating reactive oxygen species and secreting inflammatory mediators, while elevated levels of IL-6, IL-8, and IL-10 perpetuate inflammation. Multiple pathogenic factors including hepatitis B and C virus infection, alcohol abuse, and obesity promote hepatocellular carcinoma through chronic hepatitis-induced DNA injury and genomic mutations, with endothelin-1 driving hepatic stellate cell proliferation that leads to liver fibrosis and cirrhosis. TNF and IL-6 promote tumor cell survival and angiogenesis, while IL-10, IL-4, and TGF-β induce immunosuppression; conversely, IFN-γ, IL-2, IL-12, and IL-15 enhance lymphocyte cytotoxicity to suppress tumor proliferation, highlighting the dual role of cytokines in disease progression versus immune control.

title: Dynamics of Macrophage Polarization Reveal Unique Phenotypes During Mouse Postpartum Uterine Remodeling
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates the dynamic polarization of macrophages during postpartum uterine remodeling in mice, revealing a complex temporal orchestration of immune responses essential for tissue repair and involution. Macrophages exhibit distinct phenotypic shifts across the postpartum period: an initial M2-dominant anti-inflammatory state on postpartum day 1 (with a notable CD86+CD206+ hybrid subset) facilitates epithelial repair and barrier restoration, while subsequent polarization toward M1 pro-inflammatory macrophages on days 3-14 promotes host defense and tissue remodeling through production of matrix metalloproteinases and elastase. The study identifies spatially distinct macrophage populations, including F4/80+ macrophages in subepithelial regions supporting antimicrobial defense and debris clearance, and CD206+ cells at the myometrial-endometrial junction involved in neovascularization and smooth muscle regeneration. Notably, a previously unreported secondary peak in M2-like macrophages appears on postpartum day 21, suggesting ongoing tissue restoration. The research highlights that dysregulated macrophage polarization, as demonstrated in RBPJ-deficient mice with abnormally elevated M1 activation and impaired uterine repair, can lead to pathological outcomes, suggesting macrophage balance is critical for preventing postpartum complications.

title: Immunomodulatory Mechanisms and Therapeutic Potential of Vitamin D in Immune Thrombocytopenia
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D functions as a critical immunomodulatory agent in immune thrombocytopenia (ITP), extending far beyond its classical role in calcium-phosphate metabolism. The active metabolite 1,25(OH)2D3 exerts potent anti-autoimmune effects by suppressing B-cell proliferation and differentiation, reducing autoantibody (IgG and IgM) production, and promoting B-lymphocyte apoptosis, while simultaneously shifting T-cell responses from pro-inflammatory Th1 toward regulatory Th2 phenotypes. Vitamin D deficiency is frequently observed in ITP patients and strongly correlates with increased disease severity, elevated bleeding scores, and reduced platelet counts, with serum 25(OH)D3 levels inversely associated with platelet-surface autoantibodies and complement deposition. The immunomodulatory pathway operates through vitamin D receptor (VDR) signaling, which regulates expression of key regulators like BAFF and APRIL that perpetuate B-cell activation and autoimmunity in ITP. Clinical evidence demonstrates that vitamin D supplementation, either alone or synergistically combined with glucocorticoids, reduces pathogenic mediators and improves disease outcomes, suggesting therapeutic potential in restoring immune homeostasis in this autoimmune disorder.

title: Engineered cellular drug delivery: Strategies and applications
summary:
# Summary

This review examines engineered cellular drug delivery systems that exploit immune cells as carriers for therapeutic agents, with particular relevance to inflammatory diseases like endometriosis. The CD47-SIRPα axis on red blood cells and platelets enables prolonged circulation and immune evasion, allowing these cells to deliver drugs while evading macrophage-mediated clearance. Immune cells including macrophages, neutrophils, natural killer cells, and T cells have been engineered to enhance anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor functions—for example, M1-to-M2 macrophage transformation via AMPK activation restores immune homeostasis, while N1 neutrophil-associated cells kill pathogenic cells through myeloperoxidase and reactive oxygen species. CAR-engineered T and NK cells have been designed to precisely target cancer antigens and cross the blood-brain barrier, carrying cytotoxic drugs like paclitaxel and cisplatin. These cellular drug delivery platforms represent innovative strategies to modulate the inflammatory immune microenvironment and combat diseases characterized by dysregulated immune responses, including endometriosis-associated inflammation.

title: SLC25A1-Mediated Cholesterol Accumulation Promotes Endometriosis Progression by Enhancing Endometrial Stromal Cell Proliferation, Invasion, and M2 Macrophage Polarization
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that SLC25A1, a transporter upregulated in ectopic endometrial lesions, drives endometriosis progression by promoting cholesterol accumulation. SLC25A1-mediated increases in intracellular and extracellular cholesterol enhance ectopic endometrial stromal cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while simultaneously activating the STAT6/PPARγ signaling pathway to polarize macrophages toward the immunosuppressive M2 phenotype. This M2 polarization creates a permissive immune microenvironment that further promotes aggressive behavior of ectopic cells through reciprocal paracrine interactions. Elevated cholesterol also serves as a precursor for local estrogen synthesis via the aromatase system in ectopic stromal cells, establishing a metabolic-immune axis that perpetuates the chronic inflammatory disorder. Pharmacological inhibition of STAT6 suppresses both cholesterol-driven M2 polarization and stromal cell aggression, suggesting that targeting the SLC25A1-cholesterol-STAT6/PPARγ axis may represent a therapeutic strategy for endometriosis. These findings mechanistically link metabolic dysregulation and immune dysfunction as dual drivers of endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cell-Derived Extracellular Vesicles in Alzheimer’s Disease: A Novel Cell-Free Therapeutic Strategy and Diagnostic Biomarker
summary:
# Summary

This review examines Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis and the therapeutic potential of mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles (EVs), with particular emphasis on inflammatory mechanisms. The article establishes that amyloid-beta (Aβ) initiates a cascade of neuronal damage by directly causing neuronal injury, activating microglia and astrocytes, and promoting tau hyperphosphorylation through aberrant kinases GSK-3β and CDK5. Aβ oligomers bind NMDA receptors triggering calcium influx and oxidative stress, while hyperphosphorylated tau impairs axonal transport and forms neurofibrillary tangles. Chronically activated disease-associated microglia perpetuate inflammation by producing TNF-α, IL-1β, and IFN-γ, which enhance APP processing and trigger nitric oxide production that facilitates peptide aggregation. The article identifies multiple microRNAs within circulating EVs—including elevated miR-23a-3p, miR-106b-5p, and miR-485-3p, and reduced miR-100-3p—as potential biomarkers for AD diagnosis. Finally, synaptic proteins (GAP43, SNAP25, synaptotagmin-1) and phosphorylated tau in plasma-derived small EVs emerge as diagnostic indicators, suggesting that EV-based biomarkers represent a non-invasive strategy for early AD detection and monitoring.

title: From menses to ovulation: the immunomodulatory role of monocyte cytokines in menstrual cycle physiology and reproductive health – a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Monocytes serve as critical regulators of reproductive immunology by secreting diverse cytokines that coordinate menstrual cycle physiology and reproductive health. During the follicular phase, rising estrogen levels stimulate monocytes to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) that mediate endometrial breakdown and tissue remodeling, while also recruiting immune cells and promoting angiogenesis. Conversely, during the luteal phase, progesterone shifts the monocyte cytokine profile toward anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10, TGF-β) that promote tissue healing and immune tolerance necessary for embryo implantation. Dysregulation of this hormone-dependent monocyte cytokine balance contributes to reproductive pathologies including endometriosis, where excess TNF-α and IL-6 drive chronic inflammation and tissue damage, as well as polycystic ovary syndrome and recurrent pregnancy loss. This review establishes monocyte cytokine secretion as a central mechanism linking hormonal fluctuations to immune regulation across the menstrual cycle and highlights how aberrant monocyte activation underlies key reproductive disorders.

title: MIS-C pathogenesis: immune dysregulation & viral triggers
summary: Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children (MIS-C) emerges as a severe post-infectious complication following SARS-CoV-2 exposure, characterized by dysregulated hyperinflammatory responses affecting multiple organ systems. The condition presents with persistent fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, rash, and life-threatening cardiac and neurological manifestations, accompanied by markedly elevated inflammatory biomarkers including C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, ferritin, and cardiac troponins. MIS-C pathogenesis involves a complex interplay of autoimmune mechanisms, including expansion of cross-reactive T cells (TCRVβ21.3+ CD8+ cells expanded in 82% of patients) targeting EBV epitopes, elevated HLA risk alleles, and production of autoantibodies against endothelial and cardiac antigens that drive multi-organ damage. SARS-CoV-2 appears to facilitate this autoimmune dysregulation by evading interferon responses and impairing transforming growth factor-beta signaling, thereby enabling unrestricted EBV reactivation and autoreactive lymphocyte expansion via molecular mimicry mechanisms. Excessive Toll-like and RIG-I-like receptor activation generates cytokine storms dominated by interleukin-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β, while impaired regulatory T cell function fails to restrain the inflammatory cascade. Current management with intravenous immunoglobulin and corticosteroids targets these dysregulated immune responses, though the underlying mechanisms linking viral exposure to autoimmune activation require further investigation.

title: Natural Killer (NK) Cell-Based Therapies Have the Potential to Treat Ovarian Cancer Effectively by Targeting Diverse Tumor Populations and Reducing the Risk of Recurrence
summary:
# Summary

Natural killer cell-based therapies represent a promising approach for treating ovarian cancer by targeting heterogeneous tumor populations, including cancer stem cells and differentiated tumors, thereby reducing recurrence risk. The article identifies multiple factors compromising current ovarian cancer treatment efficacy, including late diagnosis, dense stromal tissue, hypoxia, and an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment dominated by regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, and macrophages that produce inhibitory cytokines like IL-10 and TGF-β. Notably, ovarian cancer patients exhibit reduced NK cell numbers and impaired function in both peripheral blood and tumors, with tumor cells actively evading NK recognition through mechanisms such as MICA/B shedding and metabolic acidification via lactic acid production. Endometriosis, BRCA1/2 mutations, TP53 variants, oxidative stress, and inflammation emerge as significant risk factors for ovarian cancer development. Engineered NK cell therapies bypass these immunosuppressive barriers and can kill both stem-like and differentiated cancer cells, while conventional treatments like PARP inhibitors and immune checkpoint inhibitors offer complementary approaches. The article emphasizes that NK cell-based immunotherapy addresses current diagnostic and therapeutic limitations in ovarian cancer management.

title: Structure-Guided Design of Cyclic Peptide: A Potent Inhibitor Targeting PD-1/PD-L1 Axis with Antitumor Activity
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the rational design and development of PD-1-0520, a potent cyclic peptide inhibitor of the PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint axis with significant antitumor activity. PD-1-0520 effectively blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction through structure-guided optimization, whereby its tyrosine residue forms π-π stacking interactions with PD-1's Ile134, while aspartic acid and lysine establish electrostatic contacts with key residues Glu136 and Gln75. The peptide's cyclic architecture provides superior proteolytic stability and tissue penetration compared to linear peptides, enabling prolonged target engagement and sustained immune activation. In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrate that PD-1-0520 restores CD8+ T cell infiltration and cytotoxic function by upregulating activation markers and immune cytokines including granzyme B, interferon-γ, IL-2, and TNF-α, ultimately achieving a 68% tumor inhibition rate in mouse xenograft models. Notably, PD-1-0520 exerts these antitumor effects without causing systemic toxicity or immune-related adverse events observed with traditional monoclonal antibody checkpoint inhibitors, suggesting a safer immunotherapeutic profile for cancer treatment.

title: Unveiling the Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of Inflammatory Cytokines in Preeclampsia: A Review of Ιnterleukins IL-15, IL-16, IL-17 and IL-35
summary:
# Summary

This review examines four inflammatory cytokines—IL-15, IL-16, IL-17, and IL-35—as key mediators in preeclampsia pathogenesis with diagnostic and prognostic potential. Pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-15, IL-16, and IL-17 are consistently elevated in preeclampsia and correlate with disease severity, with IL-16 stimulating production of additional pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1α) through monocyte activation, while IL-17 secreted by Th17 cells promotes neutrophil recruitment and activation. Conversely, IL-35, an immunosuppressive cytokine produced by placental trophoblasts and regulatory T cells, is significantly reduced in preeclampsia and normally functions to inhibit T cell proliferation and suppress Th17 cell development. The dysregulation of this inflammatory milieu—characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines and deficient immunosuppressive responses—contributes to the abnormal angiogenic profiles and placental dysfunction observed in preeclampsia. These four cytokines emerge as promising biomarkers for preeclampsia diagnosis, prognosis, and disease surveillance, reflecting their central involvement in the immunopathology of this pregnancy complication.

title: Relationship Between the Levels of 91 Circulating Inflammatory Proteins and Ovarian-Related Diseases: A Bidirectional Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This bidirectional Mendelian randomization study systematically evaluated 91 circulating inflammatory proteins to identify causal relationships with ovarian-related diseases. The analysis revealed distinct inflammatory signatures across different pathologies: protective proteins including CCL4, PD-L1, and TRAIL reduced ovarian cyst risk, while elevated IL-6 and CD6 increased cyst susceptibility. For polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), the inflammatory profile diverged markedly, with IL-6 and IL-7 conferring protection despite PCOS itself elevating IL-17C, TNFB, and CD244 levels. Pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 emerged as key drivers of epithelial ovarian cancer development and progression. Additionally, ovarian diseases exhibited bidirectional inflammatory effects, with ovarian cysts and benign tumors triggering increased production of specific inflammatory mediators such as neurotrophin-3 and FGF-19. These findings establish inflammation as a central mechanistic contributor to ovarian pathology and identify specific inflammatory proteins as potential therapeutic targets or biomarkers for disease prevention and management across the spectrum of ovarian-related conditions.

title: Transcriptome profiling of peripheral blood mononuclear cells from highly susceptible adult cattle infected with a virulent strain of Babesia bovis
summary: Babesia bovis infection triggers acute bovine babesiosis through intraerythrocytic parasitism and robust activation of innate and adaptive immune responses in peripheral blood mononuclear cells. Pattern recognition receptors including toll-like receptors, CD14, and NOD2 recognize Babesia antigens and stimulate monocytes and natural killer cells to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-12, IL-18) and interferon-gamma, which activate macrophages for parasite killing. The transcriptome analysis reveals coordinated upregulation of complement components, cell adhesion molecules, and pattern recognition receptors alongside elevated apoptosis markers, indicating simultaneous immune activation and cellular stress. Paradoxically, elevated expression of IL-10 together with inflammatory cytokines is associated with non-protective immune responses during acute infection, suggesting that this particular cytokine profile fails to control parasitemia in susceptible animals. Dendritic cells present Babesia antigens to initiate adaptive immunity, leading to B cell production of IgG1 and IgG2 antibodies, while memory CD4+ T cells become central to persistent infection control. Significantly altered expression of TNIP3, ADGRE2, CACNA, and other genes distinguishes infected animals, identifying potential biomarkers for disease susceptibility.

title: Multiplexed iPSC platform for advanced NK cell immunotherapies
summary:
# Multiplexed iPSC Platform for Advanced NK Cell Immunotherapies

This article describes a comprehensive approach to generating natural killer (NK) cells from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) for cancer immunotherapy, addressing limitations of conventional NK cell sources. The study emphasizes that NK cells mediate anti-tumor immunity through multiple mechanisms: direct cytolysis of tumor cells lacking HLA-I, secretion of interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) to activate dendritic cells and macrophages, and immune-modulation that can convert immunologically "cold" tumors to "hot" tumors. IL-15 signaling through JAK/STAT, PI3K, and Ras/Raf pathways drives NK cell survival and proliferation, with distinct CD56bright and CD56dim subsets exhibiting different functional roles: CD56bright cells display stronger cytokine production and longer telomeres in lymphoid tissues, while CD56dim cells predominate in peripheral blood with superior cytotoxic capacity and natural killer activity. iPSC-derived NK cells overcome challenges of primary source inconsistency and cord blood immaturity, and genetic modifications including CISH/GSK3 deletion, TGFRBII disruption, and CCR expression enhance metabolic fitness, tumor infiltration, and resistance to immunosuppression. Cytokine-induced memory-like (CIML) NK cells generated through IL-12/IL-15/IL-18 stimulation demonstrate enhanced persistence, high IFN-γ secretion, and superior anti-tumor activity, suggesting this platform offers a promising off-the-shelf approach for scalable NK cell immunotherapy with reduced toxicity compared to CAR-T cell alternatives.

title: Temporal dynamics of uterine immune microenvironment remodeling in a murine model of adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is a debilitating gynecological condition characterized by uterine immune dysregulation, leading to pain, abnormal bleeding, and subfertility through mechanisms involving progesterone resistance and reduced endometrial receptivity. Using a tamoxifen-induced murine adenomyosis model, this study reveals dynamic temporal remodeling of the uterine immune microenvironment, marked by early macrophage depletion with skewing toward M2-like polarization, persistent elevation of interleukin-6 (IL-6) mRNA alongside transient upregulation of IL-10, and consistent enrichment of B cells with redistribution of T-cell subsets favoring CD4+ and CD8+ populations over double-negative cells. The loss of classically activated M1-like macrophages at advanced disease stages, combined with sustained IL-6 signaling despite paradoxical downregulation of IL-6 receptor and STAT3 expression at early timepoints, suggests a dysregulated inflammatory state where M2-polarized macrophages promote tissue remodeling and fibrosis while M1 macrophages—primary producers of pro-inflammatory IL-6—are selectively depleted. These findings implicate IL-6-mediated pathways as critical drivers of adenomyosis pathology and identify immune dysregulation as a key therapeutic target, with potential for developing immunomodulatory interventions and biomarkers to address the underlying inflammatory mechanisms rather than solely treating symptomatic manifestations.

title: Large Peritoneal Macrophages Promote the Resolution of Inflammation in Injured Endometrium
summary:
# Summary: Large Peritoneal Macrophages in Endometrial Repair and Inflammation Resolution

Large peritoneal macrophages (LPMs) play a critical role in promoting the resolution of inflammation and tissue repair following endometrial injury. LPMs rapidly infiltrate damaged endometrial tissue within 6 hours post-injury, where they perform essential functions including phagocytosis of apoptotic and dead cells, clearance of cellular debris, and mitigation of inflammatory responses. The hormone 17β-estradiol (E2) significantly enhances LPM migration to the injured endometrium and accelerates their reparative capabilities, suggesting a hormone-immune axis regulating tissue regeneration. Depletion of LPMs using clodronate liposomes impairs this reparative response, resulting in elevated systemic inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and accumulation of dead cells in the endometrium. These findings establish LPMs as essential mediators that prevent pathologic outcomes such as intrauterine adhesions and thin endometrium, which commonly result from procedures like curettage and contribute to infertility. Therapeutically, E2 administration following uterine injury may enhance LPM-mediated repair and prevent fibrotic regeneration of the endometrium.

title: SLC16A3 in lung adenocarcinoma regulates glycolysis and lactate release to facilitate M2 polarization of tumor-associated macrophages
summary:
# Summary

SLC16A3 (MCT4), a lactate transporter significantly elevated in lung adenocarcinoma, promotes tumor glycolysis and lactate release, which subsequently facilitates M2 polarization of tumor-associated macrophages through multiple signaling pathways. Tumor-derived lactate activates PKM2 acetylation in macrophages and engages ERK/STAT3 signaling to drive M2 polarization, enhancing the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. The resulting M2-type macrophages reinforce tumor growth, angiogenesis, and immune suppression by secreting anti-inflammatory cytokines including IL-10 and TGF-β. Functional studies demonstrate that SLC16A3 knockdown reduces cell viability, migration, and invasion while increasing apoptosis, and diminishes both lactate release and extracellular acidification rates in tumor cells. These findings establish SLC16A3 as a critical metabolic regulator linking tumor glycolysis to immune-mediated tumor progression, with potential therapeutic implications for targeting the metabolic-immunological axis in lung adenocarcinoma.

title: Chemokines in Pregnancy
summary: Chemokines function as critical regulators of reproductive physiology, orchestrating cell migration and immune tolerance throughout pregnancy by maintaining precise spatiotemporal expression patterns. The CXCL12/CXCR4 axis emerges as particularly important, activating multiple signaling cascades (PI3K/AKT, MAPK, and JAK/STAT pathways) to promote endometrial receptivity, decidualization, vascular remodeling, and natural killer cell recruitment. Beyond CXCL12, CXCL16 promotes decidualization while chemokines like CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11 recruit activated lymphocytes and may trigger abortion if dysregulated, whereas CCL5 and CXCL8 enhance trophoblast invasion and facilitate immunotolerance through regulatory T-cell recruitment and M2 macrophage polarization. Additionally, chemokines including CXCL1 and CX3CL1 drive angiogenesis and extravillous trophoblast migration essential for placental vascularization. Dysregulation of this chemokine system increases susceptibility to inflammatory disorders and pregnancy complications including preeclampsia and preterm birth, directly linking chemokine dysfunction to endometriosis pathogenesis and reproductive failure.

title: Single-Cell Analysis Links C7+ Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts with Incomplete Resection in Platinum-Sensitive Relapsed Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a distinct cancer-associated fibroblast subpopulation, CAF-C7, that is selectively enriched in platinum-sensitive relapsed ovarian cancer patients with incomplete tumor resection (non-R0 status). CAF-C7 cells, characterized by high expression of CD34, C7, FGF7, and IGF1, promote tumor cell migration, suppress anoikis, and facilitate angiogenesis primarily through the IGF1-IGF1R signaling axis. Inhibition of the IGF1-IGF1R pathway effectively attenuates these pro-tumorigenic functions, suggesting a potential therapeutic target to improve surgical resectability and reduce recurrence risk. Beyond CAF-C7, the broader cancer-associated fibroblast population contributes to disease progression through secretion of multiple cytokines including TGF-β, IL-6, and LIF, along with remodeling the extracellular matrix and modulating the immune microenvironment. These findings establish fibroblast composition as a critical determinant of surgical outcome in ovarian cancer and identify the IGF1-IGF1R axis as a druggable mechanism linking stromal biology to therapeutic resistance and incomplete resection.

title: Advancing Drug Repurposing for Rheumatoid Arthritis: Integrating Protein–Protein Interaction, Molecular Docking, and Dynamics Simulations for Targeted Therapeutic Approaches
summary:
# Summary

This study employed protein-protein interaction analysis, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify drug repurposing candidates for rheumatoid arthritis, a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by progressive joint destruction driven by synovial inflammation. The research identified five central therapeutic targets—TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, STAT3, and AKT1—which are key inflammatory mediators and signaling molecules in RA pathogenesis. Through computational screening, four existing drugs emerged as the most promising repurposing candidates: rifampicin (binding TNF-α), telmisartan (binding IL-6), danazol (binding IL-1β), and pimozide (binding STAT3), all demonstrating strong binding affinities validated by molecular dynamics simulations. These findings complement established RA therapeutics including TNF-α inhibitors, IL-6 receptor antagonists, JAK-STAT pathway inhibitors, and immunomodulatory agents, offering potential cost-effective treatment alternatives through drug repurposing.

title: Deciphering the Role of Mast Cells in HPV-Related Cancers
summary: Human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly HPV16 and HPV18, is the causative agent in multiple cancers including cervical, anal, penile, vaginal, vulvar, and head and neck cancers, with HPV-positive tumors characterized by higher immune cell infiltration and pro-inflammatory mediators despite lower COX2 levels. The viral E6, E7, and E5 oncoproteins drive tumorigenesis through multiple mechanisms: E6 degrades p53 to promote cellular immortalization and block apoptosis, E7 inactivates retinoblastoma protein and CDK inhibitors to drive cell cycle progression, and E5 activates EGFR signaling while suppressing antiviral interferon responses through interaction with STING and MAVS. Critically, both E6 and E7 antagonize innate immunity by downregulating TLR-9 expression and inhibiting interferon-α and interferon-β production through epigenetic and signaling mechanisms. Mast cells, abundant in the tumor microenvironment, play a pivotal but context-dependent role in HPV-related cancers, as increased mast cell infiltration correlates with worse survival in cervical cancer but improved prognosis in breast carcinoma, suggesting tumor-type specific immune modulatory functions. The degree of intra-tumoral immune cell infiltration in HPV-positive head and neck cancers strongly predicts improved therapeutic response and favorable clinical outcomes, highlighting the critical importance of immune landscape characterization in predicting treatment success.

title: Targeting Ovarian Neoplasms: Subtypes and Therapeutic Options
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the molecular pathways and immune mechanisms underlying ovarian neoplasms, with emphasis on therapeutic targets and inflammatory responses. Key genetic alterations characterize distinct ovarian cancer subtypes: TP53 mutations predominate in high-grade serous carcinomas (>90%), KRAS mutations drive low-grade serous tumors, and SMARCA4 alterations define small-cell carcinomas. The tumor microenvironment employs multiple immunosuppressive mechanisms, including prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) and transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β) signaling that inhibit dendritic cell function and upregulate immune checkpoints like PD-L1, while fatty acid-rich ascites activate pro-tumoral metabolic pathways and suppress antitumoral immunity. Therapeutic strategies focus on activating the cGAS–STING cytosolic DNA-sensing pathway through PARP inhibition, RNA polymerase I inhibitors, or enhanced manganese availability, which promotes type I interferon production and CD8+ T-cell priming for enhanced tumor control. Additionally, promoting M1-like macrophage differentiation through interferon-γ and tumor necrosis factor-α signaling, combined with DC-based nano-vaccines, represents promising approaches to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor landscape in ovarian cancer.

title: Mesenchymal Stromal/Stem Cells (MSCs) in Cancer Therapy: Advanced Therapeutic Strategies Towards Future Clinical Translation
summary:
# Summary

Mesenchymal stromal/stem cells (MSCs) represent a promising therapeutic avenue for cancer treatment through their inherent ability to migrate toward tumors and modulate the immune microenvironment. MSCs possess key immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive properties while expressing characteristic surface markers (CD29, CD44, CD90, CD105) and can be isolated from diverse tissue sources including bone marrow, adipose tissue, and umbilical cord. The therapeutic potential of MSCs is enhanced through genetic modifications enabling expression of anticancer proteins and microRNAs, addressing the systemic toxicity limitations of conventional chemotherapy. MSC homing to tumors involves complex chemokine signaling cascades including SDF-1/CXCR4, IL-6/STAT3, and PDGF/PI3K-Akt pathways, coupled with matrix metalloproteinase-mediated transmigration across endothelial barriers. However, MSCs exhibit a dual role in cancer biology, as they can both suppress tumors through antitumor and anti-inflammatory effects while simultaneously promoting tumor progression by differentiating into cancer-associated fibroblasts, adipocytes, and cancer stem cells. This complex pathophysiology underscores the critical importance of rational MSC engineering strategies for advancing MSC-based therapies toward clinical translation in cancer treatment.

title: Ginger Moxibustion Activates TRPV1 to Modulate the Locus Coeruleus-Spinal Cord-Uterus Pathway and Alleviates Primary Dysmenorrhea in Rats
summary:
# Summary

Ginger moxibustion alleviates primary dysmenorrhea in rats by activating the transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) channel at acupoints, which upregulates substance P and triggers a coordinated neuroimmune response through the locus coeruleus-spinal cord-uterus pathway. The treatment increases norepinephrine (NE) levels in the locus coeruleus and spinal cord while decreasing uterine NE, thereby activating beta-2 adrenergic receptors (β2-AR) that promote anti-inflammatory M2-type macrophage polarization and suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α). Pharmacological blockade of TRPV1 with capsazepine partially reversed these effects, confirming TRPV1's central role in pain reduction and inflammatory modulation. The treatment significantly reduced pain scores and elevated pain thresholds comparable to ibuprofen, while simultaneously dampening the elevated NE in uterine tissue and restoring the balance of pro- and anti-inflammatory mediators characteristic of dysmenorrhea. These findings establish ginger moxibustion as an effective non-pharmacological approach targeting inflammation and sympathetic nervous system dysregulation in primary dysmenorrhea.

title: Association between monocyte percentage and chronic endometritis among infertile patients: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study identified an inverse association between peripheral blood monocyte percentage and chronic endometritis (CE) among infertile patients, demonstrating that decreased monocyte levels correlate with increased CE risk. Chronic endometritis, characterized by abnormal plasma cell infiltration into the endometrium (detected via CD138 expression), is strongly associated with infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and recurrent implantation failure. The pathophysiology of CE involves dysregulated immune responses, including elevated Th17 cells, reduced anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), and decreased chemokine expression (CCL4, MIP-1β) that impair macrophage and natural killer cell recruitment. Monocyte percentage may serve as a non-invasive peripheral blood biomarker for CE diagnosis, offering clinical advantage over invasive endometrial biopsy for detecting subclinical inflammation, while additional risk factors include endometrial polypoid hyperplasia and follicular phase timing. The findings suggest that monocytes represent an early stage of endometrial inflammatory infiltration, with their differentiation into macrophages and subsequent B lymphocyte activation driving progression toward plasma cell-mediated CE.

title: Evaluating the predictive value of systemic inflammation indices in early pregnancy loss: a retrospective observational study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study investigates how systemic inflammation markers predict early pregnancy loss, revealing that inappropriate inflammatory responses contribute significantly to miscarriage pathogenesis. Women experiencing early pregnancy loss demonstrated notably reduced counts of white blood cells, neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, and platelets compared to controls, with the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) emerging as the strongest independent predictor among systemic immune-inflammation indices examined. While mild inflammation is necessary for normal implantation and endometrial vascular remodeling, excessive systemic or local inflammation disrupts this delicate balance and promotes pregnancy loss through mechanisms involving progesterone-stimulated decidual immune cells and inflammatory cytokine production. The study suggests that systemic inflammation indices, particularly NLR, may serve as practical biomarkers for identifying women at risk for early pregnancy loss, though the authors note that systemic inflammation alone is not strongly associated with miscarriage, indicating multiple pathogenic pathways beyond inflammation including chromosomal abnormalities and placental dysfunction. These findings support the concept that pregnancy requires a controlled pro-inflammatory microenvironment, and dysregulation of this immune balance represents a key mechanism in early pregnancy loss development.

title: The Effect of HIF‐2α on the Development of Inflammation
summary:
# Summary

HIF-2α, a hypoxia-inducible transcription factor composed of alpha and beta subunits, functions as a critical regulator of cellular adaptation to hypoxia and modulates cellular metabolism, immune cell function, and inflammation through context-dependent mechanisms. Under normoxic conditions, prolyl hydroxylase domain proteins (PHDs) hydroxylate HIF-2α proline residues, targeting it for VHL-mediated ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation; however, under hypoxic conditions, stabilized HIF-2α heterodimerizes and binds hypoxia response elements to induce proinflammatory cytokines and regulate immune responses. HIF-2α exhibits dual roles in inflammation—it can both promote and inhibit proinflammatory responses depending on cell type and tissue microenvironment, with evidence demonstrating its involvement in cytokine expression, macrophage migration, neutrophil survival, and the regulation of matrix metalloproteinases in conditions ranging from osteoarthritis to colon tumorigenesis. Additionally, HIF-2α controls metabolic processes including erythropoietin production, lipid metabolism, iron absorption through upregulation of iron transport proteins (DMT1, DCYTB, FPN), angiogenesis, and glycolysis. The pathway can be activated when VHL is knocked down, leading to HIF-2α accumulation and associated pathological outcomes including hepatic steatosis, erythrocytosis, and vascular tumorigenesis, while HIF-2α stabilization via aquaporin-1 demonstrates protective effects in ischemia-reperfusion injury through enhanced endothelial integrity.

title: Endometriosis as an immune-mediated disease: pathogenetic mechanisms and therapeutic strategies
summary: Endometriosis is characterized as an immune-mediated disease driven by inadequate immune clearance of retrograde menstrual tissue, allowing ectopic endometrial cells to establish themselves through multiple immune evasion mechanisms. The disease involves comprehensive disruption of both innate and adaptive immunity, including macrophage dysfunction with progressive shift from pro-inflammatory M1 to immunosuppressive M2 phenotype, reduced natural killer cell cytotoxicity, elevated neutrophil infiltration that resists apoptosis, and aberrant B and T cell responses characterized by increased Th2 differentiation and regulatory T cells. Endometriotic lesions actively recruit immune cells by secreting chemokines (MCP-1, IL-8, RANTES, fractalkine) that promote monocyte infiltration and M2 macrophage differentiation, while simultaneously producing elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) alongside immunosuppressive mediators (IL-10, TGF-β). The complement system contributes to pathogenesis through C3a/C5a-mediated inflammation and angiogenesis, while specific endometriosis subtypes show distinct immunological features, including activation of IDO1/COX-2/MMP-9 signaling in deep infiltrating lesions. These immune dysregulations collectively manifest as clinical symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, establishing endometriosis as a complex inflammatory condition requiring targeted immunological therapeutic strategies.

title: Therapeutic targeting of interleukin-17C signaling in carcinogenesis of endometriosis
summary: This study identifies IL-17C signaling through its receptor IL-17RE as a critical driver of endometriosis-associated ovarian carcinoma (EAOC) development. IL-17C is significantly elevated in the peritoneal fluid of endometriosis patients and correlates with cancer progression, including tumor size, stage, and patient survival outcomes. IL-17C activates the IL-17RE/PI3K signaling pathway in endometriotic epithelial cells, promoting epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) through suppression of E-cadherin and upregulation of N-cadherin and vimentin, thereby enhancing cell proliferation, migration, and invasion capacity. IL-17C knockout substantially reduces ectopic lesion growth and peritoneal IL-17C concentrations in endometriosis models. Therapeutic targeting with MOR106, an IL-17RE antagonist, effectively blocks IL-17C-induced malignant phenotypes including colony formation, migration, and invasion while reversing EMT markers. These findings establish IL-17C/IL-17RE signaling as a therapeutic target for preventing the malignant transformation of endometriosis into ovarian cancer.

title: The roles of the nerve-immune axis in modulating bone regeneration
summary:
# Summary

The nervous system and immune system work together as an integrated axis to regulate bone regeneration through interconnected cellular and molecular mechanisms. The peripheral nervous system, particularly sensory and sympathetic nerve fibers that innervate the periosteum and bone marrow, directly modulate bone regeneration via neuropeptides and neurotransmitters such as calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP). These neuropeptides orchestrate immune responses by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cell migration, promoting macrophage polarization toward anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes, and enhancing tissue-protective cytokine production. M2 macrophage-derived exosomes further amplify regeneration by promoting bone marrow stromal cell osteogenic differentiation through microRNA signaling pathways including miR-142-3p, miR-690, and miR-486-5p, which activate critical osteogenic pathways such as PI3K/AKT and TGF-β/SMAD2. Disruption of innervation impairs this coordinated nerve-immune dialogue, compromising fracture healing and promoting chronic inflammatory states, highlighting the essential role of intact neural-immune crosstalk in successful bone regeneration.

title: Peripheral B cell alteration in patients with premature ovarian insufficiency
summary:
# Summary

Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is characterized by significant alterations in peripheral B cell populations and function that contribute to its autoimmune pathogenesis. POI patients display elevated total CD19+ B cells alongside reduced proportions of memory B cells, plasmablasts, and critically, regulatory B cells (Bregs), particularly the CD19+CD24hiCD27+ and CD19+CD24hiCD38hi subsets. These regulatory B cell populations normally maintain immunological homeostasis through IL-10 production and suppression of proinflammatory cytokines, but their diminished frequencies in POI patients correlate with elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels, indicating impaired ovarian reserve. The B cell dysregulation observed in POI parallels that seen in other autoimmune conditions, with estrogen deficiency potentially contributing to altered B cell maturation, activation, and survival. These findings suggest that B-cell-mediated immune dysregulation, manifesting as reduced regulatory capacity and enhanced autoreactive potential, represents a key mechanism in POI's immune pathogenesis and could offer potential therapeutic targets for disease intervention.

title: Identification and validation of efferocytosis-related biomarkers for the diagnosis of atherosclerosis based on bioinformatics and machine learning
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatics and machine learning study identified five efferocytosis-related biomarkers—TNF, ApoE, NCF1, TREM2, and CHI3L1—that are elevated in atherosclerotic lesions and associated with disease progression. Efferocytosis, the macrophage-mediated clearance of apoptotic cells, is critical for maintaining tissue homeostasis and suppressing inflammation; impaired efferocytosis leads to accumulation of apoptotic cells, necrotic core expansion, and increased plaque vulnerability. The five hub genes were predominantly upregulated in macrophages and enriched in pathways governing the MAPK cascade, cytokine-receptor signaling, phagosomal function, and necroptosis—mechanisms that drive atherosclerotic pathogenesis. Oxidized LDL (ox-LDL) upregulated all five genes and promoted foam cell formation and pro-inflammatory cytokine production, establishing a mechanistic link between lipid oxidation and impaired efferocytosis. These findings suggest that efferocytosis dysfunction represents a central pathogenic mechanism in atherosclerosis, and the identified biomarkers may serve as diagnostic or therapeutic targets for disease detection and intervention, particularly in advanced stages of the disease.

title: Molecular mechanisms of ovarian fibrosis
summary: Repeated ovulation induces chronic inflammation and progressive ovarian fibrosis through activation of multiple signaling pathways, particularly TGF-β/Smad, Wnt/β-catenin, and PI3K/Akt. The inflammatory microenvironment triggers fibroblast hyperproliferation and myofibroblast differentiation via inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) and growth factors (TGF-β, CTGF, PDGF), leading to pathological extracellular matrix accumulation and collagen deposition (types I and III, fibronectin, hyaluronan). This fibrotic remodeling is further perpetuated by disrupted mechanotransduction pathways (integrin-FAK, Hippo-YAP/TAZ) and increased matrix stiffness, which amplifies inflammation and impairs normal cell-cell communication. Ovarian fibrosis is associated with reproductive disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, premature ovarian insufficiency, and potentially elevated ovarian cancer risk. Current therapeutic approaches targeting antifibrotic mechanisms include TGF-β inhibitors, pirfenidone (which upregulates matrix metalloproteinase-13), and emerging stem cell therapies that aim to restore normal extracellular matrix homeostasis and tissue function.

title: Gancao Xiexin Decoction ameliorates recurrent oral ulcers via TNF-α pathway-mediated suppression of oxidative stress
summary: Recurrent oral ulcers involve a complex interplay between oxidative stress and inflammatory responses, with TNF-α signaling playing a central pathogenic role by activating NF-κB and MAPK pathways, upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8), and promoting excessive reactive oxygen species generation through NADPH oxidase activation while simultaneously suppressing antioxidant enzymes. Gancao Xiexin Decoction (GCXXD), a traditional herbal formula containing ten bioactive compounds including berberine, wogonin, luteolin, and liquiritin, ameliorates recurrent oral ulcers by downregulating TNF-α and activating the Keap1-Nrf2 antioxidant signaling pathway, thereby upregulating protective enzymes like HO-1 and NQO1. In ulcer tissues, inflammatory genes (CXCL10, MMP3, MMP9, ICAM1, CASP1) are significantly elevated while protective genes (FLG, IGFL1, CRISP3) are downregulated, disrupting tight junction proteins and mucosal barrier integrity. GCXXD's therapeutic effects operate primarily through suppression of TNF-α-mediated oxidative stress and inflammatory cascades, restoring antioxidant defenses and epithelial barrier function in oral mucosa.

title: Tissue-Resident ILC2s Across Organs: Heterogeneity, Niche Crosstalk, and Shared Regulatory Circuits
summary: Type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) are tissue-resident immune cells distributed across multiple organs including the lung, intestine, pancreas, uterus, and adipose tissue, where they maintain both homeostatic and inflammatory functions. ILC2s are activated by alarmins such as IL-33 and TSLP, as well as neuropeptides like neuromedin U, and respond by producing type 2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) that parallel the responses of Th2 cells, driving both allergic inflammation and tissue remodeling. Beyond inflammation, ILC2-derived cytokines support restorative processes including wound healing, tissue regeneration, and metabolic homeostasis, with IL-13 promoting goblet cell proliferation and mucus production in response to parasitic infection. The activation and migration of tissue-resident ILC2s are regulated by multiple niche factors including gut microbiota composition, mechanical stimulation, stromal cell interactions, and neuroimmune signals such as calcitonin gene-related peptide and norepinephrine-mediated inhibition. ILC2s also produce immunoregulatory mediators like IL-10 and amphiregulin that promote epithelial repair and maintain tissue homeostasis through feedback circuits with local stromal and epithelial cells. These findings reveal ILC2s as critical orchestrators of type 2 immune responses that integrate environmental cues from multiple tissue-resident compartments to balance inflammatory and reparative functions.

title: Regulatory T-cells in Reproductive Medicine: Implications for Endometriosis-Associated Infertility and Implantation Immunity
summary:
# Summary

Regulatory T cells (Tregs) play a central role in maintaining peripheral immune tolerance and supporting successful embryo implantation and placentation through suppression of inflammation, limitation of effector T-cell activity, and regulation of vascular remodeling. In endometriosis, dysfunction and quantitative reduction of Tregs contributes to a pathological cascade of heightened inflammation, aberrant angiogenesis, and progression of ectopic lesions. Disruption of the balance between Th1/Th2/Th17 cells and Tregs is associated with immune-mediated infertility, including recurrent implantation failure and recurrent miscarriage. Preclinical evidence demonstrates that adoptive Treg supplementation and low-dose interleukin-2–induced Treg expansion can suppress endometriosis-like lesion progression and restore immune homeostasis. Assessment of Treg abundance, phenotype, and function in peri-implantation endometrium or peripheral blood may enable stratification of patients with recurrent implantation failure and optimization of embryo transfer timing. These findings suggest that Treg-targeted immunotherapies represent promising interventions for endometriosis-associated infertility and implantation failure in assisted reproductive medicine.

title: Electroacupuncture ameliorates incisional pain via suppressing IL-33 signaling-related macrophage infiltration and ROS overproduction in incised skin
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that electroacupuncture (EA) effectively ameliorates incisional pain by suppressing IL-33 signaling and its downstream inflammatory cascade. Following skin incision, keratinocytes overproduce IL-33, which binds to ST2 receptors on macrophages, promoting their infiltration into injured tissue where they release excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS). This ROS activates TRPA1 channels on sensory neurons, generating pain signals. EA treatment at 2/100 Hz significantly downregulates IL-33 expression, reducing macrophage infiltration and ROS accumulation, thereby alleviating mechanical allodynia and gait impairment in incisional pain model mice. The analgesic effects of EA are specific, as sham treatment provided no benefit, and adoptive macrophage transfer partially reversed EA's protective effects. These findings identify the IL-33/macrophage/ROS/TRPA1 axis as a critical pain pathway and establish EA as a multi-target intervention that suppresses this inflammatory cascade to achieve postoperative analgesia.

title: Molecular Crosstalk Between Intrauterine hCG and Endometrial Receptivity: Signalling Pathways, Immune Modulation, and Translational Perspectives in IVF
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates the multifaceted role of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) in establishing endometrial receptivity during early pregnancy through coordinated signaling across epithelial, stromal, and vascular compartments. hCG promotes epithelial adhesion competence and proliferation via a miR-126-3p-mediated pathway that suppresses PIK3R2 and activates PI3K/Akt/eNOS signaling, ultimately facilitating integrin engagement and pinopode maturation. In the vascular endothelium, hCG strengthens cell-cell cohesion by upregulating adhesion molecules VE-cadherin and CD146, supporting controlled trophoblast invasion and spiral artery remodeling. hCG orchestrates immune tolerance by enhancing regulatory T cells, altering uterine natural killer cell distribution, and recruiting dendritic cells and macrophages while simultaneously upregulating pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-17, VEGF) that support angiogenesis and decidualization. Through ERK1/2-mTOR signaling, hCG modulates the autophagy-apoptosis balance by upregulating autophagy markers (Beclin1, LC3) while inhibiting p62, and induces critical decidualization genes (HOXA10, ITGB3, LIF, FOXO1) across endometrial compartments. These findings provide mechanistic insights into hCG's therapeutic potential for improving implantation success in assisted reproductive technologies, particularly in recurrent implantation failure.

title: Targeting Uterine Quiescence: A Multitarget Strategy with Vitamin D, High Molecular Weight Hyaluronic Acid, Magnesium, and Palmitoylethanolamide to Prevent Preterm Birth
summary:
# Summary

Successful pregnancy requires maintaining myometrial quiescence until labor, a state disrupted when pro-inflammatory signals prematurely activate uterine contractility, leading to preterm birth and neonatal complications. The transcription factor NF-κB serves as a master regulator in this transition, coordinating inflammatory pathways including IL-1β and IL-6 upregulation in reproductive tissues, which activate COX-2 and prostaglandin production, ultimately driving cervical ripening and uterine contractions. The article proposes a multitarget preventive strategy using vitamin D, which shifts immune responses toward a protective Th2/Treg profile by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-17, TNF-α) while upregulating anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10, TGF-β), and simultaneously inhibits COX-2 and corticotropin-releasing hormone to reduce prostaglandin signaling and prevent fetal membrane degradation. Complementary compounds including high molecular weight hyaluronic acid support endogenous progesterone activity to maintain membrane homeostasis, while magnesium and palmitoylethanolamide provide additional anti-inflammatory and quiescence-supporting effects. Vitamin D supplementation alone demonstrated clinical efficacy in reducing preterm birth risk, suggesting that this integrated immunomodulatory approach targeting multiple inflammatory nodes may effectively prevent preterm labor by preserving uterine quiescence.

title: Targeting macrophages in cancer immunotherapy: Frontiers and challenges
summary:
# Summary

Macrophage polarization represents a critical regulatory mechanism in cancer immunotherapy, with M1 macrophages driving anti-tumor responses through nitric oxide production and T cell stimulation, while M2 macrophages promote tumor growth through immunosuppressive cytokine secretion. Cancer cells evade macrophage-mediated phagocytosis through multiple mechanisms, including surface expression of CD47 and CD24, which interact with SIRPα and Siglec-10 on phagocytes, respectively, as well as PD-L1 engagement and calreticulin suppression via STC1. Tumor-derived exosomes and hypoxic microenvironments orchestrate M2 polarization through microRNA-mediated pathways (including miR-301a, miR-934, and miR-155-3p) that activate PI3K/AKT and STAT3/6 signaling while suppressing PTEN expression. Therapeutic strategies to reverse tumor-promoting M2 polarization include metformin-induced M2-to-M1 conversion with enhanced CD8+ T cell recruitment, CSF-1/CSF-1R inhibition, METTL3-enhanced TLR signaling, and natural compounds such as ginseng nanoparticles that increase reactive oxygen species. Understanding these macrophage-mediated immune evasion mechanisms and polarization dynamics offers multiple therapeutic targets for enhancing anti-tumor immunity.

title: Targeted analysis of sphingolipids and cytokines in plasma of dairy cows after calving reveals distinct impacts of systemic inflammation, ketosis, and mastitis
journal: Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology
summary: Sphingolipids function as critical regulators of systemic inflammation in postpartum dairy cows, with distinct metabolic signatures emerging across inflammatory conditions. Systemic inflammation, detected through elevated haptoglobin, induces pronounced alterations in sphingolipid metabolism, including increased dihydrosphingomyelins and lactosylceramides, while activating pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways through multiple mechanisms: pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-1α) activate neutral sphingomyelinase to create feed-forward inflammatory loops, ceramides bind TLR4 to promote cytokine production, and sphingosine-1-phosphate promotes pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization via S1PR3 signaling. Notably, ceramide and dihydroceramide species (C18–20) show strong positive associations with multiple chemokines (CXCL8, CCL2, CCL3, CCL4), while specific ceramide and sphingomyelin species are significantly reduced in inflamed animals, suggesting consumption during active inflammation. Ketosis produces more modest sphingolipid changes, primarily increasing plasma dihydrosphingomyelins, whereas mastitis causes minimal alterations despite systemic immune activation. Inhibition of serine palmitoyl transferase 2, a key enzyme in de novo ceramide synthesis, successfully reduces hepatic and blood ceramide levels and prevents pro-inflammatory cytokine production, suggesting this pathway as a potential therapeutic target for managing inflammation-associated diseases.

title: CXCL10/CXCR3 axis facilitates the M2 polarization of macrophages in colorectal cancer via activating RAF
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the CXCL10/CXCR3 axis promotes M2 macrophage polarization in colorectal cancer through RAF/ERK pathway activation, facilitating tumor progression. CXCL10 is significantly elevated in colorectal cancer tissues and, when overexpressed by cancer cells, drives macrophages toward an M2 phenotype characterized by increased expression of CD206 and arginase 1 while suppressing M1 markers like CD86 and iNOS. M2-polarized macrophages exhibit reduced phagocytic capacity and lysosomal enzyme activity but produce elevated IL-10, which promotes cancer cell proliferation and invasion. Conversely, CXCL10 silencing shifts macrophages toward an M1 phenotype with enhanced antimicrobial and anti-tumor functions. The mechanistic studies reveal that CXCL10 activates the RAF1/ERK signaling cascade in macrophages, and blocking CXCR3 with the inhibitor NBI-74330 can reverse these effects, suggesting the CXCL10/CXCR3/RAF/ERK axis as a potential therapeutic target to reprogram the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

title: RNA stability: a novel perspective on gene regulatory networks in bovine mastitis
summary: This study reveals that RNA stability mechanisms play a critical role in bovine mastitis pathogenesis following Staphylococcus aureus infection. The research identified that RNA-binding proteins like HuR stabilize pro-inflammatory cytokine mRNAs (IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β) through interactions with AU-rich elements, prolonging immune responses during bacterial infection. Gene expression analysis identified distinct modules with differential stability patterns: the red module containing genes involved in NF-κB and Toll-like receptor signaling increased in stability during infection, while the yellow module enriched in cytokine production pathways similarly showed increased stability. Hub genes including TNFAIP3, IL1B, NFKB1, and IL4R drive pro-inflammatory and cytokine signaling cascades that orchestrate innate immune activation. These molecular mechanisms collectively amplify inflammatory responses, ultimately leading to mammary gland dysfunction and the significant economic losses associated with reduced milk production. The findings suggest that post-transcriptional regulation of immune-related genes represents a novel therapeutic target for managing mastitis in cattle.

title: Exosomal regulation of cellular reprogramming and polarization in the tumor microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Exosomes function as critical mediators of intercellular communication within the tumor microenvironment, facilitating the reprogramming and polarization of stromal cells that actively promote tumor initiation, progression, and metastasis. Cancer-derived exosomes reprogram normal fibroblasts into cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) through multiple molecular pathways, including delivery of proteins such as Wnt2B and Survivin, which activate fibroblast survival signaling and modulate reactive oxygen species levels to drive myofibroblast differentiation. Beyond proteins, exosomal microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs orchestrate CAF transformation by targeting tumor suppressors like TP53, activating key signaling cascades including TGF-β, Wnt/β-catenin, and PI3K/AKT, and modulating critical regulatory molecules such as telomerase and SPHK1. Once reprogrammed into CAFs, these fibroblasts promote tumor progression through secretion of cytokines, chemokines, and growth factors that enhance cancer cell invasiveness, angiogenesis, and immune evasion. This exosome-mediated transformation represents a central mechanism by which the tumor microenvironment is dynamically remodeled to support malignant progression, with implications for understanding and potentially targeting stromal-tumor interactions in cancer biology.

title: Macrophage membrane-coated nanoparticles in inflammatory diseases: from bioinspired design to translational potential
summary:
# Summary

Macrophage membrane-coated nanoparticles (MΦ-NPs) represent a bioinspired therapeutic approach to inflammatory diseases by leveraging the innate immune regulatory capabilities of macrophages. The inflammatory cascade in endometriosis and similar conditions involves coordinated recruitment of immune cells through chemokine signaling (CCL2, CCL3, MCP-1 binding to CCR2/CCR4 receptors), adhesion molecule interactions (E-selectin, ICAM-1, VCAM-1), and pattern recognition receptors (TLR2, TLR4, RAGE, SR-A/CD36) that drive macrophage accumulation and activation. MΦ-NPs achieve therapeutic effects through four key mechanisms: precise homing to inflamed tissues via membrane adhesion molecules, direct cytokine neutralization through surface receptors (TNF-αR, IL-1R), immune evasion via CD47-SIRPα interactions, and modulation of macrophage polarization toward anti-inflammatory M2-type phenotypes. These nanoparticles, constructed with biodegradable cores (PLGA or liposomes) coated with macrophage membranes, enable site-specific drug delivery while mitigating chronic inflammation that otherwise promotes tumorigenesis, offering translational potential across multiple inflammatory and malignant diseases.

title: Probiotic extracellular vesicles reprogram macrophage immunometabolism: From gut crosstalk to host health
summary:
# Summary

Probiotic-derived extracellular vesicles (PEVs) modulate macrophage immunometabolism through reprogramming of key metabolic pathways including glycolysis, oxidative phosphorylation, fatty acid oxidation, and amino acid metabolism, thereby shifting macrophage polarization states and inflammatory responses. M1 macrophages activated by lipopolysaccharides and interferon-gamma rely predominantly on aerobic glycolysis with accumulation of succinate and citrate, while M2 macrophages polarized by interleukin-4 and interleukin-10 utilize oxidative phosphorylation and fatty acid oxidation. PEVs act as potent immunoregulatory agents capable of altering macrophage metabolic pathways and cholesterol efflux, with evidence that specific bacterial-derived molecules such as miR-146b from Lactococcus lactis actively promote M2 macrophage polarization and reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine production. This metabolic-functional coupling between gut microbiota and host immunity represents a critical therapeutic target, as immunometabolic dysregulation contributes to obesity, atherosclerosis, and inflammatory bowel disease. The study elucidates how intestinal probiotics regulate host metabolic and immune homeostasis through macrophage-mediated pathways, suggesting probiotic extracellular vesicles as promising interventions for restoring immunometabolic balance in inflammatory conditions.

title: The multifaceted role of post-translational modifications in macrophage polarization: from mechanisms to therapeutic targets
summary:
# Summary

This review synthesizes how post-translational modifications—including phosphorylation, ubiquitination, methylation, acetylation, and lactylation—orchestrate macrophage polarization toward pro-inflammatory M1 or anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes. M1 macrophages activated via toll-like receptor signaling and the NF-κB and JAK/STAT pathways secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6, while M2 macrophages produce IL-10 and express markers like Arg1, Fizz1, and CD206 to promote tissue remodeling. Key signaling cascades including NF-κB, JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT, and MAPK/ERK pathways regulate macrophage phenotype through phosphorylation-driven activation of transcription factors like STAT1 and STAT6. The balance between macrophage activation is fine-tuned by ubiquitination-dependent regulation, with E3 ligases and deubiquitinases selectively promoting or inhibiting polarization states; excessive M2 activation can paradoxically lead to fibrosis and immune dysfunction. These post-translational modifications thus represent critical control points that could serve as therapeutic targets for modulating the inflammatory microenvironment in endometriosis and related inflammatory conditions.

title: Eutopic Endometrium Immune Changes Involved in Development and Progression of Endometriosis: A Review
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by profound immune dysregulation in the eutopic endometrium involving multiple cellular and molecular mechanisms. Pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages predominate and promote elevated levels of cytokines including IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-12, while protective immune cells such as natural killer cells, dendritic cells, and CD8+ T cells expressing perforin are reduced or functionally impaired. Ectopic endometrial cells drive this inflammatory milieu through elevated aromatase expression, which increases local estrogen production; estrogen then amplifies inflammation by stimulating COX-2 and prostaglandin synthesis while suppressing perforin expression in cytotoxic lymphocytes. Additionally, dysbiotic shifts in both endometrial and gut microbiota—characterized by reduced Lactobacillus dominance and elevated pathogenic organisms and β-glucuronidase—further elevate systemic and local estrogen concentrations, perpetuating the inflammatory cycle. These interconnected immune, hormonal, and microbial alterations collectively drive lesion growth, angiogenesis, and the epithelial-mesenchymal transition of endometrial cells, mechanisms that ultimately contribute to endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: The role of interleukins in peripheral nerve injury and the current status of treatment
summary:
# Summary

Following peripheral nerve injury, pro-inflammatory interleukins orchestrate a cascade of cellular responses that impair nerve regeneration. IL-1β rapidly initiates Wallerian degeneration and triggers Schwann cell pyroptosis, while also activating the JAK-STAT signaling pathway to promote glial scar formation. IL-6, produced sequentially by Schwann cells, macrophages, and fibroblasts, amplifies the inflammatory response by recruiting macrophages, enhancing oxidative stress, and inducing fibroblast epithelial-mesenchymal transition and excessive fibrosis that mechanically obstructs axon regeneration. IL-17, produced by Th17 cells and other immune cells, activates the IL-17B/IL-17RB pathway in Schwann cells to upregulate chemokines (CCL2, CCL3, CCL5) and MMP-9, which degrade the extracellular matrix and promote demyelination. Together, these interleukins converge on multiple signaling pathways—including NF-κB, MAPK, and STAT3—to drive chronic neuroinflammation, mechanical allodynia, and persistent functional impairment following nerve injury.

title: Perioperative neurocognitive disorders: Advances in molecular mechanisms and bioactive molecules
summary:
# Perioperative Neurocognitive Disorders: Inflammatory Mechanisms and Therapeutic Targets

Perioperative neurocognitive disorders (PNDs) involve a complex interplay of neuroinflammatory mechanisms, including microglial activation, interleukin imbalance, NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis, neurotransmitter dysfunction, epigenetic modifications, and gut–brain axis disruption. Both anesthesia and surgery impair synaptic plasticity through disruption of GABAA and NMDA receptor signaling, while surgical trauma triggers metabolic reprogramming in the hippocampus that promotes pro-inflammatory microglial polarization and subsequent A1 astrocyte activation. Key drivers of this cascade include neuronal lipocalin-2 upregulation, which promotes microglial activation independent of other inflammatory triggers, and elevated extracellular glutamate that disrupts hippocampal network balance by suppressing pyramidal neurons while enhancing GABAergic inhibition. Therapeutic interventions targeting these mechanisms show promise: glycolysis inhibition, Kv1.3 potassium channel blockade, neuronal LCN2 silencing, and CD200R1 agonism all mitigate neuroinflammation and cognitive impairment, while propofol-based anesthesia combined with esketamine, along with appropriate pain management through opioids and sedatives, facilitate postoperative cognitive recovery. These findings highlight microglial regulation and metabolic control as critical intervention points for preventing PND development in perioperative patients.

title: HPV-driven inflammatory pathways in ovarian carcinogenesis: molecular mechanisms and emerging therapeutic interventions
summary:
# Summary

High-risk HPV (HR-HPV) drives ovarian carcinogenesis through multiple inflammatory mechanisms, primarily by deploying oncoproteins E6 and E7 that inactivate critical tumor suppressors p53 and retinoblastoma protein while simultaneously activating pro-inflammatory signaling pathways including NF-κB and STAT3. HR-HPV simultaneously suppresses innate antiviral defenses by inhibiting Toll-like receptor and cGAS-STING pathways, allowing persistent infection to establish chronic inflammation that generates elevated levels of pro-tumorigenic mediators including IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and reactive oxygen species. This chronic inflammatory state causes progressive DNA damage and genomic instability while recruiting and polarizing immune cells—particularly tumor-associated macrophages and regulatory T cells—that suppress anti-tumor immunity through upregulation of immune checkpoint molecules PD-1, CTLA-4, and LAG-3. Estrogen further amplifies viral activity and inflammatory signaling, and endometriosis-associated chronic inflammation synergizes with HPV infection to increase ovarian cancer risk, particularly through persistent infection in the fallopian tube epithelium. The sustained activation of NF-κB and JAK/STAT pathways maintains this pro-inflammatory microenvironment while promoting angiogenesis and tumor cell survival, establishing a self-perpetuating cycle of inflammation-driven carcinogenesis.

title: Neuroinflammation and Pediatric Chronic Pain – A Review
summary:
# Summary

Neuroinflammation emerges as a critical mechanism driving the transition from acute to chronic pain in pediatric populations through activation of central nervous system glial cells, particularly microglia and astrocytes. These activated glial cells release pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α) and chemokines (CCL2, CCL5, CXCL1) that sensitize nociceptive neurons and amplify pain signaling, while simultaneously reducing protective anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-4, IL-10). Central sensitization, characterized by heightened nervous system responsiveness and allodynia/hyperalgesia, is directly driven by neuroinflammatory processes including oxidative stress, microglia-mediated cytokine and free radical production, and sustained A1 astrocyte activation.
