TNF-α plays a particularly pivotal role by activating NF-κB signaling, promoting pro-inflammatory mediator cascades, and disrupting blood-brain barrier integrity, while life stressors and hormonal changes further promote neuroinflammatory activation. Additionally, elevated brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) perpetuates pro-nociceptive environments, and toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) activation on microglia initiates pro-inflammatory gene expression linked to allodynia. These interconnected neuroinflammatory pathways collectively explain the neurobiological basis of chronic pain persistence in pediatric populations.

title: Decoding inflammatory regulation in ovarian cancer at single-cell resolution
summary:
# Summary

Chronic inflammation, particularly in conditions like endometriosis and pelvic inflammatory disease, drives ovarian cancer initiation and progression through dysregulated cytokine production. Epithelial and ovarian cancer cells produce IL-6, IL-1, and M-CSF, which create an immunosuppressive peritoneal microenvironment that polarizes monocytes into M2 tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs). These M2 TAMs further amplify immunosuppression by secreting IL-10 and TGF-β while promoting tumor angiogenesis via VEGF and PDGF, and recruiting regulatory T cells. Additionally, cancer-associated fibroblasts derived from mesothelial-to-mesenchymal transition degrade extracellular matrix through metalloproteinases and produce pro-tumoral cytokines like IL-6 that activate JAK/STAT signaling. At the single-cell level, exhausted CD8+ T cells expressing high levels of checkpoint molecules (PD-1, LAG-3, TIM-3) and inflammatory CAF subsets expressing complement factors and chemokines (CXCL1/2/10/12) cooperatively suppress anti-tumor immunity. CYR61 expression associates with advanced disease stage and tumor recurrence, suggesting it participates in metastatic progression through IL-6 production in the inflammatory microenvironment.

title: Umbilical cord blood natural killer cells for adoptive immunotherapy: identifying optimal starting material and processing parameters
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies optimal parameters for expanding natural killer cells from umbilical cord blood for adoptive immunotherapy applications. Natural killer cells represent a critical component of innate immune surveillance, eliminating infected and cancerous cells through multiple mechanisms including perforin and granzyme-mediated lysis, as well as death ligand pathways (Fas-L and TRAIL). The researchers found that CD3-depleted umbilical cord blood mononuclear cells provide superior starting material, achieving a dramatic increase in CD3-CD56+ NK cell populations from 12.8% to 96.8% over 21 days of culture with IL-2 and IL-15 supplementation. Early isolation combined with cryopreservation of cells from caesarean section deliveries significantly enhanced expansion potential and final cytotoxic function. Day 7 measurements of NK cell expansion and K562-specific lysis emerged as reliable predictors of day 21 outcomes, and baseline blood parameters including red cell distribution width and nucleated red blood cell counts correlated with immunotherapy efficacy. These findings establish practical parameters for developing allogeneic "off-the-shelf" NK cell cancer immunotherapies with reduced inter-donor variability.

title: Single‐Cell RNA Sequencing Analysis Reveals Correlation Between Immune Cell Composition and Gene Expression in Cervical Cancer
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing analysis reveals that TNFRSF18 (GITR), a co-stimulatory molecule expressed on regulatory T cells, plays a critical role in shaping immune cell composition and promoting immune suppression in cervical cancer. TNFRSF18 expression correlates positively with innate immune cells (neutrophils, monocytes, dendritic cells, and NK cells) while inversely correlating with adaptive immune populations (CD4+ T cells, memory B cells, and plasma cells), suggesting a shift toward an immunosuppressive microenvironment. TNFRSF18-activated Tregs suppress anti-tumor immunity through multiple mechanisms: secretion of immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10 and TGF-β), expression of CTLA-4 which inhibits T cell co-stimulation, and recruitment of myeloid cells via CCL2 and CCL5. Additionally, the SPP1 signaling pathway, produced primarily by epithelial cells and active in macrophages, upregulates pro-angiogenic and immunosuppressive factors (VEGFA, MMP9, CD274/PD-L1, and IL-10) while promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition. High-risk cervical cancer patients are characterized by elevated expression of KRT14 and CASP14 alongside diminished expression of immune markers, indicating that immune suppression driven by TNFRSF18+ Tregs and SPP1-activated macrophages contributes to poor clinical outcomes.

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cells Modulate Granulosa Cell Function Under Inflammatory and Hypoxic Conditions
summary:
# Summary

Hypoxia and inflammation represent major contributors to premature ovarian insufficiency by inducing oxidative stress and suppressing granulosa cell function, specifically reducing estradiol secretion and promoting apoptosis that leads to follicular atresia. Mesenchymal stem cells emerge as a promising therapeutic approach due to their anti-inflammatory, anti-apoptotic, and immunomodulatory properties, effectively reversing the detrimental effects of both hypoxic and inflammatory conditions on granulosa cells. When co-cultured with granulosa cells under normal, hypoxic, or inflammatory conditions, MSCs significantly enhance estradiol secretion, an effect mediated through soluble factors including growth factors, angiogenic factors, cytokines, and chemokines delivered via conditioned medium. These findings demonstrate that MSCs can modulate granulosa cell hormone production and survival in the pathological microenvironments characteristic of conditions such as endometriosis that associate with premature ovarian insufficiency. This work supports the therapeutic potential of MSC-based autologous therapy for restoring ovarian function in patients with compromised follicular development and hormone production.

title: Intrauterine Administration of PBMC Modulated with IFN-τ Before Embryo Transfer Improves Clinical Outcomes of IVF Patients—A Randomized Control Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that intrauterine administration of autologous peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) activated with interferon tau (IFN-τ) significantly improves implantation and live birth rates in IVF patients, with implantation rates improving from 27.7% to 38.3% and live birth rates increasing from 17.6% to 28.7%. IFN-τ, a type I interferon that serves as the maternal recognition signal in ruminants, promotes a Th2-biased immune shift in patient PBMCs by reducing proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) while enhancing immunomodulatory effects through its widely-expressed receptor IFNAR on human immune and endometrial cells. The mechanism involves IFN-τ-activated PBMCs secreting beneficial cytokines (IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α, GM-CSF) that enhance endometrial receptivity, promote leukocyte recruitment, and improve trophoblast-endometrial dialogue through STAT3 activation. Notably, IFN-τ-activated PBMC administration did not increase miscarriage rates and posed no safety concerns, positioning this immunomodulatory approach as a promising intervention for improving pregnancy outcomes in assisted reproductive technology.

title: The Role of Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts and Tumor-Associated Macrophages in the Tumor Microenvironment and Their Impact on Ovarian Cancer Survival and Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) and tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) are major components of the ovarian cancer tumor microenvironment that substantially promote disease progression through complementary mechanisms. CAFs contribute to cancer advancement by remodeling the extracellular matrix, promoting angiogenesis through VEGF secretion, and activating survival pathways via cytokines including TGF-β and IL-6, while simultaneously suppressing antitumor immunity through immunosuppressive factor secretion. TAMs predominantly adopt an M2 phenotype that suppresses immune surveillance and promotes metastasis, contrasting with the antitumor functions of M1 macrophages. Interactions between CAFs and TAMs with cancer cells activate key signaling pathways including TGF-β, IL-6, and PI3K/AKT, which drive therapy resistance and immune evasion, with elevated CAF risk scores strongly correlating with poor overall survival and resistance to immunotherapies. Current therapeutic approaches including TGF-β signaling inhibitors show promise by reducing epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and enhancing immune responses, offering potential strategies to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

title: Endometriosis as a Systemic and Complex Disease: Toward Phenotype-Based Classification and Personalized Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, systemic inflammatory disorder in which ectopic endometrial tissue produces elevated levels of inflammatory mediators, prostaglandins, and matrix metalloproteinases that drive pathological features including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. The ectopic lesions survive and proliferate through activation of key signaling pathways—including PI3K/Akt, Wnt/β-catenin, NF-κB, and HIF-1α—which upregulate angiogenic factors (VEGF, IL-8, CXCL8, CXCL2, WNT5A) and promote a pro-inflammatory microenvironment. Central to lesion persistence is estrogen dominance driven by aberrant local aromatase expression, elevated steroidogenic enzyme activity (StAR, CYP19A1, HSD17β1, STS), and progesterone resistance arising from reduced progesterone receptor expression and altered signaling through PI3K/Akt, MAPK, and NOTCH1 pathways. A feedforward loop amplifies COX-2 and PGE2 production through NF-κB, IL-1β, VEGF, and HIF-1α signaling, which further promotes steroidogenesis and ectopic tissue survival. Systemic molecular "spillover" of circulating cytokines and microRNAs contributes to systemic manifestations including chronic pain, fatigue, and immune evasion, indicating endometriosis functions as a multifaceted disease requiring phenotype-based classification and targeted therapeutic approaches.

title: Pro-inflammatory cytokines disrupt in vitro preantral follicle development by targeting granulosa and theca cell functions
summary:
# Summary

Pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 significantly impair in vitro preantral follicle development by suppressing FSH-induced granulosa cell (GC) proliferation and steroid hormone production while paradoxically promoting theca cell (TC) expansion. These cytokines inhibit the expression of key steroidogenic enzymes (Cyp19a1, Cyp17a1, Cyp11a1) and FSH signaling components (Fshr, Lhcgr), resulting in reduced estradiol and testosterone synthesis. Additionally, the pro-inflammatory cytokines elevate reactive oxygen species accumulation in granulosa cells and upregulate transforming growth factor-beta signaling, promoting collagen deposition in the theca layer. Notably, follicles with mature multi-layered theca structures demonstrate partial resistance to cytokine-induced oxidative stress and granulosa cell dysfunction, suggesting that follicular development stage modulates sensitivity to inflammatory insults. These findings demonstrate how chronic low-grade inflammation compromises ovarian function and may contribute to endometriosis-associated and other inflammation-driven forms of female infertility.

title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
summary:
# Summary: Neutrophils as Critical Orchestrators of Chronic Inflammation

Neutrophils serve as central drivers of chronic inflammation through multiple interconnected mechanisms that perpetuate tissue damage and inflammatory signaling. These cells generate sustained inflammatory environments by producing neutrophilic extracellular traps (NETs), reactive oxygen species (ROS), proteases, and inflammatory cytokines including CXCL8, CCL2, and BAFF, while simultaneously engaging in phagocytosis and degranulation. Excessive neutrophil activation—regulated through G-CSF signaling and transcription factors like C/EBPε and C/EBPβ—leads to aberrant cell death, uncontrolled oxidative bursts, and pathological NET formation, mechanisms that shift acute inflammation toward chronic pathology when inflammatory resolution becomes impaired. Neutrophils orchestrate tissue remodeling and perpetuate inflammation through active crosstalk with endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, T cells, and B cells, collectively contributing to collateral tissue damage characteristic of chronic inflammatory diseases including endometriosis, rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, and systemic lupus erythematosus. ROS production depends on NOX2-mediated electron transfer involving gp91phox, p22phox, and Rac proteins, with careful regulation by superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase to prevent excessive oxidative injury.

title: The immune milieu after local endometrial injury in women with recurrent implantation failure
summary:
# Summary

Local endometrial injury (LEI) improves live birth rates in women with recurrent implantation failure by provoking a coordinated shift in the endometrial immune milieu during the implantation window. LEI induces an inflammatory response characterized by increased lymphocytes (particularly T cells and NK cells) and decreased antigen-presenting cells, which collectively enhance endometrial receptivity through upregulation of adhesion molecules and increased secretion of pro-implantation cytokines. The predominant T cells (34.6–40.2% of endometrial immune cells) are activated by innate immunity and regulate other immune populations, while NK cells contribute to pregnancy success by secreting multiple angiogenic and immunomodulatory factors including vascular endothelial growth factor, placental growth factor, and interleukins that promote blood vessel remodeling and feto-maternal tolerance. Dendritic cells bridge innate and adaptive immunity by processing antigens and producing pro-inflammatory cytokines, with their reduction following LEI potentially facilitating immune tolerance. This dynamic remodeling of endometrial immune composition demonstrates that LEI leverages inflammatory signaling to create a permissive immunological environment essential for successful embryo implantation.

title: Intestinal aging-related immune dysfunction: mechanisms and interventions
summary: Intestinal aging-related immune dysfunction arises from multiple interconnected mechanisms that compromise barrier integrity and amplify chronic inflammation. The intestine, housing 70-80% of the body's immune cells, undergoes immunosenescence characterized by decreased tight junction proteins, thinned mucus layers, and reduced microbial diversity—together promoting increased intestinal permeability and dysbiosis that disrupts the gut barrier. Senescent intestinal cells activate the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP), elevating pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IFN-γ that activate p38-MAPK, JAK-STAT, and NF-κB pathways, driving intestinal mucosal damage and M1 macrophage polarization while suppressing protective M2 responses. Age-related reductions in toll-like receptor expression—particularly TLR2, TLR3, and TLR8 in dendritic cells and myeloid cells—impair innate immune recognition and adaptive immune activation, while TLR4 overactivation paradoxically escalates inflammatory cytokine production. These cascading immune failures associate with increased susceptibility to infections, persistent low-grade inflammation, poor vaccine responses, and conditions including inflammatory bowel disease, with implications for age-related inflammatory pathologies.

title: Selection for Function in Complex Distributed Pathological Systems
summary: Endometriosis exemplifies how distributed pathological systems maintain themselves through complex inflammatory and intercellular communication networks. Ectopic endometrial lesions establish multiple foci of growth across the peritoneum and ovaries, each recruiting vascularization and interacting with local immune cells to function as autonomous nodes. These lesions communicate systemically through hormonal cycles, inflammatory mediators, vascular networks, circulating cytokines, endocrine factors, and regulatory RNAs carried by extracellular vesicles, creating cross-talk that reinforces lesion persistence and drives recurrence and therapeutic resistance. Peritoneal fluid transports mesenchymal stem cells and their secreted factors between lesions, further sustaining the distributed system. Like other multifocal pathologies such as adenomyosis and fibrotic diseases, endometriosis illustrates how "selection for function" can explain reproductive disorders, where multiple lesions behave as interconnected functional units that resist clearance and therapeutic intervention.

title: Imperfect repair in aging: senescent cells and the hepatic fibrotic niche
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how cellular senescence and senescent cells drive hepatic fibrosis through imperfect wound repair mechanisms during aging. Key drivers of senescence include p16Ink4a, p21Cip1, and epigenetic regulators (BRD4, KAT7, AP-1, GATA4, C/EBPβ), which establish a stable senescent state characterized by the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP). Senescent cells throughout the hepatic niche—including hepatocytes, hepatic stellate cells (HSCs), macrophages, and liver sinusoidal endothelial cells—secrete pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic factors (IL-6, IL-1α, IL-8, TGF-β, PDGF, CTGF) that accumulate and reinforce fibrogenesis while simultaneously impairing immune clearance through PD-L1 upregulation and TGF-β-mediated suppression of T cells and NK cells. The fibrotic niche is centrally orchestrated by scar-associated macrophages (SAMs), particularly OLR1+ and TREM2+ subsets, which undergo ADAM17-mediated TREM2 shedding when TNF-α and IL-1β signaling is elevated, thereby reducing their capacity for efferocytosis and promoting myofibroblast activation. Critically, senescent cells also lose pro-resolution signaling pathways (Wnt9b-Sfrp2 disruption), further compromising tissue regeneration and establishing a pathogenic cycle where aging-associated senescence transitions normal wound healing into chronic, progressive fibrosis.

title: Hypoxia and oxidative stress related to inflammation and vascular aspects of the pathogenesis of psoriasis
summary:
# Summary

This article elucidates the interconnected roles of hypoxia, oxidative stress, and immune dysfunction in psoriasis pathogenesis. Hypoxia activates the transcription factors HIF-1α and HIF-2α in psoriatic plaques, which regulate vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and its receptor Flt-1 to drive angiogenesis and increased capillary permeability—key vascular features of the disease. Oxidative stress amplifies both vascular alterations and inflammation while reducing microRNA-150 expression, allowing unopposed HIF-1α elevation. HIF signaling broadly enhances innate immune cell survival and function (mast cells, macrophages, neutrophils, and dendritic cells), which subsequently produce pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-12, IL-17, IL-6, and TNF-α. These cytokines promote differentiation and expansion of pathogenic Th17 cells through HIF-1α-dependent mechanisms involving IL-6, TGF-β, and the mTOR-HIF-1α axis regulating RORγt transcription factor activity. Environmental factors such as tobacco and alcohol use exacerbate this cascade by increasing oxidative stress and inflammation, while genetic variants in antioxidant enzymes and psoriasis susceptibility loci (PSORS1) predispose individuals to disease development.

title: Menstrual blood-derived mesenchymal stromal cell secretome modulates macrophage polarization in a preconditioning-dependent manner
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Menstrual blood-derived mesenchymal stromal cells (MenSCs) modulate macrophage polarization through their secretome in a preconditioning-dependent manner, with distinct effects between basal and inflammatory preconditioned states. Basal secretome (S-bMenSCs) partially inhibits the shift toward pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages while reinforcing M1 traits in pre-polarized cells, whereas preconditioned secretome (S-pMenSCs) promotes M1 phenotype switching, demonstrating that inflammatory preconditioning with interferon-gamma and TNF-alpha substantially alters the immunomodulatory capacity of MenSC-derived secretomes. Proteomic analysis revealed that preconditioning strategies effectively modify secretome composition, including the release of immunomodulatory molecules such as IDO1 and alterations in extracellular vesicle components. These findings highlight MenSCs' significant potential role in regulating innate immune responses and inflammatory contexts through paracrine mechanisms, with important implications for understanding how preconditioning strategies can enhance the therapeutic application of MenSC secretomes in inflammatory diseases such as endometriosis.

title: Titanium Surface Roughness Mediated Macrophages Polarization-Influenced Osteogenic Differentiation of Periodontal Ligament-Derived Mesenchymal Stromal Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that titanium implant surface roughness critically regulates macrophage polarization and subsequently influences osteogenic differentiation of periodontal ligament-derived mesenchymal stromal cells. Medium roughness (Ti-MR) surfaces preferentially promote M2 macrophage polarization by downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) while upregulating anti-inflammatory and tissue-regenerative factors (IL-10, TGF-β, VEGF). The conditioned medium from M2 macrophages significantly enhanced PDL MSC proliferation and osteogenic gene expression, including increased osteopontin, alkaline phosphatase, osteocalcin, RUNX2, and calcium deposition. Low roughness (Ti-LR) surfaces conversely maintained higher M1/M2 ratios and pro-inflammatory cytokine production, inhibiting MSC proliferation and osteogenesis. These findings suggest that implant surface topography exerts its osteogenic effects through a macrophage-mediated immunomodulatory mechanism, with medium surface roughness optimally steering immune responses toward tissue regeneration rather than inflammation.

title: Selective Glucocorticoid Receptor Modulators of Immune Checkpoint Function
summary:
# Summary

Selective glucocorticoid receptor modulators (SGRMs) represent a refined approach to glucocorticoid therapy that selectively regulates immunity and inflammation while minimizing metabolic side effects associated with conventional glucocorticoids like dexamethasone. Unlike traditional glucocorticoids, which broadly suppress immune function and cause hyperglycemia, obesity, and osteoporosis, leading SGRMs such as SR38946, SR38951, and SR42364 suppress pro-inflammatory T cell cytokines (IL-6 and TNFα) and promote memory T cell differentiation while preserving effector T cell functions including interferon-gamma production critical for anti-tumor immunity. These SGRMs achieve superior selectivity by limiting the induction of immune checkpoint proteins (PD-1 and CTLA-4) and avoiding the pro-tumoral M2 macrophage polarization induced by dexamethasone, instead maintaining more pro-inflammatory macrophage phenotypes. The molecular mechanism involves allosteric modulation of the glucocorticoid receptor through solvent-channel substituents that drive dynamic oscillations in the steroid core, enabling selective transcriptional programming that dissociates anti-inflammatory benefits from immunosuppressive liabilities. This selective glucocorticoid receptor modulation represents a promising strategy for treating inflammatory conditions like endometriosis while preserving anti-tumoral immune responses.

title: Spatial profiling of the metabolism-immune axis in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study employs spatial metabolomics to characterize the metabolism-immune axis in ovarian cancer, revealing distinct metabolic subtypes with profound immunological consequences. High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) exhibits elevated glycolysis through HK2, LDHA, and PFKFB3 overexpression, generating lactate that acidifies the tumor microenvironment and promotes chemoresistance, tumor invasion, and immune evasion by suppressing CD8+ T cell function while enhancing regulatory T cell immunosuppression. Complementary metabolic pathways including glutaminolysis via ASCT2/GLS1, fatty acid oxidation, and lipid accumulation show subtype-specific patterns, with endometriosis-associated clear cell ovarian carcinoma displaying distinct lipid dependency and glutamine sensitivity. The immunosuppressive microenvironment is orchestrated by regulatory T cells, M2-polarized macrophages, and myeloid-derived suppressor cells, which collectively secrete IL-10 and TGF-β to inhibit effector T cell function, while cancer-associated fibroblasts and adipocytes provide additional metabolic and cytokine support. The study identifies spatial concordance between lactate and glutamate metabolic zones and proposes targeted therapeutic strategies including glycolysis inhibition, glutaminase blockade via CB-839, and immune checkpoint inhibitor combinations to overcome metabolically-driven chemoresistance and immunosuppression.

title: Peri-Transfer Glucocorticoid Therapy in Oocyte-Donation IVF Bridging the Immunological Gap
summary:
# Summary

Embryo transfer triggers immune system activation at the maternal-fetal interface, particularly in oocyte donation cycles where allogeneic recognition creates distinct immunological challenges. Transcriptomic analyses of donor egg endometria reveal elevated complement components (C1q, C3) and chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10, CXCL11), alongside increased populations of activated uterine natural killer cells and M1-like macrophages, along with altered trophoblast expression of HLA-G and matrix metalloproteinases. Glucocorticoid receptor alpha is expressed across endometrial epithelial cells, decidual stromal cells, immune cells, and T-cell populations, positioning glucocorticoids to modulate this inflammatory milieu by inhibiting NF-κB and AP-1 pathways and reducing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-17) while protecting implantation-critical factors like HOXA10 and HOXA11 and diminishing uNK cell cytotoxicity. Despite this mechanistic rationale, randomized controlled trials consistently demonstrate that peri-transfer glucocorticoid treatment provides no clinical advantage in live birth rates for autologous IVF cycles, highlighting the disconnect between immunological biomarkers and reproductive outcomes.

title: IL-8 and follicular fluid: insights into the mechanisms of endometriosis development
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how follicular fluid (FF) and its key inflammatory mediator IL-8 promote endometriosis pathogenesis through mechanisms affecting endometrial cell behavior. The researchers found that FF enhances endometrial cell viability and induces potent chemotactic migration, with this migration effect strongly dependent on IL-8 concentration—particularly in FF pools with elevated IL-8 levels. FF treatment upregulates IL-8 receptors CXCR1 and CXCR2 on endometrial cells, and blocking these receptors with reparixin or receptor inhibitors substantially reduces FF-induced migration, confirming IL-8 as a critical mediator. Additionally, FF exposure promotes a hybrid epithelial-mesenchymal transition state by inducing the mesenchymal marker N-cadherin while enhancing E-cadherin expression, facilitating more aggressive cellular phenotypes associated with ectopic lesion development. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that IL-8 signaling through CXCR1/CXCR2 represents a central mechanism by which follicular fluid in the peritoneal microenvironment drives endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and phenotypic changes that underlie endometriosis pathogenesis, suggesting IL-8 pathway inhibition as a potential therapeutic target.

title: Targeting Itga8 Mitigates Neurogenic Bladder Fibrosis Driven by Trem2⁺ Macrophage‐Derived Fn1 via FAK/RhoA/ROCK Signaling
summary:
# Summary

Neurogenic bladder-induced fibrosis, which causes irreversible bladder dysfunction, is driven by a pathogenic macrophage-fibroblast axis in which Trem2⁺ macrophages secrete fibronectin (Fn1) to engage integrin alpha-8 (Itga8) on fibroblasts. This Fn1-Itga8 interaction activates fibroblasts through the FAK/RhoA/ROCK signaling pathway, promoting cytoskeletal remodeling, fibroblast contraction and migration, and excessive collagen deposition. Itga8⁺ fibroblasts, which expand rapidly after nerve injury, exhibit elevated expression of pro-fibrotic genes including Acta2 and Cthrc1 and produce high levels of extracellular matrix proteins. Targeting Itga8 through genetic deletion or lentivirus-mediated knockdown significantly attenuates collagen accumulation and fibrotic remodeling while restoring bladder function, and pharmacological inhibition of downstream FAK or Rho kinase similarly abrogates fibroblast activation. These findings identify Itga8 as a key therapeutic target for preventing neurogenic bladder fibrosis and suggest that disrupting macrophage-fibroblast communication via the Fn1-Itga8 axis could preserve bladder function in patients with denervation-induced injury.

title: The dysregulation of the immune microenvironment during endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia serves as a marker of endometrial carcinogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how dysregulation of the endometrial immune microenvironment facilitates progression from endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia (EIN) to endometrial cancer. Estrogen and progesterone normally maintain immune homeostasis through periodic reshaping of the immune microenvironment, with progesterone suppressing inflammatory responses via IFN-γ inhibition and T cell suppression. However, continuous non-antagonistic estrogen exposure—promoted by obesity—drives excessive endometrial epithelial proliferation and activates endothelial cells through the SHP2-RIPK1Y380-AP1 axis, triggering a pathogenic feedback loop involving macrophage infiltration and amplified inflammation. The resulting immune dysregulation is marked by elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios, accumulation of regulatory T cells, and skewed macrophage polarization that collectively suppress anti-tumor immunity while enabling malignant progression. While endometrial cancer ultimately develops immune infiltration with increased CD8+ T cells and M1-like macrophages in immunologically "hot" subtypes (POLE-mutated and MSI-H), immunosuppressive cytokines within the tumor microenvironment dampen cytotoxic T cell function, highlighting how immune dysregulation sustains carcinogenesis throughout the precancerous-to-cancerous transition.

title: Diabetic dry eye: advances in pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, and therapeutic approaches
summary:
# Summary

Diabetes mellitus significantly increases dry eye risk through multiple interconnected pathways, with poor glycemic control and elevated HbA1c levels serving as independent risk factors. Sustained hyperglycemia activates the polyol and hexosamine pathways, driving oxidative stress and promoting advanced glycation end product (AGE) formation, which subsequently activates pattern recognition receptors RAGE and TLR4. These receptors trigger downstream inflammatory cascades including NF-κB, MAPK, and JAK/STAT signaling, resulting in aberrant expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and matrix metalloproteinases that accumulate in tear fluid and degrade corneal epithelial components and tight junction proteins. Corneal dendritic cell infiltration recruits Th1 and Th17 T cells that secrete additional inflammatory mediators, further elevating tear osmolarity and perpetuating local inflammation. Additionally, hyperglycemia disrupts tear film production through reduced lacrimal gland function, decreased conjunctival goblet cell density, meibomian gland dropout, and impaired corneal sensory nerve function, collectively compromising protective mechanisms like substance P and CGRP signaling. These multifactorial mechanisms—combining metabolic dysfunction, innate and adaptive immunity, neurodegeneration, and structural gland damage—collectively explain the high prevalence and severity of dry eye in diabetic populations.

title: Functional Equivalence of Heat-Inactivated (HI) and Live Probiotic RSB11 in Suppressing Inflammation: Expanding Formulation and Application Potential
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that both live and heat-inactivated forms of the probiotic strain Lactiplantibacillus plantarum RSB11 are functionally equivalent in suppressing bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS)- and E. coli-induced inflammatory responses across multiple cell types. Both RSB11 formulations robustly suppressed the production of key inflammatory mediators—TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, and MMP-9—which are central drivers of chronic inflammation in gastrointestinal and reproductive disorders. Notably, the heat-inactivated form (RSB11-HI) demonstrated an additional metabolic advantage by enhancing β-glucuronidase activity, leading to increased estrogen bioavailability through enhanced enterohepatic recirculation. This dual capacity to dampen inflammation while modulating estrogen metabolism positions RSB11-HI as a promising postbiotic therapeutic with enhanced stability and reproducibility compared to live probiotics, with potential applications in endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease, and other estrogen-dependent inflammatory conditions.

title: The effect of inflammatory factors on unstable angina risk, from the gene level
summary:
# Summary

Inflammatory cytokines play a central role in unstable angina (UA) pathogenesis through both elevated protein levels and genetic polymorphisms. Serum levels of IL-1β, IL-6, IL-10, IL-17, IFN-γ, and TNF-α were significantly elevated in UA patients compared to controls, with IL-10 showing the largest relative increase (approximately 3.2-fold). Specific genetic polymorphisms in cytokine genes, particularly IL-1β −511 C/T and TNF-α −308 G/A, were strongly associated with UA risk and demonstrated robust discriminatory ability (AUC = 0.87 and 0.84, respectively). Risk alleles (IL-1β T and TNF-α A) were functionally linked to higher serum cytokine levels, indicating that genetic variation directly influences inflammatory activity. The study identifies inflammatory cascades and endothelial dysfunction as key mechanisms connecting genetic predisposition to coronary instability in UA, positioning cytokine gene polymorphisms as potential biomarkers for identifying high-risk individuals with coronary heart disease susceptibility.

title: Neutrophils and NETosis in polycystic ovary syndrome: unraveling the immuno-metabolic thromboinflammatory axis
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized by chronic low-grade immuno-metabolic inflammation involving dysregulated neutrophil activation that links endocrine disturbance, metabolic dysfunction, and vascular pathology. Neutrophils respond rapidly to the metabolic and hormonal milieu of PCOS—including hyperinsulinemia, androgen excess, and adipose-derived cytokines—through signaling pathways such as PI3K/Akt and MAPK that enhance their oxidative burst, prolong survival, and promote degranulation and reactive oxygen species generation. In obese and insulin-resistant states, activated neutrophils infiltrate visceral adipose tissue where they secrete pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-8, TNF-α, elastase) that drive macrophage polarization toward the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, perpetuating adipose inflammation, lipotoxicity, and insulin resistance. Neutrophil extracellular trap formation represents a pathological amplification mechanism whereby expelled DNA and associated proteins enhance thrombo-inflammatory signaling, endothelial dysfunction, and tissue injury across multiple compartments including the ovary, endometrium, and vascular endothelium. IL-8 emerges as a central orchestrator that recruits neutrophils via CXCR1/2 engagement and stimulates their directed migration, degranulation, and integrin-mediated adhesion to vascular endothelium. NET-associated biomarkers (citH3, MPO-DNA) show promise as diagnostic or stratification tools for identifying individuals with inflammatory endotypes of PCOS.

title: Phase 1 dose-escalation trial of sub-endometrial injection of human embryonic stem cells-derived immunity-and-matrix-regulatory cells to promote endometrial angiogenesis in refractory intrauterine adhesion
summary:
# Summary

This Phase 1 clinical trial demonstrates that sub-endometrial injection of human embryonic stem cell-derived immunity-and-matrix-regulatory cells (IMRCs) effectively treats refractory intrauterine adhesion by promoting endometrial angiogenesis and reducing fibrosis. IMRCs directly interact with endometrial stromal cells through paracrine signaling via multiple pathways including PDGF, FGF, ANGPTL4, and MDK, thereby enhancing angiogenic potential and tissue repair mechanisms. Treatment with IMRCs significantly reduces pro-inflammatory TNF-α while increasing anti-inflammatory IL-10, simultaneously upregulating key angiogenic factors including VEGF, MMP-2, FGF2, and EGF, alongside pro-angiogenic mediators like ANGPTL4. In a rabbit intrauterine adhesion model, IMRC injection substantially increased microvessel density and ultrasonic blood flow while decreasing fibrotic area compared to untreated controls, with enhanced activation of pathways governing cell proliferation, T cell differentiation, and PI3K-Akt signaling. These findings establish IMRCs as a regenerative medicine approach that restores endometrial function through dual immunomodulation and matrix remodeling, potentially improving pregnancy outcomes in patients with adhesion-related infertility.

title: Multivariate model predicts immune imbalance in recurrent pregnancy loss and recurrent implantation failure
summary:
# Summary

This study identified distinct immune imbalances underlying recurrent pregnancy loss and recurrent implantation failure through multivariate analysis of peripheral blood immune signatures. Natural killer cells display dysregulated receptor profiles in both conditions, characterized by elevated activating NKp30+ cells and reduced expression of inhibitory checkpoint receptors like TIGIT and CD69, which normally suppress cytotoxicity and promote fetal tolerance. Monocyte subsets are aberrantly activated across classical, intermediate, and non-classical populations, with increased CD11b activation markers and elevated expression of the chemokine receptor CX3CR1, indicating enhanced inflammatory potential. Myeloid-derived suppressor cells, which should expand during healthy pregnancy to dampen inflammation, are paradoxically elevated rather than depleted in affected women, while regulatory T cells often decline numerically or lose suppressive capacity. The final predictive models integrated multiple parameters including NKp30+ cytotoxic NK cells, activated monocyte subsets, myeloid-derived suppressor cell abundance, and specific immune cell clusters, suggesting that coordinated disruption of innate immune regulation—rather than single pathway dysfunction—drives both recurrent pregnancy loss and implantation failure. Notably, endometriosis was overrepresented in the recurrent implantation failure group and showed activation of the NKp30-BAG6 axis, indicating tissue-specific immune alterations in this inflammatory condition.

title: Immune Microenvironment of Hair Follicles in Tumor Immunotherapy
summary:
# Summary

This article examines how immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) used in cancer immunotherapy disrupt the normally balanced immune microenvironment of hair follicles, leading to therapy-associated alopecia. ICIs block the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint pathway to reinvigorate anti-tumor T cell responses, but simultaneously activate excessive T cell and macrophage activity within hair follicles through enhanced NF-κB signaling and upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and interferon-gamma. This aberrant immune activation overwhelms the regulatory mechanisms that normally maintain immune homeostasis—particularly regulatory T cells and their immunosuppressive cytokines IL-10 and TGF-β—resulting in increased CD8+ T cell infiltration that triggers follicular cell apoptosis via perforin and granzyme release. The accumulation of inflammatory cytokines directly inhibits hair follicle stem cell proliferation and differentiation while promoting progression to the catagen phase, ultimately manifesting as alopecia correlating with TNF-α expression levels in scalp tissue. These findings underscore how the immunological mechanisms that benefit systemic anti-tumor immunity create pathological consequences when applied to immune-privileged tissue microenvironments like hair follicles.

title: Guttate Psoriasis Triggered by Streptococcal Pharyngitis in Older Patients: A Case Report
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents guttate psoriasis triggered by group A streptococcal pharyngitis in an older patient, highlighting the underlying immunological mechanisms driving this post-infectious condition. Group A streptococcal infection initiates disease through molecular mimicry, wherein streptococcal M proteins share epitopes with keratin, leading to T-cell cross-reactivity and skin-targeted inflammation. The dysregulated Th17 helper axis subsequently activates pathogenic cytokines including IL-17, IL-22, IL-23, and TNF-α, which drive keratinocyte hyperproliferation and characteristic guttate lesions. The patient's medical history of endometriosis and colon cancer, both associated with immune dysregulation, likely increased her susceptibility to developing this post-infectious immune-mediated condition. Treatment with topical clobetasol propionate effectively suppressed the inflammatory cascade by inhibiting these Th17-derived cytokines, with alternative approaches including phototherapy, vitamin D supplementation, and biologic agents targeting IL-17 and IL-23 pathways available for extensive disease.

title: Inflammation-Mediated Immune Imbalance in the Pathogenesis of Diabetic Cataracts
summary: Diabetes increases cataract risk fivefold through immune-mediated inflammation characterized by disruption of the blood-aqueous barrier and altered monocyte dynamics. Diabetes shifts classical monocytes toward a non-classical phenotype and enhances their migration and adhesion, leading to increased immune cell infiltration into the lens. This immune dysregulation is reflected in elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and IFN-γ) alongside reduced anti-inflammatory IL-10 in both peripheral blood and aqueous humor, with TNF and IFN-γ signaling pathways upregulated. The resulting intraocular inflammation triggers apoptotic pathways in lens epithelial cells, evidenced by elevated caspase-3 and IL-6 levels, ultimately accelerating cataract development. Key marker genes including TGFβ2, MYLK, and VEGFC regulate barrier integrity and monocyte trafficking, while phagocytosis and lipid raft pathways drive enhanced immune cell migration. This multi-layered inflammatory cascade demonstrates how diabetes-induced immune imbalance, combining both increased pro-inflammatory signaling and impaired barrier function, drives progressive lens damage and cataract formation.

title: Exploring the Endocannabinoid System’s Influence on Mammary Tissue and Breast Milk Inflammation in Maternal Chronic Obesity
summary:
# Summary

Maternal chronic obesity triggers a hyperactive endocannabinoid system that drives low-grade systemic inflammation, primarily through pro-inflammatory signaling from white adipose tissue and macrophage infiltration and M1 polarization in obese adipose tissue. This inflammatory state extends to mammary tissue, where obesity disrupts gland development by reducing prolactin secretion, decreasing myoepithelial cell numbers, and promoting adipose tissue expansion and fibrosis, ultimately impairing lactogenesis and milk letdown. Notably, cannabinoid receptors 1 and 2 are expressed in the mammary gland and implicated in these inflammatory processes, while elevated endocannabinoid levels in the breast milk of obese women may further influence infant metabolism. The resulting structural and functional changes in the mammary gland correlate with reduced breastfeeding initiation and duration in women with obesity, suggesting that the endocannabinoid system's dysregulation during obesity creates persistent lactation complications alongside broader metabolic dysfunction.

title: Periodontitis-Induced Immune Reprogramming: Implications for Cancer Immunotherapy Response
summary:
# Summary

Chronic periodontitis induces systemic immune reprogramming through bacterial lipopolysaccharides from pathogens such as *Porphyromonas gingivalis* and *Fusobacterium nucleatum*, which activate pattern recognition receptors (TLR2, TLR4, NOD2) and trigger NF-κB and MAPK signaling cascades. This leads to elevated circulating levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and C-reactive protein, alongside upregulation of endothelial adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1, E-selectin) that promote persistent low-grade systemic inflammation. At the mechanistic level, toll-like receptor engagement activates ATP-citrate lyase and drives metabolic reprogramming toward aerobic glycolysis and glutaminolysis in macrophages, which generates lactate that serves as a substrate for histone lactylation—a post-translational modification that directly activates inflammatory gene expression. Continuous NF-κB and STAT3 signaling perpetuates oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, while trained monocytes maintain elevated basal cytokine production that sustains chronic inflammation. Additionally, the inflammatory microenvironment promotes T-cell exhaustion through PD-1 upregulation, potentially impairing anti-tumor immunity and cancer immunotherapy efficacy.

title: IL-37 Ameliorates Chronic Endometritis by Attenuating Epithelial—Mesenchymal Transition and Promoting M2 Macrophage Polarization
summary:
# Summary

IL-37 ameliorates chronic endometritis by simultaneously inhibiting epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and promoting M2 macrophage polarization through coordinated activation of STAT6 and Smad3 signaling pathways. IL-37 upregulates the epithelial marker E-cadherin while downregulating mesenchymal markers vimentin and N-cadherin, thereby reducing pathological cell migration and preserving endometrial structural integrity. The cytokine suppresses pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages (reducing CD86, IL-6, iNOS, and IL-1β expression) while enhancing M2 polarization (increasing CD206 and ARG1 expression), effectively reversing the LPS-induced pro-inflammatory environment. Intracellularly, IL-37 binds Smad3 and promotes nuclear translocation of both STAT6 and Smad3, where they upregulate PTPN expression to inhibit downstream inflammatory cascades including ERK, p38, JNK, PI3K, and NF-κB signaling. These coordinated mechanisms reduce inflammatory infiltration, collagen deposition, and tissue damage while restoring endometrial homeostasis, offering therapeutic potential for treating a condition that causes infertility, recurrent implantation failure, and miscarriage.

title: The Role of Rarely Studied Chemokines in Tumor Progression in Multiple Myeloma (MM)
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the roles of rarely studied chemokines—CXCL13, CXCL14, CXCL16, and CXCL17—in multiple myeloma progression, with implications for understanding inflammatory immune responses in hematologic malignancies. CXCL13, produced by myeloid cells, mesenchymal stromal cells, and MM cells themselves, binds CXCR5 on B cells and MM cells to promote tumor cell proliferation, migration, and recruitment of immunosuppressive myeloid-derived suppressor cells and M2 macrophages; elevated CXCL13 is driven by TGF-β signaling and correlates with chemoresistance to bortezomib through increased anti-apoptotic factors. CXCL14, CXCL16, and CXCL17 display context-dependent roles: CXCL14 exhibits angiostatic activity and can suppress leukemia-initiating stem cells, while CXCL16 promotes MM cell adhesion and correlates with improved prognosis in certain molecular subgroups. Therapeutically, disrupting the CXCL13-CXCR5 axis with inhibitors or neutralizing antibodies shows promise, and Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors can reduce macrophage-derived CXCL13. These findings highlight how chemokine signaling in the bone marrow microenvironment orchestrates both pro-tumoral inflammation and therapeutic resistance in multiple myeloma.

title: Benign, persistent, and invasive: mechanistic and translational approaches to middle‑ear cholesteatoma
summary:
# Summary

Cholesteatoma pathogenesis involves a coordinated inflammatory cascade wherein proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8) converge on NF-κB, MAPK, and JAK–STAT signaling hubs to promote keratinocyte proliferation while suppressing apoptosis. Concurrently, stromal fibroblasts and inflammatory cells secrete RANKL, activin A, and PTHrP, which drive osteoclastogenesis and bone erosion; this process is amplified by matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9, MMP-14) that mediate extracellular matrix degradation. Persistent microbial biofilms activate toll-like receptors and sustain NF-κB signaling, while reactive oxygen species amplify inflammation and further promote matrix remodeling. Emerging mechanistic insights reveal that epithelial plasticity involving partial epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, dysregulated microRNAs (miR-223-3p, miR-142-5p), epigenetic modifications (H3K27ac-mediated super-enhancer activity), and oxidative stress collectively drive chronicity and postoperative recurrence, establishing cholesteatoma as a multifactorial destructive inflammatory disease.

title: From Tumor Marker to Congestion Indicator: The Expanding Role of CA‐125 in Heart Failure and Beyond
summary: CA-125, a high-molecular-weight glycoprotein encoded by the MUC16 gene, is synthesized and released by mesothelial cells in response to mechanical stress, inflammation, and fluid overload, with JNK signaling pathway activation playing a central regulatory role. The proinflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IFN-γ stimulate MUC16 gene expression, while IL-1β directly triggers CA-125 secretion, and CA-125 levels correlate positively with pro-inflammatory markers including VEGF, TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, and IL-1β. Beyond its traditional role as a tumor marker, elevated CA-125 has emerged as a robust biomarker for systemic and venous congestion across multiple organ systems, correlating with clinical signs of fluid overload including jugular venous distension, ascites, pulmonary edema, and pleural effusions. In heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and cirrhosis, elevated CA-125 levels independently predict worse outcomes including increased hospitalization rates, mortality, and disease progression, with predictive power equal to or superior to traditional biomarkers like NT-proBNP. The underlying mechanism involves venous congestion triggering inflammatory mediator and pro-oxidant factor release, activating endothelial dysfunction and perpetuating a cycle of chronic inflammation and volume overload that sustains elevated CA-125 production.

title: Mechanistic insights into inflammatory cytokines in adenomyosis-induced infertility (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, a chronic inflammatory disease dependent on aberrant cytokine expression, directly causes infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Elevated levels of key inflammatory cytokines—including IL-6, CXCL8, IL-1B, TNF-α, and TGF-β—disrupt the immune barrier at the endometrial-myometrial junction and are associated with implantation failure and reduced ovarian responsiveness. IL-6 signaling operates centrally in disease pathogenesis, binding to soluble IL-6 receptors and activating the JAK2/STAT3 pathway, which suppresses expression of genes critical for endometrial receptivity (HOXA10, HOXA11, and leukemia inhibitory factor) and promotes both ectopic endometrial proliferation and fibrosis. Additionally, the CXCL8-CXCR1 axis promotes invasive growth of ectopic tissue, while dysregulated immune cells (macrophages, NK cells, and mast cells) perpetuate chronic inflammation driving adenomyosis progression. Patients with adenomyosis exhibit significantly reduced clinical pregnancy rates and elevated miscarriage risk. Therapeutic targeting of these inflammatory axes, including IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibodies like tocilizumab and TGF-β inhibitors, shows promise in reversing disease-associated reproductive impairment.

title: Co-delivery of endometrial mesenchymal stem cells and macrophages with an electrospun patch suppresses endometrial fibrosis via IL-10 related signaling
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine adhesion, characterized by endometrial fibrosis and a leading cause of uterine infertility, can result from trauma, infection, or repeated curettage. This study demonstrates that co-delivery of human endometrial mesenchymal stem cells (H-EMSCs) and macrophages via a PCL-HA electrospun scaffold significantly reduces endometrial fibrosis through IL-10-dependent mechanisms. The co-delivery system promotes macrophage polarization from pro-inflammatory M1 (CD80+/CD86+) to anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes while enhancing IL-10 expression and reducing TNF-α, thereby shifting the endometrial microenvironment toward tissue repair and wound healing. H-EMSCs and macrophages synergistically regulate each other through paracrine signaling, with the scaffold supporting H-EMSCs attachment and proliferation while facilitating their expression of healing-related genes including IL-10, VEGFA, and TGF-β. IL-10 emerges as a critical anti-fibrotic factor in suppressing endometrial fibrosis, offering a novel cell-based and scaffold-mediated therapeutic approach to treat intrauterine adhesion.

title: Exosome-delivered bioactive molecules regulate macrophage polarization in atherosclerosis and myocardial infarction: mechanisms and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

Exosomes regulate macrophage polarization through delivery of bioactive molecules including microRNAs, serving as critical mediators of intercellular communication that influence disease progression in atherosclerosis and cardiac pathology. The dynamic balance between pro-inflammatory M1 and anti-inflammatory M2 macrophage phenotypes is a key determinant of atherosclerosis plaque stability and cardiac repair, with dysregulation contributing to adverse remodeling and heart failure. Multiple exosomal microRNAs—including miR-205, miR-26-5p, miR-934, and miR-1246—promote M2 polarization primarily through suppression of PTEN and activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling axis, while exosomal miR-221 drives pro-inflammatory M1 polarization via STAT1 activation. These polarized macrophages subsequently secrete effector molecules like CXCL13 that promote pathological outcomes including metastasis and tissue remodeling. The exosome biogenesis pathway, involving ESCRT machinery, nSMase2-mediated MVB formation, and SNARE/RabGTPase-dependent release, represents a therapeutic target for modulating macrophage phenotype and mitigating chronic inflammatory disease.

title: TDO2-Associated Tryptophan Metabolism Correlates with Impaired Tertiary Lymphoid Structure Maturation and Reduced B Cell Class Switching in Breast Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that elevated TDO2-mediated tryptophan catabolism creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment in breast cancer by impairing tertiary lymphoid structure (TLS) maturation and B cell function. TDO2 converts tryptophan to kynurenine, which activates the aryl hydrocarbon receptor to expand regulatory T cells and suppress effector T cell activity, thereby creating an immune-cold phenotype particularly enriched in Luminal A tumors. High TDO2 expression is spatially associated with TLS-deficient regions and correlates inversely with TLS maturation signatures, resulting in suppressed B cell class-switch recombination and reduced infiltration of CD20+ B cells and CXCL9+ immune cells. The impaired B cell class switching reflects incomplete TLS maturation and compromises the generation of high-affinity IgG antibodies critical for humoral immunity and antitumor responses. These findings suggest that targeting the TDO2-kynurenine axis represents a therapeutic strategy to restore TLS formation, enhance B cell immunity, and improve immunotherapy efficacy in breast cancer.

title: Nephroprotective effects of visnagin through modulation of macrophage polarization, oxidative stress, inflammation and apoptosis in renal I/R injury
summary:
# Summary

Renal ischemia-reperfusion injury triggers oxidative stress, inflammation, and apoptosis through elevated malondialdehyde, pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), and apoptotic markers (Bax, caspase-3), while depleting protective glutathione. Visnagin, the active component of Ammi visnaga seed extract, attenuates these pathological processes at 60 mg/kg by reducing oxidative stress markers and inflammatory cytokines while restoring glutathione levels and suppressing apoptotic signaling. The compound exerts additional protection by reversing injury-induced macrophage polarization, decreasing M1 pro-inflammatory markers (CD86) and increasing M2 anti-inflammatory markers (CD163), thereby shifting the immunological environment toward tissue repair. These multi-targeted nephroprotective mechanisms—addressing oxidative stress, inflammation, apoptosis, and macrophage polarization—suggest visnagin as a promising therapeutic candidate for preventing renal ischemia-reperfusion injury.

title: Integrating Preoperative NLR and PLR with Perioperative Clinical Factors: A Nomogram for Predicting Postoperative CRP Elevation After Laparoscopic Hysterectomy
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a nomogram to predict postoperative C-reactive protein (CRP) elevation following laparoscopic hysterectomy by integrating preoperative inflammatory markers with perioperative clinical factors. CRP is synthesized by hepatocytes in response to interleukin-6 (IL-6), with elevated postoperative CRP levels associated with serious complications including surgical site infections and prolonged ileus. The analysis identified multiple independent predictors of postoperative CRP elevation: preoperative neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), body mass index (BMI), heart rate, estimated blood loss, and intraoperative fluid infusion quantity. Elevated preoperative NLR reflects pre-existing subclinical inflammation that predisposes to exaggerated postoperative inflammatory responses, as surgical trauma triggers rapid neutrophil release and stress hormone secretion (catecholamines and cortisol) that redistribute lymphocytes and amplify systemic inflammation. Adipose tissue contributes to baseline inflammatory burden by secreting IL-6 and TNF-α, particularly in patients with elevated BMI, while platelets actively mediate inflammatory signaling through cytokine and chemokine release. This integrated nomogram provides a practical tool for identifying patients at risk for pronounced postoperative inflammation after hysterectomy.

title: Functional restoration of uterine architecture and fertility via a 3D-printed biomimetic scaffold: The role of macrophage-driven immunomodulation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that a three-layered poly(L-lactide-co-caprolactone) (PLCL) scaffold fabricated via melt electrowriting effectively restores uterine architecture and fertility following surgical damage by leveraging macrophage-driven immunomodulation. The biomimetic scaffold enhances macrophage recruitment and polarization toward the pro-regenerative M2 phenotype, characterized by upregulation of key genes including SPP1, LGALS3, and TREM2, which establish a pro-regenerative tissue microenvironment. This favorable immunological context stimulates parenchymal cell proliferation and migration through activation of JAK-STAT3 and YAP-Hippo signaling pathways, ultimately culminating in myofibroblast activation, α-SMA upregulation, and robust tissue regeneration. The PLCL scaffold's superior biocompatibility, tunable degradation profile, and precise fiber architecture—providing mechanical integrity while facilitating controlled cell infiltration—overcome limitations of conventional biomaterials and enable restoration of functional uterine tissue. These findings highlight the critical role of immunomodulation in regenerative medicine and demonstrate that orchestrating M2 macrophage-mediated responses through engineered scaffolds represents a promising therapeutic strategy for repairing uterine defects and improving pregnancy outcomes in women of reproductive age.

title: Targeting the CMKLR1‐Mediated Signaling Rebalances Immunometabolism State in Middle‐Age Testicular Macrophages
summary: This study reveals that CMKLR1, a receptor for adipose-derived signals including chemerin and resolvins, drives a pro-inflammatory immunometabolic shift in testicular macrophages during aging. In middle-aged mice, CMKLR1-positive macrophages undergo metabolic reprogramming toward glycolysis and M1 pro-inflammatory polarization, characterized by elevated expression of glycolytic enzymes (LDHA, HK1, PKM), pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL1B, TNF), and reduced oxidative phosphorylation. This immunometabolic dysfunction impairs spermatogenesis and contributes to age-related reproductive decline. Inhibiting CMKLR1 through genetic ablation or treatment with the antagonist peptide P12C5 reverses this pro-inflammatory phenotype and restores spermatogenesis, while high-intensity interval training (HIIT) similarly rescues reproductive function by suppressing the CMKLR1-mediated inflammatory signature. These findings establish CMKLR1-mediated metabolic reprogramming as a key mechanism linking obesity-associated adipose signaling to inflammaging and age-related testicular dysfunction, offering potential therapeutic targets for age-related reproductive decline.

title: In vitro assays for investigating the immunomodulatory properties of human mesenchymal stromal cells
summary: Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) function primarily as orchestrators of the immune response through their potent immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties, which are mediated by paracrine signaling via a complex secretome containing cytokines, chemokines, growth factors, and extracellular vesicles. MSCs suppress general immune cell proliferation while selectively promoting regulatory T cells and regulatory B cells, and they polarize macrophages and dendritic cells toward anti-inflammatory phenotypes, thereby modulating and resolving inflammatory responses. The immunomodulatory capacity of MSCs varies significantly depending on tissue source (bone marrow, adipose tissue, umbilical cord), donor health status, donor age, and glucose conditions, highlighting the importance of standardization in therapeutic applications. MSC function can be enhanced by glucocorticoids such as hydrocortisone and dexamethasone, or blocked by immune inhibitors including infliximab, tacrolimus, and cyclosporin, indicating that their immunosuppressive effects depend on specific molecular pathways. In vitro assays predominantly utilize peripheral blood mononuclear cells, isolated T cells, and macrophages as target immune cells to assess MSC-mediated changes in proliferation and polarization. These findings underscore MSCs' potential as cell-based therapeutics for inflammatory and autoimmune conditions, though their variable properties necessitate careful characterization before clinical implementation.

title: Mechanisms of IL‐17A Neutralisation in Alleviating Renal Fibrosis and Inflammation in Spontaneously Hypertensive Rats
summary: This study demonstrates that IL-17A is a critical mediator of renal fibrosis and hypertension in spontaneously hypertensive rats, operating through multiple interconnected pathways. IL-17A directly stimulates fibroblasts and tubular epithelial cells to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines and TGF-β, while simultaneously promoting extracellular matrix synthesis and inhibiting its degradation, thereby driving renal fibrosis. Treatment with IL-17A neutralizing antibodies significantly attenuated hypertension progression, improved renal function markers (reducing serum creatinine, urea, and proteinuria), and ameliorated renal tissue damage including glomerular disorganization and tubular atrophy. The therapeutic effects of IL-17A neutralization were mediated by suppression of M2 macrophage polarization and concurrent downregulation of the TGF-β/Smad pathway, along with inhibition of pro-inflammatory signaling cascades including JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT, and NF-κB pathways. These findings establish IL-17A as a targetable node in the inflammatory cascade driving renal fibrosis, with potential implications for anti-fibrotic therapeutic strategies in hypertension-related renal disease.

title: Shaping the future of abdominal and pelvic pain research with novel scientific and technological advances
summary: This article examines the inflammatory and immune mechanisms underlying chronic pelvic pain (CPP) across multiple organ systems. Inflammaging, a state of chronic low-grade systemic inflammation, emerges as a central driver that activates pro-inflammatory macrophages and mast cells, subsequently elevating proinflammatory cytokines and contributing to conditions ranging from irritable bowel syndrome to interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS). Mast cells play a critical role in pain pathways through direct interactions with sensory neurons via the MrgprB2 receptor, promoting visceral hypersensitivity and cross-organ sensitization between bladder and colon. In IC/BPS specifically, the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway drives symptom severity through recruitment of CXCR3+ T cells, while in vulvodynia and localized provoked vulvodynia, elevated nerve growth factor (NGF) triggers B cell infiltration and mucosal immune responses. The article highlights that altered immune cell infiltration, cytokine imbalances, and mast cell-sensory neuron circuits are unifying mechanisms across diverse pelvic pain conditions, suggesting that targeting inflammaging and innate immune pathways could provide therapeutic benefits for multiple CPP disorders.

title: Exosomal miR-199a-3p derived from follicular fluid modulates the immune microenvironment in polycystic ovary syndrome by regulating macrophage polarization
summary: This study identifies a novel mechanism by which polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) establishes a pro-inflammatory ovarian microenvironment through exosomal miR-199a-3p-mediated macrophage polarization. Exosomes derived from PCOS follicular fluid carry elevated levels of miR-199a-3p, which is transferred to macrophages where it suppresses mTOR expression, thereby promoting M1 (pro-inflammatory) polarization while simultaneously suppressing M2 (anti-inflammatory) polarization. This shift in macrophage balance increases pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 while decreasing anti-inflammatory factors IL-10 and TGF-β1, creating the chronic low-grade inflammation characteristic of PCOS that disrupts normal follicular development and ovulation. The mTOR pathway emerges as a critical regulatory node through which miR-199a-3p orchestrates the M1/M2 imbalance. These findings illuminate how follicular fluid exosomes contribute to PCOS pathogenesis through immune dysregulation, suggesting potential therapeutic targets for restoring ovarian immune homeostasis and reproductive function in affected patients.

title: Osteoimmunologic and Immune-Aging Signatures in Postmenopausal Women with Periodontitis and Low Bone Mineral Density: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study reveals a convergent osteoimmunologic mechanism linking periodontitis and low bone mineral density in postmenopausal women through dysregulated bone remodeling. Both conditions are associated with elevated salivary RANKL/OPG ratios and increased systemic inflammatory burden, suggesting that estrogen deficiency disrupts the RANKL/OPG balance, leading to enhanced osteoclast activity and bone loss. Bacterial biofilms in periodontal lesions trigger multiple immune cell types (T cells, B cells, fibroblasts, and osteoblast-lineage cells) to produce RANKL, while inflammatory mediators including IL-6 and TNF-α further promote osteoclastogenesis. Additionally, the study identified immune-aging signatures characterized by late-differentiated CD8+ T cell phenotypes and CMV seropositivity-associated immunosenescence markers in affected women, with an inflammaging index incorporating elevated hs-CRP, IL-6, and TNF-α. These findings suggest that periodontitis contributes to systemic inflammatory burden through a large chronically inflamed tissue compartment and that age-related immune remodeling compounds the effects of estrogen deficiency, establishing a bidirectional link between oral and skeletal health that may extend to other chronic inflammatory conditions including endometriosis.

title: Prostaglandin E2 induces dendritic cell dysfunction in skin involvement of breast cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elevated prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) levels in skin lesions of breast cancer patients directly impair dendritic cell (DC) function, contributing to immune evasion and poor clinical outcomes. PGE2 inhibits DC activation and antigen-presenting capacity by suppressing expression of HLA-DR, CD80, and CD86, while simultaneously downregulating critical antigen-processing genes including TAP1, TAP2, and HLA family members. As a result, DCs from lesional skin exhibit reduced ability to prime CD8+ T lymphocytes, leading to diminished T cell proliferation and cytotoxic activity. Notably, patients with abundant activated CD141+ DCs and low PGE2 expression in skin lesions showed significantly improved treatment response and prolonged overall survival, establishing an inverse relationship between PGE2 levels and DC infiltration. Pharmacological inhibition of PGE2 through EP2/EP4 antagonists or COX inhibition restored DC activation and T cell responses while attenuating skin involvement, identifying PGE2 as a therapeutic target for managing breast cancer patients with cutaneous manifestations.

title: CXCL-10: a new candidate for melanoma therapy?
summary:
# Summary

CXCL10 is a chemokine ligand that binds to CXCR3 and is predominantly expressed by multiple immune cell types including T helper cells, cytotoxic T lymphocytes, dendritic cells, macrophages, and natural killer cells. CXCL10 expression is induced by interferon-gamma, interferon-alpha/beta, TNF-alpha, and NF-κB signaling under pro-inflammatory conditions. The CXCL10/CXCR3 axis plays a critical role in regulating immune responses by promoting Th1 cell migration while inhibiting Th2 cell migration, thereby facilitating lymphocyte infiltration and chemotaxis to tumor microenvironments. In melanoma, CXCL10 demonstrates dual functionality acting as a double-edged sword: it can reduce melanoma invasion, proliferation, and metastasis through immune-mediated mechanisms, yet overexpression of CXCL10 and CXCR3 is associated with advanced tumor stages and paradoxically promotes angiogenesis and leukocyte trafficking through paracrine tumor-stromal interactions. The differential activation of CXCR3 isoforms contributes to these opposing effects, with CXCR3-A promoting cell proliferation and chemotaxis while CXCR3-B suppresses cellular growth and angiogenesis.

title: Plasma extracellular vesicle modulate immune cell transcriptional responses following acute myocardial infarction
summary: Following acute myocardial infarction, plasma extracellular vesicles (EVs) enriched in miRNA-320b are rapidly elevated and mediate a coordinated inflammatory immune response. Endothelial cells upregulate and selectively package miRNA-320b into EVs upon TNF-α stimulation, and these EV-associated miRNAs subsequently transfer to circulating neutrophils and monocytes, where they modulate target gene expression involved in adhesion signaling, cytokine production, and myeloid cell activation. The EV-delivered miRNA-320b enhances immune cell adhesion capacity and promotes their interaction with endothelial cells through mechanisms involving VCAM-1 and VLA-4 interactions, while simultaneously priming cells for enhanced cytokine production including IL-6 and TNF. EV-associated VCAM-1 and miRNA-320b are also required for mobilizing splenic immune cell reserves to the peripheral circulation early after MI. This study reveals a mechanistic pathway in which endothelial cell-derived EVs function as intercellular communication vehicles that prime immune cells for both enhanced adhesion and inflammatory responses during acute cardiac injury, establishing EVs as critical mediators of post-MI immune mobilization.

title: Tumor-associated macrophages: role in cancer development and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) play critical roles in cancer development through dual mechanisms involving both pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive pathways. M1 macrophages, stimulated by interferon-γ, TNF-α, and lipopolysaccharide, promote tumor initiation and differentiate naïve CD4+ T cells into pro-inflammatory Th1 and Th17 effector cells through IL-12 and IL-23 secretion. Conversely, M2 macrophages, activated by IL-4, M-CSF, and IL-10, promote tumor progression and angiogenesis by secreting VEGF, MMP9, and IL-10 while suppressing adaptive immunity through recruitment of regulatory T cells and expression of immunosuppressive ligands like PD-L1. Key signaling pathways including NF-κB, STAT3, and HIF-1 promote the protumoral TAM phenotype, while the chemokine CXCL12 drives TAM migration, aggregation, and differentiation toward immunosuppressive states in hypoxic tumor regions. Therapeutic interventions targeting COX-2, CXCL12, or IKKα show promise in reducing TAM-mediated tumor progression, suggesting that modulating macrophage polarization represents a viable strategy for cancer treatment.

title: Immune cytokines as a bridge linking the gut–liver–ovary axis in the pathogenesis of premature ovarian failure
summary:
# Summary

Premature ovarian failure (POF) involves dysregulation of the gut–liver–ovary axis, where immune tolerance mediated by regulatory T cells (Tregs) is critically compromised. Tregs, which depend on the transcription factor Foxp3 and secrete immunosuppressive cytokines including IL-10, TGF-β, and IL-2, normally suppress autoreactive T cell activation and protect ovarian function. However, in POF, excessive interferon-γ (IFN-γ) production by activated CD4+ T cells and Th17 cells disrupts this balance by suppressing bile acid metabolism, inducing intestinal epithelial apoptosis, and triggering aberrant MHC class II expression on ovarian granulosa cells. Gut dysbiosis and impaired bile acid metabolism create a vicious cycle: dysbiotic microbiota fail to produce short-chain fatty acids and promote beneficial bacteria like Clostridium clusters IV and XIVa that normally enhance Treg differentiation, while reduced bile acid signaling through FXR and TGR5 receptors diminishes anti-inflammatory responses. Therapeutic interventions including probiotic supplementation (particularly Lactobacillus reuteri), fecal microbiota transplantation, traditional Chinese medicine formulations, and mesenchymal stem cell therapies restore Treg/Th17 balance and ovarian function by reestablishing the gut–liver–ovary axis homeostasis.

title: Inflammatory milieu and role of epigenetic modifications in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
summary: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) is characterized by near-universal TP53 mutations and homologous recombination deficiencies, and emerges from recurrent ovulatory injury that creates a chronic pro-inflammatory microenvironment enriched in cytokines and reactive oxygen species. This chronic inflammation activates NF-κB and STAT3 signaling cascades, which drive tumor proliferation, angiogenesis, invasion, and metastasis while simultaneously promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition and immune suppression. Epigenetic mechanisms including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs contribute to tumor plasticity and chemoresistance, particularly through cytokine-mediated recruitment of DNMT1 and EZH2 that silence tumor suppressors via DNA hypermethylation and H3K27me3. Chronic interferon signaling further promotes tumor adaptation and therapy resistance by upregulating PD-L1 expression through STAT1-BCL3 signaling and recruiting immunosuppressive regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting these pathways—including histone deacetylase inhibitors, DNA methyltransferase inhibitors, immune checkpoint inhibitors, and anti-inflammatory drugs—show promise in overcoming chemoresistance. Current standard treatment incorporates platinum-taxane chemotherapy alongside PARP inhibitors and anti-angiogenic agents, yet many patients experience relapse, underscoring the need for multi-targeted approaches addressing both the inflammatory microenvironment and epigenetic drivers of HGSOC progression.

title: miRNA/mRNA analysis of increased TGF-β pathways drive epithelial-mesenchymal transition and regulatory T cell differentiation
summary:
# Summary

Chlamydia trachomatis infection drives endometrial pathology through dysregulation of microRNA-mediated immune and epithelial processes, ultimately promoting tissue fibrosis and reproductive complications. The study reveals that endometriotic tissues upregulate key components of the TGF-β signaling pathway—including TGFB2, TGFBR2, SMAD3, and ZEB2—which collectively activate epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and suppress expression of miRNAs that normally restrain EMT and fibrosis. Concurrently, this pathway promotes regulatory T cell (Treg) differentiation while inhibiting pathogenic Th17 responses, creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment characterized by elevated IL-10 and TGF-β secretion. Chlamydial infection also triggers innate immune activation through pathogen recognition receptors, leading to damaging neutrophil infiltration that releases reactive oxygen species and matrix metalloproteases. Additionally, endometrial tissue shows robust upregulation of genes supporting germinal center formation (BCL6, CXCL13, FCAR) and proinflammatory mediators (IL1B, PTGS2), which correlate with pain and chronic endometritis-associated infertility. Together, these findings demonstrate that increased TGF-β pathway activity represents a central mechanism linking chlamydial infection to EMT, fibrotic scarring, and the reproductive complications of endometriosis.
