title: The inhibition of deep peritoneal endometriosis by Andrographolide through macrophage M1 activity in an endometriosis mice model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that andrographolide, a bioactive compound from *Andrographis paniculata*, inhibits deep peritoneal endometriosis in a mouse model through macrophage polarization toward the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype. Andrographolide activates M1 macrophages while suppressing M2 macrophages, which are responsible for promoting endometriosis progression through angiogenesis and tissue remodeling. The mechanistic pathway involves andrographolide activation of the PI3K/AKT/eNOS signaling cascade and downstream suppression of pro-inflammatory and pro-angiogenic cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and VEGF. In the experimental model, intermediate and high doses of andrographolide significantly reduced endometriosis lesion area and increased the M1/M2 macrophage ratio in peritoneal fluid, with the highest dose (P3) achieving the smallest lesion areas and highest M1/M2 ratios. These findings suggest that andrographolide functions as an immunomodulator capable of shifting the peritoneal immune microenvironment away from the pro-angiogenic M2 activity that sustains endometriosis pathology.

title: Nanomedicine-Driven Modulation of Signaling Pathways in Hepatic Fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

Hepatic fibrosis develops through complex signaling pathways centered on hepatic stellate cell (HSC) activation and excessive extracellular matrix accumulation. TGF-β1 and PDGF are primary drivers of HSC transdifferentiation into myofibroblasts, while multiple etiologies including viral hepatitis B, alcoholic liver disease, and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease converge on shared inflammatory mechanisms—particularly NF-κB-mediated inflammation that promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production. HSC activation is sustained by interconnected pathways including Wnt/β-catenin signaling and paracrine communication from Kupffer cells and apoptotic hepatocytes, while dysregulated autophagy and impaired matrix metalloproteinase activity create a pathological feedback loop favoring collagen deposition. Progressive fibrosis leads to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma, with senescent HSCs contributing to hepatocyte genomic instability through their secretory phenotype. Emerging nanomedicine and gene editing approaches, including CRISPR/Cas9 technology and autophagy modulation strategies, offer promising avenues for interrupting these fibrotic cascades and restoring matrix homeostasis.

title: Exosomal lncRNA OTUD6B-AS1 as a pathogenic nanocarrier promotes inflammatory macrophage polarization in endometritis via a targetable ceRNA circuit
summary:
# Summary

Endometritis-associated inflammation is driven by a pathogenic exosomal circuit in which lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from bacterial pathogens enhances exosome biogenesis and secretion from endometrial epithelial cells via AKT-mediated phosphorylation of CREB and subsequent ATG16L1 upregulation. These LPS-derived exosomes efficiently transfer the long non-coding RNA OTUD6B-AS1 to macrophages, where it functions as a competing endogenous RNA to sequester miR-128, thereby relieving miR-128-mediated repression of Notch2 and amplifying NF-κB signaling to promote M1 macrophage polarization. This pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, characterized by elevated iNOS and reduced Arg1 expression, promotes inflammation, apoptosis, and extracellular matrix degradation that contribute to endometritis-associated infertility. Clinical validation demonstrated elevated exosomal lncRNA OTUD6B-AS1 and Notch2 alongside reduced miR-128 in endometritis tissues, establishing this ceRNA circuit as a targetable therapeutic axis for inflammatory disease resolution.

title: Diagnostic Utility of Neutrophil-to-Lymphocyte Ratio in Differentiating Benign and Malignant Ovarian Masses: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Malignant ovarian masses are associated with a significantly elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) compared to benign masses, suggesting potential diagnostic utility for differentiating between these conditions. Malignant tumors activate pro-inflammatory signaling pathways including NF-κB and JAK/STAT3, triggering production of multiple cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, G-CSF, GM-CSF, and IL-8) that stimulate neutrophil production while simultaneously suppressing lymphocyte-mediated antitumor immune responses. Tumor-associated neutrophils promote malignant progression through angiogenesis, extracellular matrix remodeling, and metastatic factors including VEGF, MMPs, and ROS. However, NLR elevation is not specific to malignancy—it is also elevated in benign inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, infections, and inflamed ovarian cysts, which limits its diagnostic specificity. While the meta-analysis demonstrates significantly higher mean NLR in malignant versus benign ovarian masses, the authors conclude that NLR exhibits only moderate diagnostic performance when used alone for ovarian mass differentiation.

title: Endometriosis immune microenvironment timing shifts: from immune escape to immune exhaustion
summary:
# Endometriosis Immune Microenvironment Dynamics: From Escape to Exhaustion

Endometriosis development is fundamentally linked to progressive immune system dysfunction that evolves through distinct phases, beginning with immune escape and culminating in immune exhaustion. Ectopic endometrial cells (EECs) actively suppress natural killer (NK) cell function through multiple mechanisms: they directly inhibit NK cells while simultaneously reducing the expression of activating receptors (NKG2D, NKP46, NK cell protein 30) and elevating inhibitory receptors (KIR2DL1, NKG2A), resulting in markedly reduced NK cell cytotoxicity in both peripheral blood and peritoneal cavity. This immunosuppressive microenvironment is sustained by EECs and associated stromal cells, which produce elevated levels of immunosuppressive factors including interleukin-6 (IL-6), transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β), interleukin-15 (IL-15), and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) that collectively impair macrophage phagocytosis, suppress mTOR-dependent NK cell growth and cytotoxicity, and reduce chemotaxis of NK cells to the peritoneal cavity. Additional defects include decreased CD8+ T cell activation, reduced NKT cell populations, and diminished dendritic cell maturation, all contributing to an exhausted immune phenotype incapable of controlling EEC proliferation and invasion. Hypoxic signaling through HIF-1α further perpetuates immune dysfunction by upregulating immunosuppressive microRNAs, establishing endometriosis as a disease of progressive immune tolerance rather than inflammation alone.

title: An Immunomodulating Peptide With Potential to Counteract Endometriosis
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by dysfunctional immune responses that fail to eliminate ectopic endometrial lesions, involving deficiencies in NK cells, CD8+ T cells, and immature dendritic cells that produce excess IL-12p40 and IFN-γ. The IL-12p40 isoform competitively inhibits IL-12p70-mediated cytotoxicity by NK cells, while elevated IFN-γ drives lesion progression without inducing apoptosis in endometrial stromal cells. The immunomodulating peptide IK14004 addresses this dysfunction by selectively enhancing IL-12p70 production while suppressing IL-12p40, and simultaneously boosting IL-2, IFN-β, and IL-12Rβ2 expression in NK and CD8+ T cells. These modifications restore the capacity of NK and CD8+ T cells to recognize and eliminate ectopic endometrial tissue. By uncoupling Type I from Type II interferon signaling and shifting the IL-12 balance toward the pro-cytotoxic p70 isoform, IK14004 represents a novel immunomodulatory approach to counteract the aberrant immune tolerance characteristic of endometriosis.

title: Peripheral Nerve–Cancer Interactions in the Tumor Microenvironment: A Three-Dimensional Framework Integrating Mechanisms, Modulators, and Therapeutic Strategies
summary:
# Summary

The peripheral nervous system plays a foundational role in cancer biology by actively shaping tumor initiation, progression, metastasis, and immune regulation through dynamic interactions between autonomic and sensory neural pathways and tumor cells within the microenvironment. Environmental factors including stress, psychological states, and pain modulate these nerve-cancer interactions, while perineural invasion—a distinct form of metastasis independent of lymphatic or vascular routes—associates with aggressive tumor phenotypes and poor survival, particularly in pancreatic cancer where incidence reaches 80-100%. Schwann cells, macrophages, and fibroblasts constitute a specialized perineural invasion niche where Schwann cells adopt dedifferentiated, repair-like phenotypes and secrete neurotrophic factors, neurotransmitters, and chemokines (notably CCL2) that promote cancer cell attraction and migration along nerves through L1CAM and adhesion molecule signaling. In metabolically stressed tumors, Schwann cells sense lactate and reprogram into immunosuppressive METTL16+/CD276+/NECTIN2+ phenotypes that suppress CD8+ T cell function and accelerate progression. Additionally, newly formed axons and neurites extending into tumors during neurogenesis release signaling molecules that promote malignant transformation and tumor progression, with increased adrenergic and sensory innervation documented during early precancerous lesions, establishing neural circuits as pivotal drivers of malignancy from inception through advanced disease.

title: The diagnostic value of CXCL8 in diabetes mellitus co-morbidity with tuberculosis
summary:
# Summary

Diabetes mellitus impairs immune function and significantly elevates the risk of tuberculosis (TB) co-infection, with elevated blood glucose levels facilitating drug-resistant TB development and treatment complications. CXCL8, a chemokine produced by white blood cells, emerges as a pivotal component of host immunity during Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection and is required for proper granuloma formation and immune responses. Plasma CXCL8 expression is markedly elevated in diabetic patients and especially in those with concurrent TB, correlating strongly with key TB infection markers (IFN-γ and IL-2) and neutrophil-related parameters including white blood cell count and neutrophil percentage. CXCL8 concentrations increase significantly when peripheral blood mononuclear cells are stimulated with MTB alone or in combination with elevated glucose, suggesting a synergistic inflammatory response in diabetes-TB co-morbidity. Plasma CXCL8 alone distinguishes diabetic from diabetic-TB patients with 65% sensitivity and 69% specificity, while combined detection with IFN-γ and IL-2 achieves superior diagnostic performance with 90% sensitivity and 89% specificity. These findings establish CXCL8 as a potential molecular biomarker for diagnosing TB in diabetic patients and suggest CXCL8-targeting interventions may mitigate excessive neutrophil infiltration in this high-risk population.

title: Involvement of increased arginase-1 +  group 2 innate lymphoid cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells in endometrial carcinoma: A pilot study
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: This pilot study reveals a critical immunological imbalance in endometrial carcinoma characterized by elevated arginase-1-expressing group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) and monocytic myeloid-derived suppressor cells (Mo-MDSCs) that work synergistically to promote immunosuppression. Patients with endometrial carcinoma displayed significantly increased levels of arginase-1+ ILC2s and Mo-MDSCs in both peripheral blood and tumor tissue, while levels of protective ILC1s and ILC3s were reduced. These immunosuppressive cells establish an ILC2-MDSC axis that enhances type II immune responses through elevated interleukin-25 and arginine metabolism dysregulation, thereby inhibiting T and B cell proliferation and function. Importantly, elevated arginase-1+ ILC2s and Mo-MDSCs were both independently associated with worse progression-free survival in endometrial carcinoma patients. Following chemotherapy, these immunosuppressive populations decreased while ILC3s recovered, correlating with clinical improvement. These findings suggest that the ILC2-MDSC-arginase-1 axis represents a key mechanism driving immune evasion in endometrial carcinoma and may be a target for therapeutic intervention to enhance anti-tumor immunity.

title: Immunology and ovarian cancers
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Epithelial ovarian cancer evades immune surveillance through multiple mechanisms involving immune checkpoint proteins and immunosuppressive cytokines, ultimately leading to chemoresistance and poor clinical outcomes. Tumor cells express checkpoint inhibitors including PD-L1, PD-L2, CTLA-4, B7-H3, and B7-H4, while simultaneously secreting immunosuppressive factors such as TGF-β, VEGF, IL-1, and PGE2 that directly suppress anti-tumor immunity. Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) further promote an immunosuppressive microenvironment through secretion of chemokines and inhibitory molecules, with elevated CD163+ M2 macrophage density correlating with poor overall and progression-free survival. Natural killer cells, which normally provide immune surveillance, become suppressed in this hostile immune microenvironment, while immunosuppressive M1/M2 macrophage ratios favor tumor progression. Multiple therapeutic approaches have been developed to reverse immune evasion, including checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, durvalumab, ipilimumab) that directly block inhibitory pathways, as well as indirect strategies targeting TAMs through CSF1R antagonists, macrophage repolarization via CD40 agonists and paclitaxel, and HDAC3 inhibition to prevent M2 polarization. These multi-targeted immunotherapeutic approaches represent promising strategies to restore anti-tumor immunity and overcome chemoresistance in ovarian cancer.

title: Role of prognostic biomarker decoy receptor 3 and immunomodulation in kidney diseases
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Decoy receptor 3 (DcR3), also known as TNFRSF6B, emerges as a critical immunomodulatory factor and prognostic biomarker in kidney disease progression. Elevated serum DcR3 levels are strongly associated with progression of chronic kidney disease and predict severe clinical outcomes including cardiovascular mortality, all-cause mortality, peritonitis in dialysis patients, and protein-energy wasting. DcR3 functions through dual mechanisms: it acts as a decoy receptor neutralizing three pro-apoptotic ligands (Fas ligand, LIGHT, and TL1A) and simultaneously promotes inflammation through NF-κB-mediated expression of adhesion molecules and cytokines. In kidney tissue, renal tubular epithelial cells expressing DcR3 suppress T cell apoptosis while protecting renal myofibroblasts from Fas-induced death, thereby promoting renal fibrosis and allograft dysfunction. Therapeutic DcR3 plasmid administration suppresses progressive IgA nephropathy and autoimmune crescentic glomerulonephritis in animal models by modulating dendritic cells and macrophages toward anti-inflammatory M2/Th2-like phenotypes. These findings establish DcR3 as both a biomarker of sustained low-grade systemic inflammation in kidney disease and a potential therapeutic target for immunomodulation.

title: Comparative effects of adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells and their exosomes on interleukin-1 beta-induced testicular inflammation in rats: An experimental study
summary:
# Summary

Interleukin-1 beta drives testicular inflammation (orchitis) by activating the NLRP3 inflammasome and disrupting the blood-testis barrier, leading to leukocyte infiltration, Sertoli cell damage, and impaired spermatogenesis. Both adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells (ASCs) and their exosomes mitigated this inflammation by suppressing NF-κB signaling and NLRP3 inflammasome activation while reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor-alpha and IL-6. Exosomes demonstrated superior therapeutic efficacy compared to whole ASCs, significantly reducing leukocyte infiltration and achieving near-complete histopathological repair of interstitial edema, vascular congestion, and Sertoli cell displacement. This superior performance was attributed to exosomes' efficient delivery of bioactive cargo including miR-21, miR-146a, and Annexin A1 across biological barriers, combined with their ability to inhibit chemokine-mediated leukocyte recruitment and promote tissue repair pathways. Notably, exosome treatment overcame limitations of conventional corticosteroid therapy, which fails to penetrate the blood-testis barrier and risks germ cell apoptosis, offering a dual mechanism of immunosuppression with tissue regeneration essential for sustaining testicular function recovery.

title: Global proteomics insights for a novel small compound targeting the non-integrin Laminin Receptor in a macrophage cell model
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a novel small molecule compound (C3) that targets the non-integrin laminin receptor (LR) as a potential anti-inflammatory therapeutic agent. Macrophages and monocytes, key components of the innate immune system, produce pro-inflammatory mediators that contribute to inflammatory diseases including endometriosis. The compound C3 mimics the anti-inflammatory properties of PEDF, a natural ligand of the laminin receptor known to reduce multiple pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-17A, TNF-α) and suppress MAPK and NF-κB signaling pathways. Global proteomics analysis revealed that C3 treatment of activated macrophages significantly reduced IL-1β expression and modulated multiple cellular pathways including oxidative phosphorylation, mTORC1 signaling, actin cytoskeleton reorganization, and translation. By targeting the LR-PEDF interface, compound C3 represents a novel pharmacological approach to suppress macrophage-driven inflammatory responses that may have therapeutic applications in inflammatory conditions.

title: Impact of pituitary pars intermedia dysfunction on inflammation within the equine reproductive tract of the mare
summary:
# Summary: PPID-Associated Reproductive Tract Inflammation in Mares

Pituitary pars intermedia dysfunction (PPID) is an age-related endocrinopathy that extends systemic inflammation into the reproductive tract through elevated circulating adrenocorticotropin hormone (ACTH) and increased secretion of pro-opiomelanocortin peptides. The dysregulation leads to marked upregulation of the pro-inflammatory cytokine interleukin-8 (IL-8/CXCL8) across systemic, endometrial, and follicular fluid compartments, with serum ACTH levels strongly correlating with endometrial CXCL8 expression and endometrial leukocyte infiltration. This persistent inflammatory environment compromises both uterine and ovarian function—impairing sperm and embryo receptivity in the endometrium while damaging follicular development and oocyte quality in the ovary—ultimately contributing to the subfertility observed in PPID-affected mares. The chronic elevation of CXCL8 mirrors mechanisms previously associated with endometriosis in humans, where this chemokine drives excessive leukocyte recruitment that disrupts reproductive competence. These findings demonstrate that PPID-associated inflammation extends beyond systemic manifestations to directly impair reproductive function through inflammatory cytokine-mediated immune dysregulation.

title: Soluble gp130 inhibits Th17 polarization in neutrophilic asthma by blocking IL-6 trans-signaling in dendritic cells
summary: IL-6 trans-signaling drives neutrophilic asthma pathogenesis by promoting Th17 polarization through dendritic cell-mediated IL-23 production. The IL-6/soluble IL-6 receptor complex activates gp130 on cells lacking membrane-bound IL-6 receptor, triggering STAT3 phosphorylation and upregulation of RORγt, the master transcription factor for Th17 differentiation. Soluble gp130 (sgp130), a selective inhibitor of IL-6 trans-signaling, effectively blocks this pathway by suppressing IL-23 expression in dendritic cells and reducing both Th17 polarization and airway neutrophilia while simultaneously enhancing regulatory T cell responses. The therapeutic potential of sgp130 is demonstrated through in vivo and ex vivo studies showing that IL-6 trans-signaling inhibition mitigates neutrophilic airway inflammation more effectively than classical IL-6 signaling inhibition, as the latter is linked to essential homeostatic functions. These findings suggest that selective blockade of IL-6 trans-signaling represents a mechanistically targeted therapeutic strategy for neutrophilic asthma, particularly relevant given the disease's association with corticosteroid resistance.

title: Molecular biology of targeting lymphangiogenesis in cancer: Therapeutic strategies and future promise
summary:
# Summary

Lymphangiogenesis—the formation of new lymphatic vessels—is a critical driver of tumor metastasis to lymph nodes, a process mediated primarily by the VEGF-C/D-VEGFR3 signaling axis. Tumor cells and stromal cells within the microenvironment produce high levels of VEGF ligands that activate lymphatic endothelial cells to proliferate, migrate, and form functional vessels; this signaling can be regulated at multiple levels by molecules including OTUD3, PEDF, and specific microRNAs. Newly formed lymphatic vessels facilitate both direct tumor cell invasion and immune suppression by creating permissive routes for metastatic dissemination while simultaneously promoting immune escape through altered lymph node architecture and lymphocyte filtration. Therapeutic strategies targeting lymphangiogenesis through VEGFR3 blockade, NRP2 inhibition, or disruption of pro-lymphangiogenic signaling have shown promise in preclinical models for reducing lymph node metastasis and tumor progression. The dual role of lymphatic vessels in supporting both normal immune surveillance and tumor-promoting processes underscores the complexity of exploiting lymphangiogenesis as a therapeutic target.

title: HDAC6 Promotes Host Defense Against Chlamydial Lung Infections by Regulating M2-Th2 Responses
summary:
# Summary

HDAC6 plays a critical protective role in host defense against Chlamydia muridarum respiratory infections by regulating immune responses through epigenetic mechanisms and cytoskeletal dynamics. Upon bacterial infection, C. muridarum upregulates HDAC6 expression, which acts as a key regulator of pathogen clearance by deacetylating α-tubulin to inhibit bacterial dissemination and facilitating autophagy-mediated bacterial elimination. HDAC6 deficiency severely impairs host defense, leading to exacerbated disease including weight loss, severe pulmonary inflammation, impaired bacterial clearance, and extensive alveolar damage. Mechanistically, HDAC6 loss causes enhanced STAT6 acetylation, which promotes a pathological shift toward Th2 and M2 macrophage responses characterized by elevated IL-4, IL-10, and TGF-β production, creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment that fails to clear infection. These findings highlight HDAC6 as a central epigenetic regulator that coordinates appropriate immune responses by suppressing excessive Th2-M2 polarization, which would otherwise compromise the Th1-M1 pro-inflammatory responses necessary for effective bacterial elimination.

title: Endogenous mediators in regulating primary dysmenorrhea
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary: Endogenous Mediators in Primary Dysmenorrhea

Primary dysmenorrhea results from a complex inflammatory cascade triggered by progesterone withdrawal in the late luteal phase, which initiates excessive production of prostaglandins and pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6. Estradiol amplifies this response by promoting prostaglandin production and activating calcium channels (TRPV6), while progesterone normally provides analgesic effects through receptor binding and inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis. The dysmenorrheic state involves multiple converging mechanisms: elevated vasopressin and oxytocin promote myometrial contractions, endothelin-1 induces vasospasm and synergizes with PGF2α, and chemokines like IL-8 and MCP-1 recruit leukocytes to the endometrium. Oxidative stress markers including reactive oxygen species, malondialdehyde, and nitric oxide are elevated and drive inflammation through NF-κB activation, while protective mechanisms such as β-endorphin and the AMPK signaling pathway are reduced, creating an imbalance that perpetuates pain and uterine dysfunction. These findings identify multiple therapeutic targets beyond prostaglandin inhibition, including modulation of cytokine signaling, oxidative stress reduction, and restoration of endogenous analgesic systems.

title: Differential expression of vaginal microenvironment cytokines in relation to delivery modes and their impact on postpartum pelvic floor recovery
summary:
# Summary

Childbirth triggers a coordinated inflammatory and tissue repair response in the vaginal microenvironment, with delivery mode significantly influencing cytokine expression patterns. Spontaneous vaginal delivery and instrumental delivery upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines—IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8—that recruit immune cells and matrix-degrading enzymes (MMP-1 and MMP-2), whereas cesarean section produces attenuated local immune activation. The resolution phase depends on anti-inflammatory mediators IL-10 and TGF-β, which promote macrophage polarization toward reparative phenotypes, stimulate collagen synthesis and fibroblast proliferation, and mediate angiogenesis through VEGF signaling. A healthy vaginal microbiota dominated by Lactobacillus species produces lactic acid that modulates mucosal immunity and suppresses pro-inflammatory IL-6 and TNF-α, with Lactobacillus abundance correlating directly with pelvic floor muscle recovery. Dysregulation of this cytokine balance—characterized by elevated IL-6 and TNF-α with insufficient IL-10 and TGF-β—contributes to chronic inflammation, excessive collagen deposition, and pelvic floor dysfunction including prolapse and urinary incontinence. Therapeutic interventions targeting microbiota restoration, manual lymphatic drainage, and pelvic floor electrical stimulation can modulate inflammatory mediators and promote tissue healing.

title: An Optimized Conversion of Spermatogonial Stem Cells Into Spinal Cord Neurons Enhances Functional Recovery in Rats After Spinal Cord Injury
summary: This study demonstrates that optimized differentiation of spermatogonial stem cells (SSCs) into spinal cord neurons, achieved through suppression of the JAK2/STAT3 signaling pathway, significantly enhances functional recovery following spinal cord injury in rats. The inflammatory response after spinal cord injury critically exacerbates neural damage and impedes repair by facilitating infiltration of inflammatory cells that release damaging cytokines and oxidative stress products. To overcome this hostile inflammatory environment, the researchers employed olfactory ensheathing cells (OECs) activated with curcumin, which suppress inflammatory responses while secreting neurotrophic factors including brain-derived neurotrophic factor that promote neuronal survival and inhibit excessive inflammatory activation. The OEC induction system dramatically enhanced SSC differentiation efficiency into spinal cord neurons, an effect further improved by JAK2/STAT3 pathway inhibition, while reducing stem cell apoptosis. Transplantation of these optimized differentiated neurons into the lesioned spinal cord successfully restored sensory and motor function in treated animals, establishing a promising therapeutic strategy that combines stem cell engineering with inflammatory modulation.
